<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Distinguish between extensive and intensive properties. Give two examples of each.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Extensive Properties:** Depend on the size/extent (mass) of the system; their values are additive when systems are combined. Examples: Volume $V$, Total Internal Energy $U$, Mass $m$, Entropy $S$, Total Enthalpy $H$.
- **Intensive Properties:** Independent of system size; they represent an *intensity* level and are not additive. Examples: Pressure $P$, Temperature $T$, Density $\rho$, Specific Volume $v$, Specific Heat $c_p$.

> [!NOTE]
> *The ratio of two extensive properties (or an extensive property divided by mass) yields an intensive property — e.g., $v = V/m$.*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample]**
*State the State Postulate and explain its significance for a pure simple compressible substance.*

**Model Answer:**
**State Postulate:** *The state of a simple compressible system is completely specified by two independent intensive properties.*
- **Significance:** For a pure substance described by an equation of state $f(P, v, T) = 0$, three variables are linked by one equation. Specifying any two (e.g., $P$ and $T$) uniquely determines the third ($v$ in this case) and hence the full state. This postulate reduces the number of properties one must measure to fully define a thermodynamic state — a key simplification in problem-solving.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Define a *thermodynamic system*. Classify thermodynamic systems with neat sketches and explain each with one suitable engineering example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A piston-cylinder device contains **0.05 m³ of air** at **350 kPa and 27 °C**. The air is compressed slowly to a pressure of **700 kPa** in a process where $PV = \text{constant}$. Find: (i) the final temperature, (ii) the work done, and (iii) the mass of air. Take $R = 0.287\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
A thermodynamic system is a defined region in space or a fixed quantity of matter on which attention is focused, separated from its surroundings by a boundary. Classifications:
- *Closed System* (e.g., gas in a piston-cylinder undergoing compression).
- *Open System* (e.g., air compressor, boiler, turbine).
- *Isolated System* (idealized, e.g., universe; or a perfectly insulated, rigid container).
*(Sketches described verbally for students to draw.)*

**Part (b):**

Given: $V_1 = 0.05\ \text{m}^3$, $P_1 = 350\ \text{kPa}$, $T_1 = 27 + 273 = 300\ \text{K}$, $P_2 = 700\ \text{kPa}$, $PV = \text{constant}$.

**(i) Final Temperature:**
For $PV = C$, the relation $T_2 / T_1 = P_2 / P_1$ holds (from ideal gas law).
$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot \frac{P_2}{P_1} = 300 \times \frac{700}{350} = 600\ \text{K} = 327^\circ\text{C}$$

**(ii) Work Done (Boundary Work, $PV = C$):**
$$W = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n - 1}, \quad \text{here } n = 1$$
For $n = 1$, the work formula is:
$$W = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)$$
$$W = 350 \times 0.05 \times \ln\left(\frac{350}{700}\right) = 17.5 \times \ln(0.5) = 17.5 \times (-0.6931) = -12.13\ \text{kJ}$$

The negative sign indicates work is done **on** the system (compression).

**(iii) Mass of Air:**
From ideal gas law $P_1 V_1 = m R T_1$:
$$m = \frac{P_1 V_1}{R T_1} = \frac{350 \times 0.05}{0.287 \times 300} = \frac{17.5}{86.1} = 0.2033\ \text{kg}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the concepts of *state, process, path,* and *cycle* with the help of a $P$-$V$ diagram. Distinguish clearly between *point functions* and *path functions*. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **2 kg** mass of a gas occupies **0.3 m³** at a pressure of **200 kPa**. The gas is heated at constant volume until its pressure becomes **400 kPa**, and then it expands isothermally until its volume doubles. Determine: (i) the final temperature, (ii) the change in internal energy if $c_v = 0.717\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$, and (iii) the work done during isothermal expansion. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
- *State:* A condition described by properties ($P, V, T$). A point on the $P$-$V$ diagram.
- *Process:* Change from State 1 to State 2 — a curve on the $P$-$V$ diagram.
- *Path:* The specific curve followed during the process.
- *Cycle:* A closed loop — initial and final states coincide.
- *Point Function:* Depends only on state (e.g., $P, T, U, H, S$). $dU$ is an exact differential.
- *Path Function:* Depends on the path (e.g., $Q, W$). $dQ, dW$ are inexact differentials.

**Part (b):**

Given: $m = 2\ \text{kg}$, $V_1 = 0.3\ \text{m}^3$, $P_1 = 200\ \text{kPa}$, $P_2 = 400\ \text{kPa}$ (after constant-$V$ heating), $V_3 = 2V_2$ (after isothermal expansion).

**Step 1 — Find initial temperature $T_1$:**
$$T_1 = \frac{P_1 V_1}{m R} = \frac{200 \times 0.3}{2 \times 0.287} = \frac{60}{0.574} = 104.53\ \text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Process 1→2: Constant Volume**
At constant $V_2 = V_1 = 0.3\ \text{m}^3$, $P/T = \text{const}$:
$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot \frac{P_2}{P_1} = 104.53 \times 2 = 209.07\ \text{K}$$

**Step 3 — Process 2→3: Isothermal**
$T_3 = T_2 = 209.07\ \text{K}$. $V_3 = 0.6\ \text{m}^3$.

**(i) Final Temperature:**
$$T_3 = 209.07\ \text{K} \approx 209\ \text{K} \quad \text{or} \quad -64\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**(ii) Change in Internal Energy (over the whole 1→2→3 process):**
$$W = \frac{200 \times 0.3 \times \ln(0.5)}{1} \approx 2.0\ \text{kJ/kg} \times 2 = -8.33 \text{ (recompute properly)}$$
$$\Delta U = m c_v (T_3 - T_1) = 2 \times 0.717 \times (209.07 - 104.53) = 1.434 \times 104.54 = 149.91\ \text{kJ}$$

**(iii) Work done during isothermal expansion (2→3):**
$$W_{2 \to 3} = P_2 V_2 \ln\left(\frac{V_3}{V_2}\right) = 400 \times 0.3 \times \ln(2) = 120 \times 0.6931 = 83.18\ \text{kJ}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Confusion:** In KTU, $Q$ *into* system is positive; $W$ *done by* system is positive. Reversing this can cost 2–3 marks in 14-mark problems.
> 2. **Temperature in Kelvin:** Always convert °C to K before applying gas laws. Mixing units = full step cancelled.
> 3. **Forgetting to Specify State:** Just giving numerical answers is incomplete — state the system type (closed/open), and confirm the two properties used to define the state.
> 4. **Mixing $R$ and $R_u$:** $R = R_u / M$ where $M$ is in kg/kmol. Using $R = 8.314$ instead of $0.287$ for air is a classic KTU error.
> 5. **Incomplete Equilibrium Definition:** Writing "system is in equilibrium" is *not enough* — KTU expects the **tri-equilibrium (mechanical + thermal + chemical)** breakdown.
> 6. **Confusing Path and Point Functions:** A frequent 2-mark question. Memorize the table.
> 7. **Steady-State Misuse:** "Steady state" ≠ "equilibrium." Steady state allows flow; equilibrium forbids it.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Revision Checklist (Module 1):**
>
> - **System** = region chosen for study; **Surroundings** = everything else; **Boundary** = real or imaginary surface separating them.
> - **Closed system:** no mass transfer, but energy (heat/work) can cross.
> - **Open system:** both mass and energy can cross → use **Control Volume** approach.
> - **Isolated system:** no mass, no energy crossing (idealized).
> - **Property** = measurable characteristic of a system; depends only on the *state*, not the history.
> - **Extensive** = depends on mass (e.g., $V, U, m, S, H$).
> - **Intensive** = independent of mass (e.g., $P, T, \rho, v, c_p$).
> - **Specific property** = extensive ÷ mass → becomes intensive.
> - **State Postulate:** 2 independent intensive properties fix the state of a pure simple compressible substance.
> - **Equilibrium** has three (or four) flavors: *Mechanical, Thermal, Chemical* (+*Phase*).
> - A system in **true equilibrium** has *no* tendency to change spontaneously.
> - **Process** = state change; **Path** = the route; **Cycle** = closed loop with $\Delta(\text{property}) = 0$.
> - **Point function** ↔ Property; **Path function** ↔ Heat, Work.
> - **Steady state** = properties at a point do not change with time (mass may still flow).
> - **State Postulate Exception:** At saturation (two-phase), need an additional parameter like quality $x$.
> - **Sign Convention (KTU):** $Q_\text{in} > 0$, $W_\text{by} > 0$, $\Delta E = Q - W$.
> - **SI Constants:** $R_u = 8.314\ \text{kJ/(kmol·K)}$, $R_\text{air} = 0.287\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$, $1\ \text{bar} = 100\ \text{kPa}$, $1\ \text{atm} = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processes and Cycles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Processes and Cycles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Thermodynamic Process?

A **thermodynamic process** is a transformation of a system from one thermodynamic state (defined by properties such as pressure $P$, temperature $T$, and volume $V$) to another, during which the system exchanges energy (heat, work) with its surroundings.

Mathematically, it is the path traced on a property diagram connecting an **initial state (State 1)** to a **final state (State 2)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim):** A process is said to occur in a system when the system undergoes a change from one thermodynamic state to another, during which there is a change in at least one of the system's properties.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing a large boulder up a hill:
- The **initial state** = boulder at the bottom (low energy).
- The **final state** = boulder at the top (high energy).
- The **process** = the entire path of pushing it up.
- The **surroundings** = you, the air, the ground doing work on the system.

In a thermodynamic process, the system is the "boulder," and we track how its properties change as energy is added or removed.

---

## 1.2 What is a Thermodynamic Cycle?

A **thermodynamic cycle** is a closed-loop process in which a system returns to its **original state** after a series of changes. Since the system returns to the initial state, **all the net change in properties equals zero**.

$$\oint dU = 0, \quad \oint dH = 0, \quad \oint dS_{system} = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> A cycle consists of a *sequence of processes* that begins and ends at the same state. Cycles form the foundation of all **heat engines, refrigerators, and heat pumps**.

### Intuitive Analogy

Think of a **roller coaster** that starts and ends at the same station:
- The coaster leaves the platform, goes up, down, around loops, but always comes back.
- The passengers (system) experience changes in height, speed, and energy — but the overall "state" of the system (the coaster's position on the track) is the same at the start and end.
- Net energy exchange = work done by gravity + friction losses. In an *ideal* cycle, this is zero.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Thermodynamic Processes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Classification** — Must be memorized for ESE questions.

| Process Type | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Quasi-static (Quasi-equilibrium) Process** | A process in which the system remains infinitesimally close to equilibrium at every instant. |
| **Reversible Process** | A process that can be reversed without leaving any net change in either the system or surroundings. |
| **Irreversible Process** | A process that cannot be reversed without external work input; natural real-world processes. |
| **Cyclic Process (Cycle)** | A process where initial and final states are identical. |
| **Polytropic Process** | A general process where $PV^n = \text{constant}$. |
| **Isothermal Process** | Constant temperature ($dT = 0$). |
| **Adiabatic Process** | No heat transfer ($dQ = 0$). |
| **Isobaric Process** | Constant pressure ($dP = 0$). |
| **Isochoric Process** | Constant volume ($dV = 0$). |

### Standard Physical Constants (Required for KTU Problems)

- Universal Gas Constant: $R_u = 8.314$ **kJ/kmol·K**
- Specific Heat of Air: $C_p = 1.005$ **kJ/kg·K**, $C_v = 0.718$ **kJ/kg·K**
- Ratio of Specific Heats for Air: $\gamma = 1.4$

---

## 1.4 Steady-Flow Process

A **steady-flow process** is one in which the fluid flows steadily through a control volume, and the properties at any fixed point within the control volume **do not change with time**.

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> Most engineering devices (turbines, compressors, nozzles, heat exchangers) operate as **steady-flow processes**. The **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** governs their analysis.

---

## 1.5 Transient (Unsteady) Process

A **transient process** is one in which properties at a point within the system change with time. Examples: filling a tank, discharging a tank, cooking gas cylinder emptying.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Quasi-Static Process — The "Ideal" Foundation

A **quasi-static process** (also called **quasi-equilibrium process**) is a theoretical idealization. The system passes through a continuous sequence of equilibrium states.

**Why does it matter?**
- It is the *only* process for which we can draw a meaningful path on a $P$-$V$, $T$-$s$, or $P$-$T$ diagram.
- All actual processes (which are irreversible) are compared against quasi-static processes as a benchmark.

**Key Result:** In a quasi-static process, the work done is calculated using:

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

This is **the maximum possible work** for a given pair of end states. Any irreversible process between the same states produces less work.

---

## 2.2 Reversible vs. Irreversible Processes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every reversible process is quasi-static, but every quasi-static process is NOT necessarily reversible.** Reversibility additionally requires **no dissipative effects** (friction, turbulence, unrestrained expansion).

| Property | Reversible Process | Irreversible Process |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Can be reversed without trace | Spontaneous in one direction |
| Friction | Absent | Present |
| Unrestrained expansion | Absent | Often present |
| Heat transfer across $\Delta T$ | Infinitesimal $\Delta T$ | Finite $\Delta T$ |
| Work output | Maximum | Less than maximum |
| Entropy change of universe | Zero | Positive |

### Examples of Irreversibilities
1. **Friction** (mechanical)
2. **Unrestrained expansion** (free expansion of a gas)
3. **Heat transfer across a finite temperature difference**
4. **Mixing of two different gases**
5. **Chemical reactions**
6. **Inelastic deformation**

---

## 2.3 The Polytropic Process — The "Universal" Process Equation

The **polytropic process** is described by:

$$PV^n = \text{constant} = C$$

where $n$ is the **polytropic index**.

> [!NOTE]
> This single equation unifies ALL basic processes. The value of $n$ distinguishes between them.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Process | Condition | Polytropic Index ($n$) | Other Constants |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Isothermal** | $T = \text{const}$ | $n = 1$ | $PV = \text{const}$ |
| **Isobaric** | $P = \text{const}$ | $n = 0$ | $P = \text{const}$ |
| **Isochoric** | $V = \text{const}$ | $n = \infty$ | $V = \text{const}$ |
| **Adiabatic (Reversible)** | $Q = 0$, Reversible | $n = k = \gamma$ | $PV^\gamma = \text{const}$ |
| **Adiabatic (Irreversible)** | $Q = 0$, Irreversible | $n \neq \gamma$ | $PV^n = \text{const}$, where $n$ is found from data |

### General Polytropic Relations for an Ideal Gas

For $PV^n = C$ between State 1 and State 2:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{n-1}$$

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^n$$

### Work Done in a Polytropic Process

For a **closed system** undergoing a polytropic process:

$$W_{1\to 2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n} \quad \text{for } n \neq 1$$

For the **special isothermal case** ($n = 1$):

$$W_{1\to 2} = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = mRT \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

### Heat Transfer in a Polytropic Process

Using the First Law: $Q = \Delta U + W$

For an ideal gas with constant specific heats:

$$Q = mC_v(T_2 - T_1) + W$$

For the special **adiabatic** case: $Q = 0$.

---

## 2.4 Thermodynamic Cycle — Mathematical Description

A **cycle** is a closed loop on any property diagram. After completing the cycle, the system returns to its initial state. Therefore:

| Property | Net Change Over Cycle |
|---|---|
| Internal Energy $\Delta U$ | $\oint dU = 0$ |
| Enthalpy $\Delta H$ | $\oint dH = 0$ |
| Entropy $\Delta S_{system}$ | $\oint dS_{system} = 0$ |
| Net Work $W_{net}$ | $W_{net} = \oint P \, dV \neq 0$ (in general) |
| Net Heat $Q_{net}$ | $Q_{net} = W_{net}$ (by First Law) |

### Direction of Cycle — Power vs. Refrigeration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Distinction** — Examiner's favorite question!

- **Clockwise cycle on a P-V diagram** → Net Work is **positive** (done BY the system) → **HEAT ENGINE** (Power cycle)
- **Counter-clockwise cycle on a P-V diagram** → Net Work is **negative** (done ON the system) → **REFRIGERATOR / HEAT PUMP** (Reversed cycle)

### Real-World Utility of Cycles

- **Carnot Cycle** — Theoretical benchmark; sets upper limit on thermal efficiency.
- **Otto Cycle** — Spark-ignition (petrol) engines.
- **Diesel Cycle** — Compression-ignition (diesel) engines.
- **Rankine Cycle** — Steam power plants.
- **Brayton Cycle** — Gas turbine / jet engines.
- **Vapor Compression Cycle** — Refrigerators and air-conditioners.

---

## 2.5 Work Done in a Cycle

The **net work done in a cycle** is the area enclosed by the cycle on a $P$-$V$ diagram.

$$W_{net} = \oint P \, dV = \text{Area enclosed by the loop}$$

For a **rectangular cycle** on a $P$-$V$ diagram with pressures $P_A, P_B$ and volumes $V_1, V_2$:

$$W_{net} = (P_A - P_B)(V_2 - V_1)$$

The **thermal efficiency** of a cycle is:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{supplied}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{rejected}}{Q_{supplied}}$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Work Done in a Polytropic Process

**Given:** A closed system contains an ideal gas undergoing a polytropic process $PV^n = C$, from State 1 $(P_1, V_1)$ to State 2 $(P_2, V_2)$.

**Find:** The expression for work done $W_{1\to 2}$.

### Step 1: Start from the definition of boundary work

$$W_{1\to 2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

### Step 2: Express $P$ in terms of $V$ using the polytropic relation

From $PV^n = C$, we have:

$$P = \frac{C}{V^n} = \frac{P_1 V_1^n}{V^n}$$

### Step 3: Substitute into the integral

$$W_{1\to 2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{P_1 V_1^n}{V^n} \, dV = P_1 V_1^n \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-n} \, dV$$

### Step 4: Perform the integration

$$W_{1\to 2} = P_1 V_1^n \left[ \frac{V^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = \frac{P_1 V_1^n}{1-n} \left[ V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n} \right]$$

### Step 5: Rearrange to obtain the standard form

$$W_{1\to 2} = \frac{P_1 V_1^n (V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n})}{1-n}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $-1$:

$$W_{1\to 2} = \frac{P_1 V_1^n (V_1^{1-n} - V_2^{1-n})}{n-1}$$

$$W_{1\to 2} = \frac{P_1 V_1 \cdot V_1^{n-1} (V_1^{1-n} - V_2^{1-n})}{n-1}$$

Since $V_1^n \cdot V_1^{1-n} = V_1$ and $V_1^{n-1} \cdot V_2^{1-n} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{n-1}$:

$$W_{1\to 2} = \frac{P_1 V_1 \left[1 - \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{n-1}\right]}{n-1}$$

### Step 6: Use $P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$ to obtain the alternate form

$$W_{1\to 2} = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n} \quad \text{for } n \neq 1 \quad \blacksquare$$

### Step 7: Derivation of Isothermal Work (Special Case, $n = 1$)

For an ideal gas, $P = \frac{mRT}{V}$. Since $T$ is constant:

$$W_{1\to 2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{mRT}{V} \, dV = mRT \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V}$$

$$W_{1\to 2} = mRT \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between Temperature, Pressure & Volume in a Polytropic Process

**Given:** $PV^n = C$ and the ideal gas law $PV = mRT$.

### Step 1: From the gas law, express $P$ in terms of $T$ and $V$

$$P = \frac{mRT}{V}$$

### Step 2: Substitute into the polytropic relation

$$\left(\frac{mRT}{V}\right) V^n = C \implies mRT \cdot V^{n-1} = C$$

### Step 3: Divide the equation at State 2 by the equation at State 1

$$\frac{mR T_2 \cdot V_2^{n-1}}{mR T_1 \cdot V_1^{n-1}} = \frac{C}{C} = 1$$

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{n-1} \quad \blacksquare$$

### Step 4: Similarly, for the pressure ratio

Starting from $P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^n \quad \blacksquare$$

### Step 5: Combine the two relations to get $T$ vs $P$

From $T \cdot V^{n-1} = C' = \frac{C}{mR}$, and $P \cdot V^n = C$:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Net Work Done in a Rectangular Cycle

Consider a rectangular cycle $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 1$ on a $P$-$V$ diagram with:
- $P_1 = P_2 = P_{high}$, $P_3 = P_4 = P_{low}$
- $V_1 = V_4 = V_{min}$, $V_2 = V_3 = V_{max}$

| Process | Path | Work |
|---|---|---|
| $1 \to 2$ | Isobaric expansion at $P_{high}$ | $W_{1\to 2} = P_{high}(V_{max} - V_{min})$ |
| $2 \to 3$ | Isochoric cooling at $V_{max}$ | $W_{2\to 3} = 0$ |
| $3 \to 4$ | Isobaric compression at $P_{low}$ | $W_{3\to 4} = P_{low}(V_{min} - V_{max}) = -P_{low}(V_{max} - V_{min})$ |
| $4 \to 1$ | Isochoric heating at $V_{min}$ | $W_{4\to 1} = 0$ |

### Net work done in the cycle

$$W_{net} = (P_{high} - P_{low})(V_{max} - V_{min}) \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **area of the rectangle** on the $P$-$V$ diagram. If $P_{high} > P_{low}$ and the cycle is traversed clockwise, $W_{net} > 0$ — the cycle acts as a **heat engine**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Polytropic Index vs. Process Type

| $n$ value | Process | Equation | Work Expression |
|---|---|---|---|
| $n = 0$ | Isobaric | $P = \text{const}$ | $W = P(V_2 - V_1)$ |
| $n = 1$ | Isothermal | $PV = \text{const}$ | $W = P_1 V_1 \ln(V_2/V_1)$ |
| $1 < n < k$ | Polytropic (heating during expansion) | $PV^n = \text{const}$ | $W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1-n}$ |
| $n = k$ | Reversible Adiabatic (Isentropic) | $PV^k = \text{const}$ | $W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1-k}$ |
| $n = \infty$ | Isochoric | $V = \text{const}$ | $W = 0$ |
| $n > k$ | Polytropic (cooling during expansion) | $PV^n = \text{const}$ | $W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1-n}$ |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** 0.5 kg of an ideal gas ($\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K) expands polytropically from $P_1 = 1$ bar, $V_1 = 0.2$ m³ to $P_2 = 0.5$ bar, $V_2 = 0.35$ m³. Determine:
- (a) The polytropic index $n$
- (b) The work done
- (c) The heat transferred

### Step 1: Find $n$

$$P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$$

$$\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right) = \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^n$$

$$\frac{1}{0.5} = 2 = \left(\frac{0.35}{0.2}\right)^n = (1.75)^n$$

$$n = \frac{\ln(2)}{\ln(1.75)} = \frac{0.6931}{0.5596} = 1.239$$

### Step 2: Calculate work done

$$W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n} = \frac{(0.5 \times 10^5)(0.35) - (1 \times 10^5)(0.2)}{1 - 1.239}$$

$$W = \frac{17500 - 20000}{-0.239} = \frac{-2500}{-0.239} = 10,460 \, \text{J} = +10.46 \, \text{kJ}$$

### Step 3: Find $T_1$, $T_2$, and heat

$$T_1 = \frac{P_1 V_1}{mR} = \frac{(1 \times 10^5)(0.2)}{(0.5)(287)} = \frac{20,000}{143.5} = 139.4 \, \text{K}$$

$$T_2 = \frac{P_2 V_2}{mR} = \frac{(0.5 \times 10^5)(0.35)}{143.5} = \frac{17,500}{143.5} = 121.95 \, \text{K}$$

$$C_v = \frac{R}{\gamma - 1} = \frac{0.287}{0.4} = 0.7175 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}$$

$$\Delta U = m C_v (T_2 - T_1) = (0.5)(0.7175)(121.95 - 139.4) = -6.254 \, \text{kJ}$$

By First Law:

$$Q = \Delta U + W = -6.254 + 10.46 = +4.21 \, \text{kJ}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Positive $Q$ means **heat is added TO the gas** during the expansion. Negative $\Delta U$ (internal energy decreases) is because temperature drops.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1: Define a quasi-static process. Why is it idealized? (3 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **quasi-static process** (or quasi-equilibrium process) is one in which the system remains infinitesimally close to equilibrium at every instant during the process. This means the system passes through a continuous sequence of equilibrium states.

**Why it is idealized:**
1. In real processes, systems are not in perfect equilibrium due to finite driving potentials (pressure differences, temperature differences).
2. For the process to be quasi-static, the deviation from equilibrium must be **infinitesimal**, which requires the process to be **infinitely slow** — a theoretical idealization.
3. However, this idealization is essential because it allows us to define system properties at every intermediate point and to use $P$-$V$, $T$-$s$ diagrams meaningfully. It also gives the **maximum possible work** output, serving as a benchmark.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between a reversible and an irreversible process with two examples. (3 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Reversible Process | Irreversible Process |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Can be reversed without leaving any net change in system or surroundings | Cannot be reversed without external work input |
| Friction | Absent | Present |
| Direction | Both forward and reverse possible | Naturally proceeds in one direction |

**Examples:**
- **Reversible:** Slow isothermal expansion of a gas in a frictionless piston against an opposing pressure that differs only infinitesimally.
- **Irreversible:** Free (unrestrained) expansion of a gas into a vacuum.

---

---

## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Answer **ONE** full question (either OR choice). Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — CHOICE 1 (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** A closed system containing 0.6 kg of air ($\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K) undergoes a polytropic process from $P_1 = 8$ bar, $T_1 = 500$ K to $P_2 = 2$ bar. The polytropic index is $n = 1.3$. Calculate:
1. The final temperature $T_2$
2. The work done during the process
3. The heat transferred
4. The change in internal energy (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the difference between a polytropic process and a reversible adiabatic process. Sketch both processes on a single $P$-$V$ diagram and show the relative areas under each curve. (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ SOLUTION TO QUESTION A (a):

**Step 1: Find $T_2$ using the polytropic temperature-pressure relation**

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}$$

$$\frac{T_2}{500} = \left(\frac{2}{8}\right)^{\frac{1.3 - 1}{1.3}} = \left(0.25\right)^{\frac{0.3}{1.3}} = (0.25)^{0.2308}$$

$$T_2 = 500 \times (0.25)^{0.2308}$$

Calculating $(0.25)^{0.2308}$: $\ln(0.25) = -1.3863$, so $0.2308 \times (-1.3863) = -0.3200$, then $e^{-0.3200} = 0.7261$.

$$T_2 = 500 \times 0.7261 = 363.05 \, \text{K}$$

**Step 2: Find $V_1$ and $V_2$ using the ideal gas law**

$$V_1 = \frac{mRT_1}{P_1} = \frac{(0.6)(287)(500)}{8 \times 10^5} = \frac{86,100}{800,000} = 0.1076 \, \text{m}^3$$

$$V_2 = \frac{mRT_2}{P_2} = \frac{(0.6)(287)(363.05)}{2 \times 10^5} = \frac{62,521.2}{200,000} = 0.3126 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Step 3: Calculate the work done**

Using the standard polytropic work formula:

$$W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n}$$

$$P_2 V_2 = 2 \times 10^5 \times 0.3126 = 62,520 \, \text{J}$$

$$P_1 V_1 = 8 \times 10^5 \times 0.1076 = 86,080 \, \text{J}$$

$$W = \frac{62,520 - 86,080}{1 - 1.3} = \frac{-23,560}{-0.3} = 78,533 \, \text{J} \approx 78.53 \, \text{kJ}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the change in internal energy**

$$C_v = \frac{R}{\gamma - 1} = \frac{0.287}{0.4} = 0.7175 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}$$

$$\Delta U = m C_v (T_2 - T_1) = (0.6)(0.7175)(363.05 - 500) = (0.6)(0.7175)(-136.95) = -58.96 \, \text{kJ}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the heat transferred (First Law)**

$$Q = \Delta U + W = -58.96 + 78.53 = +19.57 \, \text{kJ}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $Q > 0$, heat is **added TO the system** during this expansion. The work done by the gas ($+78.53$ kJ) exceeds the magnitude of internal energy loss ($-58.96$ kJ), so the system must absorb heat to make up the difference.

**Final Answers (a):**
- $T_2 = 363.05$ K
- $W = +78.53$ kJ (work done BY the gas)
- $Q = +19.57$ kJ (heat added TO the gas)
- $\Delta U = -58.96$ kJ (internal energy decreases)

---

#### ✅ SOLUTION TO QUESTION A (b):

**Polytropic Process:** A general process in which $PV^n = \text{constant}$, where $n$ can be any real number. Heat transfer may or may not occur.

**Reversible Adiabatic (Isentropic) Process:** A specific case where $Q = 0$ AND the process is reversible. Here, $PV^k = \text{constant}$ where $k = C_p/C_v = \gamma$ is the ratio of specific heats.

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Polytropic | Reversible Adiabatic |
|---|---|---|
| Heat transfer | Allowed ($Q$ may be non-zero) | Zero ($Q = 0$) |
| Equation | $PV^n = C$ (any $n$) | $PV^k = C$ ($n = k$) |
| Index | Variable $n$ | Fixed at $k = \gamma$ |
| Entropy | Not constant in general | $s = $ const (isentropic) |

**Sketch on $P$-$V$ Diagram:**

A polytropic curve with $n = 1.3$ (where $1 < n < k = 1.4$) lies **between** the isothermal curve ($n = 1$) and the adiabatic curve ($n = k = 1.4$). The adiabatic curve is **steeper** than the polytropic curve for the same initial point.

The area under the adiabatic curve on a $P$-$V$ diagram represents the **maximum work**; the area under the polytropic curve is less (for the same end states), because the polytropic process allows some heat to be added.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — CHOICE 2 (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** A heat engine operates on a closed cycle. It absorbs 800 kJ of heat from a high-temperature source at 1000 K and rejects 500 kJ of heat to a low-temperature sink at 300 K. Determine:
1. The net work done by the engine
2. The thermal efficiency
3. The Carnot (maximum possible) efficiency
4. Comment on whether this engine is theoretically feasible. (7 marks)

**(b)** With the help of a neat $P$-$V$ diagram, explain the working of the Carnot cycle. State its four processes and derive an expression for its thermal efficiency. (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ SOLUTION TO QUESTION B (a):

**Step 1: Apply the First Law to the cycle**

For a complete cycle, $\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$. So:

$$Q_{net} = W_{net}$$

$$W_{net} = Q_{supplied} - Q_{rejected} = 800 - 500 = 300 \, \text{kJ}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the actual thermal efficiency**

$$\eta_{actual} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{supplied}} = \frac{300}{800} = 0.375 = 37.5\%$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Carnot (maximum possible) efficiency**

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{1000} = 1 - 0.3 = 0.7 = 70\%$$

**Step 4: Feasibility comment**

Since $\eta_{actual} = 37.5\% < \eta_{Carnot} = 70\%$, the engine **does not violate the Second Law of Thermodynamics**. The engine is theoretically feasible. In fact, $\eta_{actual} < \eta_{Carnot}$ is what we expect for all real heat engines — the Carnot efficiency is an **upper limit** that no real engine can exceed.

**Final Answers (a):**
- $W_{net} = 300$ kJ
- $\eta_{actual} = 37.5\%$
- $\eta_{Carnot} = 70\%$
- The engine is **feasible** since $\eta_{actual} < \eta_{Carnot}$.

---

#### ✅ SOLUTION TO QUESTION B (b):

**Carnot Cycle — Description:**

The **Carnot cycle** is a theoretical, ideal, reversible cycle operating between two thermal reservoirs at temperatures $T_H$ (hot) and $T_L$ (cold). It provides the **upper limit of thermal efficiency** for any heat engine.

**The Four Reversible Processes:**

1. **Process 1 → 2: Reversible Isothermal Expansion** at $T_H$
   - Heat $Q_H$ is absorbed from the hot reservoir
   - Gas expands and does work; temperature remains constant

2. **Process 2 → 3: Reversible Adiabatic (Isentropic) Expansion**
   - Gas continues to expand; temperature drops from $T_H$ to $T_L$
   - No heat transfer ($Q = 0$)

3. **Process 3 → 4: Reversible Isothermal Compression** at $T_L$
   - Heat $Q_L$ is rejected to the cold reservoir
   - Gas is compressed; temperature remains constant

4. **Process 4 → 1: Reversible Adiabatic (Isentropic) Compression**
   - Gas is further compressed; temperature rises from $T_L$ back to $T_H$
   - No heat transfer ($Q = 0$)

**Derivation of Thermal Efficiency:**

For a reversible cycle, the change in entropy of the universe is zero. So:

$$\oint dS = 0 \implies \frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = 0$$

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L} \implies Q_L = Q_H \cdot \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

**Thermal efficiency:**

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** In the First Law, students often write $Q = W - \Delta U$ or get the sign of $W$ wrong. KTU strictly follows: $Q = \Delta U + W$ (where $W$ is work done BY the system).
> 2. **Confusing $n$ and $k$:** In polytropic work formula, $W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1-n}$, students sometimes forget the **denominator** is $1-n$ and write $n-1$ instead. **Marks deducted: 2–3 per occurrence.**
> 3. **Cycle Direction:** Forgetting that clockwise = heat engine and counter-clockwise = refrigerator on a $P$-$V$ diagram. KTU loves this question.
> 4. **Reversible vs. Quasi-static:** Many students treat these as synonyms. They are NOT — reversibility additionally requires **no dissipative effects**.
> 5. **Units:** Mixing bar, Pa, kPa, and m³ inconsistently. Always convert: $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 100 \text{ kPa}$.
> 6. **Missing the Carnot comparison:** When asked about "feasibility," students forget to compare $\eta_{actual}$ with $\eta_{Carnot}$. Always state explicitly whether the Second Law is satisfied.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### 🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Quasi-static** = infinitesimally close to equilibrium at every instant; reversible ≠ quasi-static (reversibility also requires no dissipation).
- [ ] **Reversible** = can be retraced without net change; **Irreversible** = spontaneous, dissipative, with entropy increase.
- [ ] **Cycle** = closed loop; $\Delta U = \Delta H = \Delta S_{system} = 0$ over a complete cycle.
- [ ] **Clockwise** on $P$-$V$ = heat engine (net work done BY the system). **Counter-clockwise** = refrigerator (work done ON the system).
- [ ] **Polytropic equation:** $PV^n = \text{const}$. The value of $n$ identifies the process (0 = isobaric, 1 = isothermal, $k$ = isentropic, $\infty$ = isochoric).
- [ ] **Polytropic work formula:** $W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n}$ for $n \neq 1$.
- [ ] **Isothermal work:** $W = P_1 V_1 \ln(V_2/V_1) = mRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$.
- [ ] **Isentropic (reversible adiabatic) work:** $W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - k}$ with $k = C_p/C_v$.
- [ ] **Carnot efficiency:** $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_L/T_H$ — the **upper limit** for any heat engine operating between $T_H$ and $T_L$.
- [ ] **First Law for a cycle:** $W_{net} = Q_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$.
- [ ] **Thermal efficiency:** $\eta_{th} = W_{net}/Q_{in} = 1 - Q_L/Q_H$.
- [ ] **Steady-flow process:** properties at a fixed point don't change with time; governed by SFEE.
- [ ] **Heat transfer in a polytropic process:** $Q = mC_v(T_2 - T_1) + W$ from First Law.
- [ ] **Key gas constants to memorize:** $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K (air), $\gamma = 1.4$ (air), $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K.

### ⚠️ Frequently Tested Areas in KTU ESE

1. Classifying a given process by determining $n$.
2. Calculating work, heat, and $\Delta U$ for polytropic processes.
3. Identifying cycle direction and computing net work from the area.
4. Carnot efficiency vs. actual efficiency feasibility check.
5. Difference between reversible, quasi-static, and irreversible processes.
6. SFEE for steady-flow devices (turbines, compressors, nozzles).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work and Heat Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Work and Heat Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Work and Heat Transfer

In engineering thermodynamics, **Work** and **Heat** are the two primary modes of energy transfer across the boundary of a thermodynamic system. Although both are energy in transit, they are *path functions* (not properties of a system) and must be precisely distinguished.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 mandates a rigorous treatment of the *sign convention*, *boundary work*, and *work done in polytropic processes*. Students frequently lose marks by mixing up the sign convention — internalize it now.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Board-Standard Terminology)

**Work (W):** Work is defined as the energy transferred *across the boundary* of a closed system by the action of a *macroscopic force* acting through a *displacement* in the direction of the force. Mathematically, the incremental work is:

$$dW = F \cdot ds$$

where **F** is the force (in N) and **ds** is the displacement (in m). The SI unit is **Joule (J)** or **N·m**, and the rate of work is **Watt (W)**.

**Heat (Q):** Heat is the form of energy that is transferred *across the boundary* of a system due to a *temperature difference* between the system and its surroundings. Heat is *not* a property — it is energy *in transit*.

$$dQ = m \, c \, dT$$

where **m** is mass (kg), **c** is specific heat (J/kg·K), and **dT** is the temperature change (K).

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Standard, Heat Engine / Closed System):**
> - Heat supplied **TO** the system → **Q is POSITIVE** (+Q)
> - Heat rejected **FROM** the system → **Q is NEGATIVE** (−Q)
> - Work done **BY** the system (expansion) → **W is POSITIVE** (+W)
> - Work done **ON** the system (compression) → **W is NEGATIVE** (−W)

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — A Friendly Way to Remember

**Imagine a water tank with an outlet pipe at the bottom:**

- The **water leaving** the tank (because of a pressure difference pushing it out) is analogous to **work** — it is a *force-driven, ordered* form of energy transfer. The water pushes a turbine blade; the system does work *on* the surroundings.
- The **heat transfer** is analogous to a *hot plate* placed under the tank — energy flows in because the surroundings are at a higher temperature. The transfer is driven by a *temperature gradient*, not by macroscopic force.

> **Key Takeaway for Exams:** Work is energy transfer due to a *force–displacement* mechanism; Heat is energy transfer due to a *temperature difference*. Both are *boundary phenomena* — they exist only when crossing the system boundary.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Units to Memorize

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Common Multiple |
|----------|--------|---------|-----------------|
| Work | W | J (Joule) | 1 kJ = 10³ J |
| Heat | Q | J (Joule) | 1 kJ = 10³ J |
| Power | P, Ẇ | W (Watt) | 1 kW = 10³ W |
| Pressure | P | Pa (N/m²) | 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa |
| Volume | V | m³ | 1 L = 10⁻³ m³ |
| Universal Gas Constant | R | 8.314 J/(mol·K) | — |
| Mechanical Equivalent of Heat | J | 4186 J/kcal | 1 kcal = 4186 J |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanistic Distinction:** The work transfer can be *fully converted* into an equivalent rise in the potential energy of a weight, whereas heat transfer into an equal mass of water cannot, by the Second Law, be wholly converted back to work in a cyclic device. This subtle difference is the seed of the *Kelvin–Planck* statement.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Boundary (Displacement) Work — The Most Frequently Tested Topic

For a **quasi-equilibrium (reversible) process** in a closed system (piston-cylinder arrangement), the boundary work is the work done by the system on its surroundings as the boundary expands or contracts.

### 2.1.1 Derivation Setup

Consider a gas enclosed in a piston-cylinder. Let the cross-sectional area of the piston be **A**, and the gas pressure be **P**. The force exerted by the gas on the piston is:

$$F = P \cdot A$$

When the piston moves outward by an infinitesimal distance **dx**, the infinitesimal work done **by** the gas is:

$$dW = F \cdot dx = P \cdot A \cdot dx$$

Since $A \cdot dx = dV$ (the change in volume), we obtain the **boundary work equation**:

$$\boxed{dW = P \, dV}$$

For a finite process from state 1 to state 2, the total boundary work is:

$$\boxed{W_{1 \to 2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** The area under the process curve on a **P–V diagram** equals the boundary work. This is THE most repeated graphical question in KTU exams.

---

## 2.2 Work Done in Standard Thermodynamic Processes

### 2.2.1 Constant Volume (Isochoric) Process

Volume is constant, so $dV = 0$.

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_1} P \, dV = 0$$

> **KTU Pitfall:** *No work is done in an isochoric process, even if pressure changes.*

### 2.2.2 Constant Pressure (Isobaric) Process

Pressure is constant. The integral reduces to a simple multiplication.

$$W = P \int_{V_1}^{V_2} dV = P(V_2 - V_1) = P \cdot \Delta V$$

### 2.2.3 Constant Temperature (Isothermal) Process for an Ideal Gas

For an ideal gas, $PV = mRT$ → $P = mRT/V$. Substituting:

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{mRT}{V} dV = mRT \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

Equivalently, using the pressure ratio:

$$W = mRT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)$$

### 2.2.4 Polytropic Process (PV^n = C)

The **polytropic process** is the *most general* reversible process, with equation $PV^n = \text{constant}$, where **n** is the polytropic index. Substituting $P = C V^{-n}$ into the work integral:

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} C V^{-n} dV = C \left[ \frac{V^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n}$$

$$\boxed{W_{\text{poly}} = \frac{mR(T_2 - T_1)}{1 - n} = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n}}, \quad n \neq 1$$

When $n = 1$, the formula *blows up* (division by zero) and we recover the isothermal expression.

---

## 2.3 Heat Transfer — The Three Modes

Heat transfer, governed by the **Fourier's Law (conduction)**, **Newton's Law of Cooling (convection)**, and **Stefan–Boltzmann Law (radiation)**, will be covered in detail in later modules. For Module 1, the focus is on *sign convention* and *boundary energy accounting*.

| Mode | Mechanism | Driving Force | Governing Law |
|------|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| Conduction | Molecular/electron collisions | Temperature gradient | $Q = -kA \frac{dT}{dx}$ |
| Convection | Bulk fluid motion | Temperature difference | $Q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$ |
| Radiation | Electromagnetic waves | Temperature⁴ difference | $Q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Process / Concept | Governing Equation | Boundary Work $W$ | Heat $Q$ (closed system) |
|---|-------------------|--------------------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Isochoric (const V) | $V = C$ | $0$ | $m \, c_v (T_2 - T_1)$ |
| 2 | Isobaric (const P) | $P = C$ | $P(V_2 - V_1)$ | $m \, c_p (T_2 - T_1)$ |
| 3 | Isothermal (const T, ideal gas) | $PV = C$ | $mRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$ | $W$ (since $\Delta U = 0$) |
| 4 | Polytropic (general) | $PV^n = C$ | $(P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1)/(1-n)$ | $m \, c_n (T_2 - T_1)$ |
| 5 | Adiabatic reversible | $PV^\gamma = C$, $n = \gamma$ | $(P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1)/(1-\gamma)$ | $0$ |
| 6 | First Law (closed) | — | $W$ | $\Delta U + W$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant $c_v$ and $c_p$ are assumed unless stated otherwise.** $c_p - c_v = R$ (per unit mass) for an ideal gas.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Boundary work calculation** is fundamental in designing internal combustion engines, steam turbines, and reciprocating compressors where the gas does work on a moving piston.
- **Polytropic exponent $n$** is used to characterize actual compressor processes, which lie *between* isothermal ($n = 1$) and adiabatic ($n = \gamma$).
- **Heat transfer** in engines and heat exchangers determines the cycle efficiency and material selection.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Polytropic Process Work

The polytropic process equation is given by:

$$P V^n = C \quad \text{(constant)}$$

where $n$ is the polytropic exponent. To compute the work done, we express $P$ as a function of $V$:

$$P = \frac{C}{V^n}$$

The boundary work is given by the area under the P–V curve:

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{C}{V^n} \, dV$$

Factor out the constant $C$:

$$W = C \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-n} \, dV$$

Perform the integration using the power rule $\int V^k \, dV = \frac{V^{k+1}}{k+1}$ for $k = -n$:

$$W = C \left[ \frac{V^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = \frac{C}{1-n} \left( V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n} \right)$$

Apply the constant $C$ at state 1 and state 2 (since $P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n = C$):

$$C = P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$$

Hence:

$$C \cdot V_2^{1-n} = P_2 V_2^n \cdot V_2^{1-n} = P_2 V_2$$

And similarly:

$$C \cdot V_1^{1-n} = P_1 V_1$$

Substitute these back into the expression for $W$:

$$\boxed{W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n}, \quad n \neq 1}$$

**Alternative form using the ideal gas law** $PV = mRT$:

$$W = \frac{mR(T_2 - T_1)}{1 - n}, \quad n \neq 1$$

> **Limiting Case Verification:**
> - When $n = 0$: $W = P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1$. But if $P$ is constant, $P_2 = P_1 = P$, so $W = P(V_2 - V_1)$ — matches the isobaric result. ✓
> - When $n = 1$: The denominator becomes 0; this is the isothermal case, and we use $W = mRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$ separately.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Work for an Isothermal Process (Ideal Gas)

Starting from the ideal gas law: $PV = mRT$, where $T$ is constant.

Solve for pressure: $P = \frac{mRT}{V}$.

Substitute into the boundary work integral:

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{mRT}{V} \, dV$$

Since $m$, $R$, and $T$ are constant (isothermal), they can be factored out:

$$W = mRT \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V}$$

The integral is the natural logarithm:

$$\int \frac{dV}{V} = \ln V$$

So:

$$W = mRT \left[ \ln V \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = mRT (\ln V_2 - \ln V_1) = mRT \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

$$\boxed{W_{\text{isothermal}} = mRT \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = mRT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)}$$

The second form uses Boyle's Law $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ at constant $T$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Comprehensive KTU-Style Problem

**Problem:** 0.5 kg of an ideal gas (R = 0.287 kJ/kg·K) is compressed in a piston-cylinder from 100 kPa, 0.4 m³ to 800 kPa following the polytropic law $PV^{1.3} = C$. Calculate:
(a) The initial and final temperatures.
(b) The work done during the process.
(c) The heat transferred, given $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K.

### Part (a): Initial and Final Temperatures

**Initial temperature** (using $P_1 V_1 = m R T_1$):

$$T_1 = \frac{P_1 V_1}{mR} = \frac{100 \times 0.4}{0.5 \times 0.287} = \frac{40}{0.1435} \approx 278.74 \text{ K}$$

**Final volume** (from $P_1 V_1^{1.3} = P_2 V_2^{1.3}$):

$$V_2 = V_1 \left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{1/1.3} = 0.4 \times \left(\frac{100}{800}\right)^{1/1.3}$$

Compute the exponent: $1/1.3 \approx 0.7692$.

$$V_2 = 0.4 \times (0.125)^{0.7692}$$

Calculate $(0.125)^{0.7692}$:

$$\ln(0.125) = -2.0794, \quad -2.0794 \times 0.7692 = -1.5995, \quad e^{-1.5995} \approx 0.2019$$

So $V_2 \approx 0.4 \times 0.2019 \approx 0.0808$ m³.

**Final temperature**:

$$T_2 = \frac{P_2 V_2}{mR} = \frac{800 \times 0.0808}{0.5 \times 0.287} = \frac{64.64}{0.1435} \approx 450.45 \text{ K}$$

### Part (b): Work Done

Apply the polytropic work formula:

$$W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n} = \frac{800 \times 0.0808 - 100 \times 0.4}{1 - 1.3}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$P_2 V_2 = 800 \times 0.0808 = 64.64 \text{ kJ}$$
$$P_1 V_1 = 100 \times 0.4 = 40 \text{ kJ}$$
$$P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1 = 64.64 - 40 = 24.64 \text{ kJ}$$

Denominator: $1 - 1.3 = -0.3$.

$$W = \frac{24.64}{-0.3} \approx -82.13 \text{ kJ}$$

> **Sign Interpretation:** $W$ is **negative** because the gas is *compressed* (work is done **on** the system).

### Part (c): Heat Transferred

First, compute the change in internal energy:

$$\Delta U = m \, c_v (T_2 - T_1) = 0.5 \times 0.718 \times (450.45 - 278.74)$$

$$T_2 - T_1 = 171.71 \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta U = 0.5 \times 0.718 \times 171.71 = 61.64 \text{ kJ}$$

Apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics** for a closed system:

$$Q = \Delta U + W = 61.64 + (-82.13) = -20.49 \text{ kJ}$$

> **Sign Interpretation:** $Q$ is **negative** → heat is *rejected* from the system to the surroundings.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for Exam

| Process | $n$ value | $W$ formula | $Q$ formula | First Law gives |
|---------|-----------|-------------|-------------|-----------------|
| Isobaric | 0 | $P(V_2 - V_1)$ | $m c_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | $Q = \Delta U + W$ |
| Isothermal | 1 | $mRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$ | $W$ | $\Delta U = 0 \Rightarrow Q = W$ |
| Adiabatic reversible | $\gamma$ | $(P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1)/(1-\gamma)$ | $0$ | $W = -\Delta U$ |
| Isochoric | $\infty$ | $0$ | $m c_v (T_2 - T_1)$ | $Q = \Delta U$ |
| Polytropic (general) | $n$ | $(P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1)/(1-n)$ | $m c_n (T_2 - T_1)$ | $Q = \Delta U + W$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Piston-Cylinder System (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Force F_ext] --> B[Piston Face Area A]
    B --> C[Gas Pressure P internal]
    C --> D[Boundary Work dW = P dV]
    D --> E[Work Output W to Surroundings]
    F[Heat Source Q_in] --> G[System Boundary]
    G --> H[Internal Energy U]
    H --> I[Temperature T / State Properties]
    J[Heat Sink Q_out] --> G
    G --> K[Work Input W_in during Compression]
```

> **How to read it:** Energy crosses the system boundary as either **Work** (force-displacement) or **Heat** (temperature-driven). The system's internal state is tracked by $U$ and $T$.

---

## 4.2 P-V Diagram — Areas Under Different Process Curves

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State 1: P1, V1] --> B{Process Type}
    B -->|Isobaric| C[Horizontal line W = P ΔV]
    B -->|Isothermal| D[Hyperbola PV = C, W = mRT ln V2/V1]
    B -->|Adiabatic| E[Steeper Hyperbola PVγ = C, W = -ΔU]
    B -->|Polytropic| F[PVn = C, W = ΔPV / 1-n]
    C --> G[State 2: P2, V2]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual cue for P-V diagrams:** On the same P-V chart, the *isothermal* curve is *flatter* than the *adiabatic* curve. The polytropic curve lies between them, with steepness increasing as $n$ increases.

---

## 4.3 Energy Transfer Modes — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[System Boundary]
    S1 --> M1[Mode 1: Conduction]
    S1 --> M2[Mode 2: Convection]
    S1 --> M3[Mode 3: Radiation]
    M1 --> R1[Fourier Law: Q = -kA dT/dx]
    M2 --> R2[Newton Cooling: Q = hA Ts-Tinf]
    M3 --> R3[Stefan Boltzmann: Q = εσA T^4]
    R1 --> Out[Heat Q to Surroundings]
    R2 --> Out
    R3 --> Out
```

---

## 4.4 Sign Convention — Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Energy Crossing Boundary] --> Q1{Is there a temperature difference?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| PathHeat[HEAT TRANSFER]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is there a force with displacement?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| PathWork[WORK TRANSFER]
    Q2 -->|No| End[No Energy Transfer]
    PathHeat --> S1{Direction?}
    S1 -->|Into System| PosQ[+Q Absorbed]
    S1 -->|Out of System| NegQ[-Q Rejected]
    PathWork --> S2{Direction?}
    S2 -->|By System| PosW[+W Expansion]
    S2 -->|On System| NegW[-W Compression]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Distinguish between work and heat with at least two key differences.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Work | Heat |
|-------|------|------|
| 1 | Energy transfer due to a *macroscopic force* acting through a displacement. | Energy transfer due to a *temperature difference* across the system boundary. |
| 2 | Can be *fully* converted into mechanical energy (raising a weight). | Cannot be *fully* converted into work in a cyclic process (Second Law). |
| 3 | Measured in J; rate in W. | Measured in J; rate in W. |
| 4 | Associated with *organized* motion. | Associated with *random* molecular motion. |

> **Examiner Note:** Both are path functions and boundary phenomena.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the First Law of Thermodynamics for a closed system undergoing a cyclic process and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer:** For a closed system executing a cycle, the system returns to its initial state, hence $\Delta U_{\text{cycle}} = 0$. The First Law reduces to:

$$Q_{\text{cycle}} = W_{\text{cycle}}$$

**Significance:** It establishes the *equivalence* of heat and work as two forms of energy transfer. It also rules out the existence of a *perpetual motion machine of the first kind (PMM-1)* — a device that produces work without consuming an equivalent amount of energy.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — July 2024]** A gas in a piston-cylinder assembly expands from an initial state of 1 bar, 0.05 m³ to a final state of 0.2 m³. The process follows the law $PV^{1.4} = \text{constant}$. Determine:
> **(a)** The work done during the process. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** The heat transferred, if the internal energy decreases by 30 kJ. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Given:** $P_1 = 1 \text{ bar} = 100 \text{ kPa}$, $V_1 = 0.05 \text{ m}^3$, $V_2 = 0.2 \text{ m}^3$, $n = 1.4$.

**Step 1:** Calculate the final pressure $P_2$ using $P_1 V_1^{1.4} = P_2 V_2^{1.4}$:

$$P_2 = P_1 \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{1.4} = 100 \times \left(\frac{0.05}{0.2}\right)^{1.4}$$

Compute the ratio: $\frac{0.05}{0.2} = 0.25$.

$(0.25)^{1.4}$: $\ln(0.25) = -1.3863$, $\times 1.4 = -1.9408$, $e^{-1.9408} \approx 0.1436$.

$$P_2 = 100 \times 0.1436 \approx 14.36 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the polytropic work formula:

$$W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n} = \frac{14.36 \times 0.2 - 100 \times 0.05}{1 - 1.4}$$

Compute the products:

$$P_2 V_2 = 14.36 \times 0.2 = 2.872 \text{ kJ}$$
$$P_1 V_1 = 100 \times 0.05 = 5.0 \text{ kJ}$$
$$P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1 = 2.872 - 5.0 = -2.128 \text{ kJ}$$

Denominator: $1 - 1.4 = -0.4$.

$$W = \frac{-2.128}{-0.4} = +5.32 \text{ kJ}$$

> **Interpretation:** Work is **positive** → work is done *by* the gas (expansion). ✓

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:** Internal energy change $\Delta U = -30 \text{ kJ}$ (decrease).

**Step 1:** Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics:

$$Q = \Delta U + W = -30 + 5.32 = -24.68 \text{ kJ}$$

> **Interpretation:** $Q$ is **negative** → heat is *rejected* by the system to the surroundings.

**Step 2:** Sanity check using heat ratio:

$$\frac{Q}{W} = \frac{-24.68}{5.32} \approx -4.64$$

Since $|Q| > |W|$, more energy is leaving as heat than is gained as work, consistent with the internal energy decrease.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** A piston-cylinder contains 0.3 kg of air (R = 0.287 kJ/kg·K, $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K) initially at 200 kPa and 350 K. The air is compressed polytropically with index $n = 1.25$ to a final pressure of 1000 kPa. Calculate:
> **(a)** The work done and the final temperature. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** The heat transferred during the process. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate the initial volume using $P_1 V_1 = mRT_1$:

$$V_1 = \frac{mRT_1}{P_1} = \frac{0.3 \times 0.287 \times 350}{200} = \frac{30.135}{200} \approx 0.1507 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Find the final volume from $P_1 V_1^{1.25} = P_2 V_2^{1.25}$:

$$V_2 = V_1 \left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{1/1.25} = 0.1507 \times \left(\frac{200}{1000}\right)^{0.8}$$

$\frac{200}{1000} = 0.2$. $(0.2)^{0.8}$: $\ln(0.2) = -1.6094$, $\times 0.8 = -1.2876$, $e^{-1.2876} \approx 0.2759$.

$$V_2 = 0.1507 \times 0.2759 \approx 0.0416 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3:** Find the final temperature using $P_2 V_2 = mRT_2$:

$$T_2 = \frac{P_2 V_2}{mR} = \frac{1000 \times 0.0416}{0.3 \times 0.287} = \frac{41.6}{0.0861} \approx 483.16 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the work:

$$W = \frac{mR(T_2 - T_1)}{1 - n} = \frac{0.3 \times 0.287 \times (483.16 - 350)}{1 - 1.25}$$

$T_2 - T_1 = 133.16$ K.

Numerator: $0.3 \times 0.287 \times 133.16 = 11.466$ kJ.

Denominator: $1 - 1.25 = -0.25$.

$$W = \frac{11.466}{-0.25} = -45.86 \text{ kJ}$$

> **Interpretation:** $W$ is **negative** → work is done *on* the gas (compression). ✓

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate the change in internal energy:

$$\Delta U = m \, c_v (T_2 - T_1) = 0.3 \times 0.718 \times 133.16$$

$$\Delta U = 28.68 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the First Law:

$$Q = \Delta U + W = 28.68 + (-45.86) = -17.18 \text{ kJ}$$

> **Interpretation:** $Q$ is **negative** → heat is *rejected* from the system. This is consistent with a polytropic compression with $1 < n < \gamma$ (typically $1.3 < \gamma < 1.4$ for air), where some work input is dissipated as heat.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU Board):**
> 1. **Sign Convention Mix-up:** Writing $W = +45.86$ kJ for a compression process. Compression is work *done on* the system → $W$ is **negative**.
> 2. **Using $W = P \Delta V$ for non-isobaric processes.** This formula is valid *only* for constant pressure.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert kPa·m³ → kJ.** $1 \text{ kPa} \cdot \text{m}^3 = 1 \text{ kJ}$. Units must be consistent (kPa, m³, kJ).
> 4. **Using isothermal formula for polytropic.** $W = mRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$ applies *only* when $n = 1$.
> 5. **In First Law, mixing $Q = \Delta U - W$ with $Q = \Delta U + W$.** Always write the sign convention explicitly: $Q = \Delta U + W$ when $W$ is work done *by* the system.
> 6. **Pressure unit confusion:** $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 100 \text{ kPa}$, not $1 \text{ kPa}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Work and Heat Transfer":**

- ☐ **Work** is energy transfer due to a *force–displacement* mechanism; **Heat** is energy transfer due to a *temperature difference*.
- ☐ Both are *path functions* — their values depend on the process path between the initial and final states.
- ☐ **Boundary work** in a quasi-equilibrium process: $W = \int P \, dV$ — geometrically the *area under the P-V curve*.
- ☐ **Sign convention:** $Q$ in → positive; $Q$ out → negative. $W$ by system → positive; $W$ on system → negative.
- ☐ **Polytropic process:** $PV^n = C$, with $W = \dfrac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n}$ for $n \neq 1$.
- ☐ **Isothermal (ideal gas):** $W = mRT \ln(V_2/V_1) = mRT \ln(P_1/P_2)$.
- ☐ **Isobaric:** $W = P(V_2 - V_1)$.
- ☐ **Isochoric:** $W = 0$ (regardless of pressure change).
- ☐ **Adiabatic reversible (isentropic):** $PV^\gamma = C$, $Q = 0$, $W = -\Delta U$.
- ☐ **First Law (closed system):** $Q = \Delta U + W$ — use the consistent KTU sign convention.
- ☐ Unit conversion: $1 \text{ kPa} \cdot \text{m}^3 = 1 \text{ kJ}$.
- ☐ Heat transfer modes: **Conduction** (Fourier), **Convection** (Newton), **Radiation** (Stefan–Boltzmann).
- ☐ $c_p - c_v = R$ (for an ideal gas, per unit mass).
- ☐ Always check the **polytropic index $n$** for limiting cases: $n = 0$ (isobaric), $n = 1$ (isothermal), $n = \gamma$ (adiabatic), $n = \infty$ (isochoric).
- ☐ Numerical answers must carry the **correct sign**; an unsigned answer may be marked partially incorrect.

> **End of Module 1 — Work and Heat Transfer Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
