# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "triple point" of a pure substance. State the triple point coordinates of water.**

**Model Answer:**
The triple point of a pure substance is the unique thermodynamic state at which the **solid, liquid, and vapor phases coexist in equilibrium**. At this point, the molar Gibbs free energies of all three phases are equal: $g_s = g_f = g_g$. By the Gibbs phase rule, for $C = 1$ and $P = 3$, we get $F = 0$, making it an invariant point. For pure water, the triple point coordinates are **$T_{tp} = 273.16 \text{ K}$** and **$P_{tp} = 611.657 \text{ Pa}$**.

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why does the triple point have zero degrees of freedom? Explain using the Gibbs phase rule.**

**Model Answer:**
The Gibbs phase rule is $F = C - P + 2$. For a pure substance at its triple point, the number of components $C = 1$ and the number of coexisting phases $P = 3$. Substituting: $F = 1 - 3 + 2 = \mathbf{0}$. With zero degrees of freedom, neither temperature nor pressure can be varied independently without causing one of the three phases to disappear. Hence the triple point is a fixed, invariant point on the P-T diagram, fully determined by the intrinsic properties of the substance.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat P-T diagram, explain the triple point of a pure substance. Show the three phase regions and the three coexistence curves. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Clapeyron equation and apply it to evaluate the slope of the fusion curve of water at its triple point. Use the following data:
- $h_{sf} = 333.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $v_f = 0.001000 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$
- $v_s = 0.001091 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$
- $T_{tp} = 273.16 \text{ K}$

Comment on the sign of the slope. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) P-T Diagram and Triple Point Explanation:**

A P-T phase diagram of a pure substance is a 2-D plot with **pressure (P)** on the vertical (logarithmic) axis and **temperature (T)** on the horizontal axis. The diagram is divided into three regions by three curves:

1. **Solid Region** — lies to the left of the fusion curve, at low $T$ and high $P$.
2. **Liquid Region** — lies between the fusion and vaporization curves, at moderate $T$ and high $P$.
3. **Vapor Region** — lies to the right of the vaporization and sublimation curves, at high $T$ and low $P$.

The three coexistence curves are:
- **Fusion Curve (solid–liquid boundary):** Slope is positive for most substances; negative for water.
- **Vaporization Curve (liquid–vapor boundary):** Extends from the triple point upward, terminating at the critical point.
- **Sublimation Curve (solid–vapor boundary):** Extends from the triple point downward toward the origin.

These three curves **intersect at exactly one point — the triple point** — where all three phases coexist in equilibrium. For water, this point is at $(T_{tp}, P_{tp}) = (273.16 \text{ K}, 611.657 \text{ Pa})$. By the Gibbs phase rule, $F = C - P + 2 = 1 - 3 + 2 = 0$, so the triple point is invariant.

**(b) Derivation and Numerical Evaluation:**

**Step 1 — Start with the Clapeyron equation:**

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta v} = \frac{\Delta h}{T \, \Delta v}$$

**Step 2 — Apply along the fusion curve:**

$$\frac{dP}{dT}\bigg\vert_{\text{fus}} = \frac{h_f - h_s}{T_{tp}\,(v_f - v_s)} = \frac{h_{sf}}{T_{tp}\,v_{sf}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\frac{dP}{dT}\bigg\vert_{\text{fus}} = \frac{333.4 \times 10^{3} \text{ J/kg}}{(273.16 \text{ K})\,(0.001000 - 0.001091) \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}}$$

$$= \frac{333400}{(273.16)\,(-9.1 \times 10^{-5})}$$

$$= \frac{333400}{-0.024858}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dT}\bigg\vert_{\text{fus, water}} \approx -1.341 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa/K} = -13.41 \text{ MPa/K}}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on the sign:**

The slope is **negative** because $v_s > v_f$ for water (ice is less dense than liquid water). This is the famous anomaly of water: increasing pressure on ice at the triple point can cause it to **melt**, which is why ice skates glide on a thin film of liquid water under high pressure. For most other substances where the solid is denser, the slope is positive.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** What is the Gibbs phase rule? Apply it to a pure substance at the triple point and explain why the triple point is invariant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the Clapeyron equation, derive the Clausius–Clapeyron equation for the vaporization curve, assuming the vapor behaves as an ideal gas and the latent heat is constant. Use it to compute the vapor pressure of water at $T_2 = 373.15 \text{ K}$, given:
- $T_{tp} = 273.16 \text{ K}$
- $P_{tp} = 611.657 \text{ Pa}$
- $h_{fg} = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $R_{\text{water}} = 461.5 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Gibbs Phase Rule and Invariance of the Triple Point:**

The Gibbs phase rule for a simple compressible system is:

$$F = C - P + 2$$

where $F$ = degrees of freedom, $C$ = number of components, $P$ = number of phases, and the constant "2" accounts for the two independent intensive variables $T$ and $P$.

For a **pure substance** at the **triple point**:
- $C = 1$ (only one chemical component)
- $P = 3$ (solid, liquid, and vapor coexist)
- Therefore, $F = 1 - 3 + 2 = \mathbf{0}$

With zero degrees of freedom, neither $T$ nor $P$ can be varied independently without disturbing the three-phase equilibrium. Any attempt to change $T$ or $P$ causes one phase to vanish, and the system shifts onto one of the three coexistence curves (fusion, vaporization, or sublimation). Hence the triple point is **invariant** — it is a unique, fixed point determined entirely by the substance's intrinsic molecular properties.

**(b) Derivation of Clausius–Clapeyron and Numerical Application:**

**Step 1 — Start with the Clapeyron equation along the vaporization curve:**

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T \, v_g}$$

**Step 2 — Assume ideal gas behavior for the vapor:** $v_g = \dfrac{RT}{P}$

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg} \cdot P}{R \, T^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables:**

$$\frac{dP}{P} = \frac{h_{fg}}{R} \cdot \frac{dT}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from the triple point $(T_{tp}, P_{tp})$ to $(T_2, P_2)$:**

$$\int_{P_{tp}}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{h_{fg}}{R} \int_{T_{tp}}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^{2}}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_{tp}}\right) = -\frac{h_{fg}}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_{tp}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{611.657}\right) = -\frac{2257000}{461.5}\!\left(\frac{1}{373.15} - \frac{1}{273.16}\right)$$

Computing the term in parentheses:

$$\frac{1}{373.15} - \frac{1}{273.16} = 0.0026798 - 0.0036610 = -0.0009812 \text{ K}^{-1}$$

Multiplying:

$$-\frac{2257000}{461.5} \times (-0.0009812) = -4890.6 \times (-0.0009812) = 4.799$$

Therefore:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{611.657}\right) = 4.799$$

$$\frac{P_2}{611.657} = e^{4.799} \approx 121.5$$

$$\boxed{P_2 \approx 611.657 \times 121.5 \approx 74320 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.7432 \text{ bar}}$$

This is the saturation pressure of water at 100 °C, in good agreement with the known value of 1.01325 bar (the small discrepancy arises from the constant-$h_{fg}$ approximation and ideal-gas assumption, both of which become less accurate as we move far from the triple point).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost KTU students marks on Triple Point questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the units of $R$:** Always confirm that $R$ is in **J/(kg·K)** for per-unit-mass calculations or **J/(mol·K)** for molar calculations. Mixing units is the **#1 cause** of lost marks.
> 2. **Sign error in the fusion slope for water:** The slope is **negative** because $v_s > v_f$. A common mistake is to compute $(v_f - v_s)$ as positive by accident. Always write $(v_f - v_s)$ explicitly and evaluate it numerically *before* putting it in the denominator.
> 3. **Misapplying Gibbs Phase Rule:** Students often write $F = C - P + 1$ (without the "+2"). This is **wrong** for a simple compressible system where both $T$ and $P$ are independently variable.
> 4. **Confusing triple point with critical point:** Triple point has three phases; critical point has only two (liquid and vapor become indistinguishable). Critical point is *not* at 273.16 K — that is triple point.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the assumption of constant $h_{fg}$** when using Clausius–Clapeyron. Examiners will deduct 1 mark if this assumption is not explicitly mentioned.
> 6. **Not drawing a proper P-T diagram:** In 7-mark questions, a hand-drawn P-T diagram with three labelled curves and the triple point clearly marked is *mandatory*. Failing to draw the diagram costs 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for Triple Point (KTU PCAUT403):**
>
> - The **triple point** is the unique state where **solid + liquid + vapor coexist** in equilibrium.
> - For **water**: $T_{tp} = 273.16 \text{ K}$, $P_{tp} = 611.657 \text{ Pa}$ (both are **exact by international definition**).
> - The **Gibbs phase rule** $F = C - P + 2$ gives **$F = 0$** at the triple point → **invariant point**.
> - The **P-T diagram** has **three coexistence curves** (fusion, vaporization, sublimation) meeting at one point.
> - The **P-V-T surface** has a horizontal "triple-point line" where three specific volumes $v_s, v_f, v_g$ coexist.
> - The **Clapeyron equation** applies to *any* phase boundary: $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta h}{T \, \Delta v}$.
> - The **Clausius–Clapeyron equation** is the ideal-gas simplification for the vaporization curve: $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_{tp}}\right) = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{tp}} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$.
> - **Enthalpy additivity** at the triple point: $h_{sg} = h_{sf} + h_{fg}$.
> - **Water anomaly:** Fusion slope is **negative** (≈ $-13.41 \text{ MPa/K}$) because $v_s > v_f$.
> - **ITS-90** temperature scale is anchored at the water triple point: **273.16 K = 0.01 °C**.
> - **Triple-point cells** are sealed glass ampoules of ultra-pure water used for thermometer calibration.
> - **Critical point ≠ Triple point** — critical point is where liquid and vapor become identical (no meniscus).
> - Triple-point pressure of water is **far below atmospheric** ($P_{\text{atm}} \approx 101325 \text{ Pa} \gg 611.657 \text{ Pa}$); therefore ice, liquid water, and vapor never simultaneously coexist at sea level.
> - The triple point is **fixed by the substance itself**, not by external conditions — no instrument can shift it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# MODULE 2 — PROPERTIES DURING CHANGE OF PHASE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> A **Change of Phase** (or phase transition) is a physical transformation of a thermodynamic system from one state of matter (solid, liquid, or gas) to another, occurring at a specific combination of **pressure** and **temperature** known as the **saturation state**. During this transition, the substance absorbs or releases **latent heat** without any change in its sensible temperature, even though its internal energy and specific volume alter significantly.

### 1.1 The Six Common Phase Change Processes

| # | Process | Physical Meaning | Heat Flow |
|---|---------|------------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Melting** | Solid → Liquid | Heat **absorbed** (Endothermic) |
| 2 | **Vaporization** | Liquid → Vapour | Heat **absorbed** (Endothermic) |
| 3 | **Sublimation** | Solid → Vapour directly | Heat **absorbed** (Endothermic) |
| 4 | **Freezing / Solidification** | Liquid → Solid | Heat **rejected** (Exothermic) |
| 5 | **Condensation** | Vapour → Liquid | Heat **rejected** (Exothermic) |
| 6 | **Deposition** | Vapour → Solid directly | Heat **rejected** (Exothermic) |

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Ice-Cube on a Stove"**
> Imagine heating a tray of ice cubes on a stove. The temperature of the ice rises steadily from $-18^\circ\text{C}$ to $0^\circ\text{C}$ — this is **sensible heating** (you can *sense* the temperature change). Once the ice hits $0^\circ\text{C}$, even though you keep the burner on, the thermometer reading **stops moving**. The energy being supplied is being used entirely to break the rigid crystalline bonds and rearrange water molecules into the liquid state. This hidden energy is **latent heat of fusion** ($h_{if}$). The same phenomenon recurs at $100^\circ\text{C}$ during boiling, where the **latent heat of vaporization** ($h_{fg}$) is absorbed while the temperature remains pinned to the saturation value.

### 1.3 Key Thermodynamic Constants & Terminology

- **Saturation Temperature ($T_{sat}$ or $T_s$):** The temperature at which a liquid boils (or a solid melts) for a given pressure. **Unit: K or °C**.
- **Saturation Pressure ($P_{sat}$ or $P_s$):** The pressure at which a phase change occurs at a given temperature. **Unit: kPa or bar**.
- **Latent Heat of Fusion ($h_{if}$ or $h_f$):** Energy required to melt a unit mass of solid at constant pressure. **Unit: kJ/kg**.
- **Latent Heat of Vaporization ($h_{fg}$ or $h_g$):** Energy required to vaporize a unit mass of liquid at constant pressure. **Unit: kJ/kg**.
- **Latent Heat of Sublimation ($h_{is}$ or $h_s$):** Energy required to convert solid directly to vapour. **Unit: kJ/kg**.
- **Dryness Fraction ($x$ or $y$):** The mass of vapour per unit mass of a liquid–vapour mixture. **Dimensionless**, $0 \leq x \leq 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — The Clausius Equation Bond:**
> By **conservation of energy** during a phase transition, the latent heats are additive along any thermodynamic path:
> $$h_{is} = h_{if} + h_{fg}$$
> This is a **high-yield 3-mark question** frequently asked in KTU ESE papers.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Concept of Saturation: Single-Component P–V–T Surface

For a **pure substance** (single component), the three primary properties $P$, $v$, and $T$ are linked by an equation of state. During a phase change at constant pressure:

- The substance does **not** follow a single ideal-gas relationship.
- Instead, a **two-phase region** (liquid + vapour coexistence) appears, bounded by:
  - **Saturated Liquid Line** (left boundary — all liquid)
  - **Saturated Vapour Line** (right boundary — all vapour)
- Inside the dome, temperature and pressure are **NOT independent**; specifying one fixes the other.

### 2.2 Critical Point, Triple Point & Reference States

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Point:** The highest temperature and pressure at which the liquid and vapour phases can coexist in equilibrium. Above this point, no clear distinction exists between liquid and gas — the substance is a **supercritical fluid**.
> - For **water**: $T_c = 373.95^\circ\text{C}$, $P_c = 22.064 \text{ MPa}$
>
> **Triple Point:** The unique condition (single P, single T) where **all three phases** — solid, liquid, and vapour — coexist in thermodynamic equilibrium.
> - For **water**: $T_{tp} = 0.01^\circ\text{C}$, $P_{tp} = 0.6113 \text{ kPa}$

### 2.3 Wet Steam, Dryness Fraction & Steam Quality

In a **liquid–vapour mixture** (commonly encountered in boilers, condensers, and Rankine cycles), the **dryness fraction** $x$ is defined as:

$$x = \frac{m_v}{m_v + m_l} = \frac{m_v}{m_{total}}$$

where $m_v$ is the mass of vapour and $m_l$ is the mass of liquid.

**Complementary quantity — Wetness Fraction:**
$$y = 1 - x = \frac{m_l}{m_{total}}$$

### 2.4 Property Calculation for a Liquid–Vapour Mixture

Any **extensive specific property** $\phi$ (specific volume $v$, enthalpy $h$, internal energy $u$, entropy $s$) of a wet mixture is given by the **lever rule** (linear interpolation between the saturated liquid and saturated vapour values):

$$\phi = \phi_f + x \cdot (\phi_g - \phi_f)$$

$$\phi = (1 - x)\phi_f + x \cdot \phi_g$$

> [!TIP]
> The above formula is **universally valid** for $v, u, h, s$ — making it the single most important 14-mark derivation tool in this module.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table must be memorized verbatim for KTU ESE — 2024 Scheme.**

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Key Condition |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | Enthalpy of wet mixture | $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | $0 \leq x \leq 1$ |
| 2 | Internal energy of wet mixture | $u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | $0 \leq x \leq 1$ |
| 3 | Entropy of wet mixture | $s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$ | kJ/kg·K | $0 \leq x \leq 1$ |
| 4 | Specific volume of wet mixture | $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$ | m³/kg | $0 \leq x \leq 1$ |
| 5 | Sensible + Latent additivity | $h_{is} = h_{if} + h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | Energy conservation |
| 6 | Latent heat empirical (water) | $h_{fg} = 2501.7 - 2.381\,T_s$ | kJ/kg | $T_s$ in °C |
| 7 | Clausius–Clapeyron (basic) | $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}$ | Pa/K | Phase equilibrium |
| 8 | Clausius–Clapeyron (integrated) | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | — | Assuming const. $h_{fg}$ |
| 9 | Dryness fraction (mass-based) | $x = \dfrac{m_v}{m_v + m_l}$ | — | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Dryness fraction (volume-based) | $x = \dfrac{v - v_f}{v_g - v_f}$ | — | $v_f \leq v \leq v_g$ |
| 11 | Steam quality (enthalpy) | $x = \dfrac{h - h_f}{h_{fg}}$ | — | $h_f \leq h \leq h_g$ |
| 12 | Total mass in vessel | $m = m_f + m_g$ | kg | Mass balance |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All vertical bars above are rendered with the LaTeX safe `\vert` semantics in actual equations. Never write absolute-value bars as plain `|` inside markdown table cells.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Generation (Rankine Cycle):** Phase change in boiler produces high-quality vapour; condenser rejects $h_{fg}$.
- **Refrigeration & HVAC:** Evaporator absorbs latent heat; condenser releases it — phase change is the *primary* heat-transfer mechanism.
- **Cryogenics:** Liquefaction of gases (N₂, O₂, H₂, He) relies on understanding saturation curves and latent heats.
- **Chemical Process Industries:** Distillation columns exploit repeated vaporization–condensation to separate mixtures.
- **Metallurgy:** Casting and solidification processes depend on latent heat of fusion.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Property of a Liquid–Vapour Mixture (Lever Rule)

### Statement to Prove
> *For any specific property $\phi$ of a saturated liquid–vapour mixture, $\phi = \phi_f + x\,(\phi_g - \phi_f)$.*

### Step 1 — Define the system
Consider a closed vessel of total mass $m$ containing a saturated liquid of mass $m_f$ and a saturated vapour of mass $m_g$, coexisting in equilibrium at pressure $P$ and temperature $T$.

By mass conservation:

$$m = m_f + m_g$$

### Step 2 — Express the total property
The total property $\Phi$ (e.g., total enthalpy $H$, total internal energy $U$, total volume $V$) is the sum of the partial properties of each phase:

$$\Phi = \Phi_f + \Phi_g = m_f\,\phi_f + m_g\,\phi_g$$

where lowercase $\phi_f$ and $\phi_g$ denote **specific** properties of saturated liquid and saturated vapour, respectively.

### Step 3 — Introduce the specific (per-unit-mass) property for the mixture
The specific property of the mixture $\phi$ is defined as:

$$\phi \equiv \frac{\Phi}{m} = \frac{m_f\,\phi_f + m_g\,\phi_g}{m_f + m_g}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the dryness fraction
By definition:

$$x = \frac{m_g}{m_f + m_g} \quad \text{and} \quad 1 - x = \frac{m_f}{m_f + m_g}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute into Step 3

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi &= (1 - x)\,\phi_f + x\,\phi_g \\
&= \phi_f - x\,\phi_f + x\,\phi_g \\
&= \phi_f + x\,(\phi_g - \phi_f)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Conclusion
The required expression is proven:

$$\boxed{\;\phi = \phi_f + x \cdot (\phi_g - \phi_f)\;}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** This derivation, when written in 7–8 crisp lines as above, is worth **all 7 marks** in a 14-mark question. Memorize the logical chain: *Mass balance → Total property → Specific property → Substitute $x$ → Simplify*.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Clausius–Clapeyron Equation

### Physical Premise
At the **liquid–vapour saturation boundary**, the chemical potentials of the two phases are equal. The differential change of saturation pressure with respect to saturation temperature is governed by the **Clausius–Clapeyron relation**.

### Step 1 — Start from the equality of Gibbs free energy
At equilibrium between two phases $\alpha$ and $\beta$:

$$g_\alpha = g_\beta \quad \Rightarrow \quad dg_\alpha = dg_\beta$$

Since $dg = v\,dP - s\,dT$, we have:

$$v_\alpha\,dP - s_\alpha\,dT = v_\beta\,dP - s_\beta\,dT$$

### Step 2 — Rearrange

$$
(s_\beta - s_\alpha)\,dT = (v_\beta - v_\alpha)\,dP
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $dP/dT$

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{s_\beta - s_\alpha}{v_\beta - v_\alpha}
$$

### Step 4 — Use the second T-dS equation
During a reversible phase change at constant $T$ and $P$:

$$s_g - s_f = \frac{h_{fg}}{T}$$

where $h_{fg}$ is the **latent heat of vaporization**.

### Step 5 — Substitute

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T\,(v_g - v_f)}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Clausius–Clapeyron Equation (Exact Form)

$$\boxed{\;\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The SI units give $\dfrac{\text{Pa}}{\text{K}}$, confirming dimensional consistency.

### Step 7 — Integrated (Approximate) Form
Assuming $h_{fg} \approx$ constant and treating vapour as an **ideal gas** so that $v_g = RT/P$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dP}{dT} &= \frac{h_{fg} \cdot P}{T \cdot R\,T} = \frac{h_{fg}\,P}{R\,T^2} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \int_{P_1}^{P_2}\frac{dP}{P} &= \int_{T_1}^{T_2}\frac{h_{fg}}{R\,T^2}\,dT \\
\Rightarrow \quad \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) &= -\frac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) \\
&= \frac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8 — Final Integrated Form

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\;}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample Question (Analytical Matrix Style):**
> A closed rigid vessel of volume $V = 0.05 \text{ m}^3$ contains $0.4 \text{ kg}$ of a liquid–vapour mixture of water at $200 \text{ kPa}$. The enthalpy of the mixture is $600 \text{ kJ/kg}$. Determine:
> 1. The dryness fraction $x$
> 2. The volume occupied by the liquid phase
> 3. The volume occupied by the vapour phase

**Given Data (from steam tables at 200 kPa):**
- $v_f = 0.001061 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$
- $v_g = 0.8857 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$
- $h_f = 504.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $h_{fg} = 2201.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$

### Part 1 — Dryness Fraction
Using the enthalpy formula $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
600 &= 504.7 + x \times 2201.9 \\
2201.9\,x &= 600 - 504.7 = 95.3 \\
x &= \frac{95.3}{2201.9} = 0.04328
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $x \approx 0.0433$ (mostly liquid, very wet steam)

### Part 2 — Specific Volume of the Mixture
Using $v = v_f + x \cdot (v_g - v_f)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= 0.001061 + 0.04328 \times (0.8857 - 0.001061) \\
&= 0.001061 + 0.04328 \times 0.884639 \\
&= 0.001061 + 0.038296 \\
&= 0.039357 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part 3 — Volume of Liquid and Vapour
Total volume:
$$V = m \cdot v = 0.4 \times 0.039357 = 0.01574 \text{ m}^3$$

Mass of liquid:
$$m_f = (1 - x) \cdot m = (1 - 0.04328) \times 0.4 = 0.3827 \text{ kg}$$

Mass of vapour:
$$m_g = x \cdot m = 0.04328 \times 0.4 = 0.01731 \text{ kg}$$

Volume of liquid:
$$V_f = m_f \cdot v_f = 0.3827 \times 0.001061 = 4.061 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

Volume of vapour:
$$V_g = m_g \cdot v_g = 0.01731 \times 0.8857 = 0.01533 \text{ m}^3$$

**Verification:** $V_f + V_g = 0.000406 + 0.01533 = 0.01574 \text{ m}^3$ ✓

> [!TIP]
> **Solution Complete.** This problem structure (steam tables + dryness fraction + volume split) is the **exact pattern** of KTU 14-mark numericals in Module 2.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Phase Diagram (P–T Coordinates) — Schematic

The following Mermaid block renders the **Pressure–Temperature phase diagram** for a pure substance, including the triple point, critical point, and all three phase regions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["P coordinate vertical axis"] --> B["CRITICAL POINT at Pc 22.064 MPa and Tc 373.95 C"]
    C["T coordinate horizontal axis"] --> D["TRIPLE POINT at Ttp 0.01 C and Ptp 0.6113 kPa"]
    B --> E["SUPERCRITICAL FLUID REGION above critical point"]
    D --> F["SOLID REGION left of sublimation curve"]
    D --> G["LIQUID REGION between fusion and vaporisation curves"]
    D --> H["VAPOUR REGION right of vaporisation curve"]
    B --> I["SUBLIMATION CURVE connecting triple point and origin"]
    D --> J["FUSION CURVE almost vertical above triple point"]
    D --> K["VAPORISATION CURVE from triple point to critical point"]
    I --> L["Solid transforms directly to Vapour"]
    J --> M["Solid transforms to Liquid"]
    K --> N["Liquid transforms to Vapour"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above is a **block-level functional flow** of the P–T phase diagram, valid for any pure substance (water shown for numerical reference). The sublimation, fusion, and vaporization curves partition the diagram into three single-phase regions plus the supercritical region.

## 4.2 P–V Diagram with Two-Phase Dome

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["P vertical axis"] --> P2["Critical Point P c"]
    V1["v horizontal axis"] --> V2["Critical Specific Volume v c"]
    P2 --> DOME["TWO PHASE DOME bell shaped envelope"]
    DOME --> L1["Saturated Liquid Line on left boundary of dome"]
    DOME --> L2["Saturated Vapour Line on right boundary of dome"]
    L1 --> TOP["Critical Point top of dome"]
    L2 --> TOP
    DOME --> INT["Inside the dome liquid plus vapour coexist at constant P and T"]
    L1 --> BOT["L state fully liquid high density low v"]
    L2 --> BOT
    BOT --> INT
    INT --> CALC["Use lever rule phi equals phi f plus x times phi g minus phi f"]
```

## 4.3 Phase-Change Process Flow (Heating Path on P–V Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["State 1 Subcooled Liquid region outside dome to the left"] --> B["State 2 Saturated Liquid on left boundary of dome"]
    B --> C["State 3 Inside the Dome Liquid Vapour Mixture"]
    C --> D["State 4 Saturated Vapour on right boundary of dome"]
    D --> E["State 5 Superheated Vapour region outside dome to the right"]
    C --> F["Dryness fraction x increases from 0 to 1 during heating at constant P"]
    A --> G["Sensible Heat Region 1 to 2 temperature rises"]
    B --> H["Latent Heat Region 2 to 4 temperature constant"]
    D --> I["Superheat Region 4 to 5 temperature rises again"]
```

## 4.4 Energy Flow During Phase Change (Mollier Diagram Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Solid Phase initial state"] --> B["Sensible Heating raises T at constant P"]
    B --> C["Saturated Solid at Melting Point"]
    C --> D["Latent Heat of Fusion h if absorbed isothermally"]
    D --> E["Saturated Liquid at Boiling Point"]
    E --> F["Sensible Heating raises T of liquid"]
    F --> G["Saturated Liquid Vapour boundary"]
    G --> H["Latent Heat of Vaporization h fg absorbed isothermally"]
    H --> I["Saturated Vapour at same P and T"]
    I --> J["Superheating further raises T of vapour"]
    J --> K["Final Superheated Vapour State"]
```
