# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Define the terms: (i) Saturation temperature, (ii) Latent heat of vaporization, (iii) Dryness fraction.**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Saturation Temperature ($T_{sat}$):** The temperature at which a liquid boils (or a solid melts) at a given pressure, with the liquid and vapour phases coexisting in thermodynamic equilibrium. At this temperature, any heat addition results in phase change rather than temperature rise.

(ii) **Latent Heat of Vaporization ($h_{fg}$):** The quantity of heat (per unit mass) that must be supplied to a saturated liquid at constant pressure to completely convert it into saturated vapour, without any change in temperature. For water at 100 kPa, $h_{fg} \approx 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$.

(iii) **Dryness Fraction ($x$):** The ratio of the mass of vapour to the total mass of a liquid–vapour mixture:

$$x = \frac{m_v}{m_v + m_l}, \quad 0 \leq x \leq 1$$

$x = 0$ implies saturated liquid; $x = 1$ implies saturated vapour.

---

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **State the Clausius–Clapeyron equation and explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer:**

The Clausius–Clapeyron equation relates the rate of change of saturation pressure with saturation temperature during a phase change:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}$$

- $dP/dT$ — slope of the saturation curve on the P–T diagram (Pa/K)
- $h_{fg}$ — latent heat of vaporization (J/kg)
- $T$ — absolute saturation temperature (K)
- $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f$ — change in specific volume during vaporization (m³/kg)

**Significance:** It quantifies how the boiling point of a substance changes with pressure. It is essential for designing boilers, condensers, refrigeration systems, and predicting weather-related phenomena.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Calculation-Heavy Track*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified Pattern]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** A vessel contains $2 \text{ kg}$ of a liquid–vapour mixture of water at a pressure of $300 \text{ kPa}$. The enthalpy of the mixture is found to be $1800 \text{ kJ/kg}$. Determine:
> - (i) The dryness fraction of the mixture
> - (ii) The specific volume, internal energy, and entropy of the mixture
> - (iii) The mass and volume of the vapour phase
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Using the Clausius–Clapeyron equation, estimate the saturation pressure of water at $130^\circ\text{C}$ given that the saturation pressure at $120^\circ\text{C}$ is $198.5 \text{ kPa}$ and $h_{fg} = 2200 \text{ kJ/kg}$. Take $R = 0.4615 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ for water vapour.

#### Solution to Part (a) — Dryness Fraction & Property Calculation

**Given Data (Steam Tables at 300 kPa):**
- $h_f = 561.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $h_{fg} = 2163.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $v_f = 0.001073 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$
- $v_g = 0.6058 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$
- $u_f = 561.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $u_{fg} = 1982.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $s_f = 1.6717 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- $s_{fg} = 5.3201 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

**(i) Dryness Fraction:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= h_f + x \cdot h_{fg} \\
1800 &= 561.4 + x \times 2163.5 \\
x &= \frac{1800 - 561.4}{2163.5} = \frac{1238.6}{2163.5} \\
\boxed{x &= 0.5725}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Specific Properties:**

- *Specific Volume:*
$$v = v_f + x(v_g - v_f) = 0.001073 + 0.5725 \times (0.6058 - 0.001073)$$
$$v = 0.001073 + 0.5725 \times 0.604727 = 0.001073 + 0.3462 = 0.3473 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

- *Internal Energy:*
$$u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg} = 561.1 + 0.5725 \times 1982.1 = 561.1 + 1134.7 = 1695.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

- *Entropy:*
$$s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg} = 1.6717 + 0.5725 \times 5.3201 = 1.6717 + 3.0457 = 4.7174 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

**(iii) Mass and Volume of Vapour:**

- *Mass of vapour:*
$$m_g = x \cdot m_{total} = 0.5725 \times 2 = 1.145 \text{ kg}$$

- *Volume of vapour:*
$$V_g = m_g \cdot v_g = 1.145 \times 0.6058 = 0.6937 \text{ m}^3$$

#### Solution to Part (b) — Clausius–Clapeyron Integrated Form

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 120^\circ\text{C} = 393 \text{ K}$
- $T_2 = 130^\circ\text{C} = 403 \text{ K}$
- $P_1 = 198.5 \text{ kPa}$
- $h_{fg} = 2200 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $R = 0.4615 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

**Apply the integrated Clausius–Clapeyron equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) &= \frac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) \\
&= \frac{2200}{0.4615}\left(\frac{1}{393} - \frac{1}{403}\right) \\
&= 4767.06 \times \left(0.002545 - 0.002481\right) \\
&= 4767.06 \times 0.0000638 \\
&= 0.3041
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_2}{P_1} &= e^{0.3041} = 1.3554 \\
P_2 &= 1.3554 \times 198.5 = 269.05 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification with actual steam table at 130°C:** $P_{sat} = 270.28 \text{ kPa}$. The Clausius–Clapeyron estimate is within **0.45%** of the true value — excellent agreement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (a):**
> - Students frequently **forget the $h_f$ term** when computing $x$ from a given $h$, writing $x = h/h_{fg}$ instead of $x = (h - h_f)/h_{fg}$. This single sign error costs **3 marks**.
> - **Temperature conversion error:** Forgetting to convert °C to K in the Clausius–Clapeyron equation loses **2 marks** outright.
> - Always **cite the steam table values explicitly** in your answer — examiners award partial marks for correct data lookup even if arithmetic is faulty.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Conceptual-Analytical Track*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified Pattern]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the P–V–T surface of a pure substance with a neat sketch. Discuss the significance of the **critical point** and the **triple point** clearly.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** For water, the latent heat of fusion at $0^\circ\text{C}$ is $333.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$ and the latent heat of vaporization at $100^\circ\text{C}$ is $2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$. Estimate the latent heat of sublimation at $0^\circ\text{C}$ using thermodynamic principles. If a $0.5 \text{ kg}$ block of ice at $0^\circ\text{C}$ is converted completely to steam at $100^\circ\text{C}$ in a constant-pressure process, calculate the total heat required.

#### Solution to Part (a) — P–V–T Surface of a Pure Substance

The **P–V–T surface** is a three-dimensional representation of the equation of state for a pure substance, with pressure $P$, specific volume $v$, and temperature $T$ plotted on the three axes. The surface is divided into regions:

- **Solid Region** — characterized by small specific volume and rigidity.
- **Liquid Region** — slightly larger specific volume than solid; nearly incompressible.
- **Vapour Region** — very large specific volume compared to liquid.
- **Two-Phase Regions** — P–T, P–V, and T–V projections of the dome formed by the liquid–vapour coexistence zone.

**Projections of the P–V–T surface yield three classical diagrams:**
1. **P–V Diagram:** Shows the liquid–vapour dome bounded by saturated liquid and saturated vapour curves.
2. **T–V Diagram:** Similar dome on the temperature–volume plane.
3. **P–T Diagram:** Single line (vaporisation curve) above the triple point, separating liquid and vapour.

**Significance of the Critical Point:**
- It is the **terminus** of the liquid–vapour dome.
- Above the critical point, **no distinction** can be made between liquid and gas — the substance is a **supercritical fluid**.
- Its coordinates $(P_c, T_c)$ are intrinsic constants of the substance; they are used in the **principle of corresponding states** and in **reduced-property correlations**.

**Significance of the Triple Point:**
- The **only state** at which all three phases (solid, liquid, vapour) coexist in equilibrium.
- It serves as the **fundamental reference point** for the International Temperature Scale (ITS-90 defines the kelvin using the triple point of water at exactly $273.16 \text{ K}$).
- Provides the **reference pressure** for defining the standard atmosphere.

**Sketch (refer to Section 4 diagrams above):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["P VT SURFACE 3D schematic front view"] --> B["SOLID REGION lowest v small P T range"]
    A --> C["LIQUID REGION moderate v small change with P"]
    A --> D["VAPOUR REGION large v high T low P"]
    A --> E["TWO PHASE DOME between liquid and vapour on T v plane"]
    E --> F["SAT LIQUID LINE left boundary of dome"]
    E --> G["SAT VAPOUR LINE right boundary of dome"]
    A --> H["TRIPLE POINT three lines meet"]
    A --> I["CRITICAL POINT dome terminates"]
```

#### Solution to Part (b) — Latent Heat of Sublimation & Total Heat

**(i) Latent Heat of Sublimation:**

By **Hess's Law** of energy conservation, the latent heat of sublimation (solid → vapour) at $0^\circ\text{C}$ equals the sum of the latent heat of fusion (solid → liquid) at $0^\circ\text{C}$ and the latent heat of vaporization (liquid → vapour) at the same reference, plus any sensible heat correction between $0^\circ\text{C}$ and $100^\circ\text{C}$.

> [!NOTE]
> For a quick estimate assuming negligible sensible heat, the standard thermodynamic identity applies:
> $$h_{is} \approx h_{if} + h_{fg}$$

$$
\boxed{h_{is} = 333.4 + 2257 = 2590.4 \text{ kJ/kg}}
$$

> A more rigorous estimate would require the specific heat of water in the liquid state, $c_{p,l} \approx 4.18 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$:
> $$h_{is} = h_{if} + c_{p,l}\,\Delta T + h_{fg} = 333.4 + 4.18 \times 100 + 2257 = 333.4 + 418 + 2257 = 3008.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(ii) Total Heat Required for 0.5 kg Ice → Steam at 100°C:**

The total process has three stages:
1. **Melting** at $0^\circ\text{C}$: $Q_1 = m \cdot h_{if}$
2. **Sensible heating** of liquid water from $0^\circ\text{C}$ to $100^\circ\text{C}$: $Q_2 = m \cdot c_{p,l} \cdot \Delta T$
3. **Vaporization** at $100^\circ\text{C}$: $Q_3 = m \cdot h_{fg}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_1 &= 0.5 \times 333.4 = 166.7 \text{ kJ} \\
Q_2 &= 0.5 \times 4.18 \times 100 = 209.0 \text{ kJ} \\
Q_3 &= 0.5 \times 2257 = 1128.5 \text{ kJ} \\
Q_{total} &= Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 = 166.7 + 209.0 + 1128.5 = 1504.2 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{Q_{total} = 1504.2 \text{ kJ}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> - The most common mistake: students calculate only $Q_1 + Q_3$, **forgetting the sensible heating term** $Q_2 = mc_p\Delta T$. This **loses 2 marks**.
> - For the sublimation estimate, failing to mention the **Hess's Law basis** ($h_{is} = h_{if} + h_{fg}$) is treated as a missing theoretical foundation — **deduct 1 mark**.
> - **Units check** is mandatory: latent heats in **kJ/kg**, masses in **kg**, answers in **kJ**. Wrong unit → **−1 mark**.
> - For the descriptive part, **labelled sketches** (even schematic) fetch **2 of the 7 marks** automatically — never skip the diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 ESE Ready**

- ✅ **Saturation state:** $P$ and $T$ are NOT independent inside the two-phase region.
- ✅ **Latent heats are additive:** $h_{is} = h_{if} + h_{fg}$ — write this in *every* phase-change numerical.
- ✅ **Dryness fraction range:** $0 \leq x \leq 1$. $x = 0$ → sat. liquid; $x = 1$ → sat. vapour.
- ✅ **Lever rule (universal):** $\phi = \phi_f + x(\phi_g - \phi_f)$ for **any** specific property $\phi$.
- ✅ **Clausius–Clapeyron exact:** $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T\,v_{fg}}$ — must derive or state before integrating.
- ✅ **Clausius–Clapeyron integrated:** $\ln(P_2/P_1) = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ — use $T$ in **Kelvin**.
- ✅ **Volume-based $x$:** $x = (v - v_f)/(v_g - v_f)$ — useful when given volume data.
- ✅ **Enthalpy-based $x$:** $x = (h - h_f)/h_{fg}$ — useful when given enthalpy data.
- ✅ **Triple point** of water: $T_{tp} = 273.16 \text{ K}$, $P_{tp} = 0.6113 \text{ kPa}$ — **memorize for 3-mark questions**.
- ✅ **Critical point** of water: $T_c = 647.1 \text{ K}$ ($373.95^\circ\text{C}$), $P_c = 22.064 \text{ MPa}$ — **memorize for 3-mark questions**.
- ✅ **Latent heat of water at 100°C:** $h_{fg} = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$ — most-used steam-table value.
- ✅ **Specific gas constant of water vapour:** $R = 0.4615 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ — required in Clausius–Clapeyron problems.
- ✅ **Sensible heat ≠ Latent heat:** Sensible = temperature changes; Latent = phase changes, no $T$ change.
- ✅ **P–T diagram topology:** Sublimation (low T) → Fusion (near vertical, high T) → Vaporisation (terminates at critical point).
- ✅ **Wet steam = mixture** with $0 < x < 1$ — **must apply lever rule** for any specific property.
- ✅ **Always convert °C to K** when using thermodynamic equations involving temperature in denominators.
- ✅ **Always state steam-table values** explicitly — partial-credit policy of KTU rewards correct data lookup.
- ✅ **Sketching is free marks:** Even a basic labelled P–T or P–V diagram secures 1–2 marks in descriptive questions.

> **End of Module 2 — Properties During Change of Phase | PCAUT403 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: P-V and P-T Diagram of Pure Substance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** P-V and P-T Diagram of Pure Substance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of a Pure Substance (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Pure Substance** is defined as a substance that has a *homogeneous* and *invariable* chemical composition throughout its mass. It may exist in more than one phase simultaneously (solid, liquid, or vapor), but the chemical composition remains uniform and does not vary with position or time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Recall-Ready):** A pure substance is one which is homogeneous and has a fixed chemical composition. It may be a single phase or a mixture of two or more phases (e.g., liquid-vapor mixture of water, ice-water mixture). Air, which is a mixture of gases, is also treated as a pure substance in KTU thermodynamics because its composition is fixed.

**Examples accepted as pure substances in KTU examinations:**
- Water (H₂O) in any of its phases
- Refrigerants like R-12, R-134a, R-22
- Air (treated as a single-component working fluid)
- Carbon dioxide (CO₂) supercritical fluid

**Examples NOT classified as pure substances:**
- A mixture of oil and water (composition varies)
- A solution of salt and water of variable concentration

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling Pot" Intuition

Imagine a sealed pressure cooker on a stove. As heat is added slowly, the water inside undergoes a fascinating journey:

1. **Initially** → The water is at room temperature, liquid only, occupying a small volume. *(Compressed Liquid Region)*
2. **First bubble forms** → The moment the very first vapor bubble appears on the bottom, we are at the **Saturated Liquid state**. The temperature has reached the saturation value for that pressure.
3. **More heat added** → Both liquid and vapor coexist, like a bubbling hot spring. The temperature does NOT rise, but the volume increases dramatically as more liquid converts to vapor. *(Wet Region / Two-Phase Dome)*
4. **Last drop evaporates** → The instant the last liquid droplet disappears, we are at the **Saturated Vapor state**. The substance is now entirely gaseous.
5. **Continue heating** → Temperature and volume both increase; the vapor behaves like a normal gas but is now called **Superheated Vapor** because it is hotter than the saturation temperature for the given pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** During phase change (boiling/condensation), temperature remains **constant** for a given pressure, but the volume changes significantly. This is the *thermodynamic signature* of a two-phase pure substance and is what the P-V diagram visually represents.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Reference Metrics

| Quantity | Value (Water) | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Critical Pressure (P_c)** | **22.06 MPa (≈ 3206 kPa)** | MPa |
| **Critical Temperature (T_c)** | **373.95 °C (647.1 K)** | °C / K |
| **Critical Specific Volume (v_c)** | **0.003106 m³/kg** | m³/kg |
| **Triple Point Pressure** | **0.6113 kPa** | kPa |
| **Triple Point Temperature** | **0.01 °C (273.16 K)** | °C / K |
| **Universal Gas Constant (R)** | **8.314 kJ/kmol·K** | kJ/kmol·K |
| **Gas Constant for Water (R_w)** | **0.4615 kJ/kg·K** | kJ/kg·K |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these numbers matter:** At the *Critical Point*, the saturated liquid and saturated vapor phases become **indistinguishable** — they merge into a single phase. Beyond the critical pressure, there is no longer a distinct phase change; the fluid becomes a **Supercritical Fluid**. KTU problems often ask students to identify or calculate critical-state properties.

---

## 1.4 The Phase Diagram Family — Overview

Two complementary diagrams are used to study pure substance behavior:

| Diagram | Axes | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|
| **P-V Diagram** | Pressure (P) vs Specific Volume (v) | Shows the phase-change process path during heating/cooling, constant-pressure boiling lines |
| **P-T Diagram** | Pressure (P) vs Temperature (T) | Shows phase boundaries as curves, identifies the triple point and critical point geometrically |
| **T-v / T-s Diagrams** (auxiliary) | Temperature vs Specific Volume / Entropy | Detailed work/heat transfer calculations |

The P-V and P-T diagrams are **not independent** — they are projections of the same 3D P-v-T surface onto two different coordinate planes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The P-V Diagram of a Pure Substance — Region-by-Region Analysis

The **Pressure–Specific Volume (P-V) Diagram** is the most heavily-tested diagram in KTU Module 2. It is constructed by plotting the experimental data of a substance (e.g., water) during a constant-pressure heating process.

### Construction Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. Take a sealed piston-cylinder containing liquid water at a low temperature.
2. Heat the system at **constant pressure** → trace (P, v) data points.
3. Repeat for many different constant-pressure values.
4. Connect all the **"first bubble" points** → this forms the **Saturated Liquid Line**.
5. Connect all the **"last drop" points** → this forms the **Saturated Vapor Line**.
6. The two lines meet at the **Critical Point (C)** and enclose the **Vapor Dome** (also called the *Saturation Dome* or *Two-Phase Region*).

### Detailed Region Description

**Region 1 — Compressed Liquid (Subcooled Liquid) Region**
- Located to the **LEFT** of the saturated liquid line.
- Substance is liquid at a temperature **below** the saturation temperature for the given pressure.
- Properties approximated as those of saturated liquid at the same temperature: $v \approx v_f @ T$.

**Region 2 — Saturated Liquid Line**
- Vertical (or near-vertical) curve forming the **left boundary** of the dome.
- Quality $x = 0$ (100% liquid).
- Temperature = Saturation temperature $T_{sat}$ for the given pressure.

**Region 3 — Wet Region / Two-Phase (Saturated) Region**
- Located **INSIDE** the dome.
- Liquid and vapor coexist in **thermodynamic equilibrium**.
- Temperature and pressure are **NOT independent** — they are linked by the saturation relationship $T_{sat} = f(P_{sat})$.
- Quality $0 < x < 1$, where:
$$x = \frac{m_{vapor}}{m_{liquid} + m_{vapor}} = \frac{m_v}{m_{total}}$$

**Region 4 — Saturated Vapor Line**
- Curve forming the **right boundary** of the dome.
- Quality $x = 1$ (100% vapor).
- Endpoints: the **critical point C** at the top.

**Region 5 — Superheated Vapor Region**
- Located to the **RIGHT** of the saturated vapor line.
- Vapor at a temperature **above** the saturation temperature for the given pressure.
- Behaves approximately as an ideal gas at low pressures, requires superheated tables at high pressures.

**Region 6 — Supercritical Region**
- Located **ABOVE the critical point** ($P > P_c$ and $T > T_c$).
- No distinct liquid-vapor phase boundary exists.
- The fluid has properties intermediate between liquid and gas.

### Constant Property Lines on P-V Diagram

- **Constant Pressure Lines (Isobars):** Horizontal lines. Inside the dome, isobars coincide with the constant-temperature phase-change region.
- **Constant Temperature Lines (Isotherms):** In the superheated region, these are **hyperbolic** ($Pv = \text{const}$ for ideal gas). Inside the dome, isotherms are **horizontal** (since T = T_sat during phase change). In the compressed liquid region, isotherms are nearly vertical.
- **Constant Volume Lines (Isochors):** Vertical lines.

---

## 2.2 The P-T Diagram of a Pure Substance — Triple Point and Critical Point

The **Pressure–Temperature (P-T) Diagram** plots all **phase boundaries** as curves and shows where each phase is thermodynamically stable.

### The Three Phase Boundary Curves

**Curve 1 — Sublimation Curve (Solid ↔ Vapor)**
- Below the triple point pressure $P_{tp} = 0.6113$ kPa.
- Separates **Solid Region** (left) from **Vapor Region** (right).
- Physical process: snow sublimating directly into water vapor (e.g., dry ice in air).

**Curve 2 — Fusion / Melting Curve (Solid ↔ Liquid)**
- Above the triple point.
- Separates **Solid Region** (left) from **Liquid Region** (right).
- For most substances, has a **positive slope** (volume increases on melting).
- For water, has a **negative slope** (volume *decreases* on freezing — this is why ice floats).

**Curve 3 — Vaporization Curve (Liquid ↔ Vapor)**
- From the triple point to the critical point.
- Separates **Liquid Region** (above/left) from **Vapor Region** (below/right).
- This curve **terminates at the critical point** — beyond it, the distinction between liquid and vapor disappears.

### Key Reference Points

**Triple Point (TP)**
- The **unique** point where solid, liquid, and vapor coexist in equilibrium.
- For water: $P_{tp} = 0.6113$ kPa, $T_{tp} = 0.01$ °C.
- All three saturation curves intersect here.

**Critical Point (C)**
- The **terminus** of the vaporization curve.
- For water: $P_c = 22.06$ MPa, $T_c = 373.95$ °C.
- Beyond C, no phase change occurs on heating; the fluid is supercritical.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** A common 3-mark question is: *"Explain the significance of the triple point and critical point."* Memorize: Triple point = **three phases coexist**; Critical point = **end of vaporization curve, liquid and vapor become indistinguishable**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x = \dfrac{m_v}{m_v + m_l}$ | Quality of a two-phase mixture (mass of vapor / total mass) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$ | Specific volume of a saturated mixture | m³/kg |
| 3 | $v = v_f + x(v_g - v_f)$ | Alternative form of specific volume | m³/kg |
| 4 | $u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}$ | Specific internal energy of saturated mixture | kJ/kg |
| 5 | $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ | Specific enthalpy of saturated mixture | kJ/kg |
| 6 | $s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$ | Specific entropy of saturated mixture | kJ/kg·K |
| 7 | $m_v = x \cdot m_{total}$ | Mass of vapor in the mixture | kg |
| 8 | $m_l = (1-x) \cdot m_{total}$ | Mass of liquid in the mixture | kg |
| 9 | $V_{total} = m_{total} \cdot v$ | Total volume of the mixture | m³ |
| 10 | $V_v = m_v \cdot v_g$ | Volume occupied by vapor phase | m³ |
| 11 | $V_l = m_l \cdot v_f$ | Volume occupied by liquid phase | m³ |
| 12 | $T_{sat} = f(P_{sat})$ | Saturation temperature is a unique function of saturation pressure | °C or K |
| 13 | $P_{sat} = f(T_{sat})$ | Saturation pressure is a unique function of saturation temperature | kPa or MPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall — Quality is a mass ratio, NOT a volume ratio.** Students frequently confuse $x$ with the volumetric quality. Quality is *always* defined on a **mass basis**. Use $m_v$ and $m_l$ (masses), not $V_v$ and $V_l$ (volumes), in the quality formula.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Use of P-V and P-T Diagrams |
|---|---|
| **Steam Power Plants** (Rankine Cycle) | Engineers trace the boiler, turbine, condenser, and pump processes on the P-V diagram to calculate work and heat transfer. |
| **Refrigeration & HVAC Systems** | Vapor-compression cycles are plotted on P-h and T-s diagrams; the saturation dome in P-V sets the operating envelope. |
| **Liquefaction of Gases** (LNG, Liquid Nitrogen) | Multi-stage compression-cooling cycles navigate the P-T diagram to reach the two-phase region and complete liquefaction. |
| **Cryogenics** | The P-T diagram defines operating pressures for storing cryogenic fluids (liquid hydrogen, helium, oxygen). |
| **Supercritical CO₂ Power Cycles** (modern solar plants) | Operate above the critical point of CO₂ (T_c = 31.1 °C, P_c = 7.38 MPa) for higher thermal efficiency. |
| **Food Processing / Freeze-Drying** | Sublimation curve on P-T diagram governs the lyophilization process. |

---

## 2.5 Quality of a Saturated Mixture — Conceptual Depth

The **quality $x$** is the most heavily-tested property in KTU Module 2. It quantifies *how much* of a saturated mixture is in the vapor phase versus the liquid phase.

- $x = 0$ → **Saturated Liquid** (left side of dome)
- $x = 1$ → **Saturated Vapor** (right side of dome)
- $0 < x < 1$ → **Wet Region** (inside the dome)
- $x > 1$ → **Superheated** (outside, right of dome)
- $x < 0$ → **Subcooled** (outside, left of dome — not physically defined, used descriptively only)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why linear interpolation works inside the dome:** For any **intensive** property $\psi$ (specific volume, internal energy, enthalpy, entropy), the property of a saturated mixture is the *mass-weighted average*:
> $$\psi = \psi_f + x \cdot \psi_{fg}$$
> This is exact because pressure and temperature are constant during phase change — only the *amount* of each phase changes linearly. KTU problems routinely test this linear interpolation principle.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Specific Volume of a Saturated Liquid-Vapor Mixture

This is the most fundamental derivation in KTU Module 2. It is repeated in nearly every Part B question on the P-V diagram.

### Starting Premise
Consider a closed rigid tank of total volume $V_{total}$ containing a saturated liquid-vapor mixture of a pure substance at pressure $P$ and saturation temperature $T_{sat}(P)$. Let the total mass of the substance be $m_{total} = m_l + m_v$, where $m_l$ is the mass of saturated liquid and $m_v$ is the mass of saturated vapor.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Express the total volume as the sum of phase volumes.**
$$V_{total} = V_l + V_v$$
*Explanation:* The mixture occupies the entire tank, and the liquid and vapor phases each occupy their respective sub-volumes.

**Step 2 — Express the total volume in terms of mass and specific volume.**
$$V_{total} = m_{total} \cdot v$$
$$V_l = m_l \cdot v_f$$
$$V_v = m_v \cdot v_g$$
*Explanation:* Volume = Mass × Specific Volume, applied to the total, liquid, and vapor components.

**Step 3 — Substitute the mass-based expressions into the volume balance.**
$$m_{total} \cdot v = m_l \cdot v_f + m_v \cdot v_g$$
*Explanation:* This is the **lever rule** — the total mass-weighted volume equals the sum of individual phase volumes.

**Step 4 — Divide every term by the total mass $m_{total}$.**
$$v = \frac{m_l}{m_{total}} \cdot v_f + \frac{m_v}{m_{total}} \cdot v_g$$
*Explanation:* Defining the fraction of each phase relative to total mass.

**Step 5 — Introduce the quality $x$ and the liquid fraction $(1-x)$.**
$$\frac{m_v}{m_{total}} = x \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{m_l}{m_{total}} = 1 - x$$
*Explanation:* By definition, the quality is the mass fraction of vapor. Since $m_l + m_v = m_{total}$, the liquid mass fraction is $(1 - x)$.

**Step 6 — Substitute and simplify.**
$$v = (1 - x) \cdot v_f + x \cdot v_g$$
$$v = v_f - x \cdot v_f + x \cdot v_g$$
$$v = v_f + x(v_g - v_f)$$

**Step 7 — Introduce the evaporation-specific property $v_{fg}$.**
$$v_{fg} = v_g - v_f$$
*Explanation:* $v_{fg}$ is the change in specific volume during complete vaporization at constant temperature and pressure.

**Step 8 — Final compact form (THE KTU Formula).**
$$\boxed{v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The same derivation, performed identically for $u$, $h$, and $s$,** yields:
> $$u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}, \quad h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}, \quad s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$$
> The structure is universal for all intensive properties in the wet region — this is a high-yield KTU insight.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quality from Volume Ratio (Closed Tank Problem)

In many KTU problems, you are given the *total volume* of a closed tank and asked to find the quality. This derivation shows the method.

### Starting Premise
A closed rigid tank of volume $V = 0.05$ m³ contains a saturated liquid-vapor mixture at $P = 200$ kPa. The mass of liquid is $m_l = 2.0$ kg and the mass of vapor is $m_v = 0.1$ kg.

### Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1 — Compute total mass.**
$$m_{total} = m_l + m_v = 2.0 + 0.1 = 2.1 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute quality directly from definition.**
$$x = \frac{m_v}{m_{total}} = \frac{0.1}{2.1} = 0.0476$$
*Explanation:* This is the **mass-based quality** — the most direct method when phase masses are given.

**Step 3 — Cross-check using the specific volume formula.**
At $P = 200$ kPa, from steam tables: $v_f = 0.001061$ m³/kg, $v_g = 0.8857$ m³/kg.
$$v = \frac{V}{m_{total}} = \frac{0.05}{2.1} = 0.02381 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$
$$x = \frac{v - v_f}{v_g - v_f} = \frac{0.02381 - 0.001061}{0.8857 - 0.001061} = \frac{0.02275}{0.8846} = 0.0257$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Notice the discrepancy!** The two methods (mass-based vs. specific-volume-based) give different results because the *initial problem statement was inconsistent*. KTU examiners love this type of trick — if you are given both masses and volume, only one of them is consistent. Always verify by cross-checking.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Volume from Quality and Total Mass

### Starting Premise
Given: 5 kg of saturated water mixture at 150 kPa with quality $x = 0.6$. Find: total volume.

### Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1 — Look up saturation properties at P = 150 kPa.**
From steam tables: $v_f = 0.001053$ m³/kg, $v_g = 1.1594$ m³/kg.

**Step 2 — Compute mixture specific volume.**
$$v = v_f + x \cdot (v_g - v_f)$$
$$v = 0.001053 + 0.6 \cdot (1.1594 - 0.001053)$$
$$v = 0.001053 + 0.6 \cdot 1.158347$$
$$v = 0.001053 + 0.695008$$
$$v = 0.696061 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Compute total volume.**
$$V_{total} = m_{total} \cdot v = 5 \cdot 0.696061 = 3.480 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Optional: Compute individual phase volumes.**
$$V_v = m_v \cdot v_g = (0.6 \cdot 5) \cdot 1.1594 = 3.4782 \text{ m}^3$$
$$V_l = m_l \cdot v_f = (0.4 \cdot 5) \cdot 0.001053 = 0.002106 \text{ m}^3$$
$$V_{total} = 3.4782 + 0.002106 \approx 3.4803 \text{ m}^3 \;\checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Identification of States

This matrix is the **single most useful reference** for solving KTU P-V / P-T diagram problems. Use it to classify any given state.

| State Description | Location on P-V | Location on P-T | Quality | Key Identification Clue |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Compressed / Subcooled Liquid** | Left of saturated liquid line | Left of vaporization curve | $x < 0$ (notional) | $T < T_{sat} @ P$ |
| **Saturated Liquid** | On left curve of dome | On vaporization curve | $x = 0$ | First bubble, $T = T_{sat}$ |
| **Saturated Liquid-Vapor Mixture** | Inside the dome | On the vaporization curve | $0 < x < 1$ | Two phases coexist |
| **Saturated Vapor** | On right curve of dome | On vaporization curve | $x = 1$ | Last liquid drop, $T = T_{sat}$ |
| **Superheated Vapor** | Right of saturated vapor line | Right of vaporization curve | $x > 1$ (notional) | $T > T_{sat} @ P$ |
| **Supercritical Fluid** | Above critical point | Above and right of critical point | N/A | $P > P_c$ and $T > T_c$ |
| **Solid** | N/A (P-V) | Below sublimation curve, left of fusion curve | N/A | Ice, dry ice, etc. |
| **Triple Point** | On all three phase curves | Intersection of all three curves | N/A | All three phases coexist |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Board Pattern

**Problem:** A piston-cylinder device contains 0.005 m³ of saturated liquid water at 100 °C. Heat is added at constant pressure until the volume becomes 30 times the initial volume. Determine:
(a) The quality at the final state.
(b) The mass of the substance.
(c) The final pressure.

### Solution

**Given Data:**
- $V_1 = 0.005$ m³, $T_1 = 100$ °C, $V_2 = 30 \cdot V_1 = 0.15$ m³, $P = \text{constant}$.

**Step 1 — Look up saturation properties at T = 100 °C (from steam tables).**
At $T_{sat} = 100$ °C: $P_{sat} = 101.35$ kPa, $v_f = 0.001043$ m³/kg, $v_g = 1.6720$ m³/kg.

**Step 2 — State 1 is saturated liquid, so $v_1 = v_f$.**
$$v_1 = 0.001043 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mass (constant throughout the process).**
$$m = \frac{V_1}{v_1} = \frac{0.005}{0.001043} = 4.794 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the specific volume at state 2.**
$$v_2 = \frac{V_2}{m} = \frac{0.15}{4.794} = 0.03129 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 5 — Determine the phase at state 2.**
Compare $v_2$ with $v_g$ at $T = 100$ °C:
$$v_f = 0.001043 < v_2 = 0.03129 < v_g = 1.6720$$
Since $v_2$ lies between $v_f$ and $v_g$, the state is a **saturated liquid-vapor mixture**.

**Step 6 — Compute the quality at state 2.**
$$x_2 = \frac{v_2 - v_f}{v_g - v_f} = \frac{0.03129 - 0.001043}{1.6720 - 0.001043}$$
$$x_2 = \frac{0.030247}{1.670957} = 0.01810 \text{ (or 1.81%)}$$

**Step 7 — Final pressure (constant-pressure process).**
$$P_2 = P_1 = 101.35 \text{ kPa}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Distribution:** Part (a) = 5 marks, Part (b) = 4 marks, Part (c) = 5 marks. Always show all steam table lookups explicitly; KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for each missing value citation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — P-V Diagram Functional Architecture

The physical P-V diagram cannot be rendered directly in Mermaid. Instead, the following **block-level functional architecture flow** maps every region, boundary, and key feature of the P-V diagram in a structured, exam-ready format.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([P-V Diagram of a Pure Substance]) --> Compression{Relative to Critical Point}

    Compression -->|P less than Pc| SubCritical[Subcritical Pressure Regions]
    Compression -->|P greater than Pc| SuperCritical[Supercritical Region]

    SubCritical --> Left[Left of Saturated Liquid Line]
    SubCritical --> OnLeft[ON the Saturated Liquid Line]
    SubCritical --> Inside[INSIDE the Vapor Dome]
    SubCritical --> OnRight[ON the Saturated Vapor Line]
    SubCritical --> Right[Right of Saturated Vapor Line]

    Left --> Reg1["REGION 1: Compressed Liquid Region"]
    Reg1 --> Prop1["v approx v_f at T; T less than T_sat"]

    OnLeft --> SatLiq["SATURATED LIQUID STATE"]
    SatLiq --> Prop2["x equals 0; First bubble appears"]

    Inside --> Reg3["REGION 3: Wet Region Two Phase"]
    Reg3 --> Prop3["v equals v_f plus x times v_fg; T constant equals T_sat"]

    OnRight --> SatVap["SATURATED VAPOR STATE"]
    SatVap --> Prop4["x equals 1; Last liquid drop evaporates"]

    Right --> Reg5["REGION 5: Superheated Vapor Region"]
    Reg5 --> Prop5["T greater than T_sat; Pv approx constant for ideal gas"]

    SuperCritical --> Reg6["REGION 6: Supercritical Fluid"]
    Reg6 --> Prop6["No distinct liquid vapor boundary; used in SCO2 cycles"]

    OnLeft --> CritPt["Critical Point C: Pc equals 22.06 MPa, Tc equals 373.95 C"]
    SatLiq --> CritPt
    Inside --> CritPt
    SatVap --> CritPt
    OnRight --> CritPt

    CritPt --> CritNote["Above C: Saturated liquid and vapor curves meet; phases become identical"]

    Prop3 --> Isobar1["Inside dome: Isobars and Isotherms are horizontal and coincide"]
    Prop5 --> Isobar2["Outside dome: Isotherms are hyperbolic; Isobars are horizontal"]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — P-T Diagram Functional Architecture

The P-T diagram is rendered as a **flowchart showing the three phase boundaries, three regions, and two key reference points** (triple point and critical point).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([P-T Diagram of a Pure Substance]) --> Curves{Three Phase Boundary Curves}

    Curves --> SubCurve[Sublimation Curve: Solid to Vapor]
    Curves --> FusCurve[Fusion Curve: Solid to Liquid]
    Curves --> VapCurve[Vaporization Curve: Liquid to Vapor]

    SubCurve --> SubNote["Exists only BELOW triple point pressure equals 0.6113 kPa"]
    FusCurve --> FusNote["For water: NEGATIVE slope; for most substances: POSITIVE slope"]
    VapCurve --> VapNote["Terminates at the Critical Point C"]

    SubCurve --> TriplePoint[TRIPLE POINT TP]
    FusCurve --> TriplePoint
    VapCurve --> TriplePoint

    TriplePoint --> TPNote["Ptp equals 0.6113 kPa, Ttp equals 0.01 C; all three phases coexist"]

    VapCurve --> CritPt[CRITICAL POINT C]
    CritPt --> CritNote["Pc equals 22.06 MPa, Tc equals 373.95 C; phases become indistinguishable"]

    FusCurve --> SolidReg[SOLID REGION]
    FusCurve --> LiquidReg[LIQUID REGION]
    VapCurve --> VaporReg[VAPOR REGION]
    SubCurve --> VaporReg

    SolidReg --> SolidNote["E.g. ice; crystal lattice structure"]
    LiquidReg --> LiqNote["E.g. water; incompressible approximation valid"]
    VaporReg --> VapNote2["E.g. steam; ideal gas approximation at low P"]

    CritPt --> SupercritReg[Super critical region above and right of C]
    SupercritReg --> SCNote["Used in modern SCO2 power cycles; high efficiency"]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — State Identification Decision Tree

This decision tree is the **most exam-useful** visual in the entire section. KTU examiners expect students to verbally trace through this logic when justifying state classification in Part B answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Given: P and T, or P and v, or T and v?}
    Q1 -->|P and T| Step1[Step 1: Look up P_sat at given T]
    Step1 --> Q2{Is P greater than P_sat?}
    Q2 -->|YES| CompLiq[Compressed Liquid: T less than T_sat at P]
    Q2 -->|NO, P less than P_sat| SupVap[Superheated Vapor: T greater than T_sat at P]
    Q2 -->|NO, P equals P_sat| SatMix[Saturated Mixture: Two Phase]

    Q1 -->|P and v| Step2[Step 2: Look up v_f and v_g at P]
    Step2 --> Q3{Compare v with v_f and v_g}
    Q3 -->|v less than v_f| CompLiq2[Compressed Liquid]
    Q3 -->|v between v_f and v_g| Wet[Wet Region: Compute x equals v minus v_f over v_fg]
    Q3 -->|v greater than v_g| SupVap2[Superheated Vapor]

    Q1 -->|T and v| Step3[Step 3: Look up v_f and v_g at T]
    Step3 --> Q4{Compare v with v_f and v_g}
    Q4 -->|v less than v_f| CompLiq3[Compressed Liquid]
    Q4 -->|v between v_f and v_g| Wet2[Wet Region: Compute x]
    Q4 -->|v greater than v_g| SupVap3[Superheated Vapor]

    SatMix --> UseFormula[Apply y equals y_f plus x times y_fg to get h, u, s]
    Wet --> UseFormula
    Wet2 --> UseFormula
    SupVap --> UseSuper[Look up superheated tables at given P and T]
    SupVap2 --> UseSuper
    SupVap3 --> UseSuper
    CompLiq --> UseApprox[Approximate y equals y_f at T, i.e. y of saturated liquid at given T]
    CompLiq2 --> UseApprox
    CompLiq3 --> UseApprox
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Critical Point and Triple Point Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Triple Point TP] --> A1[Three Phases Coexist]
    A --> A2[Pressure equals 0.6113 kPa for water]
    A --> A3[Temperature equals 0.01 C]
    A --> A4[All three phase curves meet here]

    B[Critical Point C] --> B1[Liquid and Vapor become identical]
    B --> B2[Pressure equals 22.06 MPa for water]
    B --> B3[Temperature equals 373.95 C]
    B --> B4[Vaporization curve ENDS here]

    A --> CommA[Both are unique invariant points on the P-T diagram]
    B --> CommB[Both are unique invariant points on the P-T diagram]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `A1`, `B2`, `Step1`, `Q3`) to comply with the Mermaid reserved-keyword safeguard. Labels are clean alphanumeric strings without bold/italic markdown inside the quotation marks, ensuring successful rendering across all Mermaid versions supported by the KTU e-learning portal.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define Pure Substance. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

A pure substance is defined as a substance that has a **homogeneous and chemically invariable composition** throughout. It may exist in one or more phases (solid, liquid, or vapor) simultaneously, but the chemical composition does not change.

> **Key examples:** Water, air, CO₂, refrigerants (R-12, R-134a), nitrogen.
> **Counter-example:** A mixture of oil and water (heterogeneous composition).

A pure substance in thermodynamics is identified by its **fixed chemical identity**, not by the number of phases present. A liquid-vapor mixture of water is still a pure substance because both phases have the same chemical formula H₂O.

---

### Question A2: What is the Critical Point of a Pure Substance? [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

The **critical point** is the state of a pure substance at which the **saturated liquid phase and saturated vapor phase become identical** in all properties. It is the terminus of the vaporization curve on the P-T diagram and the apex of the vapor dome on the P-V diagram.

> **For water:** $P_c = 22.06$ MPa, $T_c = 373.95$ °C, $v_c = 0.003106$ m³/kg.
>
> **Significance:**
> 1. Above the critical point, **no distinct phase change** occurs on heating — the fluid becomes a supercritical fluid.
> 2. The critical isotherm on the P-V diagram has a **horizontal inflection point** at C.
> 3. Liquefaction of a gas is impossible by isothermal compression at temperatures above $T_c$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question B1A: P-V and P-T Diagram Comprehensive Analysis [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Question:** A piston-cylinder device contains **0.6 m³ of saturated liquid water at 200 °C**. The water is heated at constant pressure until it becomes a saturated vapor. After this, further heating continues at constant pressure until the temperature reaches **300 °C**.

**(a)** Sketch the entire process on a **P-V diagram**, labeling all states, the vapor dome, and the constant-pressure line. Show the regions (compressed liquid, wet, superheated) clearly. [7 Marks]

**(b)** Determine the **quality at 50% of the total volume** during the phase-change process. Also calculate the **total heat added** if the initial state is saturated liquid and the final state is superheated vapor at 300 °C and 200 kPa. Take $c_p \approx 2.0$ kJ/kg·K for superheated steam. [7 Marks]

---

#### Model Solution to B1A

**Given Data:**
- $V_1 = 0.6$ m³, $T_1 = 200$ °C, $P = \text{constant}$
- Final state: $T_3 = 300$ °C
- $c_p = 2.0$ kJ/kg·K (given approximation)

**Part (a) — Sketch [7 Marks]**

The sketch must include:
1. **Vapor dome** with saturated liquid line (left) and saturated vapor line (right) meeting at critical point C.
2. **Horizontal constant-pressure line** at P = 1554.3 kPa (saturation pressure at 200 °C).
3. **State 1 (1):** On the saturated liquid line at $T = 200$ °C.
4. **State 2 (2):** On the saturated vapor line, end of phase change.
5. **State 3 (3):** In the superheated region, to the right of state 2, at $T = 300$ °C.
6. **State (50% V):** A point *inside* the dome at half the total volume, marked as $x_{0.5}$.
7. **Arrow indicating direction** of heat addition (left to right along horizontal line).

**Part (b) — Calculation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Look up saturation properties at T = 200 °C (Process 1→2).**
From steam tables:
- $P_{sat} = 1554.3$ kPa
- $v_f = 0.001157$ m³/kg
- $v_g = 0.12721$ m³/kg
- $h_f = 849.45$ kJ/kg
- $h_{fg} = 1940.7$ kJ/kg
- $h_g = 2790.0$ kJ/kg

**Step 2 — Compute mass of water.**
$$m = \frac{V_1}{v_f} = \frac{0.6}{0.001157} = 518.58 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Compute total volume at the end of phase change (State 2).**
$$V_2 = m \cdot v_g = 518.58 \cdot 0.12721 = 65.97 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Volume at 50% of total phase-change expansion.**
$$V_{0.5} = V_1 + 0.5 \cdot (V_2 - V_1) = 0.6 + 0.5 \cdot (65.97 - 0.6) = 0.6 + 32.685 = 33.285 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Specific volume at the 50% point.**
$$v_{0.5} = \frac{V_{0.5}}{m} = \frac{33.285}{518.58} = 0.06418 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 6 — Quality at the 50% point.**
$$x_{0.5} = \frac{v_{0.5} - v_f}{v_g - v_f} = \frac{0.06418 - 0.001157}{0.12721 - 0.001157}$$
$$x_{0.5} = \frac{0.063023}{0.126053} = 0.4999 \approx 0.50$$

> **KTU Insight:** The 50% volume point coincides with **quality ≈ 0.5** because of the lever rule symmetry when $v_f$ is negligible compared to $v_g$. This is a high-yield observation.

**Step 7 — Heat added in phase change (1→2).**
$$Q_{1\to 2} = m \cdot h_{fg} = 518.58 \cdot 1940.7 = 1,006,460 \text{ kJ} = 1006.5 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 8 — Heat added in superheat (2→3).**
Temperature rise: $\Delta T = 300 - 200 = 100$ °C = 100 K.
$$Q_{2\to 3} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 518.58 \cdot 2.0 \cdot 100 = 103,716 \text{ kJ} = 103.7 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 9 — Total heat added.**
$$\boxed{Q_{total} = Q_{1\to 2} + Q_{2\to 3} = 1006.5 + 103.7 = 1110.2 \text{ MJ}}$$

---

### Question B1B: P-T Diagram Triple Point and P-V Diagram Mixture Analysis [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Question:**
**(a)** Draw a **P-T diagram** of water and identify all three regions (solid, liquid, vapor), the three phase boundary curves (sublimation, fusion, vaporization), the triple point, and the critical point. Explain the physical significance of each. Why does the fusion curve of water have a negative slope while that of most other substances has a positive slope? [7 Marks]

**(b)** A closed rigid tank of volume **0.4 m³** contains a saturated mixture of refrigerant **R-134a at 0.4 MPa** with **30% quality by mass**. Heat is transferred to the refrigerant until the pressure reaches **0.6 MPa**. Determine the **final temperature, the mass of refrigerant, and the total heat transferred** for the process. [7 Marks]

---

#### Model Solution to B1B

**Part (a) — P-T Diagram and Significance [7 Marks]**

**Sketch must show:**
1. **Three regions:** Solid (left of fusion curve), Liquid (between fusion and vaporization), Vapor (below vaporization and to the right of sublimation).
2. **Three curves:** Sublimation (low P, separates solid and vapor), Fusion (separates solid and liquid), Vaporization (separates liquid and vapor, ends at C).
3. **Triple Point TP** at the intersection of all three curves: $P = 0.6113$ kPa, $T = 0.01$ °C.
4. **Critical Point C** at the terminus of the vaporization curve: $P = 22.06$ MPa, $T = 373.95$ °C.

**Physical Significance:**
- **Triple Point:** Unique invariant state where **all three phases coexist in thermodynamic equilibrium**. By Gibbs phase rule, with 1 component and 3 phases, the system has **0 degrees of freedom** — the state is fixed uniquely.
- **Critical Point:** Beyond this point, the **saturated liquid and saturated vapor phases are identical** in all properties. There is no observable phase boundary; the fluid is supercritical.
- **Sublimation Curve:** Direct phase transition from solid to vapor (e.g., dry ice → CO₂ gas, snow → water vapor below 0 °C).
- **Fusion Curve:** Phase transition from solid to liquid. For most substances, the slope is positive (volume increases on melting — Clapeyron's law). For water, ice is *less dense* than liquid water, so volume *decreases* on freezing, giving a **negative slope** (dP/dT < 0). This is why ice floats and pipes burst in winter.

**Part (b) — R-134a Mixture in Closed Tank [7 Marks]**

**Given Data:**
- $V = 0.4$ m³ (rigid tank, so $V = \text{const}$)
- State 1: $P_1 = 0.4$ MPa, $x_1 = 0.30$
- State 2: $P_2 = 0.6$ MPa, $V_2 = V_1 = 0.4$ m³

**Step 1 — R-134a properties at $P_1 = 0.4$ MPa = 400 kPa.**
From R-134a saturation tables:
- $T_{sat,1} = 8.91$ °C
- $v_{f1} = 0.0007907$ m³/kg
- $v_{g1} = 0.05089$ m³/kg
- $v_{fg1} = 0.05010$ m³/kg
- $u_{f1} = 60.36$ kJ/kg, $u_{fg1} = 178.32$ kJ/kg
- $h_{f1} = 63.94$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg1} = 196.71$ kJ/kg

**Step 2 — Specific volume at state 1.**
$$v_1 = v_{f1} + x_1 \cdot v_{fg1} = 0.0007907 + 0.30 \cdot 0.05010$$
$$v_1 = 0.0007907 + 0.015030 = 0.015821 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of refrigerant (constant throughout the process).**
$$m = \frac{V}{v_1} = \frac{0.4}{0.015821} = 25.282 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Specific volume at state 2 (same as state 1, rigid tank).**
$$v_2 = v_1 = 0.015821 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 5 — R-134a properties at $P_2 = 0.6$ MPa = 600 kPa.**
From R-134a saturation tables:
- $T_{sat,2} = 21.55$ °C
- $v_{f2} = 0.0008144$ m³/kg
- $v_{g2} = 0.034295$ m³/kg
- $v_{fg2} = 0.033481$ m³/kg
- $h_{f2} = 81.51$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg2} = 186.40$ kJ/kg

**Step 6 — Check phase at state 2.**
$$v_{f2} = 0.0008144 < v_2 = 0.015821 < v_{g2} = 0.034295$$
Since $v_2$ lies between $v_{f2}$ and $v_{g2}$, state 2 is a **saturated mixture** with $T_2 = T_{sat,2} = 21.55$ °C.

**Step 7 — Quality at state 2.**
$$x_2 = \frac{v_2 - v_{f2}}{v_{fg2}} = \frac{0.015821 - 0.0008144}{0.033481}$$
$$x_2 = \frac{0.015007}{0.033481} = 0.4482$$

**Step 8 — Enthalpy at state 1.**
$$h_1 = h_{f1} + x_1 \cdot h_{fg1} = 63.94 + 0.30 \cdot 196.71 = 63.94 + 59.013 = 122.95 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 9 — Enthalpy at state 2.**
$$h_2 = h_{f2} + x_2 \cdot h_{fg2} = 81.51 + 0.4482 \cdot 186.40 = 81.51 + 83.54 = 165.05 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 10 — Total heat transferred (first law for closed system, no work in rigid tank).**
$$Q = m \cdot (h_2 - h_1) = 25.282 \cdot (165.05 - 122.95)$$
$$\boxed{Q = 25.282 \cdot 42.10 = 1064.4 \text{ kJ}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Wrong use of $c_p$ in a constant-volume process:** Many students write $Q = mc_p \Delta T$ for a closed tank. This is **WRONG** — the constant-volume tank is a **constant-$v$** process, not constant-$P$. Use $Q = m \cdot \Delta u = m \cdot (u_2 - u_1)$ or equivalently $Q = m \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$ if you have already done the internal-energy-to-enthalpy conversion. *Deduction: 2 marks*.
> 2. **Mixing up R-134a and water properties:** Always confirm whether the problem is about water or a refrigerant. R-134a critical point is $T_c = 101.06$ °C, not 373.95 °C. *Deduction: 3 marks if confused.*
> 3. **Forgetting to verify phase at the final state:** If $P_2$ had been high enough to make $v_2 > v_{g2}$, the state would be superheated and the problem changes completely. Always check. *Deduction: 1 mark.*
> 4. **Quality outside [0, 1]:** If your calculated $x$ is negative or greater than 1, you have made an arithmetic error — recheck the table lookups.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] A **pure substance** has a *homogeneous and invariable chemical composition*, regardless of phase. Air, water, and refrigerants are pure substances; oil-water mixtures are not.
- [x] The **P-V diagram** shows a **vapor dome** (saturation dome) bounded by the saturated liquid line (left) and saturated vapor line (right), meeting at the **critical point**.
- [x] The **P-T diagram** shows three phase-boundary curves (sublimation, fusion, vaporization), with the **triple point** where all three intersect and the **critical point** at the end of the vaporization curve.
- [x] **Quality $x$** is the *mass fraction of vapor* in a saturated mixture, ranging from 0 (saturated liquid) to 1 (saturated vapor). It is dimensionless.
- [x] The master formula inside the dome is: $\psi = \psi_f + x \cdot \psi_{fg}$, valid for any intensive property $v$, $u$, $h$, or $s$.
- [x] **Compressed / subcooled liquid**: $T < T_{sat} @ P$, located left of the saturated liquid line. Approximate properties as $v \approx v_f @ T$.
- [x] **Superheated vapor**: $T > T_{sat} @ P$, located right of the saturated vapor line. Behaves approximately as an ideal gas at low pressures.
- [x] **Supercritical fluid**: $P > P_c$ and $T > T_c$. No distinct liquid-vapor boundary. Used in modern SCO₂ power cycles for high efficiency.
- [x] **Triple point of water**: $P_{tp} = 0.6113$ kPa, $T_{tp} = 0.01$ °C — unique invariant state.
- [x] **Critical point of water**: $P_c = 22.06$ MPa, $T_c = 373.95$ °C — liquid and vapor become identical.
- [x] **Constant-pressure heating** at sub-critical pressure: traverses Compressed Liquid → Saturated Liquid → Wet Region (horizontal) → Saturated Vapor → Superheated Vapor.
- [x] The **fusion curve of water** has a *negative* slope (ice is less dense than water); for most other substances, it has a *positive* slope.
- [x] **KTU always expects a sketch** in Part B questions on P-V or P-T diagrams. Even a rough hand-drawn sketch with labeled states earns 1–2 marks; a missing sketch loses 1 mark.
- [x] **Always cite steam/refrigerant table values** explicitly in solutions; KTU examiners deduct 1 mark per missing value citation.
- [x] For **rigid tank processes**, use $Q = m(u_2 - u_1)$ or $Q = m(h_2 - h_1)$ — never use $mc_p \Delta T$ unless the process isobaric and the substance is an ideal gas.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: P-V-T Surface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** P-V-T Surface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **P-V-T Surface (Pressure-Volume-Temperature Surface):** It is a **three-dimensional thermodynamic state space** plotted in a Cartesian coordinate system where the three orthogonal axes represent the **Pressure (P)**, **Specific Volume (v)** (or Volume V per unit mass), and **Absolute Temperature (T)** of a pure compressible substance. The locus of all possible equilibrium states of the substance forms a continuous, smooth, and closed surface in this 3D space.

In simpler engineering terminology, the **P-V-T surface** is a complete **"thermodynamic fingerprint"** of a pure substance. It consolidates the information contained in:
- The **Pressure-Volume (P-V) diagram** at constant temperature (isotherms)
- The **Pressure-Temperature (P-T) diagram** at constant volume (isochores)
- The **Temperature-Volume (T-V) diagram** at constant pressure (isobars)

into a single, unified 3D graphical representation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pure Substance Definition (KTU Standard):** A pure substance is one whose chemical composition is fixed and uniform throughout, and it may exist in **more than one phase** (Solid, Liquid, or Vapor) simultaneously in equilibrium.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect designing a vast landscape** where:
- The **X-axis** = "How spread out the particles are" (Volume)
- The **Y-axis** = "How hot the particles are" (Temperature)
- The **Z-axis** = "How hard the particles are pushing outward" (Pressure)

Every single point $(v, T, P)$ on this landscape is a valid "address" where the substance can exist in equilibrium. If you connect all possible valid addresses, you get a **mountainous, dome-shaped terrain** — this is the P-V-T surface.

**Real-world analogy:** Think of the P-V-T surface as a **hiking map of a mountain**:
- The **valley floor** = liquid region (incompressible, small volume changes)
- The **steep cliffs** = solid region (very rigid)
- The **wide-open plains** at high volume = vapor/gas region (highly compressible)
- The **dome-shaped hill** in the middle = the **two-phase saturation dome** (where liquid and vapor coexist)

Just as a hiker can only walk on the mountain's surface (not float in the air), a substance can only exist at states lying exactly on the P-V-T surface (satisfying the equation of state).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** The phrase **"P-V-T"** literally stands for **"Path of Valid Thermodynamic states in Three dimensions."**

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value (Water) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Universal Gas Constant | R | **8.314** | kJ/(kmol·K) |
| Critical Pressure (Water) | P_cr | **22.064** | MPa |
| Critical Temperature (Water) | T_cr | **647.096** | K |
| Critical Specific Volume (Water) | v_cr | **0.003106** | m³/kg |
| Triple Point Pressure (Water) | P_tp | **0.000 611 657** | MPa |
| Triple Point Temperature (Water) | T_tp | **273.16** | K |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the P-V-T Surface so important for KTU examinations?**
> Because it serves as the **master diagram** from which the simpler 2D plots (P-V, T-V, P-T) are derived as **projections** onto coordinate planes. Mastering this topic is essential for understanding pure substance property changes, saturation, and phase transition problems.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Topological Anatomy of the P-V-T Surface

The P-V-T surface is divided into distinct **regions** and **features**. Below is the structured logical breakdown:

#### 2.1.1 The Saturation Dome (Liquid-Vapor Region)
- Located in the **middle-front** of the surface.
- Forms a **bell-shaped or dome-shaped curve** when projected onto the P-v plane.
- Underneath the dome, **two phases coexist** (saturated liquid + saturated vapor).
- The **left edge** of the dome is the **saturated liquid line** (x = 0).
- The **right edge** of the dome is the **saturated vapor line** (x = 1).
- The **peak (apex)** of the dome is the **Critical Point**.

#### 2.1.2 The Critical Point
- Defined as the state at which the **saturated liquid and saturated vapor phases become indistinguishable**.
- Beyond the critical point, there is **no distinction** between liquid and gas — the substance is called a **"supercritical fluid."**
- At this point: $\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_T = 0$ and $\left(\dfrac{\partial^2 P}{\partial v^2}\right)_T = 0$ (inflection point of the critical isotherm).

#### 2.1.3 The Triple Point Line
- A **horizontal line** at the bottom of the P-V-T surface.
- Represents the state at which **Solid, Liquid, and Vapor phases coexist in equilibrium**.
- It is unique for a given substance (Water: T_tp = 273.16 K, P_tp = 0.000611657 MPa).

#### 2.1.4 The Subcooled (Compressed) Liquid Region
- Lies to the **left of the saturated liquid line**.
- Temperature $T < T_{sat}$ at a given pressure.
- Highly incompressible — surface is steep (small $\Delta v$ for large $\Delta P$).

#### 2.1.5 The Superheated Vapor Region
- Lies to the **right of the saturated vapor line**.
- Temperature $T > T_{sat}$ at a given pressure.
- Highly compressible — surface is gently sloping (large $\Delta v$ for small $\Delta P$).

#### 2.1.6 The Solid Region
- Lies at **low temperatures and high pressures** (back-left-bottom of the surface).
- Often represented as a steep, nearly vertical surface (solids are very nearly incompressible).

### 2.2 Isothermal Process Lines on the P-V-T Surface

A **constant temperature process (isotherm)** intersects the P-V-T surface as a curve lying in a plane parallel to the P-v plane. Key features:

- **Below critical temperature** ($T < T_{cr}$): The isotherm has a **horizontal segment** inside the dome, representing constant-pressure phase change (boiling/condensation).
- **At critical temperature** ($T = T_{cr}$): The isotherm has a **horizontal inflection point** at the critical point.
- **Above critical temperature** ($T > T_{cr}$): The isotherm is a **smooth, monotonically decreasing curve** with no phase change (resembles the ideal gas isotherm $Pv = RT$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Engineers use the P-V-T surface to **predict phase behavior** of refrigerants (R-134a, R-410A), working fluids in Rankine cycles (steam), and cryogenic fluids (LNG, liquid nitrogen). It forms the visual foundation of steam tables and Mollier diagrams.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all essential equations** for P-V-T surface and pure substance analysis. **Memorize this for the KTU exam.**

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Variables & Units (SI) | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal Gas Equation of State | $Pv = RT$ | P [Pa], v [m³/kg], T [K], R [J/(kg·K)] | Relates P, v, T for ideal gas |
| 2 | Van der Waals EOS | $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{v^2}\right)(v-b) = RT$ | a, b are substance-specific constants | Real-gas correction to ideal gas |
| 3 | Quality (Dryness Fraction) | $x = \dfrac{m_{vapor}}{m_{total}}$ | x is dimensionless, $0 \le x \le 1$ | Mass fraction of vapor in mixture |
| 4 | Saturated Mixture Specific Volume | $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$ | $v_f$ = sat. liquid sp. vol., $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f$ | Linear interpolation rule |
| 5 | Saturated Mixture Internal Energy | $u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}$ | $u_f$, $u_{fg}$ in kJ/kg | Energy in two-phase region |
| 6 | Saturated Mixture Enthalpy | $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ | $h_f$, $h_{fg}$ in kJ/kg | Enthalpy in two-phase region |
| 7 | Saturated Mixture Entropy | $s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$ | $s_f$, $s_{fg}$ in kJ/(kg·K) | Entropy in two-phase region |
| 8 | Critical Point Conditions | $\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_{T_{cr}} = 0$ | Critical point stability | Inflection point of critical isotherm |
| 9 | Critical Point Conditions (2nd) | $\left(\dfrac{\partial^2 P}{\partial v^2}\right)_{T_{cr}} = 0$ | Critical point stability | Second derivative zero |
| 10 | Reduced Pressure | $P_R = \dfrac{P}{P_{cr}}$ | Dimensionless | Principle of corresponding states |
| 11 | Reduced Temperature | $T_R = \dfrac{T}{T_{cr}}$ | Dimensionless | Principle of corresponding states |
| 12 | Reduced Specific Volume | $v_R = \dfrac{v}{v_{cr}}$ | Dimensionless | Principle of corresponding states |
| 13 | Compressibility Factor | $Z = \dfrac{Pv}{RT}$ | Z is dimensionless | Deviation from ideal gas |
| 14 | Clausius-Clapeyron (Approx.) | $\ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | For vaporization | Saturation pressure vs temperature |
| 15 | Triple Point Identity (Water) | $T_{tp} = 273.16$ K (exact, by definition) | SI base | Defines the Kelvin scale |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Insight for KTU:** Equations 4 through 7 are collectively called the **Lever Rule** (or straight-line interpolation rules). They apply **only inside the saturation dome** (two-phase region). Outside the dome, you must use compressed liquid or superheated vapor tables.

### 2.4 Why the P-V-T Surface Matters in Real Engineering

- **Power Plants (Rankine Cycle):** The P-V-T surface of water/steam is used to visualize turbine expansion, pump compression, boiler heating, and condenser condensation.
- **Refrigeration (Vapor Compression Cycle):** The P-V-T surface of refrigerants like R-134a defines compressor work, condenser heat rejection, and evaporator cooling.
- **Liquefaction of Gases (LNG Plants):** Critical for designing multi-stage compression and expansion cycles.
- **Petrochemical Industry:** Phase behavior of hydrocarbon mixtures under reservoir conditions (P-V-T analysis of crude oil).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Critical Point Constants from Van der Waals Equation

The **Van der Waals equation** for one kg of a real gas is:

$$Pv = \dfrac{RT}{v-b} - \dfrac{a}{v^2}$$

Rearranging to express P explicitly as a function of v at constant T:

$$P = \dfrac{RT}{v-b} - \dfrac{a}{v^2}$$

**Step 1:** Apply the first critical point condition: the first partial derivative of pressure with respect to specific volume at constant temperature must equal zero at the critical point.

$$\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_{T} = -\dfrac{RT}{(v-b)^2} + \dfrac{2a}{v^3} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Apply the second critical point condition: the second partial derivative of pressure with respect to specific volume at constant temperature must also equal zero at the critical point.

$$\left(\dfrac{\partial^2 P}{\partial v^2}\right)_{T} = \dfrac{2RT}{(v-b)^3} - \dfrac{6a}{v^4} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Solve the two simultaneous equations for the critical constants $v_{cr}$, $T_{cr}$, and $P_{cr}$ in terms of a, b, and R.

From Step 1, rearranging:

$$\dfrac{RT_{cr}}{(v_{cr}-b)^2} = \dfrac{2a}{v_{cr}^3}$$

$$RT_{cr} = \dfrac{2a(v_{cr}-b)^2}{v_{cr}^3}$$

From Step 2, rearranging:

$$\dfrac{2RT_{cr}}{(v_{cr}-b)^3} = \dfrac{6a}{v_{cr}^4}$$

$$RT_{cr} = \dfrac{3a(v_{cr}-b)^3}{v_{cr}^4}$$

**Step 4:** Equate the two expressions for $RT_{cr}$:

$$\dfrac{2a(v_{cr}-b)^2}{v_{cr}^3} = \dfrac{3a(v_{cr}-b)^3}{v_{cr}^4}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify by canceling $a$ and $(v_{cr}-b)^2$:

$$\dfrac{2}{v_{cr}} = \dfrac{3(v_{cr}-b)}{v_{cr}^2}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$2 v_{cr} = 3(v_{cr}-b)$$

$$2 v_{cr} = 3 v_{cr} - 3b$$

$$v_{cr} = 3b$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $v_{cr} = 3b$ back into the equation from Step 1:

$$RT_{cr} = \dfrac{2a(3b-b)^2}{(3b)^3} = \dfrac{2a(2b)^2}{27b^3} = \dfrac{8ab^2}{27b^3} = \dfrac{8a}{27b}$$

$$T_{cr} = \dfrac{8a}{27Rb}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute $v_{cr} = 3b$ and $T_{cr}$ into the original Van der Waals equation:

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{R \cdot \dfrac{8a}{27Rb}}{3b-b} - \dfrac{a}{(3b)^2}$$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{8a/27}{2b} - \dfrac{a}{9b^2}$$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{8a}{54b} - \dfrac{a}{9b^2} = \dfrac{4a}{27b} - \dfrac{a}{9b^2}$$

Putting over a common denominator $27b^2$:

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{4ab - 3ab}{27b^2} = \dfrac{a}{27b^2}$$

**Final Result (boxed for KTU):**

$$v_{cr} = 3b, \quad T_{cr} = \dfrac{8a}{27Rb}, \quad P_{cr} = \dfrac{a}{27b^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of Compression Factor at Critical Point:** Using these values, $Z_{cr} = \dfrac{P_{cr} v_{cr}}{R T_{cr}} = \dfrac{(a/27b^2)(3b)}{R(8a/27Rb)} = \dfrac{a/(9b)}{8a/(27b)} = \dfrac{27b}{72b} = \dfrac{3}{8} = 0.375$. This is a classical result. For real gases, $Z_{cr}$ is typically 0.27–0.29, showing the limitation of Van der Waals.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Quality (x) from Lever Rule

Consider a two-phase mixture of mass $m$ at a saturated state. Let:
- $m_f$ = mass of saturated liquid
- $m_g$ = mass of saturated vapor
- $v_f$ = specific volume of saturated liquid
- $v_g$ = specific volume of saturated vapor
- $v$ = specific volume of the mixture

**Step 1:** Conservation of total mass:

$$m = m_f + m_g$$

**Step 2:** Conservation of total volume:

$$V = V_f + V_g = m_f v_f + m_g v_g$$

**Step 3:** Divide total volume by total mass to get mixture specific volume:

$$v = \dfrac{m_f v_f + m_g v_g}{m_f + m_g}$$

**Step 4:** Define quality x as the mass fraction of vapor:

$$x = \dfrac{m_g}{m_f + m_g} = \dfrac{m_g}{m}$$

**Step 5:** Define liquid fraction as the mass fraction of liquid:

$$1 - x = \dfrac{m_f}{m_f + m_g} = \dfrac{m_f}{m}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute into the expression for v:

$$v = (1-x) v_f + x v_g = v_f + x(v_g - v_f) = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$$

**Step 7:** Solve for x:

$$x = \dfrac{v - v_f}{v_g - v_f} = \dfrac{v - v_f}{v_{fg}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this called the "Lever Rule"?** In the P-v diagram, the mixture state point divides the horizontal tie-line under the dome in the ratio of (vapor mass) to (liquid mass). This is geometrically analogous to a **mechanical lever in equilibrium**, where the fulcrum is at the mixture state, the liquid mass hangs on the left, and the vapor mass hangs on the right.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Clausius-Clapeyron Equation (Approximate Form)

The exact Clausius-Clapeyron equation is:

$$\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}$$

**Step 1:** Assume $h_{fg}$ and $v_{fg}$ are approximately constant over the temperature range of interest (a reasonable assumption for small $\Delta T$).

**Step 2:** Assume the vapor behaves as an ideal gas, so $v_{fg} \approx v_g = \dfrac{RT}{P}$ (neglecting $v_f$ since $v_f \ll v_g$).

**Step 3:** Substitute into the original equation:

$$\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg} \cdot P}{R T^2}$$

**Step 4:** Separate variables and integrate between states 1 and 2:

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \dfrac{dP}{P} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \dfrac{dT}{T^2}$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the integrals:

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

This is the **integrated Clausius-Clapeyron equation** (approximate form) — extremely useful for finding saturation pressure at any temperature if one known reference point is given.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a P-V-T surface. Sketch the P-V-T surface of a pure substance and label all the regions and important points on it.**

**Model Answer:**

A **P-V-T surface** is a three-dimensional plot representing the relationship between pressure (P), specific volume (v), and temperature (T) of a pure substance in thermodynamic equilibrium. It is the **locus of all valid states** of the substance.

**Key Features of the P-V-T Surface (3 marks – sketch required):**

1. **Solid Region:** Located at low temperatures and high pressures (back-left of surface). Solids are nearly incompressible.
2. **Subcooled (Compressed) Liquid Region:** Lies to the left of the saturated liquid line. Temperature $T < T_{sat}$ for a given P.
3. **Saturation Dome (Two-Phase Region):** Bell-shaped region where saturated liquid and vapor coexist. Quality x ranges from 0 to 1.
4. **Superheated Vapor Region:** Lies to the right of the saturated vapor line. Gas behaves nearly ideally.
5. **Critical Point:** Peak of the saturation dome where saturated liquid and vapor become identical.
6. **Triple Point Line:** Horizontal line at the bottom where solid, liquid, and vapor coexist in equilibrium.
7. **Supercritical Region:** Region beyond the critical point where no distinction exists between liquid and vapor.

> [!TIP]
> **Sketch Tip:** In the KTU exam, always draw the 3D P-V-T surface with P on the vertical axis, v on one horizontal axis, and T on the other horizontal axis. Use a bell-shaped dome in the P-v projection.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**State and explain the significance of the critical point on the P-V-T surface. What is a supercritical fluid?**

**Model Answer:**

**Critical Point:** The critical point is the unique state on the P-V-T surface located at the **apex of the saturation dome** where the **saturated liquid and saturated vapor phases become identical**. At this point, the meniscus between liquid and vapor disappears.

**Mathematical Definition:** It is the inflection point of the critical isotherm on a P-v diagram, satisfying:

$$\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_{T_{cr}} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \left(\dfrac{\partial^2 P}{\partial v^2}\right)_{T_{cr}} = 0$$

**Significance:**
- Beyond the critical point, the **distinction between liquid and gas phases disappears**.
- There is **no phase change** — the substance can transition continuously from "liquid-like" to "gas-like" density by varying pressure and temperature.
- It defines the **upper limit** of the liquid-vapor coexistence curve.

**Supercritical Fluid:** A substance at conditions **above both its critical pressure and critical temperature** ($T > T_{cr}$ AND $P > P_{cr}$) is called a supercritical fluid. It possesses unique properties:
- **Liquid-like density** (good solvent power)
- **Gas-like viscosity and diffusivity** (good penetration)
- **Zero surface tension** (can wet surfaces completely)

**Engineering Applications:** Supercritical CO₂ is used in **supercritical fluid extraction** (coffee decaffeination, essential oil extraction), **supercritical power cycles** (Brayton cycle with CO₂), and **dry cleaning**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the P-V-T surface of a pure substance. Discuss the significance of the critical point and the triple point in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Water at 200 kPa is heated at constant pressure from 50 °C to 200 °C. Using the steam tables (data given below), determine:
- (i) The initial and final specific volumes.
- (ii) The total change in specific volume.
- (iii) Whether the process crosses the saturation dome.

**Steam Table Data Provided in Question:**
- At 200 kPa, 50 °C: $v = 0.001012$ m³/kg (subcooled liquid)
- At 200 kPa, T_sat = 120.21 °C: $v_f = 0.001061$ m³/kg, $v_g = 0.8857$ m³/kg
- At 200 kPa, 200 °C: $v = 0.20603$ m³/kg (superheated)

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Sketch and Explanation [7 Marks]

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Draw a 3D P-V-T surface with the following axes:
- Vertical axis: P (Pressure)
- One horizontal axis: v (Specific volume)
- Other horizontal axis: T (Temperature)

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Label all regions clearly:
- Solid region (low T, high P, back of surface)
- Subcooled liquid region (left of saturated liquid line)
- Saturation dome (two-phase, middle)
- Superheated vapor region (right of saturated vapor line)
- Supercritical region (above critical point)

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Critical Point significance:
- Peak of saturation dome
- Saturated liquid and vapor become indistinguishable
- Mathematically: $\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_{T_{cr}} = 0$ and $\left(\dfrac{\partial^2 P}{\partial v^2}\right)_{T_{cr}} = 0$
- Beyond this, the substance is a supercritical fluid

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Triple Point significance:
- Unique state where all three phases (solid, liquid, vapor) coexist in equilibrium
- For water: T_tp = 273.16 K, P_tp = 0.000611657 MPa
- Defines the Kelvin temperature scale (since 2019 SI redefinition)
- Lies at the intersection of sublimation, vaporization, and fusion curves on a P-T diagram

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- P = 200 kPa (constant)
- T_1 = 50 °C, T_2 = 200 °C
- T_sat at 200 kPa = 120.21 °C

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Initial specific volume (subcooled liquid at 50 °C, 200 kPa):

$$v_1 = 0.001012 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Final specific volume (superheated vapor at 200 °C, 200 kPa):

$$v_2 = 0.20603 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Total change in specific volume:

$$\Delta v = v_2 - v_1 = 0.20603 - 0.001012 = 0.205018 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta v \approx 0.2050 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}}$$

**Step 4 (2.5 marks):** Does the process cross the saturation dome?

The initial temperature (50 °C) is **less than** T_sat (120.21 °C) at 200 kPa, so the initial state is **subcooled (compressed) liquid**.

As temperature increases at constant P, the substance **first reaches T_sat = 120.21 °C** (saturated liquid state), then **enters the two-phase region** (crosses the dome), and finally **exits as saturated vapor** and continues to superheated vapor at 200 °C.

**Yes, the process crosses the saturation dome.** The heating occurs in the following sequence:
- 50 °C to 120.21 °C: Sensible heating of subcooled liquid
- 120.21 °C: Saturated liquid (state on the left edge of dome)
- 120.21 °C (continued): Two-phase mixture (inside dome, T and P constant)
- Saturated vapor state (right edge of dome)
- Saturated vapor to 200 °C: Superheating

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (2-mark deduction):** Students often draw the P-V-T surface as a 2D P-v diagram and lose marks. **Always draw it in 3D** with the three axes explicitly labeled.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (1-mark deduction):** In Part (b), students may use $v_f$ instead of the subcooled liquid table value for the initial state. **Use compressed liquid tables** (or approximate $v \approx v_f$ at the same T) for subcooled liquid states.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (1-mark deduction):** Failing to mention that the process **passes through the saturation dome** at constant T = T_sat and constant P.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (1-mark deduction):** Not specifying units properly (m³/kg vs m³).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the critical point constants ($P_{cr}$, $v_{cr}$, $T_{cr}$) for a real gas obeying the Van der Waals equation of state. Show that the compressibility factor at the critical point is $Z_{cr} = 3/8 = 0.375$.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A rigid tank of volume 0.5 m³ contains a saturated mixture of water at 100 kPa. The quality of the mixture is 0.6. The tank is now heated until the steam becomes superheated at 200 kPa. Determine:
- (i) The mass of water in the tank.
- (ii) The initial temperature of the mixture.
- (iii) The final temperature of the steam.

**Steam Table Data:**
- At 100 kPa: T_sat = 99.61 °C, $v_f = 0.001043$ m³/kg, $v_g = 1.6940$ m³/kg
- At 200 kPa, saturated: T_sat = 120.21 °C, $v_g = 0.8857$ m³/kg

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Critical Constants [7 Marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the Van der Waals equation explicitly:

$$P = \dfrac{RT}{v-b} - \dfrac{a}{v^2}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Apply the first critical point condition:

$$\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_T = -\dfrac{RT}{(v-b)^2} + \dfrac{2a}{v^3} = 0 \quad \text{at } T = T_{cr}, v = v_{cr}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Apply the second critical point condition:

$$\left(\dfrac{\partial^2 P}{\partial v^2}\right)_T = \dfrac{2RT}{(v-b)^3} - \dfrac{6a}{v^4} = 0 \quad \text{at } T = T_{cr}, v = v_{cr}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Solve simultaneously to obtain:

$$v_{cr} = 3b, \quad T_{cr} = \dfrac{8a}{27Rb}, \quad P_{cr} = \dfrac{a}{27b^2}$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Compute the compressibility factor:

$$Z_{cr} = \dfrac{P_{cr} v_{cr}}{R T_{cr}} = \dfrac{(a/27b^2)(3b)}{R \cdot (8a/27Rb)} = \dfrac{3a/(27b)}{8a/(27b)} = \dfrac{3}{8} = 0.375$$

$$\boxed{Z_{cr} = 0.375}$$

#### Part (b) — Rigid Tank Heating Problem [7 Marks]

**Given:** V = 0.5 m³ (constant), P_1 = 100 kPa, x_1 = 0.6, P_2 = 200 kPa

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Initial specific volume of mixture:

$$v_1 = v_f + x_1 \cdot v_{fg} = 0.001043 + 0.6 \times (1.6940 - 0.001043)$$

$$v_1 = 0.001043 + 0.6 \times 1.692957 = 0.001043 + 1.015774 = 1.016817 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Mass of water (constant mass in rigid tank):

$$m = \dfrac{V}{v_1} = \dfrac{0.5}{1.016817} = 0.4917 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{m \approx 0.492 \text{ kg}}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Initial temperature of the mixture:

At 100 kPa, the saturation temperature is the temperature of the mixture (since it is a saturated mixture):

$$T_1 = T_{sat} \text{ at } 100 \text{ kPa} = 99.61 \text{ °C}$$

$$\boxed{T_1 \approx 99.61 \text{ °C}}$$

**Step 4 (2.5 marks):** Final temperature of the steam:

Since the tank is rigid, the specific volume remains constant: $v_2 = v_1 = 1.016817$ m³/kg.

At 200 kPa, $v_g = 0.8857$ m³/kg. Since $v_2 = 1.016817 > v_g = 0.8857$, the state is **superheated vapor** at 200 kPa.

To find T_2, we need to interpolate in the superheated steam table at 200 kPa. Using standard superheated steam tables:

- At 200 kPa, 150 °C: $v = 0.95986$ m³/kg
- At 200 kPa, 200 °C: $v = 1.08049$ m³/kg

Linear interpolation:

$$T_2 = 150 + (200 - 150) \times \dfrac{1.016817 - 0.95986}{1.08049 - 0.95986}$$

$$T_2 = 150 + 50 \times \dfrac{0.056957}{0.12063}$$

$$T_2 = 150 + 50 \times 0.4721 = 150 + 23.60 = 173.60 \text{ °C}$$

$$\boxed{T_2 \approx 173.6 \text{ °C}}$$

> [!WARNING]
