> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout for Question B:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (2-mark deduction):** Forgetting that the **specific volume is constant** in a rigid tank (constant volume process). Students often mistakenly assume constant pressure.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (1.5-mark deduction):** Using the wrong lever rule formula. Always write the full derivation: $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$, not just plug numbers.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (1-mark deduction):** In the Van der Waals derivation, students often skip the **substitution steps** between the two derivative conditions. KTU examiners **require all algebraic steps** explicitly shown.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (1-mark deduction):** In Part (b), not verifying that $v_2 > v_g$ to confirm the state is superheated. This **physical sanity check** is expected.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for P-V-T Surface**

- **Definition:** A P-V-T surface is the **3D plot of all equilibrium states** of a pure substance, with axes P, v, and T.
- **Key Regions (Memorize the Order):** Solid → Subcooled Liquid → **Saturation Dome (Two-Phase)** → Superheated Vapor → **Supercritical Fluid** (beyond critical point).
- **Saturation Dome:** Bell-shaped region in the P-v projection; represents two-phase (liquid + vapor) equilibrium.
- **Critical Point:** The **peak of the dome**; saturated liquid and vapor become **identical**; defines $P_{cr}$, $T_{cr}$, $v_{cr}$.
- **Triple Point:** Unique state where **all three phases coexist** (for water: T = 273.16 K, P = 0.000611657 MPa).
- **Lever Rule (Straight-Line Interpolation):** Applies **only inside the two-phase region**:
  - $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$
  - $u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}$
  - $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$
  - $s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$
- **Quality (x):** Mass fraction of vapor in a two-phase mixture; $0 \le x \le 1$.
- **Van der Waals Critical Constants:** $v_{cr} = 3b$, $T_{cr} = \dfrac{8a}{27Rb}$, $P_{cr} = \dfrac{a}{27b^2}$, $Z_{cr} = 0.375$.
- **Compressibility Factor:** $Z = \dfrac{Pv}{RT}$ — measures deviation from ideal gas behavior; $Z = 1$ for ideal gas.
- **Principle of Corresponding States:** All substances behave similarly at the **same reduced conditions** ($P_R$, $T_R$, $v_R$).
- **Projections:** P-V-T surface yields three 2D diagrams — P-v, P-T, and T-v. P-v (with the dome) is the most commonly used in KTU problems.
- **Isotherm Behavior:** $T < T_{cr}$ → has horizontal segment (phase change); $T = T_{cr}$ → inflection point; $T > T_{cr}$ → smooth curve (no phase change).
- **Rigid Tank (Constant Volume):** Specific volume $v = V/m$ remains **constant** during heating/cooling.
- **Units (SI):** P in Pa or kPa, v in m³/kg, T in K (or °C with $\Delta$ preserved), h in kJ/kg, s in kJ/(kg·K).
- **Phase Change:** Occurs at **constant P and constant T** inside the dome; only the quality x changes.
- **Supercritical Fluid:** State at $T > T_{cr}$ AND $P > P_{cr}$; no phase distinction; unique solvent properties used in industrial extraction.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, the P-V-T surface topic is most commonly tested through:
> 1. A 3-mark **definition + labeled sketch** question
> 2. A 7-mark **numerical problem** involving the lever rule (finding x, v, h, or s)
> 3. A 7-mark **process on the P-V-T surface** (heating, cooling, rigid tank, piston-cylinder)
>
> **Master the lever rule and the 3D sketch** — these two alone account for >70% of marks on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Saturation Pressure and Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT TRANSFER > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Saturation Pressure and Temperature ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Saturation Temperature ($T_{sat}$)** Saturation temperature of a pure substance is the temperature at which vaporization (or condensation) occurs at a given pressure, where the liquid and vapor phases coexist in thermodynamic equilibrium. At this temperature, any addition of heat at constant pressure results in phase change rather than temperature rise. **Saturation Pressure ($P_{sat}$)** Saturation pressure (also called vapor pressure or equilibrium vapor pressure) of a pure substance is the pressure at which vaporization (or condensation) occurs at a given temperature, where the liquid and vapor phases coexist in thermodynamic equilibrium. It is the pressure exerted by a vapor in thermodynamic equilibrium with its condensed phases (liquid or solid) at a given temperature in a closed system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > Saturation pressure is a unique function of saturation temperature for a given pure substance. Conversely, saturation temperature is a unique function of saturation pressure. This one-to-one correspondence forms the foundation of steam tables, refrigeration tables, and psychometric charts used throughout thermal engineering. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Analogy — The Boiling Kettle and Mountain Climbing:** Imagine a kettle of water on a stove at sea level. - At **100 °C** and **1.01325 bar (101.325 kPa)**, water vigorously boils. Here, $T_{sat} = 100°C$ corresponds to $P_{sat} = 1.01325$ bar. - Now, if you carry the same kettle up a mountain (say, to Manali at ~2000 m), the atmospheric pressure drops to about **0.798 bar**. The water in the kettle now boils at a *lower* temperature — approximately **94 °C**! Why? Because the saturation pressure has dropped, and so has the saturation temperature required for boiling. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world manifestation:** This is precisely why food takes longer to cook at high altitudes — water boils at a lower temperature, so cooking proceeds more slowly. Pressure cookers exploit the *opposite* principle — increasing pressure raises the saturation temperature, allowing faster cooking. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics For **water**, the standard reference values are: - **Normal boiling point:** $T_{sat} = 100°C$ at $P_{sat} = 1.01325$ bar (1 atm) - **Critical point:** $T_{cr} = 373.95°C$, $P_{cr} = 220.64$ bar - **Triple point:** $T_{tp} = 0.01°C$, $P_{tp} = 0.006112$ bar - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R_u = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K) > [!TIP] > **Engineering takeaway:** Always remember that for any pure substance, there is a definite, tabulated $P_{sat} \leftrightarrow T_{sat}$ relationship. This is why steam tables are organized as $T_{sat} \rightarrow P_{sat}$ or $P_{sat} \rightarrow T_{sat}$ lookups. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The $P_{sat} - T_{sat}$ Relationship — Theory For a pure substance undergoing phase change at equilibrium, the **Phase Rule (Gibbs Phase Rule)** applies: $$F = C - P + 2$$ Where: - $F$ = degrees of freedom - $C$ = number of components - $P$ = number of phases in equilibrium **Application to pure substance with two phases (liquid + vapor):** - $C = 1$ (single component) - $P = 2$ (liquid and vapor) - Therefore: $F = 1 - 2 + 2 = 1$ This means **only ONE intensive property can be specified independently** — fixing either $T_{sat}$ OR $P_{sat}$ automatically determines the other. This is the theoretical basis of the $P_{sat}-T_{sat}$ relationship. ### 2.2 Saturation Curve on P-T Diagram On a Pressure-Temperature (P-T) diagram for a pure substance: - The **sublimation line** separates solid and vapor regions (low $P$, low $T$). - The **fusion (melting) line** separates solid and liquid regions. - The **vaporization line** separates liquid and vapor regions — this is the **saturation curve**. - All three lines meet at the **triple point**. - The vaporization line **terminates** at the **critical point**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Clausius-Clapeyron (integrated form, constant $h_{fg}$) | $P$ in kPa, $T$ in K, $h_{fg}$ in kJ/kmol, $R = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K) | | 2 | $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}$ | Clapeyron equation (exact) | $P$ in kPa, $T$ in K, $h_{fg}$ in kJ/kg, $v_{fg}$ in m³/kg | | 3 | $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg} P}{R T^2}$ | Clausius-Clapeyron (differential form, ideal gas vapor) | $P$ in kPa, $T$ in K | | 4 | $P_{sat} = f(T_{sat})$ | Functional relationship (tabular) | — | | 5 | $F = C - P + 2 = 1$ (for pure substance, 2 phases) | Gibbs Phase Rule | — | | 6 | $P_{sat} T_{sat}$ — unique correspondence | Saturation property table | — | | 7 | $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f$ | Specific volume change of vaporization | m³/kg | | 8 | $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$ | Latent heat of vaporization | kJ/kg | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** The $P_{sat}-T_{sat}$ relationship is the backbone of: > - Steam power plant design (Rankine, Brayton cycles) > - Refrigeration and HVAC (pressure-enthalpy charts) > - Distillation columns (chemical engineering) > - Pressure cooker and autoclave safety > - Boiling water reactor (BWR) nuclear plant operation ### 2.4 Logical Steps of Concept - **Step 1:** A pure substance in a closed container at constant temperature reaches equilibrium between liquid and vapor. - **Step 2:** At equilibrium, the rate of evaporation equals the rate of condensation. - **Step 3:** The pressure exerted by the vapor at this state is, by definition, the **saturation pressure** corresponding to that temperature. - **Step 4:** Increase the temperature → more molecules escape → higher vapor pressure → new equilibrium at higher $P_{sat}$. - **Step 5:** This monotonic relationship $P_{sat} \uparrow$ as $T_{sat} \uparrow$ is the **saturation curve**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Pattern:** Examiner often tests the *direction* of the $P_{sat}-T_{sat}$ relationship. Always state: "Saturation pressure increases monotonically with saturation temperature for a pure substance." --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Clapeyron Equation (Exact Form) **Starting principle:** At liquid-vapor equilibrium, the specific Gibbs free energy of liquid equals that of vapor: $$g_f = g_g$$ **Along the saturation curve (phase equilibrium), the differential change in Gibbs free energy is:** $$dg_f = dg_g$$ **Recall the fundamental thermodynamic relation:** $$dg = v \, dP - s \, dT$$ **Therefore, along the saturation curve:** $$v_f \, dP_{sat} - s_f \, dT_{sat} = v_g \, dP_{sat} - s_g \, dT_{sat}$$ **Rearranging by collecting $dP_{sat}$ and $dT_{sat}$ terms on opposite sides:** $$(v_g - v_f) \, dP_{sat} = (s_g - s_f) \, dT_{sat}$$ **Define $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f$ and $s_{fg} = s_g - s_f$:** $$v_{fg} \, dP_{sat} = s_{fg} \, dT_{sat}$$ **Therefore:** $$\frac{dP_{sat}}{dT_{sat}} = \frac{s_{fg}}{v_{fg}}$$ **Using the identity $s_{fg} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T_{sat}}$ (from the second law at constant temperature phase change):** $$\boxed{\frac{dP_{sat}}{dT_{sat}} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T_{sat} \cdot v_{fg}}}$$ This is the **exact Clapeyron equation**. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Clausius-Clapeyron Equation (Approximate Form) **Assumption 1:** The vapor phase behaves as an **ideal gas**, so: $$v_g \approx \frac{R T_{sat}}{P_{sat}}$$ **Assumption 2:** The specific volume of liquid $v_f \ll v_g$, so $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f \approx v_g$. **Assumption 3:** $h_{fg}$ (latent heat of vaporization) is approximately **constant** over the temperature range of interest. **Substituting these into the Clapeyron equation:** $$\frac{dP_{sat}}{dT_{sat}} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T_{sat} \cdot v_{fg}} \approx \frac{h_{fg}}{T_{sat} \cdot v_g} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T_{sat} \cdot \dfrac{R T_{sat}}{P_{sat}}}$$ **Simplifying the denominator:** $$\frac{dP_{sat}}{dT_{sat}} = \frac{h_{fg} \cdot P_{sat}}{R \cdot T_{sat}^2}$$ **Separating variables:** $$\frac{dP_{sat}}{P_{sat}} = \frac{h_{fg}}{R} \cdot \frac{dT_{sat}}{T_{sat}^2}$$ **Integrating between states 1 and 2:** $$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP_{sat}}{P_{sat}} = \frac{h_{fg}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT_{sat}}{T_{sat}^2}$$ **Evaluating the integrals:** $$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{h_{fg}}{R} \left[ -\frac{1}{T_{sat}} \right]_{T_1}^{T_2} = \frac{h_{fg}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)$$ **Final integrated Clausius-Clapeyron equation:** $$\boxed{\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{h_{fg}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)}$$ ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** Water boils at 100 °C at 1.01325 bar. Using the Clausius-Clapeyron equation, estimate the boiling point of water at a pressure of 0.85 bar. Given: $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg, $R = 0.4615$ kJ/(kg·K) for water vapor. **Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin:** $$T_1 = 100 + 273.15 = 373.15 \text{ K}$$ $$P_1 = 1.01325 \text{ bar}, \quad P_2 = 0.85 \text{ bar}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Clausius-Clapeyron:** $$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{h_{fg}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)$$ **Step 3 — Compute the LHS:** $$\ln\left(\frac{0.85}{1.01325}\right) = \ln(0.83893) = -0.17568$$ **Step 4 — Compute the prefactor:** $$\frac{h_{fg}}{R} = \frac{2257}{0.4615} = 4890.79 \text{ K}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute and solve for $T_2$:** $$-0.17568 = 4890.79 \left( \frac{1}{373.15} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)$$ $$\frac{1}{373.15} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{-0.17568}{4890.79} = -3.5922 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$\frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{373.15} + 3.5922 \times 10^{-5} = 2.6799 \times 10^{-3} + 0.0359 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\frac{1}{T_2} = 2.7158 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$ **Step 6 — Final answer:** $$T_2 = \frac{1}{2.7158 \times 10^{-3}} = 368.21 \text{ K} = 95.06 °C$$ **Comparison with steam table value at 0.85 bar:** $T_{sat} \approx 94.96 °C$ — excellent agreement! > [!NOTE] > The small discrepancy (0.1 °C) arises because $h_{fg}$ is not perfectly constant over this range. For board examinations, this approximation is standard and accepted. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Pressure-Temperature Diagram with Saturation Curve (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[Triple Point
0.01 C, 0.006112 bar] --> B1[Solid Region
Low T High P] A1 --> C1[Liquid Region
High P Above Vaporization Line] A1 --> D1[Vapor Region
Below Vaporization Line] C1 --> E1[Subcooled Liquid
T less than Tsat at P] C1 --> F1[Saturated Liquid Line
x equals 0] F1 --> G1[Liquid Vapor Mixture
0 less than x less than 1] G1 --> H1[Saturated Vapor Line
x equals 1] H1 --> I1[Superheated Vapor
T greater than Tsat at P] C1 --> J1[Critical Point
373.95 C, 220.64 bar] J1 --> I1 H1 --> J1 F1 -.Saturation Curve.-> H1 style A1 fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style J1 fill:#ff6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F1 fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style H1 fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G1 fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Saturation Process Flow (Heating at Constant Pressure) ```mermaid flowchart LR node1[Subcooled Liquid] -->|Heat addition at constant P| node2[Saturated Liquid
T equals Tsat] node2 -->|Latent heat hfg absorbed| node3[Two Phase Mixture
Quality x increases] node3 -->|More heat, x approaches 1| node4[Saturated Vapor
T equals Tsat] node4 -->|Further heat raises T| node5[Superheated Vapor
T greater than Tsat] style node1 fill:#4a90e2,color:#fff style node2 fill:#7ec8e3,color:#000 style node3 fill:#b19cd9,color:#000 style node4 fill:#ff9f43,color:#000 style node5 fill:#ee5253,color:#fff ``` ### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — How $P_{sat}$ Depends on $T_{sat}$ ```mermaid flowchart TD in1[Input: Specify T_sat] --> block1[Look up in Steam Table
or use Clausius-Clapeyron] in2[Input: Specify P_sat] --> block2[Interpolate in Steam Table
or solve for T_sat] block1 --> out1[Output: P_sat value] block2 --> out2[Output: T_sat value] block1 --> note1[Note: 1 to 1 correspondence
from Gibbs Phase Rule] block2 --> note1 note1 --> app1[Application: Power Cycles] note1 --> app2[Application: Refrigeration Cycles] note1 --> app3[Application: Distillation Columns] note1 --> app4[Application: Pressure Cooker Design] style in1 fill:#ffd93d style in2 fill:#ffd93d style out1 fill:#6bcf7f style out2 fill:#6bcf7f style block1 fill:#4d96ff,color:#fff style block2 fill:#4d96ff,color:#fff style note1 fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram interpretation guide:** In the P-T diagram above, the saturation curve (vaporization line) connects the triple point (bottom) to the critical point (top). The region to the LEFT of this curve is the liquid region, and to the RIGHT (below the critical point) is the vapor region. Any horizontal line cutting through this curve represents an isobaric (constant pressure) phase change process. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] *Define saturation temperature and saturation pressure. Explain their relationship with the help of a suitable example.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Saturation Temperature** (1 mark): The temperature at which a pure substance changes phase (boils/condenses) at a given pressure, with liquid and vapor in equilibrium. At this temperature, addition of heat causes phase change, not temperature rise. - **Saturation Pressure** (1 mark): The pressure at which a pure substance changes phase at a given temperature, with liquid and vapor in equilibrium. It is the pressure exerted by the vapor in equilibrium with its liquid at a given temperature. - **Relationship** (1 mark): They are uniquely related — for a given pure substance, $P_{sat}$ is a single-valued function of $T_{sat}$ and vice versa (per Gibbs Phase Rule, $F=1$ for single component, two phases). *Example: Water at 1 atm boils at 100°C; if pressure is reduced to 0.85 bar, water boils at ~95°C.* **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] *State the Clausius-Clapeyron equation and explain the assumptions made in its derivation.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Equation** (1 mark): $\ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ - **Assumption 1** (1 mark): The vapor phase behaves as an **ideal gas**, so $v_g \approx RT/P$. - **Assumption 2** (1 mark): The specific volume of the liquid phase is negligible compared to vapor ($v_f \ll v_g$), and $h_{fg}$ is approximately constant over the temperature range considered. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style] **(a)** Derive the Clausius-Clapeyron equation starting from the Clapeyron equation. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Water boils at 100 °C at 1.01325 bar. Estimate its saturation pressure at 150 °C using the Clausius-Clapeyron equation. Given: $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg, $R = 0.4615$ kJ/(kg·K). Compare with the steam table value. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** **(7 marks)** **Step 1 — Clapeyron equation (2 marks):** Starting from $g_f = g_g$ at phase equilibrium, we derived: $$\frac{dP_{sat}}{dT_{sat}} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T_{sat} \cdot v_{fg}}$$ **Step 2 — Assumption of ideal gas vapor (1 mark):** $$v_g \approx \frac{R T_{sat}}{P_{sat}}, \quad v_{fg} \approx v_g$$ **Step 3 — Substitution and simplification (2 marks):** $$\frac{dP_{sat}}{dT_{sat}} = \frac{h_{fg} \cdot P_{sat}}{R \cdot T_{sat}^2}$$ Separating variables: $$\frac{dP_{sat}}{P_{sat}} = \frac{h_{fg}}{R} \cdot \frac{dT_{sat}}{T_{sat}^2}$$ **Step 4 — Integration (2 marks):** Integrating between states 1 and 2 with constant $h_{fg}$: $$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{h_{fg}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)$$ **Assumptions recap:** (counted in steps above) Ideal gas vapor, $v_f \ll v_g$, $h_{fg}$ constant. --- **Model Solution for (b):** **(7 marks)** **Given:** - $T_1 = 100 + 273.15 = 373.15$ K, $P_1 = 1.01325$ bar - $T_2 = 150 + 273.15 = 423.15$ K, $P_2 = ?$ - $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg, $R = 0.4615$ kJ/(kg·K) **Step 1 — Calculate prefactor (1 mark):** $$\frac{h_{fg}}{R} = \frac{2257}{0.4615} = 4890.79 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Clausius-Clapeyron (2 marks):** $$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{1.01325}\right) = 4890.79 \left( \frac{1}{373.15} - \frac{1}{423.15} \right)$$ **Step 3 — Compute temperature term (1 mark):** $$\frac{1}{373.15} - \frac{1}{423.15} = 2.6799 \times 10^{-3} - 2.3634 \times 10^{-3} = 3.1651 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$ **Step 4 — Compute RHS (1 mark):** $$4890.79 \times 3.1651 \times 10^{-4} = 1.5479$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $P_2$ (1 mark):** $$\frac{P_2}{1.01325} = e^{1.5479} = 4.7019$$ $$P_2 = 1.01325 \times 4.7019 = 4.7642 \text{ bar}$$ **Step 6 — Comparison (1 mark):** Steam table value at 150 °C: $P_{sat} = 4.7616$ bar. **Percentage error = $\dfrac{4.7642 - 4.7616}{4.7616} \times 100 = 0.055\%$** — Excellent agreement! --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Internal Choice Alternative] **(a)** With the help of a neat P-T diagram, explain the saturation curve, triple point, and critical point for a pure substance. What is the significance of the critical point? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A refrigerant R-134a has $h_{fg} = 178$ kJ/kg and gas constant $R = 0.08149$ kJ/(kg·K). At $T_1 = -10°C$, its saturation pressure is 2.0066 bar. Estimate its saturation pressure at 30 °C using the Clausius-Clapeyron equation. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** **(7 marks)** **Diagram description (3 marks):** A P-T diagram is drawn (refer to Section 4.1). Key features: - **Solid region** at low T, high P (left of fusion line). - **Liquid region** at high P, above vaporization line. - **Vapor region** at low P, high T. - **Triple point** (1 mark): Unique point where all three phases coexist in equilibrium. For water: $T_{tp} = 0.01°C$, $P_{tp} = 0.006112$ bar. - **Critical point** (1 mark): The end point of the saturation curve where liquid and vapor phases become indistinguishable. For water: $T_{cr} = 373.95°C$, $P_{cr} = 220.64$ bar. - **Saturation curve** (vaporization line): The locus of all $(P_{sat}, T_{sat})$ points where liquid and vapor coexist in equilibrium. **Significance of critical point (2 marks):** - Above the critical point, no distinction exists between liquid and vapor — the substance is a **supercritical fluid**. - It marks the boundary above which the substance cannot be liquefied by pressure alone, regardless of how high the pressure is. - Critical point parameters are essential for the **principle of corresponding states** and **reduced property correlations** (compressibility charts). --- **Model Solution for (b):** **(7 marks)** **Given:** - $T_1 = -10 + 273.15 = 263.15$ K, $P_1 = 2.0066$ bar - $T_2 = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15$ K, $P_2 = ?$ - $h_{fg} = 178$ kJ/kg, $R = 0.08149$ kJ/(kg·K) **Step 1 — Prefactor (1 mark):** $$\frac{h_{fg}}{R} = \frac{178}{0.08149} = 2184.32 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2 — Apply equation (1 mark):** $$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{2.0066}\right) = 2184.32 \left( \frac{1}{263.15} - \frac{1}{303.15} \right)$$ **Step 3 — Compute temperature term (1 mark):** $$\frac{1}{263.15} - \frac{1}{303.15} = 3.8005 \times 10^{-3} - 3.2987 \times 10^{-3} = 5.018 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$ **Step 4 — RHS evaluation (1 mark):** $$2184.32 \times 5.018 \times 10^{-4} = 1.0961$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $P_2$ (1 mark):** $$\frac{P_2}{2.0066} = e^{1.0961} = 2.9924$$ $$P_2 = 2.0066 \times 2.9924 = 6.0046 \text{ bar}$$ **Step 6 — Comparison with R-134a table (1 mark):** R-134a saturation pressure at 30 °C = **7.002 bar** (from standard tables). **Percentage error ≈ 14.2%** — Significant deviation because $h_{fg}$ varies considerably between -10°C and 30°C for R-134a. The Clausius-Clapeyron equation gives an approximate value; the error highlights the limitation of the constant-$h_{fg}$ assumption. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often forget to convert Celsius to Kelvin — this gives a totally wrong answer and **zero credit** for the final numerical value. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Using $R = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K) without dividing by molecular weight — check if the problem gives $R$ per kg or per kmol! > - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to take the **antilogarithm** correctly: $\ln(P_2/P_1) = x \Rightarrow P_2 = P_1 \cdot e^x$, NOT $P_2 = P_1 \cdot x$. > - **Common Mistake 4:** Not stating assumptions explicitly in derivations — **lose 1-2 marks** even if the derivation is correct. > - **Common Mistake 5:** In P-T diagram questions, failing to label the critical point and triple point — both must be marked with their coordinates for full marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Saturation temperature ($T_{sat}$):** Temperature at which phase change occurs at a given pressure under equilibrium. - [x] **Saturation pressure ($P_{sat}$):** Pressure at which phase change occurs at a given temperature under equilibrium. - [x] **One-to-one correspondence:** For a pure substance, $P_{sat}$ and $T_{sat}$ are uniquely related (Gibbs Phase Rule, $F=1$). - [x] **Monotonic relationship:** $P_{sat}$ increases with $T_{sat}$ — never the opposite. - [x] **Clapeyron equation (exact):** $\dfrac{dP_{sat}}{dT_{sat}} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T_{sat} \cdot v_{fg}}$ - [x] **Clausius-Clapeyron (integrated, ideal gas):** $\ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ - [x] **Three assumptions of Clausius-Clapeyron:** ideal gas vapor, $v_f \ll v_g$, constant $h_{fg}$. - [x] **Triple point:** All three phases coexist; for water, $T_{tp} = 0.01°C$, $P_{tp} = 0.006112$ bar. - [x] **Critical point:** End of saturation curve; for water, $T_{cr} = 373.95°C$, $P_{cr} = 220.64$ bar. - [x] **Above critical point:** No distinction between liquid and vapor (supercritical fluid); cannot be liquefied by pressure alone. - [x] **Always use Kelvin** for temperature in Clausius-Clapeyron calculations. - [x] **Steam tables and R-134a tables** are the *primary* source; Clausius-Clapeyron is an *approximate* analytical tool. - [x] **KTU 2024 focus:** Expect derivation, definition, and a 7-mark numerical in Part B.

topics.title: T-h and T-s Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** T-h and T-s Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The T-s Diagram (Temperature–Entropy Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **T-s diagram** is a thermodynamic property diagram in which **Temperature (T)** is plotted on the **ordinate (Y-axis)** and **Specific Entropy (s)** is plotted on the **abscissa (X-axis)**. It is the most fundamental diagram used for the analysis of cyclic thermodynamic processes because the area under any reversible process curve directly represents the magnitude of heat transfer.

**Key Features of the T-s Diagram:**
- **Area under the curve** = $\int T\,ds$ = Heat transferred in a reversible process ($\delta Q_{rev}$).
- **Area enclosed by a cycle** = $\oint T\,ds$ = Net heat transfer = Net work done.
- The **two-phase (vapor) dome** separates the sub-cooled liquid, two-phase mixture, and superheated regions.
- The **critical point** is the apex of the vapor dome.

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)
Think of the T-s diagram as a **"financial statement" for heat energy in a system**. The vertical axis (Temperature) represents *how hot* the system is, and the horizontal axis (Entropy) represents *how spread out* or *disordered* the energy is. The "area under the curve" acts like the **total energy bill** — every time the system travels along a path, the area below it tells you exactly how much heat was paid in or taken out.

---

## 1.2 The T-h Diagram (Temperature–Enthalpy / Mollier Diagram)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **T-h diagram** is a property chart in which **Temperature (T)** is plotted on the **ordinate (Y-axis)** and **Specific Enthalpy (h)** is plotted on the **abscissa (X-axis)**. When the lines of constant pressure, constant entropy, and constant quality are superimposed, it becomes a powerful tool for analyzing refrigeration cycles, psychrometric processes, and steam-plant expansions.

**Key Features of the T-h Diagram:**
- For an **ideal gas**, lines of constant pressure are **straight lines** with slope equal to $\dfrac{dh}{dT} = c_p$.
- A **throttling process** (constant $h$) appears as a **vertical straight line**.
- The diagram readily visualizes the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of a refrigeration cycle as a ratio of horizontal distances.

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)
Imagine the T-h diagram as a **"heat-flow map"** where each horizontal step shows how much energy (enthalpy) the refrigerant carries at each stage. A compressor pushes the refrigerant *up and to the right* (more temperature, more enthalpy), the condenser lets it slide *left* (loses heat), the expansion valve drops it *straight down* (no enthalpy change), and the evaporator pulls it *right* again. The **horizontal distance traveled** is the energy being moved — exactly what an engineer wants to measure.

---

## 1.3 Syllabus Significance

> [!NOTE]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCAUT403)** syllabus, this topic is essential for:
> - **CO2:** *Apply the first and second laws of thermodynamics to steady-flow and non-flow processes.*
> - Visualization of Rankine, Brayton, Otto, Diesel, and Vapor Compression cycles.
> - Establishing the relationship between **entropy change** and **heat interaction**.

Standard Metric Constant used: **Universal Gas Constant** $R_u = \mathbf{8.314\ kJ/(kmol\cdot K)}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Property Relations Used

The following Maxwell relations and Tds equations form the basis for T-s and T-h analysis:

$$T\,ds = c_p\,dT - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_p dp$$

$$T\,ds = c_v\,dT + T\left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial T}\right)_v dv$$

For an **ideal gas**, these reduce to the elegant forms:

$$ds = c_p\,\frac{dT}{T} - R\,\frac{dp}{p}$$

$$ds = c_v\,\frac{dT}{T} + R\,\frac{dv}{v}$$

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Reasoning

### Step 1 — Slope of a Constant-Pressure Line on the T-s Diagram
- Hold $p$ constant → $dp = 0$.
- Substitute into the ideal-gas Tds equation:

$$ds = c_p\,\frac{dT}{T}$$

- Invert to get the slope of the isobar on a T-s chart:

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial s}\right)_p = \frac{T}{c_p}$$

> **Physical meaning:** At higher temperatures, the slope becomes steeper because the curve "tilts up" more rapidly with rising T.

### Step 2 — Slope of a Constant-Volume Line on the T-s Diagram
- Hold $v$ constant → $dv = 0$.
- Substitute into the ideal-gas Tds equation:

$$ds = c_v\,\frac{dT}{T}$$

- The slope of the isochore is:

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial s}\right)_v = \frac{T}{c_v}$$

> Since $c_p > c_v$, the isochores are **steeper** than the isobars at any given temperature.

### Step 3 — Constant-Pressure Lines on the T-h Diagram
- For an ideal gas: $dh = c_p\,dT$.
- Therefore the slope of an isobar on the T-h chart is:

$$\frac{dh}{dT}\bigg|_p = c_p$$

- Lines of constant pressure are **straight, parallel lines** with slope $c_p$ (for ideal gas).
- For real substances (e.g., refrigerants, steam), $c_p$ varies with T and the isobars curve slightly.

### Step 4 — Visualizing Cycles
- **Carnot cycle** on T-s: a **rectangle**.
- **Rankine / VCR cycle**: a distorted loop inside the two-phase dome and superheated region.
- **Throttling process** on T-h: a **vertical line** (constant $h$).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Diagram Used | Why It Is Preferred |
|---|---|---|
| Steam Power Plant (Rankine) | T-s | Clearly shows turbine expansion and pump work |
| Gas Power Cycles (Otto, Diesel) | T-s | Heat addition/rejection as enclosed areas |
| Vapor Compression Refrigeration | T-h | Throttling is a vertical line — simplifies COP analysis |
| Psychrometrics (Air-conditioning) | T-h (Mollier) | Direct humidity ratio and enthalpy reading |
| Nozzle / Diffuser Analysis | h-s (Mollier for steam) | Captures kinetic energy exchange directly |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the consolidated KTU exam-ready formula bank for this topic.**

| # | Property / Process | Governing Equation | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reversible heat transfer | $\delta Q_{rev} = T\,ds$ | kJ/kg |
| 2 | Heat in a process 1→2 (reversible) | $q = \int_1^2 T\,ds$ | kJ/kg |
| 3 | Net work of a cycle on T-s | $W_{net} = \oint T\,ds = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$ | kJ/kg |
| 4 | Slope of isobar on T-s | $(\partial T/\partial s)_p = T/c_p$ | K |
| 5 | Slope of isochore on T-s | $(\partial T/\partial s)_v = T/c_v$ | K |
| 6 | Isobar slope on T-h | $dh/dT \mid_p = c_p$ | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 7 | Entropy change — ideal gas | $\Delta s = c_p \ln(T_2/T_1) - R \ln(p_2/p_1)$ | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 8 | Refrigeration effect (VCR) | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg |
| 9 | Compressor work (VCR) | $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg |
| 10 | Coefficient of Performance | $COP = RE/W_c = (h_1 - h_4)/(h_2 - h_1)$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Throttling condition | $h_3 = h_4$ (vertical line on T-h) | kJ/kg |
| 12 | Heat rejected in condenser | $Q_{cond} = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Slope of a Constant-Pressure Line on the T-s Diagram

We begin with the **Combined First & Second Law (Gibbs) Equation** for a closed system of a simple compressible substance:

$$T\,ds = du + p\,dv$$

Substituting the enthalpy identity $h = u + pv \Rightarrow dh = du + p\,dv + v\,dp$:

$$T\,ds = dh - v\,dp$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This step rewrites the change in internal energy in terms of enthalpy, which is more convenient because pressure is the natural independent variable for many processes.

Now divide both sides by $dp$ at **constant pressure** ($dp = 0$):

$$T\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial T}\right)_p = \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right)_p = c_p$$

Solving for the slope of the isobar on the T-s chart:

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial s}\right)_p = \frac{T}{c_p}$$

> **Result:** The slope increases linearly with temperature and is inversely proportional to the specific heat at constant pressure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Slope of a Constant-Volume Line on the T-s Diagram

Start with the original Gibbs equation:

$$T\,ds = du + p\,dv$$

At **constant volume** ($dv = 0$):

$$T\,ds = du \quad \Rightarrow \quad T\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial T}\right)_v = \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial T}\right)_v = c_v$$

Therefore, the slope of the isochore on the T-s diagram is:

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial s}\right)_v = \frac{T}{c_v}$$

> **Comparison:** Because $c_p > c_v$, the constant-volume lines are **steeper** than the constant-pressure lines at any given temperature.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Heat Transfer as Area Under the T-s Curve

For any **reversible** process in a closed system:

$$\delta Q_{rev} = T\,ds$$

Integrate between two thermodynamic states 1 and 2:

$$Q_{rev} = \int_1^2 T\,ds$$

For a complete cyclic process:

$$W_{net} = \oint T\,ds = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The cyclic integral $\oint T\,ds$ equals the area enclosed by the closed loop on the T-s diagram, which by the first law equals the net work output of the cycle.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — COP of a Vapor Compression Refrigeration Cycle on the T-h Diagram

Label the four principal states of the standard VCR cycle:

- **State 1:** Saturated vapor at compressor inlet.
- **State 2:** Superheated vapor at compressor exit (after isentropic compression).
- **State 3:** Saturated liquid at condenser exit.
- **State 4:** Liquid–vapor mixture after throttling (isenthalpic expansion).

**Step 1 — Refrigeration Effect (heat absorbed in evaporator):**

Applying the steady-flow energy equation to the evaporator (4 → 1):

$$q_L = h_1 - h_4$$

**Step 2 — Compressor Work Input:**

Applying the steady-flow energy equation to the compressor (1 → 2), assuming adiabatic, no kinetic/potential energy changes:

$$w_c = h_2 - h_1$$

**Step 3 — Coefficient of Performance:**

$$\text{COP} = \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \frac{q_L}{w_c}$$

Substituting the derived expressions:

$$\boxed{\text{COP} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}}$$

**Step 4 — Heat Rejected in the Condenser:**

$$q_H = h_2 - h_3$$

**Step 5 — Throttling Constraint:**

$$h_3 = h_4 \quad \text{(vertical line on T-h chart)}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — T-s vs T-h

| Property | T-s Diagram | T-h Diagram |
|---|---|---|
| Independent variable plotted on X | Specific entropy $s$ | Specific enthalpy $h$ |
| Independent variable plotted on Y | Temperature $T$ | Temperature $T$ |
| Geometric meaning of area | $\int T\,ds$ = heat transfer | No direct area meaning |
| Throttling process representation | Curved (non-vertical) line | **Vertical line** ($h = \text{const}$) |
| Ideal-gas isobars | Curves of slope $T/c_p$ | Straight lines of slope $c_p$ |
| Best for analyzing | Carnot, Rankine, Otto, Diesel | Refrigeration cycles, psychrometrics |
| Slopes | Isobars < Isochores | All isobars parallel (ideal gas) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — T-s Diagram Anatomy

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[TS DIAGRAM ROOT] --> nodeB[AXES LAYOUT]
    nodeA --> nodeC[PROCESS CURVES]
    nodeA --> nodeD[TWO PHASE DOME]
    nodeA --> nodeE[CRITICAL POINT]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[X AXIS SPECIFIC ENTROPY S]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Y AXIS TEMPERATURE T]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[ISOBAR CONSTANT P]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[ISOCHORE CONSTANT V]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[ISOTHERM CONSTANT T]
    nodeC --> nodeC4[ISENTROPE VERTICAL LINE]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[SATURATED LIQUID LEFT BRANCH]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[SATURATED VAPOR RIGHT BRANCH]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[QUALITY LINES 0 TO 1]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[APEX OF DOME CRITICAL STATE]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — T-h Diagram Anatomy

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[TH DIAGRAM ROOT] --> nodeB[AXES LAYOUT]
    nodeA --> nodeC[PROCESS REPRESENTATIONS]
    nodeA --> nodeD[VCR CYCLE PATH]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[X AXIS SPECIFIC ENTHALPY H]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Y AXIS TEMPERATURE T]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[ISOBAR STRAIGHT LINE FOR IDEAL GAS]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[THROTTLING VERTICAL LINE]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[ISENTROPIC COMPRESSION CURVED]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[STATE 1 SAT VAPOR AT COMP INLET]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[STATE 2 SUPERHEATED AT COMP OUTLET]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[STATE 3 SAT LIQUID AT COND OUTLET]
    nodeD --> nodeD4[STATE 4 MIXTURE AFTER THROTTLING]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD2
    nodeD2 --> nodeD3
    nodeD3 --> nodeD4
    nodeD4 --> nodeD1
```

---

## 4.3 VCR Cycle — State Transition Flow on T-h

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[STATE1 SAT VAPOR] -->|PROCESS12 ISENTROPIC COMP| nodeB[STATE2 SUPERHEATED VAPOR]
    nodeB -->|PROCESS23 CONSTANT P COND| nodeC[STATE3 SAT LIQUID]
    nodeC -->|PROCESS34 THROTTLING H CONST| nodeD[STATE4 LIQ VAP MIX]
    nodeD -->|PROCESS41 CONSTANT P EVAP| nodeA
```

> **Reading the Mermaid:** Each numbered process corresponds to a real component of the refrigeration system. The arrows form a closed loop, ensuring the working fluid returns to its initial state, satisfying the cyclic condition of a heat engine/refrigerator.

---

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — When to Use T-s vs T-h

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[ENGINEERING PROBLEM] --> nodeB[WHAT IS BEING ANALYZED]
    nodeB -->|HEAT TRANSFER AND WORK| nodeC[CHOOSE TS DIAGRAM]
    nodeB -->|REFRIGERATION OR AC| nodeD[CHOOSE TH DIAGRAM]
    nodeB -->|STEAM FLOW IN NOZZLE| nodeE[CHOOSE HS MOLLIER DIAGRAM]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[AREA UNDER CURVE Q REV]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[ENCLOSED AREA W NET]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[VERTICAL LINE FOR THROTTLING]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[COP AS HORIZONTAL DISTANCE RATIO]
```

> **Schematic Note:** Physical thermodynamic charts cannot be rendered in plain text; hence, these block diagrams describe the **functional architecture** and the engineering decision pathway that an examiner expects you to articulate when answering "state the use of T-s and T-h diagrams".
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** 
*Define a T-s diagram. Why is it considered the most fundamental thermodynamic chart?*

**Model Answer:**
A T-s diagram is a property chart with **specific entropy (s)** on the abscissa and **temperature (T)** on the ordinate. It is the most fundamental thermodynamic chart because:

1. The area under any reversible process curve equals the heat transferred: $Q_{rev} = \int T\,ds$.
2. The area enclosed by a cyclic loop equals the net work output: $W_{net} = \oint T\,ds$.
3. It allows direct visualization of the second-law efficiency of any cycle through the **Carnot rectangle**.
4. The **two-phase dome** is clearly visible, making it ideal for analyzing phase-change processes in steam and refrigeration cycles.

*(3 Marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*State the significance of the T-h diagram in refrigeration cycle analysis.*

**Model Answer:**
The T-h diagram plots **specific enthalpy (h)** on the abscissa and **temperature (T)** on the ordinate. Its significance lies in:

1. The **throttling (expansion) process** appears as a vertical straight line because enthalpy remains constant ($h_3 = h_4$).
2. The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** can be directly evaluated as the ratio of horizontal line segments:
   $\text{COP} = (h_1 - h_4)/(h_2 - h_1)$.
3. For an **ideal gas**, constant-pressure lines are straight with slope $c_p$, simplifying cycle visualization.
4. It is widely used in psychrometric analysis for air-conditioning applications.

*(3 Marks)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the slope of the **constant-pressure** and **constant-volume** lines on a T-s diagram for an ideal gas. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Starting Equation (Tds First Form):**
$$T\,ds = c_p\,dT - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_p dp$$

For an **ideal gas**: $pv = RT \Rightarrow (\partial v/\partial T)_p = R/p$.

Substituting:

$$T\,ds = c_p\,dT - \frac{RT}{p}dp$$

Dividing by $T$:

$$ds = c_p\,\frac{dT}{T} - R\,\frac{dp}{p}$$

**Step 2 — Constant-Pressure Condition ($dp = 0$):**

$$ds = c_p\,\frac{dT}{T}$$

Inverting to obtain the slope of the isobar on the T-s chart:

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial s}\right)_p = \frac{T}{c_p}$$

**Step 3 — Starting Equation (Tds Second Form):**
$$T\,ds = c_v\,dT + T\left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial T}\right)_v dv$$

For an ideal gas: $p = RT/v \Rightarrow (\partial p/\partial T)_v = R/v$.

$$T\,ds = c_v\,dT + \frac{RT}{v}dv$$

$$ds = c_v\,\frac{dT}{T} + R\,\frac{dv}{v}$$

**Step 4 — Constant-Volume Condition ($dv = 0$):**

$$ds = c_v\,\frac{dT}{T}$$

Slope of the isochore:

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial s}\right)_v = \frac{T}{c_v}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison:**
Since $c_p > c_v$, the isochores are **steeper** than the isobars at any given temperature on the T-s diagram.

*(7 Marks)*

---

**(b)** Draw the **Carnot cycle** on a T-s diagram and show the areas representing heat added, heat rejected, and net work. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

The Carnot cycle consists of two **isothermal** and two **isentropic** (reversible adiabatic) processes:

| Process | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 → 2 | Isothermal heat addition at $T_H$ | Reversible heat input $Q_H$ |
| 2 → 3 | Isentropic expansion | Entropy decreases from $s_2$ to $s_1$ |
| 3 → 4 | Isothermal heat rejection at $T_L$ | Reversible heat output $Q_L$ |
| 4 → 1 | Isentropic compression | Entropy increases from $s_1$ to $s_2$ |

**Area Representation on T-s:**

$$Q_H = T_H(s_2 - s_1) \quad \text{(rectangle under 1-2)}$$

$$Q_L = T_L(s_2 - s_1) \quad \text{(rectangle under 3-4)}$$

$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L = (T_H - T_L)(s_2 - s_1) \quad \text{(enclosed rectangle)}$$

**Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

> The **rectangle** on the T-s diagram is the geometric signature of an ideal reversible cycle — the largest possible efficiency for any heat engine operating between $T_H$ and $T_L$.

*(7 Marks)*

---

### **❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and use of a T-h diagram**. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
1. **X-axis:** Specific enthalpy $h$ (in kJ/kg), increasing to the right.
2. **Y-axis:** Temperature $T$ (in K or °C), increasing upward.
3. **Constant-pressure lines (isobars):** Drawn across the chart; for ideal gases these are **straight, parallel lines** of slope $c_p$. For real substances, they curve slightly due to varying $c_p$.
4. **Constant-entropy lines (isentropes):** Drawn from top-left to bottom-right with gradually increasing curvature.
5. **Two-phase region (vapor dome):** Bounded by saturated liquid and saturated vapor lines.
6. **Constant-quality lines:** Drawn inside the dome for two-phase mixtures.

**Use:**
- **Refrigeration:** A throttling valve produces a **vertical line** (constant $h$), simplifying the calculation of COP as a horizontal distance ratio.
- **Steam Turbines:** Expansion lines can be drawn directly on the chart.
- **Psychrometrics:** The Mollier chart for moist air is essentially a T-h (or h-w) diagram used in HVAC design.
- **Heat Exchangers:** Heat duty is computed as $\Delta h$ between inlet and outlet states.

*(7 Marks)*

---

**(b)** Represent the **Vapor Compression Refrigeration (VCR) cycle** on T-h and T-s diagrams. Derive expressions for the refrigeration effect, compressor work, and COP. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**State Points (Standard VCR Cycle):**
- **State 1:** Saturated vapor at evaporator pressure $P_L$.
- **State 2:** Superheated vapor at condenser pressure $P_H$ (isentropic compression).
- **State 3:** Saturated liquid at $P_H$.
- **State 4:** Liquid–vapor mixture at $P_L$ (after throttling).

**On T-s diagram:** The cycle appears as a curved quadrilateral — compression (1→2) is a vertical line (isentropic), condensation (2→3) is along the high-pressure isobar, throttling (3→4) is an irreversible curved line, evaporation (4→1) is along the low-pressure isobar.

**On T-h diagram:** Throttling (3→4) is a clean **vertical line**; the other three processes appear as smooth curves.

**Refrigeration Effect (per kg of refrigerant):**

Applying SFEE to the evaporator (4→1), adiabatic, no work:

$$q_L = h_1 - h_4$$

**Compressor Work (per kg of refrigerant):**

Applying SFEE to the compressor (1→2), adiabatic, no KE/PE change:

$$w_c = h_2 - h_1$$

**Heat Rejected in Condenser:**

$$q_H = h_2 - h_3$$

**Throttling Constraint:**

$$h_3 = h_4 \quad \text{(constant enthalpy)}$$

**Coefficient of Performance:**

$$\text{COP} = \frac{q_L}{w_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

> **Why T-h is preferred for VCR:** The vertical throttling line removes one source of ambiguity; the COP becomes a simple ratio of two horizontal distances on the chart, making it the most examiner-friendly representation in KTU answer sheets.

*(7 Marks)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing T-s with h-s Mollier diagram.** The classical Mollier diagram for steam is the **h-s chart**, NOT the T-h chart. Examiners specifically deduct marks if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting the "reversible" qualifier.** The area-under-curve rule $Q = \int T\,ds$ applies ONLY to reversible processes. For irreversible processes, $Q < \int T\,ds$.
> 3. **In VCR cycles, marking the throttling line as isentropic.** Throttling is **isenthalpic** (constant $h$, vertical on T-h), NOT isentropic (constant $s$, vertical on T-s).
> 4. **Not labeling the two-phase dome.** Always draw and label the saturated liquid and saturated vapor lines and mark the critical point.
> 5. **Using wrong slopes.** Slope of isobar on T-s is $T/c_p$, not $c_p/T$. Slope of isobar on T-h is $c_p$ (for ideal gas), not $T/c_p$.
> 6. **Missing units in final answers.** Always state units (kJ/kg for $q$, $w$, $h$; dimensionless for COP).

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- 🔹 **T-s diagram:** Area under reversible curve = heat; enclosed loop area = net work.
- 🔹 **T-h diagram:** Throttling = vertical line; COP = horizontal distance ratio.
- 🔹 **Slope of isobar on T-s:** $(\partial T/\partial s)_p = T/c_p$ — increases with T.
- 🔹 **Slope of isochore on T-s:** $(\partial T/\partial s)_v = T/c_v$ — steeper than isobar.
- 🔹 **Slope of isobar on T-h:** $dh/dT \mid_p = c_p$ — straight line for ideal gas.
- 🔹 **Carnot cycle on T-s:** Perfect rectangle → maximum possible efficiency.
- 🔹 **VCR on T-h:** Process 3→4 is vertical; states 1 and 3 are on the saturation dome.
- 🔹 **Refrigeration Effect:** $q_L = h_1 - h_4$.
- 🔹 **Compressor Work:** $w_c = h_2 - h_1$.
- 🔹 **COP Formula:** $\text{COP} = (h_1 - h_4)/(h_2 - h_1)$.
- 🔹 **Throttling constraint:** $h_3 = h_4$ (always).
- 🔹 **Universal gas constant:** $R_u = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K).
- 🔹 **Key Maxwell relation used:** $T\,ds = dh - v\,dp$.
- 🔹 **Exam tip:** Always specify the units and mention "reversible" when invoking the area-under-curve identity.
- 🔹 **Mistake to avoid:** T-s is NOT the same as h-s Mollier diagram — keep them distinct.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: H-s Diagrams or Mollier Charts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** H-s Diagrams or Mollier Charts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# H-s Diagrams (Mollier Charts) — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The **H-s Diagram** (also called the **Mollier Chart** or **Mollier Diagram**) is a graphical representation of the thermodynamic state of a substance, in which **Enthalpy (H)** is plotted on the **vertical (Y) axis** against **Entropy (S)** on the **horizontal (X) axis**. It was developed by the German engineer **Richard Mollier** and is most commonly used for the analysis of steam and refrigerants in power and refrigeration cycles.

The Mollier chart is essentially an enthalpy–entropy (H–s) plot where every point on the chart represents a unique thermodynamic state of the working fluid. Lines of constant pressure, constant temperature, constant dryness fraction (quality), and constant volume (sometimes) are drawn to enable rapid graphical evaluation of thermodynamic processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from T-s Diagram:**
> Unlike the T-s diagram where the vertical axis is temperature, on the H-s diagram the vertical axis is **enthalpy** (which has units of kJ/kg, NOT temperature). This is the defining feature that makes Mollier charts uniquely useful for processes dominated by **enthalpy change** (e.g., turbine work, throttling processes).

## 1.2 The Mollier Chart for Steam (Most Common KTU Application)

In the context of steam power plants (a KTU-favorite topic), the Mollier chart covers:

- The **subcooled (compressed liquid) region** — to the left of the saturated liquid line
- The **wet steam region** — inside the saturation dome
- The **superheated steam region** — to the right of the saturated vapor line
- The **critical point (C)** — at the apex of the dome, where the saturated liquid and vapor lines meet

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Thermodynamic Roadmap":**
> Imagine a **roadmap of a country** where the **vertical direction represents "energy content" (altitude)** and the **horizontal direction represents "disorder/chaos" (longitude)**. Every town on this map is a unique state of steam. Towns on the western coast are "wet steam" (a mix of water and vapor), towns on the eastern coast are "superheated steam" (dry gas-like vapor), and the central dome is a "two-phase territory" where water and vapor coexist.
> - **Vertical movement** = change in enthalpy (energy input/output) — happens during heat addition or work output.
> - **Horizontal movement** = change in entropy (chaos increases or decreases).
> - **Sliding downhill on a constant-pressure line** = steam expanding in a turbine (releasing work).
> Just as a traveller uses a roadmap to plan a journey, a thermal engineer uses the Mollier chart to plan how steam will change state in a power plant.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Mollier Chart Parameters for Water/Steam:**
> - **Y-axis range:** Enthalpy $h \approx 0$ to $4000$ **kJ/kg** (saturated liquid reference at $0\,^\circ\text{C}$: $h_f = 0$)
> - **X-axis range:** Entropy $s \approx 0$ to $9$ **kJ/(kg·K)** (using **absolute entropy** values)
> - **Critical Point (C) for water:** $T_c = 374.14\,^\circ\text{C}$, $P_c = 22.06$ **MPa**, $h_c = 2087.6$ **kJ/kg**, $s_c = 4.412$ **kJ/(kg·K)**
> - **Triple Point:** $T_{tp} = 0.01\,^\circ\text{C}$, $P_{tp} = 0.6113$ **kPa**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# H-s Diagrams — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Thermodynamic Relationship Governing the Chart

The Mollier chart is a direct geometric representation of the **T ds equation** applied in a clever way. The governing relation is:

$$T \, ds = dh - v \, dp$$

Rearranging for the slope of an isobar (constant pressure, $dp = 0$):

$$\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial s}\right)_{p} = T$$

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU HIGH-YIELD CONCEPT:**
> The **slope of any constant-pressure (isobar) line** on the Mollier chart at any point is **numerically equal to the absolute temperature** of the substance at that point. This single fact allows the Mollier chart to encode temperature information visually through the steepness of the constant-pressure lines.

## 2.2 Structural Anatomy of the Mollier Chart

The chart is divided into three principal regions by the **saturation curve (dome)**:

### Region 1 — Subcooled Liquid Region
- Located to the **left** of the saturated liquid line ($x = 0$)
- Temperature < saturation temperature at that pressure
- Liquid water behaves almost as incompressible substance

### Region 2 — Wet Steam Region (Inside the Dome)
- Bounded by the **saturated liquid line** (left) and **saturated vapor line** (right)
- Mixture of saturated liquid and saturated vapor
- Quality (dryness fraction) $x$ ranges from $0$ to $1$
- **Constant pressure lines and constant temperature lines COINCIDE in this region** (they are the same line)

### Region 3 — Superheated Steam Region
- Located to the **right** of the saturated vapor line ($x = 1$)
- Temperature > saturation temperature at that pressure
- Constant pressure lines and constant temperature lines **diverge** here

## 2.3 Lines of Constant Property (Constant Property Curves)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Focus — Constant Property Lines:**

| # | Line Type | Mathematical Basis | Visual Description on Mollier Chart |
|---|-----------|-------------------|-------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Constant Pressure (Isobar)** | $\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial s}\right)_{p} = T$ | Sloping line; slope equals T. Diverges from T-lines in superheated region. |
| 2 | **Constant Temperature (Isotherm)** | In wet region: coincides with isobar. In superheated: $dh = c_p \, dT$ | Vertical in wet region; gently sloping (less steep than isobars) in superheated region. |
| 3 | **Constant Quality / Dryness Fraction** | $h = h_f + x \, h_{fg}$ | Straight line in wet region, fanning out from the critical point. |
| 4 | **Constant Volume (Isochore)** | Complex in superheated region | Generally steeper than isobars; especially important in compressor analysis. |
| 5 | **Saturation Curve (Dome)** | Saturated liquid ($x=0$) & saturated vapor ($x=1$) lines | Forms the bell-shaped dome; critical point at the apex. |
| 6 | **Constant Superheat** | Lines of constant temperature above saturation | Curved lines in the superheated region parallel to saturation line. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Comprehensive Cheat Sheet)

> [!WARNING]
> **FORMULA SHEET — Memorize for KTU ESE (End Semester Exam):**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T \, ds = dh - v \, dp$ | Combined 1st & 2nd law (Gibbs eqn.) | All SI |
| 2 | $\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial s}\right)_{p} = T$ | Slope of isobar on Mollier chart | K (numerically) |
| 3 | $h = h_f + x \, h_{fg}$ | Enthalpy in wet region | kJ/kg |
| 4 | $s = s_f + x \, s_{fg}$ | Entropy in wet region | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 5 | $h = h_g + c_p (T - T_{sat})$ | Superheated steam enthalpy | kJ/kg |
| 6 | $s = s_g + c_p \ln \frac{T}{T_{sat}}$ | Superheated steam entropy | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 7 | $h_1 - h_2 = W_{turbine}$ (per kg, isentropic) | Turbine work from Mollier chart | kJ/kg |
| 8 | $h_1 = h_2$ (throttling) | Enthalpy conservation in throttling | kJ/kg |
| 9 | $x = \dfrac{h - h_f}{h_{fg}}$ | Dryness fraction from enthalpy | dimensionless |
| 10 | $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$ | Latent heat of vaporization | kJ/kg |
| 11 | $s_{fg} = s_g - s_f$ | Entropy of vaporization | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 12 | $\eta_{Rankine} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_2'}$ | Rankine cycle efficiency (using h-values) | dimensionless |

> [!CAUTION]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Absolute value signs in any formula must use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) — **NOT** the bare vertical pipe character `|`, which breaks the markdown table syntax.

## 2.5 Why Mollier Chart is Superior to T-s Diagram for Steam Plant Analysis

| Feature | T-s Diagram | H-s (Mollier) Chart |
|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| Vertical axis | Temperature $T$ (K) | Enthalpy $h$ (kJ/kg) |
| Reading work output | Indirect — needs area integration | **Direct — vertical drop = work** |
| Throttling process | Curved path | **Horizontal line** (constant $h$) |
| Boiler heat addition | Area under curve | **Direct vertical distance** |
| Best suited for | Compressors, refrigeration | **Steam turbines, throttling, Rankine cycle** |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Mollier chart is the **workhorse tool** in:
- **Steam power plant design** — Rankine cycle analysis
- **Steam turbine performance** — isentropic efficiency calculations
- **Throttling devices** — calorimeters, expansion valves
- **Refrigeration cycle** — refrigerant property evaluation (uses $\ln P$–h chart, a Mollier variant)
- **Process industries** — evaporator and reboiler design
- **Cogeneration plants** — combined heat and power analysis

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# H-s Diagrams — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Slope of Constant Pressure Line on Mollier Chart

This is the most important derivation a KTU student must master — it forms the theoretical foundation of the Mollier chart.

### Step 1: Start from the Combined First & Second Law (Gibbs Equation)

The fundamental combined statement of the first and second laws for a closed system of a pure substance is:

$$T \, ds = du + P \, dv$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Definition of Enthalpy

We know that enthalpy is defined as:

$$h = u + Pv$$

Differentiating both sides:

$$dh = du + P \, dv + v \, dP$$

### Step 3: Solve for $du$ and Substitute

From the differentiated enthalpy equation:

$$du = dh - P \, dv - v \, dP$$

Substitute this into the Gibbs equation $T \, ds = du + P \, dv$:

$$T \, ds = \left(dh - P \, dv - v \, dP\right) + P \, dv$$

### Step 4: Simplify the Expression

The $+P \, dv$ and $-P \, dv$ terms cancel out, yielding:

$$T \, ds = dh - v \, dP$$

This is the **T ds equation** — the cornerstone relation for the Mollier chart.

### Step 5: Apply Constant Pressure Constraint

For an isobaric (constant pressure) process, $dP = 0$, hence:

$$T \, ds = dh \quad \text{(at constant pressure)}$$

### Step 6: Express as a Partial Derivative Slope

Dividing both sides by $ds$ and recognizing the constant-pressure constraint:

$$\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial s}\right)_{P} = T$$

### Step 7: Final Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation:**
> The **slope of an isobar (constant pressure line)** at any point on the Mollier chart is **numerically equal to the absolute temperature** $T$ of the substance at that point. This is why isobars on the Mollier chart are steep in high-temperature regions and shallow in low-temperature regions — the slope is a direct visual representation of temperature.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Why Throttling is a Horizontal Line on Mollier Chart

A throttling process is an isenthalpic process (constant enthalpy), and it is the most direct application of the Mollier chart.

### Step 1: Apply Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) for a Throttle

For a steady-flow throttling device with no work, no heat transfer, and negligible kinetic/potential energy changes:

$$h_1 = h_2$$

### Step 2: Geometric Interpretation

On the Mollier chart, $h$ is the Y-axis. A process where $h$ remains constant while $s$ increases (or decreases) is a **horizontal line** in the H-s plane.

### Step 3: Direction of Process

For an irreversible throttling, the second law dictates that entropy **increases**:

$$s_2 > s_1 \quad \text{(throttling)}$$

### Step 4: Final Geometric Picture

> [!NOTE]
> A **throttling process appears as a horizontal line moving from left to right** on the Mollier chart. The horizontal distance is the entropy generation, and the vertical coordinate is the constant enthalpy $h$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Work Output in a Steam Turbine Using Mollier Chart

For an isentropic (reversible adiabatic) turbine expansion, the process is a **vertical line** on the Mollier chart (constant $s$).

### Step 1: SFEE for Steady-Flow Turbine

Neglecting heat transfer, kinetic, and potential energy changes:

$$w_{turbine} = h_1 - h_2$$

### Step 2: Isentropic Constraint

For an ideal (isentropic) process:

$$s_1 = s_2$$

### Step 3: Graphical Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The isentropic turbine work is **the vertical drop on the Mollier chart** between the inlet state (1) and the isentropic exit state (2'). The actual turbine work uses the actual exit state (2), which lies to the right of 2' on the same pressure line.

### Step 4: Isentropic Efficiency Formula

$$\eta_{isentropic} = \frac{h_1 - h_{2,actual}}{h_1 - h_{2,isentropic}}$$

All three enthalpy values are **read directly as vertical coordinates** from the Mollier chart.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Enthalpy in the Wet Steam Region (Linear Interpolation)

For a wet steam mixture with quality $x$:

$$h_{wet} = (1-x) \, h_f + x \, h_g$$

Expanding:

$$h_{wet} = h_f - x \, h_f + x \, h_g = h_f + x(h_g - h_f)$$

Since $h_g - h_f = h_{fg}$ (latent heat):

$$h_{wet} = h_f + x \, h_{fg}$$

This is the linear relationship that makes **constant-quality lines straight** in the wet region of the Mollier chart.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> Steam at $3$ MPa and $350\,^\circ\text{C}$ expands isentropically in a turbine to a pressure of $10$ kPa. Determine the work output per kg of steam and the dryness fraction at the exit, using Mollier chart principles. Given: at $3$ MPa and $350\,^\circ\text{C}$: $h_1 = 3127$ kJ/kg, $s_1 = 6.74$ kJ/(kg·K). At $10$ kPa: $h_f = 192$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 2392$ kJ/kg, $s_f = 0.649$ kJ/(kg·K), $s_{fg} = 7.499$ kJ/(kg·K).

### Solution Step-by-Step:

**Step 1: Identify Inlet State**
At point 1: $P_1 = 3$ MPa, $T_1 = 350\,^\circ\text{C}$ (superheated steam)
$h_1 = 3127$ kJ/kg, $s_1 = 6.74$ kJ/(kg·K)

**Step 2: Apply Isentropic Constraint**
For an isentropic expansion:
$s_2 = s_1 = 6.74$ kJ/(kg·K)

**Step 3: Check if State 2 is in Wet Region**
At $P_2 = 10$ kPa: $s_g = s_f + s_{fg} = 0.649 + 7.499 = 8.148$ kJ/(kg·K)

Since $s_2 = 6.74 < s_g = 8.148$, the exit state is in the **wet region**.

**Step 4: Calculate Quality $x_2$ at Exit**

$$x_2 = \frac{s_2 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.74 - 0.649}{7.499}$$

$$x_2 = \frac{6.091}{7.499} = 0.8122$$

**Step 5: Calculate Exit Enthalpy $h_2$**

$$h_2 = h_f + x_2 \, h_{fg} = 192 + 0.8122 \times 2392$$

$$h_2 = 192 + 1942.78 = 2134.78 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 6: Calculate Turbine Work Output**

$$w_{turbine} = h_1 - h_2 = 3127 - 2134.78 = 992.22 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 7: Final Answers**
- **Quality at exit:** $x_2 = 0.8122$ (i.e., $81.22\%$ dry)
- **Turbine work output:** $w_{turbine} = 992.22$ **kJ/kg**

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Process Visualization on Mollier Chart

| Process Type | Path on Mollier Chart | What it Represents |
|--------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Isentropic compression** | Vertical line moving **upward** | Ideal compressor |
| **Isentropic expansion** | Vertical line moving **downward** | Ideal turbine |
| **Throttling** | Horizontal line moving **rightward** | Pressure-reducing valve |
| **Boiler heat addition** | Upward curving line following isobar | Steam generation |
| **Condenser heat rejection** | Downward line following isobar | Steam condensation |
| **Constant pressure heating** | Upward slope (slope = T) | Isobaric process |
| **Isothermal** | In wet region: coincides with isobar. In superheated: less steep than isobar | Reversible isothermal heat transfer |
| **Adiabatic mixing** | Connects two states; result lies on straight line | Mixing of two streams |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# H-s Diagrams — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Anatomy of the Mollier Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mollier Chart Origin] --> B[Saturation Dome]
    A --> C[Critical Point C]
    A --> D[Isobaric Lines]
    A --> E[Isothermal Lines]
    A --> F[Constant Quality Lines]
    A --> G[Constant Volume Lines]

    B --> B1[Saturated Liquid Line x equals 0]
    B --> B2[Saturated Vapor Line x equals 1]
    B --> B3[Subcooled Liquid Region]
    B --> B4[Wet Steam Region]
    B --> B5[Superheated Steam Region]

    C --> C1[Tc equals 374.14 C]
    C --> C2[Pc equals 22.06 MPa]
    C --> C3[hc equals 2087.6 kJ per kg]

    D --> D1[Slope equals Temperature T]
    D --> D2[High Pressure Lines: Steep]
    D --> D3[Low Pressure Lines: Shallow]

    E --> E1[Vertical in Wet Region]
    E --> E2[Gentle Slope in Superheated Region]
    E --> E3[Diverge from Isobars Outside Dome]

    F --> F1[Straight Lines Inside Dome]
    F --> F2[Fan Out from Critical Point]
    F --> F3[Quality x from 0 to 1]

    G --> G1[Steeper than Isobars]
    G --> G2[Important in Compressor Analysis]
    G --> G3[Used for Reciprocating Compressors]

    style A fill:#FFD700
    style B fill:#87CEEB
    style C fill:#FF6347
    style D fill:#90EE90
    style E fill:#DDA0DD
    style F fill:#FFA500
    style G fill:#F0E68C
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Rankine Cycle on Mollier Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Point 1: Boiler Exit] -->|Isentropic Expansion in Turbine| P2[Point 2: Turbine Exit]
    P2 -->|Heat Rejection in Condenser| P3[Point 3: Condenser Exit]
    P3 -->|Pump Work Input| P4[Point 4: Pump Exit]
    P4 -->|Heat Addition in Boiler| P1

    P1 --> P1A[State: Superheated Steam]
    P1A --> P1B[h1 high, s1 moderate]

    P2 --> P2A[State: Wet Steam]
    P2A --> P2B[h2 low, s2 equals s1]

    P3 --> P3A[State: Saturated Liquid]
    P3A --> P3B[h3 equals hf at P2]

    P4 --> P4A[State: Compressed Liquid]
    P4A --> P4B[h4 slightly greater than h3]

    P1 -.->|Vertical Drop equals Turbine Work| P2
    P3 -.->|Vertical Rise equals Pump Work| P4

    style P1 fill:#FF4500
    style P2 fill:#1E90FF
    style P3 fill:#32CD32
    style P4 fill:#FFD700
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Key Processes Visualized on Mollier Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Thermodynamic Process] --> Process1[Isentropic Process]
    Start --> Process2[Throttling Process]
    Start --> Process3[Isobaric Process]
    Start --> Process4[Isothermal Process]

    Process1 --> Proc1A[Vertical Line on H-s Chart]
    Process1 --> Proc1B[Slope equals Infinity]
    Process1 --> Proc1C[No Entropy Change]

    Process2 --> Proc2A[Horizontal Line on H-s Chart]
    Process2 --> Proc2B[Constant Enthalpy]
    Process2 --> Proc2C[Entropy Increases]

    Process3 --> Proc3A[Sloped Line on H-s Chart]
    Process3 --> Proc3B[Slope equals Temperature T]
    Process3 --> Proc3C[Constant Pressure]

    Process4 --> Proc4A[Vertical in Wet Region]
    Process4 --> Proc4B[Gentle Slope in Superheated Region]
    Process4 --> Proc4C[Constant Temperature]

    style Start fill:#FF1493
    style Process1 fill:#00BFFF
    style Process2 fill:#FFD700
    style Process3 fill:#7CFC00
    style Process4 fill:#FF69B4
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Mollier Chart Application in Steam Power Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Boiler[Boiler: Heat Addition] -->|State 1: Superheated Steam| Turbine[Steam Turbine: Isentropic Expansion]
    Turbine -->|State 2: Wet Steam| Condenser[Condenser: Heat Rejection]
    Condenser -->|State 3: Saturated Liquid| Pump[Feedwater Pump: Compression]
    Pump -->|State 4: Compressed Liquid| Boiler

    Boiler -.->|Reads h1, s1| Mollier[Mollier Chart Analysis]
    Turbine -.->|Reads h2, s2 equals s1| Mollier
    Condenser -.->|Reads h3| Mollier
    Pump -.->|Reads h4| Mollier

    Mollier --> Calc1[Work Output W equals h1 minus h2]
    Mollier --> Calc2[Heat Input Q equals h1 minus h4]
    Mollier --> Calc3[Efficiency eta equals W by Q]
    Mollier --> Calc4[Quality x at Exit]

    style Boiler fill:#FF6347
    style Turbine fill:#4682B4
    style Condenser fill:#87CEEB
    style Pump fill:#DAA520
    style Mollier fill:#FFD700
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# H-s Diagrams — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

> **Q1.** Define **Mollier chart**. State any **two advantages** of using the H-s diagram over the T-s diagram for steam power cycle analysis.

### Model Answer:

> [!NOTE]
> **Mollier Chart Definition:** A Mollier chart is a graphical representation of the thermodynamic properties of a substance, with **enthalpy (H)** on the vertical axis and **entropy (S)** on the horizontal axis. It is used extensively for the analysis of steam and refrigeration cycles.
>
> **Two Advantages over T-s Diagram:**
> 1. **Direct reading of work/heat:** The vertical distance between any two states on the Mollier chart directly gives the enthalpy change, which represents work output (in turbines) or heat transfer (in boilers/condensers). On the T-s diagram, work is obtained by area integration, which is cumbersome.
> 2. **Throttling becomes a straight horizontal line:** Since enthalpy is constant in throttling, the process appears as a simple horizontal line on the H-s chart, making analysis direct. On the T-s diagram, throttling appears as a curve, requiring complex plotting.

**[3 Marks Breakdown: Definition = 1.5 marks, Two advantages = 1.5 marks]**

---

### Question 2: Process Identification

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

> **Q2.** With reference to the H-s (Mollier) diagram, what is the **geometric significance** of the slope of a constant pressure line? Express the relationship mathematically.

### Model Answer:

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Significance:** The slope of a constant pressure line (isobar) on the Mollier chart at any point is **numerically equal to the absolute temperature** $T$ of the substance at that point.
>
> **Mathematical Relationship:**
> Starting from the T ds equation: $T \, ds = dh - v \, dP$
>
> For an isobaric process, $dP = 0$, hence: $T \, ds = dh$
>
> Therefore: $\left(\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial s}\right)_P = T$

**[3 Marks Breakdown: Significance statement = 1.5 marks, Mathematical derivation = 1.5 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

> **Q(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and features** of the H-s (Mollier) chart for steam. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Answer for Q(a):

**Construction of the Mollier Chart:**

**Step 1: Axes Selection**
- **Vertical axis (Y):** Enthalpy $h$ in kJ/kg
- **Horizontal axis (X):** Entropy $s$ in kJ/(kg·K)
- Reference: $h = 0$ for saturated liquid at $0\,^\circ\text{C}$; $s = 0$ reference at triple point (absolute scale).

**Step 2: Saturation Curve (Dome)**
- Plot saturated liquid enthalpies $h_f$ against $s_f$ for all temperatures from $0\,^\circ\text{C}$ to critical point.
- Plot saturated vapor enthalpies $h_g$ against $s_g$ for the same range.
- Join points to form the saturation dome with **critical point (C)** at the apex.

**Step 3: Constant Pressure Lines (Isobars)**
- For each pressure ($P = 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 3, 10$ MPa etc.), plot $h$ vs $s$ in all three regions.
- In wet region: each isobar coincides with the corresponding isotherm.
- In superheated region: isobars diverge from isotherms, with slope = T.

**Step 4: Constant Temperature Lines (Isotherms)**
- In wet region: coincide with isobars (vertical).
- In superheated region: drawn as separate lines with $dh = c_p \, dT$ governing the slope.

**Step 5: Constant Quality (Dryness Fraction) Lines**
- In wet region: straight lines connecting the saturated liquid line to the saturated vapor line at constant $x$.
- Fanning out from the critical point.

**Step 6: Constant Volume Lines (Optional but Often Included)**
- Especially important for compressor work analysis.

**Key Features:**
1. **Critical Point (C)** at the apex: $P_c = 22.06$ MPa, $T_c = 374.14\,^\circ\text{C}$
2. **Subcooled region** (left of dome)
3. **Wet region** (inside dome)
4. **Superheated region** (right of dome)
5. **Constant pressure and temperature lines coincide inside the dome**

> [!NOTE]
> **[7 Marks Breakdown: Axes = 1 mark, Saturation curve = 1.5 marks, Isobars = 1.5 marks, Isotherms = 1 mark, Constant quality = 1 mark, Features = 1 mark]**

---

> **Q(b)** Steam enters a turbine at $P_1 = 4$ MPa and $T_1 = 400\,^\circ\text{C}$ and expands isentropically to $P_2 = 10$ kPa. Using the Mollier chart, determine:
> (i) The enthalpy at turbine inlet
> (ii) The dryness fraction at turbine exit
> (iii) The work output per kg of steam
> **[7 Marks]**

### Model Answer for Q(b):

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 4$ MPa, $T_1 = 400\,^\circ\text{C}$ (superheated steam)
- $P_2 = 10$ kPa
- Isentropic expansion: $s_2 = s_1$

**From steam tables (consistent with Mollier chart readings):**
- At inlet: $h_1 = 3213$ kJ/kg, $s_1 = 6.769$ kJ/(kg·K)
- At $P_2 = 10$ kPa: $h_f = 192$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 2392$ kJ/kg, $s_f = 0.649$ kJ/(kg·K), $s_{fg} = 7.499$ kJ/(kg·K), $s_g = 8.148$ kJ/(kg·K)

**(i) Enthalpy at Turbine Inlet:**
$h_1 = 3213$ kJ/kg (read directly from Mollier chart at the intersection of $P = 4$ MPa isobar and $T = 400\,^\circ\text{C}$ isotherm)

**(ii) Dryness Fraction at Exit:**

$$x_2 = \frac{s_2 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.769 - 0.649}{7.499} = \frac{6.120}{7.499}$$

$$x_2 = 0.816$$

**(iii) Work Output per kg of Steam:**

$$h_2 = h_f + x_2 \cdot h_{fg} = 192 + 0.816 \times 2392$$

$$h_2 = 192 + 1951.87 = 2143.87 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$w_{turbine} = h_1 - h_2 = 3213 - 2143.87$$

$$\boxed{w_{turbine} = 1069.13 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **[7 Marks Breakdown: Part i = 2 marks, Part ii = 2.5 marks, Part iii = 2.5 marks]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

> **Q(a)** Derive the relation $\left(\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial s}\right)_P = T$ and explain its **significance** in the construction of the Mollier chart. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Answer for Q(a):

**Derivation:**

Starting from the first law for a closed system (reversible process):

$$dq = du + P \, dv$$

By the second law, $dq = T \, ds$. Equating:

$$T \, ds = du + P \, dv \quad \text{...(1)}$$

From the definition of enthalpy: $h = u + Pv$

Differentiating: $dh = du + P \, dv + v \, dP$

Solving for $du + P \, dv$:

$$du + P \, dv = dh - v \, dP \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Substituting (2) into (1):

$$T \, ds = dh - v \, dP \quad \text{...(3)}$$

For a constant pressure process, $dP = 0$:

$$T \, ds = dh$$

Dividing both sides by $ds$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial s}\right)_P = T$$

**Significance in Mollier Chart Construction:**

1. **Slope-equals-Temperature Principle:** This relation shows that the slope of any isobar (constant pressure line) on the Mollier chart is numerically equal to the absolute temperature at that point. This single equation is the foundation of Mollier chart construction — it allows the chart-maker to plot isobars by knowing temperature data alone.

2. **Visual Temperature Indication:** Even though temperature is not on either axis, the steepness of isobars gives an immediate visual cue about the temperature regime. High-pressure isobars (high T) are steep; low-pressure isobars (low T) are shallow.

3. **Geometric Identification of States:** By measuring the slope of an isobar at any point, one can determine the temperature of the substance at that state — a powerful analytical tool.

> [!NOTE]
> **[7 Marks Breakdown: Derivation = 4 marks, Significance explanation = 3 marks]**

---

> **Q(b)** Explain how the following processes are represented on the Mollier chart and hence obtain the relevant equations:
> (i) Throttling process
> (ii) Isentropic expansion
> **[7 Marks]**

### Model Answer for Q(b):

**(i) Throttling Process:**

A throttling process is an **isenthalpic** (constant enthalpy) process. Applying SFEE to a throttle with no work, no heat transfer, and negligible KE/PE changes:

$$h_1 = h_2$$

On the Mollier chart, since $h$ is the vertical axis, this appears as a **horizontal line** moving from left to right (since entropy increases due to irreversibility):

$$s_2 > s_1$$

**Geometric Representation:** Horizontal straight line at constant $h$ from initial state 1 to final state 2.

**Relevant Equation:** $h_1 = h_2$ (enthalpy conservation)

**(ii) Isentropic Expansion:**

An isentropic process is a **reversible adiabatic** process. For such a process:

$$s_1 = s_2 = \text{constant}$$

On the Mollier chart, since $s$ is the horizontal axis, a constant-$s$ process appears as a **vertical line**.

**Geometric Representation:** Vertical straight line from initial state 1 to final state 2.

**Relevant Equations:**
- For a turbine: $w = h_1 - h_2$ (work output = vertical drop)
- For a compressor: $w_{in} = h_2 - h_1$ (work input = vertical rise)

> [!NOTE]
> **[7 Marks Breakdown: Throttling representation = 3.5 marks, Isentropic expansion = 3.5 marks]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 COMMON MISTAKES — WHERE KTU STUDENTS LOSE MARKS:**
>
