> 1. **Confusing T-s with H-s Axes:** The single most common error is treating the Mollier chart as a T-s diagram and reading temperatures from the Y-axis. **Mollier chart's vertical axis is ENTHALPY, not temperature.** Temperature is implicit in the slope of isobars.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Isentropic Constraint:** In turbine problems, students often forget to apply $s_1 = s_2$ for isentropic expansion and directly use $h_2$ from a different point. **Always trace a vertical line from state 1 to the exit pressure isobar.**
>
> 3. **Skipping Saturation Check:** Students assume the exit state is in the wet region without checking $s_2 < s_g$ at $P_2$. This leads to using superheated formulas in the wet region (or vice versa).
>
> 4. **Sign Convention Errors:** Work output is **positive** when $h_1 > h_2$. Mark deductions occur when students write $h_2 - h_1$ for turbine work.
>
> 5. **Ignoring Pump Work:** In Rankine cycle problems, students often forget the small but non-zero pump work term, which can affect cycle efficiency calculations.
>
> 6. **Mixing Absolute and Relative Entropy:** Mollier chart uses **absolute entropy**. If a problem gives entropy in different references, conversion is essential.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🔑 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — H-s DIAGRAM / MOLLIER CHART:**

- ✅ The Mollier chart plots **Enthalpy (H)** on Y-axis vs **Entropy (s)** on X-axis.
- ✅ The **slope of an isobar** is **$\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial s}\right)_P = T$** — this is THE foundational equation.
- ✅ The chart features a **saturation dome** with the **critical point** at its apex ($P_c = 22.06$ MPa, $T_c = 374.14\,^\circ\text{C}$).
- ✅ Three regions: **subcooled liquid** (left), **wet steam** (inside dome), **superheated steam** (right).
- ✅ **Isentropic processes** appear as **vertical lines** on the Mollier chart.
- ✅ **Throttling processes** appear as **horizontal lines** (constant $h$).
- ✅ **Isobaric processes** have **sloped lines** with slope = T.
- ✅ **Constant quality lines** are **straight lines** in the wet region, fanning from the critical point.
- ✅ **In the wet region**, isobars and isotherms **coincide** (both are vertical).
- ✅ **In the superheated region**, isobars and isotherms **diverge**; isobars are steeper than isotherms.
- ✅ Mollier chart uses **absolute entropy** values (not relative).
- ✅ **Turbine work** = vertical drop on the chart = $h_1 - h_2$.
- ✅ **Mollier chart is superior to T-s chart** for steam plant analysis because enthalpy (work/heat) can be read directly as vertical distances.
- ✅ For **wet steam enthalpy**: $h = h_f + x \, h_{fg}$.
- ✅ For **wet steam entropy**: $s = s_f + x \, s_{fg}$.
- ✅ Quality formula: $x = \dfrac{s - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \dfrac{h - h_f}{h_{fg}}$.
- ✅ Mollier chart is also used in **refrigeration** (as $\ln P$–h chart variant).
- ✅ Common applications: Rankine cycle, steam turbines, throttling calorimeters, reheating, regeneration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **END OF NOTES — H-s Diagrams / Mollier Charts (Module 2)**
> *Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme PCAUT403 — Thermodynamics and Heat Transfer*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steam Tables, Property Calculations Using Steam Tables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Steam Tables, Property Calculations Using Steam Tables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Steam Tables** are thermodynamic property tables that tabulate the equilibrium thermodynamic properties of **water and steam** as a function of **pressure** and **temperature** along the saturation (phase-change) boundary and in the **single-phase regions** (compressed/subcooled liquid, superheated vapor).

In KTU thermodynamics, the steam table is the single most-used reference tool for solving steady-flow and non-flow energy problems involving the **water–steam working fluid**. It is divided structurally into two principal sets:

| Table Set | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Table A – Saturation Table** (by Temperature **or** by Pressure) | Lists $v_f, v_g, u_f, u_g, h_f, h_g, s_f, s_g$ plus $h_{fg}, s_{fg}, v_{fg}$ at the saturated liquid–vapor coexistence line. |
| **Table B – Superheated Steam Table** | Lists $v, u, h, s$ of vapor at $T > T_{sat}(P)$ for a fixed pressure. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A key conceptual fact: **pressure and temperature are NOT independent** on the saturation line. The saturation table can therefore be entered with **either** a temperature **or** a pressure — *not both* — because the other is automatically fixed by the saturation relationship $P_{sat}(T)$.

The two extreme anchor points of the table are:

- **Triple Point:** $T_{tp} = 0.01^\circ\text{C},\; P_{tp} = 0.6113\;\text{kPa}$
- **Critical Point:** $T_{cr} = 374.14^\circ\text{C},\; P_{cr} = 22.09\;\text{MPa}$

At the critical point, the saturated liquid and saturated vapor states **merge identically**; the distinction $f \equiv g$ makes properties such as $v_{fg}$ become zero.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Library of Steam"

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a giant library that has a card-catalogue for every drop of water and every puff of steam that exists. You walk in and say either *"I want water at 200 °C"* **or** *"I want water at 1 MPa"* — but not both, because the book says: *"If it is at 200 °C, it is *automatically* at 1554 kPa, and vice versa."* The library (steam table) then hands you the values of volume, energy, enthalpy, and entropy for the saturated liquid, the saturated vapor, and the gap ($_{fg}$) in between. This card catalogue is what engineers call the **steam table**.

In simpler terms, the steam table removes the need to solve complex equations of state. The properties are *measured and tabulated once* by the international community (IAPWS-IF97 formulation in modern KTU-approved data) and reused forever.

### 1.3 The Three Regions of the P–v Diagram

A water–steam system may exist in **one of three states**, and the steam table provides a different *recipe* for each:

1. **Subcooled (Compressed) Liquid** — $T < T_{sat}(P)$ → use **compressed-liquid approximation** (often $v \approx v_f(T)$).
2. **Saturated Mixture** — $T = T_{sat}(P)$, $v_f < v < v_g$ → use **quality $x$**.
3. **Superheated Vapor** — $T > T_{sat}(P)$ → read directly from **superheated table**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants Used in This Module

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Critical Pressure $P_{cr}$ | **22.09 MPa** |
| Critical Temperature $T_{cr}$ | **374.14 °C** |
| Triple-Point Pressure $P_{tp}$ | **0.6113 kPa** |
| Triple-Point Temperature $T_{tp}$ | **0.01 °C** |
| Reference State for $h, s$ | $h_f = 0,\; s_f = 0$ at **0.01 °C (saturated liquid)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the Saturation Table

When you open the saturation table at, say, $P = 1.0\;\text{MPa}$, the table automatically reports the saturation temperature $T_{sat} = 179.91\;\text{°C}$ and gives **eight state-point properties**, plus the **differences** ($_{fg}$-properties):

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $v_f$ | specific volume of **saturated liquid** |
| $v_g$ | specific volume of **saturated vapor** |
| $u_f,\;u_g$ | specific internal energy of saturated liquid / vapor |
| $h_f,\;h_g$ | specific enthalpy of saturated liquid / vapor |
| $s_f,\;s_g$ | specific entropy of saturated liquid / vapor |
| $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f$ | change in $v$ during vaporization |
| $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$ | **latent heat of vaporization** |
| $s_{fg} = s_g - s_f$ | entropy of vaporization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Quality $x$ is defined as the **mass fraction of vapor** in a saturated liquid–vapor mixture. Thus, $x = 0 \Rightarrow$ saturated liquid, $x = 1 \Rightarrow$ saturated vapor, $0 < x < 1 \Rightarrow$ two-phase mixture.

### 2.2 Linear Interpolation in Steam Tables

Steam tables tabulate data at discrete intervals (typically every 5 °C in temperature, every 0.1–1 MPa in pressure). When the required value lies between two tabulated entries, **linear interpolation** is used:

$$
x = x_1 + \frac{(x_2 - x_1)}{(y_2 - y_1)} \cdot (y - y_1)
$$

where $y$ is the independent variable ($T$ or $P$) and $x$ is the dependent property.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners expect interpolation work to be **shown explicitly** with bracketed values, not just the final result. Half-marks are frequently deducted for "bare answers."

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$ | Specific volume of wet steam | Find $v$ from $x$ |
| 2 | $u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}$ | Internal energy of wet steam | Energy balance |
| 3 | $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ | Enthalpy of wet steam | Rankine/Brayton cycle |
| 4 | $s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$ | Entropy of wet steam | Entropy balance |
| 5 | $x = \dfrac{v - v_f}{v_g - v_f}$ | Quality from specific volume | **Most common in KTU** |
| 6 | $x = \dfrac{h - h_f}{h_{fg}}$ | Quality from enthalpy | Heat-exchanger problems |
| 7 | $x = \dfrac{u - u_f}{u_{fg}}$ | Quality from internal energy | Closed-system analysis |
| 8 | $x = \dfrac{s - s_f}{s_{fg}}$ | Quality from entropy | Nozzle / turbine exit |
| 9 | $h_g = h_f + h_{fg}$ | Saturated vapor enthalpy | Cross-check |
| 10 | $u = h - Pv$ | Internal energy from $h, v$ | Back-calculation |
| 11 | $v \approx v_f(T)$ | Compressed liquid approximation | Subcooled region |
| 12 | $u \approx u_f(T)$ | Compressed liquid approximation | Subcooled region |
| 13 | $h \approx h_f(T) + v_f(T)\cdot(P - P_{sat}(T))$ | Compressed liquid correction | High-pressure liquid |
| 14 | $s \approx s_f(T)$ | Compressed liquid approximation | Subcooled region |

> [!TIP]
> Memorize formulas 1–4 (the **lever rule**) as a single concept. They all share the same mathematical form: $y = y_f + x \cdot y_{fg}$. KTU questions often test your ability to recognize which property ($v, u, h, s$) is being requested.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Thermal Power Plants (Rankine Cycle):** Boiler exit (superheated steam), turbine inlet, condenser (saturated mixture at low $P$) — every node is a steam-table lookup.
- **Cogeneration & Combined Cycles:** Reheat and regeneration states must be evaluated from superheated tables.
- **Refrigeration (Vapor Compression Cycle):** Refrigerant properties are looked up analogously.
- **Process Industries:** Steam used as a heating medium is always characterized by **two table lookups** — saturated liquid (condensate) at delivery, and the supply state (often superheated).
- **Nuclear Reactors:** Pressurized water at 15 MPa exits the core as compressed liquid, requiring the compressed-liquid approximation.

### 2.5 Decision Logic for State Determination

The KTU-standard "**two-given, two-lookup**" procedure:

1. **Given $P$ and $T$:**
   - Read $T_{sat}$ at given $P$.
   - If $T < T_{sat}$ → **subcooled liquid** → use compressed-liquid approximation.
   - If $T = T_{sat}$ → **saturated mixture** → need $x$ to fully define state.
   - If $T > T_{sat}$ → **superheated vapor** → use superheated table at given $P$ and $T$.

2. **Given $P$ and $v$ (or $u$, $h$, $s$):**
   - Read $v_f$ and $v_g$ at given $P$.
   - If $v < v_f$ → **subcooled liquid**.
   - If $v_f \le v \le v_g$ → **saturated mixture** → solve for $x$.
   - If $v > v_g$ → **superheated vapor**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Lever Rule (Quality-Based Property Formula)

Consider a closed rigid vessel of total mass $m$ containing a saturated liquid–vapor mixture at pressure $P$. Let $m_f$ be the mass of saturated liquid occupying volume $V_f$ with specific volume $v_f$, and $m_g$ the mass of saturated vapor occupying $V_g$ with specific volume $v_g$.

**Step 1 — Mass balance:**

$$
m = m_f + m_g
$$

**Step 2 — Volume balance:**

$$
V = V_f + V_g = m_f v_f + m_g v_g
$$

**Step 3 — Define overall specific volume:**

$$
v = \frac{V}{m} = \frac{m_f v_f + m_g v_g}{m_f + m_g}
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce quality $x = m_g / m$:**

Substitute $m_g = x\,m$ and $m_f = (1-x)\,m$:

$$
v = \frac{(1-x) m \, v_f + x\, m \, v_g}{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$
v = (1-x) v_f + x\, v_g = v_f + x(v_g - v_f) = v_f + x\, v_{fg}
$$

**Step 6 — Re-arrange for quality (general lever rule):**

$$
x = \frac{v - v_f}{v_g - v_f} = \frac{v - v_f}{v_{fg}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The identical mathematical structure applies to $u$, $h$, and $s$ because these are all *extensive-per-unit-mass* properties and the mixture is treated as a **mass-weighted average** of the two phases. This is why formulas 1–4 in the formula sheet share the same shape.

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Quality from Specific Volume

**Problem:** Water exists as a saturated mixture at $P = 200\;\text{kPa}$. The specific volume is measured as $v = 0.30\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$. Determine the quality $x$ and the enthalpy $h$.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1.** From the saturation table (by pressure) at $P = 200\;\text{kPa}$:

$$
v_f = 0.001061\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg},\quad v_g = 0.8857\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

$$
h_f = 504.71\;\text{kJ/kg},\quad h_{fg} = 2201.6\;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 2.** Apply the lever rule:

$$
x = \frac{v - v_f}{v_g - v_f} = \frac{0.30 - 0.001061}{0.8857 - 0.001061}
$$

**Step 3.** Numerator:

$$
v - v_f = 0.30 - 0.001061 = 0.29894\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

**Step 4.** Denominator:

$$
v_g - v_f = 0.8857 - 0.001061 = 0.88464\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

**Step 5.** Quality:

$$
x = \frac{0.29894}{0.88464} = 0.3379 \approx 0.338
$$

**Step 6.** Enthalpy of the wet mixture:

$$
h = h_f + x \, h_{fg} = 504.71 + (0.338)(2201.6)
$$

$$
h = 504.71 + 744.14 = 1248.85\;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Result:** $x \approx 0.338$ and $h \approx 1248.85\;\text{kJ/kg}$.

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Superheated Steam Properties

**Problem:** Steam at $P = 2.0\;\text{MPa}$ and $T = 300\;^\circ\text{C}$ flows through a turbine. Find $v, h, s$.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1.** At $P = 2.0\;\text{MPa}$, the saturation temperature from the table is $T_{sat} = 212.42\;^\circ\text{C}$.

**Step 2.** Since $T = 300\;^\circ\text{C} > T_{sat} = 212.42\;^\circ\text{C}$, the state is **superheated vapor**.

**Step 3.** Enter the **superheated steam table** at $P = 2.0\;\text{MPa}$, $T = 300\;^\circ\text{C}$:

$$
v = 0.12547\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

$$
h = 3023.5\;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

$$
s = 6.7664\;\text{kJ/kg·K}
$$

**Step 4.** (Optional) Cross-check using $u = h - Pv$:

$$
u = 3023.5 - (2000)(0.12547) = 3023.5 - 250.94 = 2772.56\;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Compressed Liquid Approximation

**Problem:** Liquid water at $T = 100\;^\circ\text{C}$ and $P = 5\;\text{MPa}$ flows in a pump. Estimate $v, h, s$ using the compressed-liquid approximation.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1.** Identify the state: at $T = 100\;^\circ\text{C}$, $P_{sat} \approx 101.35\;\text{kPa}$. Since $P = 5\;\text{MPa} \gg P_{sat}$, the liquid is **compressed (subcooled)**.

**Step 2.** Use the compressed-liquid approximation. Properties of saturated liquid at the *same temperature* $T = 100\;^\circ\text{C}$:

$$
v_f(100\,°C) = 0.001043\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

$$
h_f(100\,°C) = 419.17\;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

$$
s_f(100\,°C) = 1.3072\;\text{kJ/kg·K}
$$

**Step 3.** Approximate:

$$
v \approx v_f(T) = 0.001043\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

$$
s \approx s_f(T) = 1.3072\;\text{kJ/kg·K}
$$

**Step 4.** Refined enthalpy using the correction term:

$$
h \approx h_f(T) + v_f(T)\bigl[P - P_{sat}(T)\bigr]
$$

$$
h \approx 419.17 + (0.001043)(5000 - 101.35)
$$

$$
h \approx 419.17 + (0.001043)(4898.65) = 419.17 + 5.109 = 424.28\;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The correction term $v_f \cdot (P - P_{sat})$ is **mandatory** in KTU solutions whenever a high-pressure liquid enthalpy is asked. Bare use of $h \approx h_f$ loses one mark.

### 3.5 Worked Example 4 — State Determination with Two Properties Given

**Problem:** Water is in a piston-cylinder at $P = 1.5\;\text{MPa}$ and $v = 0.20\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$. Find $T, x, u, h, s$.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1.** From saturation table at $P = 1.5\;\text{MPa}$:

$$
T_{sat} = 198.32\;^\circ\text{C}
$$

$$
v_f = 0.001053\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg},\quad v_g = 0.13177\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

$$
u_f = 843.16\;\text{kJ/kg},\; u_{fg} = 1800.9\;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

$$
h_f = 844.84\;\text{kJ/kg},\; h_{fg} = 1947.3\;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

$$
s_f = 2.3150\;\text{kJ/kg·K},\; s_{fg} = 3.7389\;\text{kJ/kg·K}
$$

**Step 2.** Compare $v$ with $v_f$ and $v_g$:

$$
v_f = 0.001053 \;\ll\; v = 0.20 \;\gg\; v_g = 0.13177 ?
$$

Wait: $0.20 > v_g = 0.13177$ → State is **superheated vapor**.

**Step 3.** Use the superheated table at $P = 1.5\;\text{MPa}$:

- At $T = 200\;^\circ\text{C}$: $v = 0.13824$, $u = 2598.1$, $h = 2805.5$, $s = 6.4550$
- At $T = 250\;^\circ\text{C}$: $v = 0.15200$ *(approx.)*, ...

Interpreting the given $v = 0.20$ lies well above $v_g$ — interpolate between $T = 300\;^\circ\text{C}$ ($v = 0.16966$) and $T = 350\;^\circ\text{C}$ ($v \approx 0.18228$) — actually at $T = 400\;^\circ\text{C}$, $v \approx 0.21780$ in standard tables. So:

$$
T \approx 380\;^\circ\text{C} \quad \text{(interpolated)}
$$

For the exam, KTU generally provides all four properties in the table; here we take $T \approx 380\;^\circ\text{C}$ and read $h, s$ from the superheated table. *(The key learning outcome: $v > v_g \Rightarrow$ superheated, not wet.)*

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — KTU Problem-Solving Pattern

| Given Quantity | Step 1 | Step 2 | Step 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P$ and $T$ | Find $T_{sat}(P)$ from sat. table | Compare with given $T$ | Branch: subcooled / sat / superheated |
| $P$ and $v$ | Read $v_f, v_g$ at $P$ | Compare $v$ with $v_f, v_g$ | Compute $x$ or enter superheated table |
| $T$ and $v$ | Read $v_f, v_g$ at $T$ | Compare $v$ with $v_f, v_g$ | Compute $x$ or determine $P$ from superheated table |
| $P$ and $h$ | Read $h_f, h_{fg}$ at $P$ | Compare $h$ with $h_f, h_g$ | Compute $x$ or read superheated |
| $P$ and $s$ | Read $s_f, s_{fg}$ at $P$ | Compare $s$ with $s_f, s_g$ | Compute $x$ or read superheated |
| $T$ and $h$ | Read $h_f, h_{fg}$ at $T$ | Compare $h$ with $h_f, h_g$ | Compute $x$ or read superheated |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 P–v Phase Diagram with Table Lookup Regions

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Liquid Region: v less than vf at P"] --> A1["Use Compressed-Liquid Table or Approximation v approx vf of T"]
    B["Saturated Liquid Line: v equals vf at P"] --> B1["Quality x equals 0: h equals hf, s equals sf"]
    C["Two-Phase Dome: vf less than v less than vg at P"] --> C1["Wet Steam Region: Apply Lever Rule"]
    C1 --> C2["x equals v minus vf divided by vg minus vf"]
    C1 --> C3["h equals hf plus x times hfg"]
    C1 --> C4["s equals sf plus x times sfg"]
    D["Saturated Vapor Line: v equals vg at P"] --> D1["Quality x equals 1: h equals hg, s equals sg"]
    E["Superheated Region: v greater than vg at P"] --> E1["Use Superheated Steam Table at given P and T"]
    F["Critical Point: Pcr equals 22.09 MPa, Tcr equals 374.14 C"] --> F1["vf equals vg: All quality distinctions vanish"]
```

### 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Steam Table Property Lookup Procedure

```mermaid
graph TD
    S["Start: Given Two Properties of Water/Steam"] --> S1["Step 1: Read Saturation Table at Given P or T"]
    S1 --> S2["Step 2: Determine T_sat or P_sat"]
    S2 --> Q1{"Is T greater than T_sat?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> R1["Compare v with vg"]
    R1 --> Q2{"Is v greater than vg?"}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> SUP["SUPERHEATED VAPOR: Use Superheated Table"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> SUP
    Q1 -- "No, T less than T_sat" --> Q3{"Is v less than vf?"}
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> SUB["SUBCOOLED LIQUID: Use Compressed-Liquid Approximation"]
    Q3 -- "No" --> WET["SATURATED MIXTURE: Compute Quality x from Lever Rule"]
    Q1 -- "Equal" --> WET
    WET --> W1["Compute h equals hf plus x times hfg"]
    WET --> W2["Compute s equals sf plus x times sfg"]
    WET --> W3["Compute u equals uf plus x times ufg"]
    SUB --> CL1["v approx vf of T"]
    SUB --> CL2["h approx hf of T plus vf times delta P"]
    SUB --> CL3["s approx sf of T"]
    SUP --> SH1["Read v, h, s from Superheated Table at P and T"]
    SUP --> SH2["Compute u equals h minus Pv"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Interpolation Engine

```mermaid
graph LR
    INP["Independent Variable Input: T or P"] --> TBL["Steam Table Database: Saturation + Superheated Sets"]
    TBL --> LOW["Lower Bracket: y1 with property y_lower"]
    TBL --> HIGH["Upper Bracket: y2 with property y_upper"]
    LOW --> INT["Linear Interpolation Engine"]
    HIGH --> INT
    INP --> INT
    INT --> OUT["Interpolated Property Output: v, h, s, or u"]
    INT --> FRAC["Interpolation Fraction Formula: y minus y1 divided by y2 minus y1"]
    FRAC --> OUT
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Map — Quality Lever Rule (Visual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Q0["Saturated Liquid: x equals 0"] --> LR["Lever Rule Bridge: y equals yf plus x times yfg"]
    LR --> Q1["Saturated Vapor: x equals 1"]
    LR --> PR["Property Variations: y is v, u, h, or s"]
    PR --> V1["v: Specific Volume in m3 per kg"]
    PR --> V2["u: Internal Energy in kJ per kg"]
    PR --> V3["h: Enthalpy in kJ per kg"]
    PR --> V4["s: Entropy in kJ per kg K"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define the term **"quality"** in the context of a saturated liquid–vapor mixture. For water at $P = 1.0\;\text{MPa}$, given that $v_f = 0.001127\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ and $v_g = 0.19444\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$, find the specific volume and enthalpy of a mixture with quality $x = 0.7$.

**Model Answer:**

> **Quality** is defined as the ratio of the mass of vapor to the total mass of the liquid–vapor mixture, expressed as $x = m_g / m$.

For the given mixture:

- $v = v_f + x(v_g - v_f) = 0.001127 + 0.7\,(0.19444 - 0.001127) = 0.001127 + 0.7 \times 0.193313 = 0.001127 + 0.135319 = 0.1364\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$
- $h_f = 762.81\;\text{kJ/kg}$, $h_{fg} = 2015.3\;\text{kJ/kg}$
- $h = h_f + x\,h_{fg} = 762.81 + 0.7 \times 2015.3 = 762.81 + 1410.71 = 2173.52\;\text{kJ/kg}$

> **(3 marks)** ✅

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** Distinguish between **saturated liquid** and **compressed (subcooled) liquid** states of water. Why is the compressed-liquid approximation $v \approx v_f(T)$ used in practice?

**Model Answer:**

> A **saturated liquid** exists at the saturation temperature corresponding to its pressure; any heat addition results in vaporization. A **compressed (subcooled) liquid** exists at a temperature *below* the saturation temperature for its pressure, i.e., $T < T_{sat}(P)$.
>
> The compressed-liquid approximation $v \approx v_f(T)$ is used because the specific volume of a liquid is **nearly incompressible**; it changes by less than ~1% over a wide pressure range. Therefore, for engineering accuracy, the value of $v_f$ at the *given temperature* is a very good estimate. This avoids the need for a separate compressed-liquid table.

> **(3 marks)** ✅

---

### 5.2 PART B — Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Pattern]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** A rigid tank contains **10 kg** of a saturated liquid–vapor mixture of water at $P = 200\;\text{kPa}$. The quality is $x = 0.4$. Determine:

1. The volume occupied by the liquid phase and the vapor phase. **(4 marks)**
2. The total internal energy $U$ of the mixture. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** Steam enters a turbine at $P_1 = 3.0\;\text{MPa}$ and $T_1 = 400\;^\circ\text{C}$ with a mass flow rate of $\dot{m} = 5\;\text{kg/s}$. It expands isentropically to $P_2 = 50\;\text{kPa}$. Determine the **isentropic exit quality** (or the **exit enthalpy**, depending on the exit state) and the power output of the turbine. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — (a):**

**Step 1.** From the steam table at $P = 200\;\text{kPa}$:

$$
v_f = 0.001061\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg},\quad v_g = 0.8857\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

$$
u_f = 504.50\;\text{kJ/kg},\quad u_{fg} = 2025.0\;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 2.** Mass of vapor and liquid:

$$
m_g = x \cdot m = 0.4 \times 10 = 4\;\text{kg}
$$

$$
m_f = (1 - x) \cdot m = 0.6 \times 10 = 6\;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 3.** Volumes:

$$
V_f = m_f \cdot v_f = 6 \times 0.001061 = 0.006366\;\text{m}^3
$$

$$
V_g = m_g \cdot v_g = 4 \times 0.8857 = 3.5428\;\text{m}^3
$$

**Step 4.** Internal energy of the mixture:

$$
u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg} = 504.50 + 0.4 \times 2025.0 = 504.50 + 810.0 = 1314.5\;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 5.** Total internal energy:

$$
U = m \cdot u = 10 \times 1314.5 = 13145\;\text{kJ}
$$

> **(7 marks — Part a)** ✅

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Step 1.** At turbine inlet ($P_1 = 3.0\;\text{MPa}$, $T_1 = 400\;^\circ\text{C}$), from the **superheated steam table**:

$$
h_1 = 3230.9\;\text{kJ/kg},\quad s_1 = 6.9212\;\text{kJ/kg·K}
$$

**Step 2.** For an isentropic process: $s_2 = s_1 = 6.9212\;\text{kJ/kg·K}$.

**Step 3.** At $P_2 = 50\;\text{kPa}$, from the saturation table:

$$
T_{sat} = 81.33\;^\circ\text{C}
$$

$$
s_f = 1.0910\;\text{kJ/kg·K},\quad s_{fg} = 6.5019\;\text{kJ/kg·K},\quad s_g = 7.5929\;\text{kJ/kg·K}
$$

**Step 4.** Check: $s_2 = 6.9212$. Since $s_f < s_2 < s_g$, exit state is **saturated mixture**.

**Step 5.** Quality at exit:

$$
x_2 = \frac{s_2 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.9212 - 1.0910}{6.5019} = \frac{5.8302}{6.5019} = 0.8966
$$

**Step 6.** Enthalpy at exit:

$$
h_2 = h_f + x_2 \cdot h_{fg} = 340.49 + 0.8966 \times 2305.4 = 340.49 + 2067.23 = 2407.72\;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 7.** Turbine power (assuming steady state, adiabatic):

$$
\dot{W} = \dot{m}\,(h_1 - h_2) = 5 \times (3230.9 - 2407.72) = 5 \times 823.18 = 4115.9\;\text{kW} \approx 4.12\;\text{MW}
$$

> **(7 marks — Part b)** ✅

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Water is contained in a piston-cylinder device at $P = 1.4\;\text{MPa}$ with a quality of $x = 0.85$. Determine:

1. The temperature of the mixture. **(2 marks)**
2. The specific volume, enthalpy, and entropy. **(5 marks)**

**(b)** A closed rigid tank of volume $0.05\;\text{m}^3$ contains a saturated mixture of water at $T = 150\;^\circ\text{C}$. If **one-third of the mass is in the liquid phase**, determine:

1. The pressure inside the tank. **(3 marks)**
2. The total mass, the volume of the liquid, and the volume of the vapor. **(4 marks)**

**Model Solution — (a):**

**Step 1.** At $P = 1.4\;\text{MPa}$ (saturation table by pressure):

$$
T_{sat} = 195.07\;^\circ\text{C}
$$

$$
v_f = 0.001080\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg},\quad v_g = 0.14084\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

$$
h_f = 830.1\;\text{kJ/kg},\; h_{fg} = 1959.7\;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

$$
s_f = 2.2844\;\text{kJ/kg·K},\; s_{fg} = 3.7782\;\text{kJ/kg·K}
$$

**Step 2.** Specific volume:

$$
v = v_f + x\,v_{fg} = 0.001080 + 0.85 \times (0.14084 - 0.001080) = 0.001080 + 0.85 \times 0.13976 = 0.001080 + 0.11880 = 0.11988\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

**Step 3.** Enthalpy:

$$
h = h_f + x\,h_{fg} = 830.1 + 0.85 \times 1959.7 = 830.1 + 1665.7 = 2495.8\;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4.** Entropy:

$$
s = s_f + x\,s_{fg} = 2.2844 + 0.85 \times 3.7782 = 2.2844 + 3.2115 = 5.4959\;\text{kJ/kg·K}
$$

> **(7 marks — Part a)** ✅

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Step 1.** Since the system is a saturated mixture, the saturation pressure at $T = 150\;^\circ\text{C}$ applies:

$$
P = P_{sat}(150\,°C) = 475.8\;\text{kPa} \approx 4.758\;\text{bar}
$$

**Step 2.** From the saturation table at $T = 150\;^\circ\text{C}$:

$$
v_f = 0.001091\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg},\quad v_g = 0.39248\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

**Step 3.** Given that one-third of the mass is liquid: $m_f / m = 1/3$, so $x = m_g / m = 2/3$.

**Step 4.** Overall specific volume:

$$
v = v_f + x\,(v_g - v_f) = 0.001091 + (2/3)(0.39248 - 0.001091) = 0.001091 + 0.6667 \times 0.391389
$$

$$
v = 0.001091 + 0.260926 = 0.262017\;\text{m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

**Step 5.** Total mass:

$$
m = \frac{V}{v} = \frac{0.05}{0.262017} = 0.1908\;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 6.** Mass of liquid and vapor:

$$
m_f = m/3 = 0.0636\;\text{kg},\quad m_g = 2m/3 = 0.1272\;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 7.** Volumes:

$$
V_f = m_f \cdot v_f = 0.0636 \times 0.001091 = 6.94 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{m}^3 \approx 0.0000694\;\text{m}^3
$$

$$
V_g = m_g \cdot v_g = 0.1272 \times 0.39248 = 0.04992\;\text{m}^3
$$

> **(7 marks — Part b)** ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to compare $v$ with $v_f$ and $v_g$** before computing quality — students directly write the lever-rule formula even when the state is superheated, producing meaningless $x > 1$ values. **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Using saturation temperature instead of saturation pressure** when only $P$ is given — leads to the wrong lookup column. **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Skipping interpolation steps** in superheated-table questions — bare final answers lose 1–2 marks even when numerically correct.
> 4. **Compressed-liquid enthalpy without the correction term** $v_f \cdot (P - P_{sat})$ — costs 1 mark in pump-inlet and boiler-feed problems.
> 5. **Mixing up $h_g$ and $h_{fg}$** — remember $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$ is the latent heat, not the vapor enthalpy.
> 6. **Not stating the assumed state explicitly** (subcooled / saturated / superheated) at the start of the solution — KTU's outcome-based evaluation (CO mapping) deducts marks for incomplete reasoning.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ **Steam tables have TWO parts**: Saturation Table (by T or P) and Superheated Table. The compressed-liquid region is *not* tabulated separately — use approximations.
- ⭐ **On the saturation line, only ONE of $P$ or $T$ is independent** — the other is fixed.
- ⭐ **Quality $x$** is mass-fraction vapor, $0 \le x \le 1$. It is *only* defined inside the two-phase dome.
- ⭐ **Lever rule (master formula):** $y = y_f + x(y_g - y_f) = y_f + x \cdot y_{fg}$ for $y \in \{v, u, h, s\}$.
- ⭐ **Three common KTU lookups:**
  1. Saturated liquid ($x=0$): read $y_f$ at given $P$ or $T$.
  2. Saturated vapor ($x=1$): read $y_g$ at given $P$ or $T$.
  3. Saturated mixture: read $y_f, y_g$ (or $y_{fg}$) and use the lever rule with $x$.
- ⭐ **Superheated table entry:** requires BOTH $P$ and $T$.
- ⭐ **Compressed-liquid approximation:** $v \approx v_f(T)$, $u \approx u_f(T)$, $s \approx s_f(T)$, $h \approx h_f(T) + v_f(T) \cdot [P - P_{sat}(T)]$.
- ⭐ **Critical point:** $P_{cr} = 22.09\;\text{MPa}$, $T_{cr} = 374.14\;^\circ\text{C}$. Properties of saturated liquid and vapor **converge** here.
- ⭐ **Triple point:** $P_{tp} = 0.6113\;\text{kPa}$, $T_{tp} = 0.01\;^\circ\text{C}$. Reference state for $h_f$ and $s_f$.
- ⭐ **KTU answer-format rule:** Always (i) state the assumed phase, (ii) write the formula, (iii) substitute numerical values, (iv) compute, (v) box the answer with units.
- ⭐ **Common error-check:** If computed $x > 1$ or $x < 0$, the assumed state is wrong — re-check whether the system is superheated or subcooled.
- ⭐ **Units policy in KTU:** Use **kJ/kg** for $u, h$; **kJ/kg·K** for $s$; **m³/kg** for $v$; **MPa or kPa** for $P$; **°C** for $T$. No mixed units in the same line of calculation.
- ⭐ **Course Outcome Alignment (KTU 2024):** This topic maps primarily to **CO2** — *"Apply the principles of thermodynamics to evaluate properties of pure substances and ideal-gas mixtures using steam tables and relations."* — and reinforces **CO3** (first-law analysis in non-flow and flow processes) in subsequent modules.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mole Fraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mole Fraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mole Fraction** ($y_i$ for gases, $x_i$ for liquids/solids) of a component $i$ in a mixture is defined as the ratio of the **number of moles of that component** to the **total number of moles of all components** present in the mixture. It is a **dimensionless, intensive thermodynamic property** used universally to express the composition of ideal and real gas mixtures, liquid solutions, and solid solutions.

For a mixture containing $k$ components, the mole fraction of component $i$ is:

$$x_i = \frac{n_i}{n_{\text{total}}} = \frac{n_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{k} n_j}$$

where $n_i$ is the number of moles of component $i$ and $n_{\text{total}}$ is the total number of moles in the mixture.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Composition of Gas Mixtures**
> Mole fraction is the **primary descriptor of composition** in thermodynamics. The 2024 scheme emphasizes its use with **Dalton's Law of Additive Pressures** and **Amagat's Law of Additive Volumes** for evaluating mixture properties such as **partial pressure, partial volume, mixture specific gas constant ($R_m$), and mixture molecular weight ($M_m$)**.

### 1.2 The Fundamental Constraint

A defining mathematical property of mole fractions is that they are **not independent**. For any mixture:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i = 1$$

This means that if a mixture has 3 components, specifying two mole fractions automatically determines the third. This single constraint is a recurring exam-tested concept.

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Tag:** This property is critical when solving **Gibbs' Phase Rule** problems (covered in advanced thermodynamics modules): $F = C - P + 2$, where $C$ is the number of components whose amounts can be varied independently.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Fruit Basket

Imagine a basket containing **10 fruits**: 4 apples, 3 oranges, and 3 bananas. The "mole fraction" of apples is the fraction of the **total count** that are apples:

$$x_{\text{apples}} = \frac{4}{10} = 0.4, \quad x_{\text{oranges}} = \frac{3}{10} = 0.3, \quad x_{\text{bananas}} = \frac{3}{10} = 0.3$$

Sum: $0.4 + 0.3 + 0.3 = 1.0$ ✓

In a **gas mixture**, replace "fruits" with "molecules" of different species (e.g., $O_2$, $N_2$, $CO_2$) and "count" with "moles." Mole fraction simply answers the question: *"Out of every 100 molecules in the mixture, how many are of type $i$?"*

### 1.4 Distinguishing Notational Convention

| Mixture Type | Symbol Convention | Common Usage |
|---|---|---|
| Gas mixtures | $y_i$ | Used in psychrometrics, combustion, HVAC |
| Liquid/solid solutions | $x_i$ | Used in Raoult's Law, distillation |
| General (any phase) | $z_i$ | Generic textbook usage |

> [!NOTE] In this module, the dominant application is **ideal gas mixtures**, so the symbol $y_i$ is used most frequently. However, the governing mathematics are identical across all phases.

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

- **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ molecules/mol
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R_u = 8.314$ **J/(mol·K)** $= 1.98588$ **BTU/(lbmol·°R)**
- Mole fraction is **dimensionless** and bounded: $0 \leq x_i \leq 1$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Mole Fraction is the Preferred Composition Variable

In thermodynamics, mole fraction (not mass fraction) is the natural variable for gas mixtures because:

1. **Equal volumes of ideal gases at the same $T$ and $P$ contain equal numbers of molecules** (Avogadro's Hypothesis). Hence mole fraction is directly proportional to **partial pressure** and **partial volume**.
2. It enables the use of **mole-based extensive properties** (e.g., molar enthalpy $\bar{h}$, molar entropy $\bar{s}$) to compute mixture properties via linear mixing rules.
3. The **Gibbs function**, the fundamental potential from which all other properties are derived, is most naturally expressed on a per-mole basis.

### 2.2 Operational Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Count molecules of each species.**  
Determine $n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_k$ using the relation $n = m/M$, where $m$ is the mass and $M$ is the molecular weight.

**Step 2: Sum to obtain total moles.**  
Compute $n_{\text{total}} = \sum n_i$.

**Step 3: Normalize to obtain mole fractions.**  
For each $i$, compute $x_i = n_i / n_{\text{total}}$. The resulting set must sum to unity.

**Step 4 (Inverse): Convert from mass fraction to mole fraction.**  
If mass fractions $mf_i$ and molecular weights $M_i$ are known:

$$x_i = \frac{mf_i / M_i}{\sum_{j} (mf_j / M_j)}$$

**Step 5: Apply to mixture property evaluation.**  
Use $x_i$ as the weight in mixing rules:

$$M_m = \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i M_i \quad ; \quad R_m = \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i R_i \quad ; \quad c_{p,m} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} y_i c_{p,i}$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT] **CRITICAL TABLE — Memorize for KTU 2024 Examinations**

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x_i = \dfrac{n_i}{\sum n_j}$ | **Primary Definition** of mole fraction |
| 2 | $\sum x_i = 1$ | **Closure relation** — always enforced |
| 3 | $P_i = x_i P$ | **Dalton's Law** — partial pressure |
| 4 | $V_i = x_i V$ | **Amagat's Law** — partial volume |
| 5 | $M_m = \sum x_i M_i$ | **Mixture molecular weight** |
| 6 | $R_m = \dfrac{R_u}{M_m} = \sum x_i R_i$ | **Mixture gas constant** |
| 7 | $x_i = \dfrac{mf_i / M_i}{\sum (mf_j / M_j)}$ | **Mass-fraction to mole-fraction conversion** |
| 8 | $mf_i = x_i M_i / M_m$ | **Mole-fraction to mass-fraction conversion** |
| 9 | $f_m = \sum x_i f_i$ | **Mixture property (linear mixing rule)** |
| 10 | $y_i = \dfrac{n_i}{n_{\text{total}}}$ | **Mole fraction (gas mixture notation)** |

> [!WARNING] **Pitfall:** Never use `|` (vertical bar) for absolute value inside markdown tables — use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking table rendering. The formula $\vert x_i \vert$ should be written as $\lvert x_i \rvert$ in LaTeX contexts.

### 2.4 Connection to Dalton's and Amagat's Laws (Engineering Utility)

**Dalton's Law of Additive Pressures** states that the total pressure of an ideal gas mixture equals the sum of partial pressures, where each partial pressure is the pressure that component $i$ would exert if it alone occupied the volume at the mixture temperature:

$$P = \sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i, \quad \text{where} \quad P_i = y_i P$$

**Amagat's Law of Additive Volumes** states the analogous principle for volumes:

$$V = \sum_{i=1}^{k} V_i, \quad \text{where} \quad V_i = y_i V$$

> [!NOTE] These laws are **exact** for **ideal gas mixtures** and are the foundation of all psychrometric calculations (air-water vapor mixtures), flue gas analysis (combustion products), and refrigeration mixture tables. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests their application in numerical problems.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Internal Combustion Engines:** Calculating the mole fraction of $CO_2$, $CO$, $H_2O$, $N_2$ in exhaust gases for emissions analysis.
- **Air-Conditioning Systems:** Psychrometrics uses mole fraction of water vapor in dry air (humidity ratio is derived from it).
- **Cryogenics:** Liquefaction and separation of air into $O_2$, $N_2$, and rare gases requires precise mole-fraction tracking.
- **Chemical Reactors:** Stoichiometric calculations of reactants and products use mole fractions throughout.
- **Gas Turbine Performance:** Combustion air-fuel ratios are specified in molar terms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean Molecular Weight of a Mixture

**Starting Point:** We have a mixture with $k$ components. Component $i$ has $n_i$ moles and molecular weight $M_i$. Total mass $m_{\text{total}} = \sum m_i = \sum n_i M_i$. Total moles $n_{\text{total}} = \sum n_i$.

**Step 1: Define the mean (mixture) molecular weight** as the total mass divided by the total moles:

$$M_m = \frac{m_{\text{total}}}{n_{\text{total}}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the expressions for total mass and total moles:**

$$M_m = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i}$$

**Step 3: Divide numerator and denominator by $n_{\text{total}}$:**

$$M_m = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} (n_i / n_{\text{total}}) \, M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} (n_i / n_{\text{total}})} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i M_i}{1}$$

**Step 4: Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{M_m = \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i M_i}$$

**Interpretation:** $M_m$ is the mole-fraction-weighted average of the individual molecular weights. Each component contributes in proportion to its share of molecules, not its share of mass.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mixture Gas Constant

**Starting Point:** For any pure gas, $R = R_u / M$. For a mixture, define the analogous mixture gas constant.

**Step 1: Apply the universal gas constant to a mixture of total mass $m_{\text{total}}$ and $n_{\text{total}}$ moles:**

$$P V = n_{\text{total}} R_u T = m_{\text{total}} R_m T$$

**Step 2: Equate the two expressions for $PV$:**

$$m_{\text{total}} R_m = n_{\text{total}} R_u$$

**Step 3: Solve for $R_m$ and substitute $m_{\text{total}} = m_{\text{total}}$:**

$$R_m = \frac{R_u}{M_m}$$

**Step 4: Substitute $M_m = \sum x_i M_i$:**

$$\boxed{R_m = \frac{R_u}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i M_i} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i R_i}$$

where the second equality follows because $x_i R_i = x_i (R_u / M_i)$, and summing yields $R_u \sum x_i/M_i = R_u / M_m$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Conversion Between Mass Fraction and Mole Fraction

**Given:** Mass fraction of component $i$: $mf_i = m_i / m_{\text{total}}$.

**Step 1: Express $m_i$ in terms of moles and molecular weight:**

$$m_i = n_i M_i$$

**Step 2: Substitute into mass fraction:**

$$mf_i = \frac{n_i M_i}{m_{\text{total}}} = \frac{n_i M_i}{n_{\text{total}} M_m} = \frac{x_i M_i}{M_m}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $x_i$:**

$$x_i = \frac{mf_i \cdot M_m}{M_i}$$

**Step 4: Eliminate $M_m$ by substituting its expansion $M_m = \sum_j x_j M_j$** — but this creates a circular relation. Instead, derive $x_i$ purely from mass fractions by using the molar form of mass:

**Step 5:** $mf_i = m_i / m_{\text{total}} = (n_i M_i) / (n_{\text{total}} M_m)$, so $n_i = mf_i \cdot m_{\text{total}} / M_i$.

**Step 6: Sum over all components to get $n_{\text{total}}$:**

$$n_{\text{total}} = \frac{m_{\text{total}}}{M_m} = m_{\text{total}} \sum_{j} \frac{mf_j}{M_j}$$

**Step 7: Form the ratio $x_i = n_i / n_{\text{total}}$:**

$$\boxed{x_i = \frac{mf_i / M_i}{\sum_{j} (mf_j / M_j)}}$$

**Key Insight:** The mass-to-mole conversion requires **division by molecular weight inside the ratio** because lighter molecules contribute more moles per unit mass.

---

### 3.4 Worked Analytical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A gas mixture consists of **2 kmol of $O_2$**, **5 kmol of $N_2$**, and **3 kmol of $CO_2$**.  
Given molecular weights: $M_{O_2} = 32$, $M_{N_2} = 28$, $M_{CO_2} = 44$ kg/kmol.  
Find: (a) Mole fractions, (b) Mass fractions, (c) Mean molecular weight $M_m$, (d) Mixture gas constant $R_m$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Mole fractions**

Total moles: $n_{\text{total}} = 2 + 5 + 3 = 10$ kmol.

$$y_{O_2} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20$$

$$y_{N_2} = \frac{5}{10} = 0.50$$

$$y_{CO_2} = \frac{3}{10} = 0.30$$

Check: $0.20 + 0.50 + 0.30 = 1.00$ ✓

**Part (b): Mass fractions**

Masses: $m_{O_2} = 2 \times 32 = 64$ kg, $m_{N_2} = 5 \times 28 = 140$ kg, $m_{CO_2} = 3 \times 44 = 132$ kg.

Total mass: $m_{\text{total}} = 64 + 140 + 132 = 336$ kg.

$$mf_{O_2} = \frac{64}{336} = 0.1905$$

$$mf_{N_2} = \frac{140}{336} = 0.4167$$

$$mf_{CO_2} = \frac{132}{336} = 0.3929$$

Check: $0.1905 + 0.4167 + 0.3929 \approx 1.0001$ ✓ (rounding error)

**Part (c): Mean molecular weight**

$$M_m = \sum y_i M_i = (0.20)(32) + (0.50)(28) + (0.30)(44)$$

$$M_m = 6.4 + 14.0 + 13.2 = 33.6 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

Cross-check: $M_m = m_{\text{total}} / n_{\text{total}} = 336 / 10 = 33.6$ kg/kmol ✓

**Part (d): Mixture gas constant**

$$R_m = \frac{R_u}{M_m} = \frac{8.314 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)}}{33.6 \text{ kg/kmol}}$$

$$R_m = 0.2474 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

> [!NOTE] **Note:** Air has $M \approx 28.97$ kg/kmol and $R \approx 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K). The mixture above is denser (higher $M_m$, lower $R_m$) because of the heavy $CO_2$ component.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram — From Moles to Mixture Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Mass of each component m_i and Molecular Weight M_i] --> B[Step 1: Calculate moles n_i = m_i / M_i]
    B --> C[Step 2: Sum total moles n_total = sum n_i]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute mole fraction x_i = n_i / n_total]
    D --> E[Step 4a: Mean Molecular Weight M_m = sum x_i times M_i]
    D --> F[Step 4b: Mixture Gas Constant R_m = R_u / M_m]
    D --> G[Step 4c: Partial Pressure P_i = x_i times P]
    D --> H[Step 4d: Partial Volume V_i = x_i times V]
    E --> I[Output: Mixture Property Set]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Apply to Thermodynamic State Equations, Mixing Rules, Combustion Analysis]
```

### 4.2 Relationship Block Diagram — Composition Variables

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[m_i: Mass of component i] --> MW[M_i: Molecular weight of i]
    MW --> N[n_i: Number of moles of i]
    N --> TOT[n_total: Total moles]
    TOT --> YF[x_i: Mole fraction of i]
    YF --> MP[Mixture Property: M_m, R_m, c_pm]
    M --> MF[mf_i: Mass fraction of i]
    MF --> MP
    YF --> DAL[P_i = x_i times P: Dalton Law]
    YF --> AMA[V_i = x_i times V: Amagat Law]
    MF -.->|Convert via: x_i = mf_i/M_i over sum mf_j/M_j| YF
    YF -.->|Convert via: mf_i = x_i times M_i over M_m| MF
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture — KTU Problem-Solving Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[KTU Problem Statement] --> P2{Is composition given in mass or moles?}
    P2 -->|Moles given| P3[Directly compute x_i = n_i / n_total]
    P2 -->|Mass given| P4[Convert: m_i to n_i using M_i, then compute x_i]
    P2 -->|Mole percent given| P5[Divide by 100 to obtain x_i]
    P3 --> P6[Compute M_m and R_m]
    P4 --> P6
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Apply to Ideal Gas Law: PV = m R_m T]
    P6 --> P8[Apply Dalton or Amagat as required]
    P7 --> P9[Final Answer: Mixture State]
    P8 --> P9
```

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Note:** All node IDs in the Mermaid blocks above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `P1`) and contain no reserved keywords. Node labels use plain uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting, per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rendering safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
Define mole fraction. State and prove the closure relation $\sum x_i = 1$.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Mole fraction $x_i$ of component $i$ in a mixture is the ratio of the number of moles of that component to the total number of moles of all components in the mixture:

$$x_i = \frac{n_i}{\sum_{j} n_j}$$

**Proof of Closure Relation:**

Starting from the definition, sum $x_i$ over all $k$ components:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{\sum_{j} n_j} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i}{\sum_{j} n_j} = \frac{n_{\text{total}}}{n_{\text{total}}} = 1$$

Hence $\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i = 1$. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
Distinguish between **mass fraction** and **mole fraction**. Give the formula to convert from mass fraction to mole fraction.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Mass Fraction $mf_i$ | Mole Fraction $x_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | $mf_i = m_i / m_{\text{total}}$ | $x_i = n_i / n_{\text{total}}$ |
| Basis | Mass | Number of moles (count) |
| Sum | $\sum mf_i = 1$ | $\sum x_i = 1$ |
| Use | Industrial process accounting | Thermodynamic analysis |

**Conversion formula:**

$$x_i = \frac{mf_i / M_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{k} (mf_j / M_j)}$$

(3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**  
**(a)** A mixture contains **4 kg of $O_2$, 7 kg of $N_2$, and 5 kg of $CO_2$**. Determine the mole fraction of each component. Given: $M_{O_2} = 32$, $M_{N_2} = 28$, $M_{CO_2} = 44$ kg/kmol. (7 marks)

**(b)** The mixture in part (a) occupies a volume of **$4 \text{ m}^3$** at a temperature of **$35^\circ$C**. Calculate the total pressure of the mixture using the ideal gas law. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for Q3:**

**Part (a):**

Step 1: Compute moles of each component.

$$n_{O_2} = \frac{4}{32} = 0.125 \text{ kmol}$$

$$n_{N_2} = \frac{7}{28} = 0.250 \text{ kmol}$$

$$n_{CO_2} = \frac{5}{44} = 0.1136 \text{ kmol}$$

Step 2: Total moles.

$$n_{\text{total}} = 0.125 + 0.250 + 0.1136 = 0.4886 \text{ kmol}$$

Step 3: Mole fractions.

$$x_{O_2} = \frac{0.125}{0.4886} = 0.2558$$

$$x_{N_2} = \frac{0.250}{0.4886} = 0.5117$$

$$x_{CO_2} = \frac{0.1136}{0.4886} = 0.2325$$

**Verification:** $0.2558 + 0.5117 + 0.2325 = 1.0000$ ✓ (7 marks)

---

**Part (b):**

Step 1: Mean molecular weight.

$$M_m = \sum x_i M_i = (0.2558)(32) + (0.5117)(28) + (0.2325)(44)$$

$$M_m = 8.186 + 14.328 + 10.230 = 32.744 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

Step 2: Mixture gas constant.

$$R_m = \frac{R_u}{M_m} = \frac{8.314}{32.744} = 0.2539 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

Step 3: Total mass.

$$m_{\text{total}} = 4 + 7 + 5 = 16 \text{ kg}$$

Step 4: Apply ideal gas law $PV = m R_m T$, with $T = 35 + 273.15 = 308.15$ K.

$$P = \frac{m R_m T}{V} = \frac{16 \times 0.2539 \times 308.15}{4}$$

$$P = \frac{1251.7}{4} = 312.9 \text{ kPa}$$

**Final Answer:** $P \approx 312.9$ kPa (7 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**  
**(a)** Derive the expression for the **mean molecular weight** of a gas mixture in terms of mole fractions. Explain its physical significance. (7 marks)

**(b)** A gas mixture has the following composition by mass: **30% $N_2$, 40% $O_2$, and 30% $CO_2$**. Compute the mole fraction of each gas and the mean molecular weight. Use $M_{N_2} = 28$, $M_{O_2} = 32$, $M_{CO_2} = 44$ kg/kmol. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for Q4:**

**Part (a): Derivation**

Let the mixture have $k$ components with $n_i$ moles and molecular weight $M_i$ each. The mass of component $i$ is $m_i = n_i M_i$. Total mass and total moles are:

$$m_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i} n_i M_i, \quad n_{\text{total}} = \sum_i n_i$$

The mean molecular weight is defined as the ratio of total mass to total moles:

$$M_m = \frac{m_{\text{total}}}{n_{\text{total}}} = \frac{\sum_i n_i M_i}{\sum_i n_i}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $n_{\text{total}}$ and using $x_i = n_i / n_{\text{total}}$:

$$M_m = \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i M_i$$

**Physical Significance:** $M_m$ represents the **effective molecular weight of the mixture** when treated as a single pseudo-pure gas. It allows direct application of the ideal gas law $PV = m R_m T$ using $R_m = R_u / M_m$, treating the mixture as a single substance for macroscopic calculations. (7 marks)

---

**Part (b): Numerical computation**

Take a basis of **1 kg** of mixture (since composition is given in mass percent, the basis is arbitrary).

Step 1: Moles of each component per kg.

$$n_{N_2} = \frac{0.30}{28} = 0.01071 \text{ kmol/kg}$$

$$n_{O_2} = \frac{0.40}{32} = 0.01250 \text{ kmol/kg}$$

$$n_{CO_2} = \frac{0.30}{44} = 0.00682 \text{ kmol/kg}$$

Step 2: Total moles per kg.

$$n_{\text{total}} = 0.01071 + 0.01250 + 0.00682 = 0.03003 \text{ kmol/kg}$$

Step 3: Mole fractions.

$$x_{N_2} = \frac{0.01071}{0.03003} = 0.3566$$

$$x_{O_2} = \frac{0.01250}{0.03003} = 0.4163$$

$$x_{CO_2} = \frac{0.00682}{0.03003} = 0.2271$$

**Verification:** $0.3566 + 0.4163 + 0.2271 = 1.0000$ ✓

Step 4: Mean molecular weight.

$$M_m = (0.3566)(28) + (0.4163)(32) + (0.2271)(44)$$

$$M_m = 9.985 + 13.322 + 9.992 = 33.30 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

**Final Answer:** $x_{N_2} = 0.357$, $x_{O_2} = 0.416$, $x_{CO_2} = 0.227$, $M_m = 33.30$ kg/kmol (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mass to moles first** when mass percentages are given. Students often treat mass percent as mole percent — this is WRONG. Heavier molecules (like $CO_2$) appear in **higher** mass fraction but **lower** mole fraction.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency with $R_u$:** Use $R_u = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K) when masses are in kg and moles in kmol. Mismatched units cause silent errors.
> 3. **Not verifying $\sum x_i = 1$** in the final answer. Examiners award 0.5–1 mark explicitly for this closure check.
> 4. **Confusing mass fraction with weight fraction in different unit systems.** Mass fraction is dimensionless in SI; in imperial (lbm/lbm), it is numerically identical.
> 5. **Using $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K) (air) for arbitrary mixtures.** This is the gas constant for dry air only. For a general mixture, **always compute $R_m = R_u / M_m$**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Mole fraction** $x_i = n_i / n_{\text{total}}$ is the **dimensionless** primary composition variable in thermodynamics.
- **Closure relation:** $\sum x_i = 1$ — always verify this in your final answer.
- **Mean molecular weight:** $M_m = \sum x_i M_i$ — mole-fraction-weighted average of component molecular weights.
- **Mixture gas constant:** $R_m = R_u / M_m$ — never use a single component's $R$ for a mixture.
- **Dalton's Law:** $P_i = x_i P$ (partial pressure is proportional to mole fraction).
- **Amagat's Law:** $V_i = x_i V$ (partial volume is proportional to mole fraction).
- **Mass-to-mole conversion:** $x_i = (mf_i / M_i) / \sum (mf_j / M_j)$ — divide by molecular weight inside the sum.
- **Mole-to-mass conversion:** $mf_i = x_i M_i / M_m$ — multiply by molecular weight, divide by $M_m$.
- **Universal gas constant** $R_u = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K) appears in **every** ideal gas law expression for mixtures.
- **Symbol convention:** Use $y_i$ for gas mixtures, $x_i$ for liquid/solid solutions — content is identical.
- **Engineering applications:** Combustion analysis, psychrometrics, refrigeration mixtures, gas separation, emissions control.
- **Exam mantra:** *Compute moles first, normalize to get mole fractions, then use them as weights in mixing rules.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mass Fraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mass Fraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the study of **non-reacting gas mixtures** and **multi-component systems** in engineering thermodynamics, the **Mass Fraction** (also called **mass fraction** or **gravimetric fraction**) of an individual component is formally defined as the ratio of the mass of that specific component to the total mass of the entire mixture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> The mass fraction of component *i* in a mixture, denoted as **$m_i$** or **$w_i$**, is defined as:
> $$m_i = \frac{m_i}{m_{total}}$$
> where $m_i$ is the mass of component *i* and $m_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i$ is the total mass of all components present in the mixture.

The unit of mass fraction is **dimensionless** (a pure ratio), and for any valid mixture, the sum of all mass fractions must always equal **unity (1)**:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i = m_1 + m_2 + m_3 + \dots + m_n = 1$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are preparing a **fruit salad** for a party. You mix:
- 300 g of apple
- 200 g of banana
- 500 g of grapes

The **total mass** of the salad is $300 + 200 + 500 = 1000$ g.

Now, if someone asks: *"What fraction of the salad is banana?"*, you would naturally say **200 out of 1000**, or **0.2 (or 20%)**.

This is exactly the **mass fraction** — it tells you the *share* of the total mass contributed by each component, expressed as a pure number between 0 and 1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Mass fraction is the "mass percentage" of each ingredient, normalized so that all fractions add up to 100% (or 1.0). If you add the share of apple (0.3) + banana (0.2) + grapes (0.5), you get back the **whole salad (1.0)**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Property | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|----------|--------|----------------------|
| Universal Gas Constant | $R_u$ | **8.314 kJ/kmol·K** |
| Sum of mass fractions | $\sum m_i$ | **1 (dimensionless)** |
| Range of individual mass fraction | $0 \le m_i \le 1$ | **Dimensionless** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Check:** Whenever you solve a problem involving a mixture, the first verification step is to check whether the sum of all mass fractions equals 1. If it does not, you have either misread the data or miscalculated — this is a **3-mark lifesaver** in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breakdown of the Concept

The mass fraction concept is built on three foundational principles that every KTU student must internalize:

- **Principle 1 — Constitutive Definition:**
  Mass fraction is fundamentally a *conservative* quantity. It does not depend on temperature, pressure, or volume. It is a pure property of the **composition** of the system at any given state.

- **Principle 2 — Normalization Constraint:**
  Since every gram of the mixture must belong to *some* component, the sum of all mass fractions is mathematically constrained to be **exactly 1**. This acts as a built-in **consistency check** for any mixture problem.

- **Principle 3 — Independence from Molecular Size:**
  Unlike mole fraction, mass fraction *does not* treat all molecules as equal. A heavy molecule (like $CO_2$, M = 44 kg/kmol) will have a *larger* mass fraction than a light molecule (like $H_2$, M = 2 kg/kmol) even if the same number of moles are present.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass fraction definition | $m_i = \dfrac{m_i}{m_{total}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le m_i \le 1$ |
| 2 | Normalization (sum rule) | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i = 1$ | Always 1 for a closed system |
| 3 | Relation to mole fraction | $m_i = \dfrac{y_i \cdot M_i}{M_{mix}}$ | Where $M_{mix}$ = mixture molecular weight |
| 4 | Mole fraction from mass fraction | $y_i = m_i \cdot \dfrac{M_{mix}}{M_i}$ | Useful in combustion analysis |
| 5 | Average molecular weight of mixture | $M_{mix} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{m_i}{M_i}} = \left( \sum \dfrac{y_i}{M_i} \right)^{-1}$ | kg/kmol or g/mol |
| 6 | Alternative mixture M.W. formula | $M_{mix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i M_i$ | In terms of mole fractions |
| 7 | Specific gas constant of mixture | $R_{mix} = \dfrac{R_u}{M_{mix}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i R_i$ | kJ/kg·K |
| 8 | Specific heat of mixture (constant pressure) | $C_{p,mix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i C_{p,i}$ | kJ/kg·K |
| 9 | Specific heat of mixture (constant volume) | $C_{v,mix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i C_{v,i}$ | kJ/kg·K |
| 10 | Partial pressure from mass fraction | $P_i = m_i \cdot \dfrac{M_{mix}}{M_i} \cdot P_{total}$ | kPa or bar |
| 11 | Mass of component from mass fraction | $m_i = m_i \cdot m_{total}$ | kg |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Trap in Tables:** Note that in the formula table above, vertical bars and absolute-value-style dividers are written using mathematical notation ($\mid$ or $\div$) — never use the raw `|` character inside a markdown table cell, as it will break the KTU PDF rendering.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Mass fraction is the workhorse of every practical engineering calculation involving **non-uniform mixtures**:

- **Internal Combustion Engines:** Used in air-fuel ratio calculations. A stoichiometric petrol engine operates at an air-fuel mass ratio of approximately **14.7:1**, meaning the mass fraction of fuel is about **0.0638**.
- **Air-Conditioning & Refrigeration (Psychrometrics):** Humidity ratio (specific humidity) is essentially a mass fraction of water vapor in dry air.
- **Combustion Analysis:** Flue gas composition (CO₂, H₂O, N₂, O₂) is routinely reported in mass fractions to determine combustion efficiency.
- **Chemical Process Industries:** Concentration of solutions (mass of solute / mass of solution) is a direct application.
- **Cryogenics:** Liquefied gas mixtures (like LNG — Liquefied Natural Gas) use mass fractions to specify methane, ethane, propane composition for energy content estimation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From First Principles — The Mass Fraction Definition

**Starting Point:** We have a mixture composed of *n* components, each with its own mass $m_1, m_2, m_3, \dots, m_n$.

**Step 1:** Define the total mass of the mixture as the scalar sum of all component masses:

$$m_{total} = m_1 + m_2 + m_3 + \dots + m_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i$$

**Step 2:** The mass fraction of the *i*-th component is defined as its share of the total mass:

$$m_i = \frac{m_i}{m_{total}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the expression for $m_{total}$:

$$\boxed{m_i = \frac{m_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i}}$$

**Step 4:** Summing all mass fractions over all components:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{m_i}{m_{total}} = \frac{1}{m_{total}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i = \frac{m_{total}}{m_{total}} = 1$$

This proves the **normalization property** rigorously from the definition itself.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relation Between Mass Fraction and Mole Fraction

This is a **high-weightage derivation** in KTU Module 3 (gas mixtures).

**Given:**
- Mole fraction of component *i* is $y_i = \dfrac{n_i}{n_{total}}$
- Mass fraction of component *i* is $m_i = \dfrac{m_i}{m_{total}}$

**Step 1:** Express the mass of component *i* in terms of moles and molecular weight:

$$m_i = n_i \cdot M_i$$

**Step 2:** Similarly, total mass:

$$m_{total} = n_{total} \cdot M_{mix}$$

where $M_{mix}$ is the **average molecular weight of the mixture**.

**Step 3:** Divide the equation for $m_i$ by the equation for $m_{total}$:

$$\frac{m_i}{m_{total}} = \frac{n_i \cdot M_i}{n_{total} \cdot M_{mix}}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the left side as the mass fraction and the fraction of $n$'s as the mole fraction:

$$m_i = y_i \cdot \frac{M_i}{M_{mix}}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for mole fraction by cross-multiplying:

$$\boxed{y_i = m_i \cdot \frac{M_{mix}}{M_i}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Average Molecular Weight of a Mixture ($M_{mix}$)

**Step 1:** By definition, the total mass of the mixture is:

$$m_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i M_i$$

**Step 2:** The total number of moles is:

$$n_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i$$

**Step 3:** The average (apparent) molecular weight is:

$$M_{mix} = \frac{m_{total}}{n_{total}} = \frac{\sum n_i M_i}{\sum n_i}$$

**Step 4:** Divide numerator and denominator by $n_{total}$:

$$M_{mix} = \frac{\sum \left(\frac{n_i}{n_{total}}\right) M_i}{\sum \frac{n_i}{n_{total}}} = \frac{\sum y_i M_i}{\sum y_i}$$

**Step 5:** Since $\sum y_i = 1$:

$$\boxed{M_{mix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i M_i}$$

**Alternative form using mass fractions:**

$$M_{mix} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{m_i}{M_i}}$$

**Derivation of alternative form:**

Starting from $y_i = m_i \cdot \dfrac{M_{mix}}{M_i}$:

Substitute into $M_{mix} = \sum y_i M_i$:

$$M_{mix} = \sum \left( m_i \cdot \frac{M_{mix}}{M_i} \right) M_i = M_{mix} \sum \frac{m_i}{M_i}$$

Dividing both sides by $M_{mix}$:

$$1 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{m_i}{M_i}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{M_{mix} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{m_i}{M_i}}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Specific Gas Constant of a Mixture

**Step 1:** By the ideal gas law, applied to component *i*:

$$P_i V = m_i R_i T$$

**Step 2:** Sum over all components (using Dalton's Law, $P = \sum P_i$):

$$V \sum P_i = T \sum m_i R_i$$

$$P \cdot V = T \cdot \sum m_i R_i$$

**Step 3:** Apply ideal gas law to the *mixture as a whole*:

$$P \cdot V = m_{total} \cdot R_{mix} \cdot T$$

**Step 4:** Equate the two expressions:

$$m_{total} \cdot R_{mix} \cdot T = T \cdot \sum m_i R_i$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $R_{mix}$:

$$R_{mix} = \frac{\sum m_i R_i}{m_{total}} = \sum \frac{m_i}{m_{total}} R_i$$

$$\boxed{R_{mix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i R_i}$$

This shows that the mixture's specific gas constant is the **mass-fraction-weighted average** of individual gas constants.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A gas mixture contains 3 kg of $O_2$, 5 kg of $N_2$, and 2 kg of $CO_2$. Calculate:
(i) The mass fraction of each component
(ii) The average molecular weight of the mixture
(iii) The specific gas constant of the mixture

**Given Molecular Weights:**
- $M_{O_2} = 32$ kg/kmol
- $M_{N_2} = 28$ kg/kmol
- $M_{CO_2} = 44$ kg/kmol
- $R_u = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K

**Solution:**

**(i) Total mass and mass fractions:**

$$m_{total} = 3 + 5 + 2 = 10 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{O_2} = \frac{3}{10} = 0.30$$

$$m_{N_2} = \frac{5}{10} = 0.50$$

$$m_{CO_2} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20$$

**Verification:** $0.30 + 0.50 + 0.20 = 1.00$ ✓

**(ii) Average molecular weight:**

$$M_{mix} = \frac{1}{\sum \frac{m_i}{M_i}} = \frac{1}{\frac{0.30}{32} + \frac{0.50}{28} + \frac{0.20}{44}}$$

$$M_{mix} = \frac{1}{0.009375 + 0.017857 + 0.004545}$$

$$M_{mix} = \frac{1}{0.031777} = 31.47 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

**(iii) Specific gas constant of mixture:**

$$R_{mix} = \frac{R_u}{M_{mix}} = \frac{8.314}{31.47} = 0.2642 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$
