Change in kJ per K]:::startend nodeStart --> nodeKnow nodeKnow --> nodeDecide nodeDecide -->|T constant| nodeIso nodeDecide -->|V constant| nodeIsoch nodeDecide -->|P constant| nodeIsob nodeDecide -->|ds zero| nodeIsen nodeDecide -->|General| nodeGen nodeIso --> nodeTotal nodeIsoch --> nodeTotal nodeIsob --> nodeTotal nodeIsen --> nodeTotal nodeGen --> nodeTotal nodeTotal --> nodeEnd classDef startend fill:#1d3557,color:#ffffff,stroke:#a8dadc,stroke-width:3px classDef decision fill:#f1c40f,color:#000000,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px classDef proc fill:#457b9d,color:#ffffff,stroke:#a8dadc,stroke-width:1px classDef pre fill:#2a9d8f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#e9c46a,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — T-s Diagram Showing Process Curves ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeT[Temperature
on Vertical Axis]:::axis nodeS[Entropy
on Horizontal Axis]:::axis nodeA[Point A
High T Low s]:::state nodeB[Point B
High T High s]:::state nodeC[Point C
Low T High s]:::state nodeD[Point D
Low T Low s]:::state nodeIso[ISOTHERMAL
Horizontal Line
T is constant]:::proc nodeIsen[ISENTROPIC
Vertical Line
s is constant]:::proc nodeIsob[ISOBARIC
Curve in T-s plane]:::proc nodeIsoch[ISOCHORIC
Steeper Curve]:::proc nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeD nodeB --> nodeC nodeD --> nodeC nodeA -.- nodeIso nodeA -.- nodeIsen nodeB -.- nodeIsob nodeD -.- nodeIsoch classDef axis fill:#6c757d,color:#ffffff,stroke:#adb5bd,stroke-width:2px classDef state fill:#e63946,color:#ffffff,stroke:#f1faee,stroke-width:2px classDef proc fill:#118ab2,color:#ffffff,stroke:#073b4c,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** In the T-s plane, an **isothermal** process is a horizontal line, an **isentropic** process is a vertical line, and an **isobaric** process is a curve (exponential-like) lying above the isochoric curve. The area under a reversible curve on a T-s diagram equals the **heat transferred**: $Q_{rev} = \int T \, ds$. # Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q:** State and prove Mayer's relation between $c_p$ and $c_v$ for an ideal gas. **Model Answer:** Mayer's relation states that for an ideal gas, the difference between specific heat at constant pressure and specific heat at constant volume is equal to the characteristic gas constant. $$c_p - c_v = R$$ **Proof:** Consider 1 kg of an ideal gas undergoing a reversible isobaric process. Applying the First Law: $$\delta q = du + p \, dv$$ At constant pressure, $\delta q = dh$. Therefore $dh = du + p \, dv$. Since $h = u + pv$, differentiating: $dh = du + p \, dv + v \, dp$. At constant $p$: $dh = du + p \, dv$. For an ideal gas: $pv = RT \Rightarrow p \, dv = R \, dT$ (since $dp = 0$). Hence: $c_p \, dT = c_v \, dT + R \, dT$, giving $c_p - c_v = R$. $\blacksquare$ --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Q:** Define entropy and write the mathematical expression for change in entropy of an ideal gas undergoing any reversible process. **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Entropy is a thermodynamic property which measures the degree of disorder of a system or the unavailability of energy for conversion into work. It is a point function and an extensive property. **Mathematical Expression (General Reversible Process for Ideal Gas):** $$\Delta s = c_v \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + R \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right)$$ Alternatively: $$\Delta s = c_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)$$ These expressions are derived from the Gibbs (Tds) equations. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Derive the first Tds equation. Using it, obtain the expression for change in entropy of an ideal gas. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Air at 1 bar and 300 K is compressed isentropically to 5 bar. Find the final temperature and the work done per kg. Take $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Solution to Question A:** #### Part (a) — Derivation of First Tds Equation and Ideal Gas Entropy Change: **Step 1:** Consider a closed system undergoing a reversible process. **Step 2:** Apply First Law: $dU = \delta Q - \delta W$. For reversible boundary work $\delta W = p \, dV$: $$dU = \delta Q_{rev} - p \, dV$$ **Step 3:** From Second Law, for a reversible process: $\delta Q_{rev} = T \, dS$. Therefore: $$dU = T \, dS - p \, dV$$ **Step 4:** Divide by mass $m$: $$du = T \, ds - p \, dv$$ Rearranging: $$\boxed{T \, ds = du + p \, dv} \quad \text{(First Tds Equation)}$$ **Step 5:** For an ideal gas, $du = c_v \, dT$. From $pv = RT$: $p \, dv = R \, dT - v \, dp$... but at general reversible state, using $p = RT/v$: $$p \, dv = \frac{RT}{v} dv = RT \, d(\ln v)$$ **Step 6:** Substitute into Tds equation: $$T \, ds = c_v \, dT + RT \, d(\ln v)$$ Divide by $T$: $$ds = c_v \, \frac{dT}{T} + R \, \frac{dv}{v}$$ **Step 7:** Integrate from state 1 to state 2: $$\Delta s = c_v \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + R \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right)$$ #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution: **Given:** $p_1 = 1$ bar, $T_1 = 300$ K, $p_2 = 5$ bar, $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. **Step 1:** Find final temperature using isentropic relation: $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ $$T_2 = 300 \times (5)^{(0.4/1.4)} = 300 \times (5)^{0.2857}$$ Calculate $(5)^{0.2857}$: $$5^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \times \ln 5} = e^{0.2857 \times 1.6094} = e^{0.4599} = 1.5838$$ Therefore: $$T_2 = 300 \times 1.5838 = 475.13 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2:** Calculate $c_v$: $$c_v = \frac{c_p}{\gamma} = \frac{1.005}{1.4} = 0.7179 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ **Step 3:** Work done in isentropic process per kg: For an isentropic (reversible adiabatic) process: $W = \frac{R(T_1 - T_2)}{\gamma - 1}$ First find $R$: $$R = c_p - c_v = 1.005 - 0.7179 = 0.2871 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ (Or directly $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K for air.) $$W = \frac{R(T_1 - T_2)}{\gamma - 1} = \frac{0.287 \times (300 - 475.13)}{0.4}$$ $$W = \frac{0.287 \times (-175.13)}{0.4} = \frac{-50.262}{0.4} = -125.66 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ The negative sign indicates work is done **on** the system (compression). $$\boxed{T_2 = 475.13 \text{ K} \quad ; \quad W = -125.66 \text{ kJ/kg (work input)}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2022 — Alternative Choice]` **(a)** Define specific heat at constant pressure and constant volume. Derive the relation $c_p - c_v = R$ for an ideal gas. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A piston-cylinder device contains 0.5 kg of air at 100 kPa and 27 °C. The air is now heated at constant pressure until the volume doubles. Determine: (i) the final temperature, (ii) the work done, (iii) the heat transferred, and (iv) the change in entropy. Take $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Solution to Question B:** #### Part (a) — Definitions and Derivation: **Definition of $c_v$:** Specific heat at constant volume is the amount of heat required to raise the temperature of unit mass of a gas by one degree Kelvin when the gas is heated at constant volume. **Definition of $c_p$:** Specific heat at constant pressure is the amount of heat required to raise the temperature of unit mass of a gas by one degree Kelvin when the gas is heated at constant pressure. **Derivation:** [Same as Section 3.1 above — Mayer's Relation] Applying First Law to a closed system at constant pressure: $$\delta q = du + p \, dv = dh$$ $$\Rightarrow c_p \, dT = dh$$ Differentiate $h = u + pv = u + RT$ for an ideal gas: $$dh = du + R \, dT \Rightarrow c_p \, dT = c_v \, dT + R \, dT$$ $$\Rightarrow c_p - c_v = R$$ #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution: **Given:** $m = 0.5$ kg, $p_1 = p_2 = 100$ kPa (constant pressure), $T_1 = 27°C = 300$ K, $v_2 = 2v_1$. **(i) Final Temperature:** For isobaric process, $v/T = \text{const}$: $$\frac{v_1}{T_1} = \frac{v_2}{T_2} \Rightarrow T_2 = T_1 \times \frac{v_2}{v_1} = 300 \times 2 = 600 \text{ K}$$ **(ii) Work Done:** First compute $v_1$ from ideal gas law: $$v_1 = \frac{R T_1}{p_1} = \frac{0.287 \times 300}{100} = 0.861 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$ Total initial volume: $$V_1 = m \cdot v_1 = 0.5 \times 0.861 = 0.4305 \text{ m}^3$$ Final volume: $$V_2 = 2V_1 = 0.861 \text{ m}^3$$ Work done (isobaric): $$W = p(V_2 - V_1) = 100 \times (0.861 - 0.4305) = 100 \times 0.4305 = 43.05 \text{ kJ}$$ **(iii) Heat Transferred:** $$Q = m \, c_p \, (T_2 - T_1) = 0.5 \times 1.005 \times (600 - 300) = 0.5 \times 1.005 \times 300 = 150.75 \text{ kJ}$$ **(iv) Change in Entropy:** For an isobaric process: $$\Delta S = m \, c_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) = 0.5 \times 1.005 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{600}{300}\right)$$ $$\Delta S = 0.5 \times 1.005 \times 0.6931 = 0.3483 \text{ kJ/K}$$ ### **Verification (First Law):** $$\Delta U = m \, c_v \, (T_2 - T_1) = 0.5 \times (1.005 - 0.287) \times 300 = 0.5 \times 0.718 \times 300 = 107.7 \text{ kJ}$$ Check: $Q = \Delta U + W = 107.7 + 43.05 = 150.75$ kJ ✓ $$\boxed{T_2 = 600 \text{ K}, \quad W = 43.05 \text{ kJ}, \quad Q = 150.75 \text{ kJ}, \quad \Delta S = 0.3483 \text{ kJ/K}}$$ --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU Exams — Avoid These Mistakes:** > > 1. **Mayer's Derivation:** Students often forget to justify that $du = c_v \, dT$ and $dh = c_p \, dT$ are valid **only for ideal gases**. Marks are deducted if this assumption is not stated. > > 2. **Sign Convention:** When asked for work done in **compression**, $W$ comes out **negative** (work done ON the system). Examiners check the sign — always state clearly whether work is done *by* or *on* the system. > > 3. **Units Mismatch:** Entropy must be in **kJ/K** (or J/K), not kJ/kg·K, when calculated for the whole system. Use mass-weighted form $\Delta S = m \cdot \Delta s$. > > 4. **Isentropic vs Adiabatic:** They are NOT always the same. **Isentropic** = reversible + adiabatic. A normal adiabatic process (with friction, etc.) is **not** isentropic because entropy is generated. > > 5. **Tds Equations:** Examiners frequently give partial marks only if students mention **reversible process assumption** explicitly. Writing $T \, ds = du + p\,dv$ without justification loses 1–2 marks. > > 6. **Numerical Errors:** Using $\gamma = 1.4$ for air but forgetting $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K (or using the universal gas constant 8.314 by mistake) is a very common error. Always cross-verify $c_p - c_v = R$ before plugging in. > > 7. **Reversible vs Irreversible:** Entropy change is a **property** and depends only on **end states**. So $\Delta s$ formula (with $T_2/T_1$ etc.) is valid even for **irreversible** processes between the same two states. Examiners test this distinction. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Day Revision — Memorise These Anchors:** > > ✅ **Two key specific heats:** $c_v$ (at const $v$), $c_p$ (at const $p$). > > ✅ **Mayer's relation (universal identity for ideal gases):** $c_p - c_v = R$. > > ✅ **Ratio of specific heats:** $\gamma = c_p/c_v = 1 + R/c_v = c_p/(c_p - R)$. > > ✅ **From R and γ:** $c_v = R/(\gamma - 1)$; $c_p = \gamma R/(\gamma - 1)$. > > ✅ **First Tds Equation:** $T \, ds = du + p \, dv$. > > ✅ **Second Tds Equation:** $T \, ds = dh - v \, dp$. > > ✅ **Ideal Gas Entropy (T-v form):** $\Delta s = c_v \ln(T_2/T_1) + R \ln(v_2/v_1)$. > > ✅ **Ideal Gas Entropy (T-p form):** $\Delta s = c_p \ln(T_2/T_1) - R \ln(p_2/p_1)$. > > ✅ **Isothermal:** $\Delta s = R \ln(v_2/v_1) = q/T$. > > ✅ **Isochoric:** $\Delta s = c_v \ln(T_2/T_1)$. > > ✅ **Isobaric:** $\Delta s = c_p \ln(T_2/T_1)$. > > ✅ **Isentropic (reversible adiabatic):** $\Delta s = 0$; relations: $Tv^{\gamma-1} = \text{const}$, $Tp^{(1-\gamma)/\gamma} = \text{const}$. > > ✅ **Entropy is a property** — depends only on end states, valid for both reversible and irreversible paths. > > ✅ **Second Law:** $\Delta S_{universe} \geq 0$ for any process (equality for reversible). > > ✅ **Entropy change of mixing (adiabatic):** $\Delta S_{tot} = \sum m_i c_{p,i} \ln(T_f/T_i) \geq 0$. > > ✅ **Speed of sound** in ideal gas: $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ — direct application of γ. > > ✅ **Area under T-s curve** = reversible heat transfer $Q_{rev} = \int T \, ds$. > > ✅ **Universal gas constant:** $\bar{R} = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K; specific $R = \bar{R}/M$.

topics.title: Introduction to Real Gas Mixtures, Kay's Rule, General Thermodynamic Relations: Combined First and Second Law Equations, Helmholtz and Gibb's Functions, Maxwell's Relations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Real Gas Mixtures, Kay's Rule, General Thermodynamic Relations: Combined First and Second Law Equations, Helmholtz and Gibb's Functions, Maxwell's Relations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Real Gas Mixtures

A **real gas mixture** is a combination of two or more real (non-ideal) gases whose intermolecular forces and finite molecular volumes cannot be neglected. Unlike ideal gas mixtures (governed by Dalton's Law of additive pressures and Amagat's Law of additive volumes), real gas mixtures require **pseudo-reduced properties** and **correspondence (or rule of mixing)** to compute the overall thermodynamic behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Real Gas Mixtures Matter:** Industrial processes (refrigeration, natural gas pipelines, cryogenics, LNG plants) operate at **high pressures (>10 bar)** where ideal gas law fails by **5–30%**. Accurate mixture analysis is critical for compressor design, phase equilibrium, and chemical reactor engineering.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a classroom of students from different colleges (different "molecular sizes" and "personalities"). An "ideal" classroom assumes every student is identical and never interacts personally. A "real" classroom accounts for the fact that engineering students chat more with each other, that some sit closer to the door, and that the room capacity shrinks when desks are large. **Kay's Rule is the "average student profile"** that lets us treat this mixed class as one homogeneous group for the purposes of finding the room's average temperature, pressure, and flow characteristics.

---

### 1.2 Kay's Rule (Pseudocritical Method)

**Kay's Rule** is the simplest correspondence principle for estimating the **pseudocritical properties** of a real gas mixture by computing the **mole-fraction weighted average** of the critical properties of each pure component.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** *Kay's Rule states that a real gas mixture can be approximated as a single equivalent pure real gas having pseudocritical pressure ($P_{pc}$) and pseudocritical temperature ($T_{pc}$) equal to the molal average of the individual component critical pressures and temperatures.*

$$P_{pc} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \, P_{c,i}, \qquad T_{pc} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \, T_{c,i}$$

The **pseudoreduced properties** are then:

$$P_{pr} = \frac{P}{P_{pc}}, \qquad T_{pr} = \frac{T}{T_{pc}}$$

These reduced properties are fed into **generalized compressibility charts** (Z-chart) to obtain the mixture compressibility factor $Z_m$, from which all other real-gas mixture properties follow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity:** Kay's Rule is most accurate for **non-polar hydrocarbon mixtures** with similar molecular weights (e.g., natural gas: CH₄, C₂H₆, C₃H₈). It is **not recommended** for highly polar mixtures (e.g., NH₃ + H₂O) or for H₂/He systems — in such cases, **Wong-Sandler** or **PR/EOS mixing rules** are used.

---

### 1.3 General Thermodynamic Relations — Overview

The **General Thermodynamic Relations** (also called the **Fundamental Property Relations** or **Combined First & Second Law Equations**) form the mathematical backbone of classical thermodynamics. They unify the **First Law** (energy conservation) and the **Second Law** (entropy principle) into exact differentials of the four fundamental thermodynamic potentials.

The four fundamental property relations are:

| Potential | Symbol | Natural Variables | Combined Relation |
|-----------|--------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Internal Energy | $U$ | $S, V$ | $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$ |
| Enthalpy | $H$ | $S, P$ | $dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$ |
| Helmholtz Function | $A$ (or $F$) | $T, V$ | $dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV$ |
| Gibbs Function | $G$ | $T, P$ | $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$ |

---

### 1.4 Helmholtz Function (A) and Gibbs Function (G)

> [!NOTE]
> **Helmholtz Function (A):** $A = U - TS$ — the maximum **non-expansion (closed-system) work** obtainable from a process at constant temperature and volume. It is the **exergy** of a closed system relative to a common dead state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Gibbs Function (G):** $G = H - TS$ — the maximum **non-expansion work** obtainable in an **open system / flow process** at constant temperature and pressure. It governs **phase equilibrium** (Gibbs phase rule) and **chemical reaction spontaneity** ($\Delta G < 0$).

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of **Helmholtz Function (A)** as the *usable mechanical energy locked inside a sealed pressure vessel* — useful when you can only do shaft work (e.g., a stirred tank reactor). **Gibbs Function (G)** is the *usable energy available at a plant battery limit* — useful when you have a continuous flow process with both shaft and flow work (e.g., a turbine inlet).

---

### 1.5 Maxwell's Relations

**Maxwell's Relations** are a set of four exact partial-differential identities derived from the **equality of mixed second partial derivatives** of the thermodynamic potentials. They allow us to replace *non-measurable quantities* (like $\partial S/\partial P\vert_T$ or $\partial S/\partial V\vert_T$) with *directly measurable quantities* (like $\partial V/\partial T\vert_P$ or $\partial P/\partial T\vert_V$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Maxwell's relations are the **bridge** between thermal/caloric properties and mechanical/p-v-T properties. They are the reason we can compute entropy changes from steam tables and equations of state alone, without calorimetric measurements.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Real Gas Mixtures — Why They Differ from Ideal

In an **ideal gas mixture**, the molecules occupy no volume and exert no intermolecular forces. The mixture compressibility is always $Z = 1$ and **Dalton's Law** holds:

$$P = \sum_{i} y_i P_i$$

For a **real gas mixture**:
1. Molecules occupy finite volume → molecules repel at close range.
2. Molecules attract at intermediate range → reduces pressure (internal pressure $\pi_T$).
3. Cross-interactions between unlike species ($i \neq j$) differ from self-interactions.

To handle this, the **principle of corresponding states** is extended to mixtures through Kay's Rule (or its more sophisticated variants).

---

### 2.2 Kay's Rule — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Identify** all $n$ components in the mixture and their mole fractions $y_i$ (for gases).
2. **Look up** the critical temperature $T_{c,i}$ and critical pressure $P_{c,i}$ for each component from data tables.
3. **Compute pseudocritical temperature** as the molar average:

$$T_{pc} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \, T_{c,i}$$

4. **Compute pseudocritical pressure** as the molar average:

$$P_{pc} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \, P_{c,i}$$

5. **Compute pseudoreduced properties** for the mixture:

$$T_{pr} = \frac{T}{T_{pc}}, \qquad P_{pr} = \frac{P}{P_{pc}}$$

6. **Read the compressibility factor** $Z_m$ from the generalized compressibility chart at $(T_{pr}, P_{pr})$.
7. **Apply real-gas equation of state** for the mixture:

$$PV = Z_m n R_u T \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = \frac{Z_m R_u T}{P} \quad \text{(per mole basis)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** This is the standard industry method for natural gas property estimation. Real gas mixtures in pipelines are characterized by their **specific gravity** (relative to air) and pseudoreduced properties — a one-line input gives compressor head, line capacity, and dew point.

---

### 2.3 Combined First & Second Law — Deep Theory

The **First Law** for a closed system:

$$dU = \delta Q - \delta W \quad \text{(reversible)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad dU = \delta Q_{rev} - P\,dV$$

The **Second Law** (Clausius Inequality for reversible processes):

$$dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta Q_{rev} = T\,dS$$

**Substituting** $\delta Q_{rev}$ into the First Law:

$$dU = T\,dS - P\,dV \quad \text{(Fundamental Relation for U)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most important equation in thermodynamics**. It is an **exact differential** in the natural variables $(S, V)$ — meaning $U$ is a *thermodynamic potential* of $(S, V)$.

By **Legendre Transformations** (a mathematical operation that switches a potential's natural variables to those more convenient for a given process), we obtain the other three relations:

**Enthalpy** $H = U + PV$:

$$dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP = T\,dS + V\,dP$$

**Helmholtz Function** $A = U - TS$:

$$dA = dU - T\,dS - S\,dT = -S\,dT - P\,dV$$

**Gibbs Function** $G = H - TS = U + PV - TS$:

$$dG = dH - T\,dS - S\,dT = -S\,dT + V\,dP$$

---

### 2.4 Maxwell's Relations — Derivation Logic

Since $U(S, V)$ is an exact differential, by **Euler's reciprocity theorem** (equality of mixed second partials):

$$\left(\frac{\partial^2 U}{\partial S\,\partial V}\right) = \left(\frac{\partial^2 U}{\partial V\,\partial S}\right)$$

From $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_V = T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V$$

Applying this to all four potentials yields the **four Maxwell's Relations**.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚠️ Note on Markdown Tables:** The vertical pipe character has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve table integrity.

| # | Relation / Identity | Mathematical Form | Natural Variables of Parent |
|---|---------------------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Internal Energy | $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$ | $(S, V)$ |
| 2 | Enthalpy | $dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$ | $(S, P)$ |
| 3 | Helmholtz | $dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV$ | $(T, V)$ |
| 4 | Gibbs | $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$ | $(T, P)$ |
| 5 | Maxwell I | $\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V$ | from $U$ |
| 6 | Maxwell II | $\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P$ | from $H$ |
| 7 | Maxwell III | $\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T$ | from $A$ |
| 8 | Maxwell IV | $\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T$ | from $G$ |
| 9 | $U = H - PV$ | $H = U + PV$ | definition |
| 10 | $A = U - TS$ | $G = H - TS$ | definitions |
| 11 | $G = A + PV$ | derived from $H$ | relation |
| 12 | $C_P - C_V = T \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | Mayer's relation | — |
| 13 | $TdS$ Eq. (1) | $TdS = C_V dT + T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V dV$ | closed system |
| 14 | $TdS$ Eq. (2) | $TdS = C_P dT - T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$ | closed system |
| 15 | Kay's Pseudocritical T | $T_{pc} = \sum y_i T_{c,i}$ | mixture |
| 16 | Kay's Pseudocritical P | $P_{pc} = \sum y_i P_{c,i}$ | mixture |
| 17 | Pseudoreduced properties | $T_{pr} = T/T_{pc}, \; P_{pr} = P/P_{pc}$ | reduced |
| 18 | Real gas mixture | $PV = Z_m n R_u T$ | EOS |

**Standard Constants & Units (for KTU):**
- $R_u = 8.314 \; \text{kJ/kmol·K}$ (Universal gas constant)
- $T$ in **K**, $P$ in **kPa**, $V$ in **m³**, $S$ in **kJ/K**, $U, H, A, G$ in **kJ**
- $C_P, C_V$ in **kJ/kg·K** or **kJ/kmol·K**

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Function Used | Why |
|-------------|---------------|-----|
| Refrigeration cycle analysis | $dH = TdS + VdP$ | Throttling & compression |
| Phase change calculations | $dG = -SdT + VdP$ | Clausius-Clapeyron |
| Engine cycle (Otto/Diesel) | $TdS$ equations | Entropy gen. analysis |
| LNG storage | $dA = -SdT - PdV$ | Low-T cryogenics |
| Compressor design | Kay's rule | Natural gas Z-factor |
| Steam turbine | $dH = TdS + VdP$ | Open system steady flow |
| Chemical reaction spontaneity | $\Delta G$ | Feasibility test |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Four Fundamental Property Relations

#### Step 1: Start from First + Second Law (Closed, Reversible System)

**Statement of First Law** (closed system, differential form):

$$\delta Q = dU + \delta W$$

**Statement of Second Law** (reversible process, where equality holds):

$$dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta Q_{rev} = T\,dS$$

**Reversible work** for a hydrostatic system:

$$\delta W_{rev} = P\,dV$$

**Substitute both into First Law:**

$$dU = \delta Q_{rev} - \delta W_{rev} = T\,dS - P\,dV \quad \text{...(Eq. 1, Fundamental Relation)}$$

> This is an **exact differential** because $U$ is a state function, with $S$ and $V$ as natural variables.

---

#### Step 2: Derive the Enthalpy Relation

**Definition of Enthalpy:**

$$H = U + PV$$

**Differential form** (apply product rule):

$$dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP$$

**Substitute Eq. 1** for $dU$:

$$dH = (T\,dS - P\,dV) + P\,dV + V\,dP$$

**The $-PdV$ and $+PdV$ terms cancel** (algebraic identity):

$$dH = T\,dS + V\,dP \quad \text{...(Eq. 2)}$$

> Natural variables of $H$ are $(S, P)$. Useful for **open-system flow processes** (turbines, compressors, nozzles).

---

#### Step 3: Derive the Helmholtz Function Relation

**Definition of Helmholtz Function:**

$$A = U - TS$$

**Differential form** (product rule on $TS$):

$$dA = dU - T\,dS - S\,dT$$

**Substitute Eq. 1** for $dU$:

$$dA = (T\,dS - P\,dV) - T\,dS - S\,dT$$

**The $TdS$ and $-TdS$ terms cancel:**

$$dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV \quad \text{...(Eq. 3)}$$

> Natural variables of $A$ are $(T, V)$. Useful for **constant-$T$, constant-$V$** processes (closed, isothermal).

---

#### Step 4: Derive the Gibbs Function Relation

**Definition of Gibbs Function:**

$$G = H - TS$$

**Differential form:**

$$dG = dH - T\,dS - S\,dT$$

**Substitute Eq. 2** for $dH$:

$$dG = (T\,dS + V\,dP) - T\,dS - S\,dT$$

**Cancel the $TdS$ terms:**

$$dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP \quad \text{...(Eq. 4)}$$

> Natural variables of $G$ are $(T, P)$. Useful for **constant-$T$, constant-$P$** processes (most chemical reactions, phase changes).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Maxwell's Relations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Theorem Used:** If $z = z(x, y)$ is a state function (i.e., an **exact differential** $dz = M\,dx + N\,dy$), then by **Euler's Reciprocity / Schwarz's Theorem**:
> $$\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$$

#### Maxwell Relation I (from $dU$)

Given:

$$dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$$

Identify coefficients: $M = T$ (coefficient of $dS$, with $S$ as $x$), $N = -P$ (coefficient of $dV$, with $V$ as $y$).

**Apply reciprocity:**

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V \quad \text{...(Maxwell I)}$$

#### Maxwell Relation II (from $dH$)

Given:

$$dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$$

Here the natural differential is $dz = T\,dS + V\,dP$ with $M = T, N = V$. Applying reciprocity (mixed partials wrt $S$ and $P$):

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P \quad \text{...(Maxwell II)}$$

#### Maxwell Relation III (from $dA$)

Given:

$$dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV$$

Coefficients: $M = -S, N = -P$. Reciprocity:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V \quad \text{...(Maxwell III)}$$

#### Maxwell Relation IV (from $dG$)

Given:

$$dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$$

Coefficients: $M = -S, N = V$. Reciprocity:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P \quad \text{...(Maxwell IV)}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Physical Meaning of Each Coefficient

| Coefficient | Definition | Physical Meaning |
|-------------|------------|------------------|
| $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_V = T$ | From $dU$ | **Absolute temperature** |
| $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_S = -P$ | From $dU$ | **Negative pressure** |
| $\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial S}\right)_P = T$ | From $dH$ | **Absolute temperature** |
| $\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_S = V$ | From $dH$ | **Specific volume** |
| $\left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_V = -S$ | From $dA$ | **Negative entropy** |
| $\left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial V}\right)_T = -P$ | From $dA$ | **Negative pressure** |
| $\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = -S$ | From $dG$ | **Negative entropy** |
| $\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_T = V$ | From $dG$ | **Specific volume** |

---

### 3.4 Worked Derivation: TdS Equations (Mayer's Relation)

**Starting point** — treat $S$ as a function of $(T, V)$: $S = S(T, V)$:

$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

**Multiply by $T$:**

$$T\,dS = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

**Identify** the first term: by definition of $C_V$,

$$C_V = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V$$

**Use Maxwell III** for the second term: $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$

$$\boxed{\;T\,dS = C_V\,dT + T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V dV \quad \text{...(TdS Eq. 1)}\;}$$

Now treat $S$ as a function of $(T, P)$: $S = S(T, P)$:

$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

**Multiply by $T$ and apply** $C_P = T(\partial S/\partial T)_P$ and **Maxwell IV**:

$$\boxed{\;T\,dS = C_P\,dT - T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP \quad \text{...(TdS Eq. 2)}\;}$$

**Equate both expressions for $T\,dS$** and rearrange to get **Mayer's Relation:**

$$\boxed{\;C_P - C_V = T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P\;}$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Kay's Rule Application

> **Problem (KTU Style):** A natural gas mixture has the following composition by mole: CH₄ = 0.85, C₂H₆ = 0.10, C₃H₈ = 0.05. The mixture is at $T = 350 \; \text{K}$ and $P = 70 \; \text{bar}$. Estimate the compressibility factor $Z_m$ using Kay's Rule. Critical data: $T_c$ (K) = 190.6, 305.4, 369.8; $P_c$ (bar) = 46.0, 48.8, 42.0 for CH₄, C₂H₆, C₃H₈ respectively.

**Solution — Step 1: Compute Pseudocritical Temperature $T_{pc}$:**

$$T_{pc} = \sum y_i T_{c,i} = (0.85)(190.6) + (0.10)(305.4) + (0.05)(369.8)$$

$$T_{pc} = 162.01 + 30.54 + 18.49 = 211.04 \; \text{K}$$

**Step 2: Compute Pseudocritical Pressure $P_{pc}$:**

$$P_{pc} = \sum y_i P_{c,i} = (0.85)(46.0) + (0.10)(48.8) + (0.05)(42.0)$$

$$P_{pc} = 39.10 + 4.88 + 2.10 = 46.08 \; \text{bar}$$

**Step 3: Compute Pseudoreduced Properties:**

$$T_{pr} = \frac{T}{T_{pc}} = \frac{350}{211.04} = 1.658$$

$$P_{pr} = \frac{P}{P_{pc}} = \frac{70}{46.08} = 1.519$$

**Step 4: Read Compressibility Factor from Z-Chart:**

At $T_{pr} = 1.66$ and $P_{pr} = 1.52$, the generalized compressibility chart gives:

$$Z_m \approx 0.91$$

**Step 5: Compute Molar Volume:**

$$V_m = \frac{Z_m R_u T}{P} = \frac{(0.91)(8.314 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{bar·m}^3/\text{kmol·K})(350)}{70}$$

$$V_m = 0.378 \; \text{m}^3/\text{kmol}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** For comparison, ideal gas would give $V_{ideal} = 0.416 \; \text{m}^3/\text{kmol}$. The real gas occupies **9.2% less** volume — a typical result for hydrocarbon mixtures near critical conditions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Thermodynamic Square — Master Relationship Map

The **Thermodynamic Square** (a.k.a. **Born Square** or **Mnemonic Square**) is the universal KTU/PG-level mnemonic for remembering the four potentials and the eight coefficients of the fundamental relations.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["S  Entropy"] --- P1["P  Pressure"]
    P1 --- V1["V  Volume"]
    V1 --- T1["T  Temperature"]
    T1 --- S1
    S1 --- U1["U  Internal Energy\n dU = TdS - PdV\n Center of S-V Edge"]
    V1 --- U1
    T1 --- G1["G  Gibbs Function\n dG = -SdT + VdP\n Center of T-P Edge"]
    P1 --- G1
    S1 --- H1["H  Enthalpy\n dH = TdS + VdP\n Center of S-P Edge"]
    P1 --- H1
    T1 --- A1["A  Helmholtz Function\n dA = -SdT - PdV\n Center of T-V Edge"]
    V1 --- A1
```

**Reading the Square (Memory Trick for KTU Exams):**
- Each **vertex** = a fundamental property ($S, P, V, T$).
- Each **side** = a potential, whose differential uses the two endpoints of that side.
- The **sign** in the differential is + if you go **clockwise** around the square, − if **counter-clockwise** (starting from the variable that matches the side's "center potential").

---

### 4.2 Maxwell's Relations — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["From dU = TdS - PdV"]
        M1["Maxwell I\n (dT/dV)S = -(dP/dS)V"]
    end
    subgraph S2["From dH = TdS + VdP"]
        M2["Maxwell II\n (dT/dP)S = (dV/dS)P"]
    end
    subgraph S3["From dA = -SdT - PdV"]
        M3["Maxwell III\n (dP/dT)V = (dS/dV)T"]
    end
    subgraph S4["From dG = -SdT + VdP"]
        M4["Maxwell IV\n (dV/dT)P = -(dS/dP)T"]
    end
    S1 --> M1
    S2 --> M2
    S3 --> M3
    S4 --> M4
    M3 --> App1["(dS/dV)T replacement:\n use measurable (dP/dT)V"]
    M4 --> App2["(dS/dP)T replacement:\n use measurable (dV/dT)P"]
    M1 --> App3["(dT/dV)S replacement:\n use measurable (dP/dS)V"]
    M2 --> App4["(dT/dP)S replacement:\n use measurable (dV/dS)P"]
```

---

### 4.3 Kay's Rule — Functional Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["Input: Component mole fractions yi"] --> A2["Look up Tci, Pci from data tables"]
    A2 --> A3["Compute Tpc = Sum yi Tci"]
    A2 --> A4["Compute Ppc = Sum yi Pci"]
    A3 --> A5["Compute Tpr = T / Tpc"]
    A4 --> A6["Compute Ppr = P / Ppc"]
    A5 --> A7["Enter Generalized Z-Chart"]
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8["Read Zm value"]
    A8 --> A9["Apply Real Gas EOS:\n PV = Zm n Ru T"]
    A9 --> A10["Compute V, h, s of mixture\n via departure functions"]
```

---

### 4.4 Combined First & Second Law — Energy Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    FL["First Law (Energy Conservation)\n dU = dQ - dW"] --> SL["Second Law (Entropy Principle)\n dS >= dQ/T"]
    FL --> COMB["Combined Relation (Exact Differential)"]
    SL --> COMB
    COMB --> R1["dU = TdS - PdV  (S,V)"]
    COMB --> R2["dH = TdS + VdP  (S,P)"]
    COMB --> R3["dA = -SdT - PdV (T,V)"]
    COMB --> R4["dG = -SdT + VdP (T,P)"]
    R1 --> MR["Maxwell's Relations\n (4 exact identities)"]
    R2 --> MR
    R3 --> MR
    R4 --> MR
    MR --> APPL["Engineering Applications:\n - Entropy change calc.\n - TdS equations\n - Mayer's relation\n - Phase equilibrium"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**
> **Define Helmholtz Function and Gibbs Function. State their physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Helmholtz Function (A):** Defined as $A = U - TS$ where $U$ is internal energy, $T$ is absolute temperature, and $S$ is entropy. Its SI unit is kJ (or kJ/kmol).
>
> **Physical significance:** It represents the **maximum useful work** obtainable from a closed system undergoing an isothermal, isochoric, reversible process. It is the **availability (exergy)** of a closed system at constant $T$ and $V$.
>
> **Gibbs Function (G):** Defined as $G = H - TS$ where $H$ is enthalpy.
>
> **Physical significance:** It represents the **maximum non-expansion work** obtainable from a system at constant $T$ and $P$. A process at constant $T$ and $P$ is **spontaneous** if $\Delta G < 0$ and at **equilibrium** if $\Delta G = 0$. It is the **key potential for phase and reaction equilibria**.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
> **State the four Maxwell's relations. Why are they important in thermodynamics?**

**Model Answer:**

**The four Maxwell's relations are:**

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V \quad \text{(I)}$$

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P \quad \text{(II)}$$

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T \quad \text{(III)}$$

$$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T \quad \text{(IV)}$$

**Importance:** Maxwell's relations allow us to **express entropy derivatives** (which are difficult to measure directly in a calorimeter) **in terms of measurable $P$-$V$-$T$ properties** (which are easily obtained from equations of state or steam tables). This enables calculation of entropy change, internal energy change, and enthalpy change using only **mechanical measurements** of $P$, $V$, $T$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model QP]**
>
> **(a)** Starting from the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics for a closed system undergoing a reversible process, derive the **four fundamental thermodynamic property relations**. Clearly identify the natural variables of each potential. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A real gas mixture contains 70% methane (CH₄) and 30% ethane (C₂H₆) by mole. The mixture is at 50 bar and 100°C. Using **Kay's Rule**, determine the **compressibility factor** of the mixture. Given: $T_c$(CH₄) = 190.6 K, $T_c$(C₂H₆) = 305.4 K, $P_c$(CH₄) = 46.0 bar, $P_c$(C₂H₆) = 48.8 bar. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — First Law (closed system):**

$$dU = \delta Q_{rev} - \delta W_{rev} = \delta Q_{rev} - P\,dV$$

**Step 2 — Second Law (reversible process):**

$$dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} \;\Rightarrow\; \delta Q_{rev} = T\,dS$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into First Law:**

$$dU = T\,dS - P\,dV \quad \text{(Natural variables: } S, V\text{)}$$

**Step 4 — Enthalpy** $H = U + PV$:

$$dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP = T\,dS + V\,dP \quad \text{(Natural variables: } S, P\text{)}$$

**Step 5 — Helmholtz** $A = U - TS$:

$$dA = dU - T\,dS - S\,dT = -S\,dT - P\,dV \quad \text{(Natural variables: } T, V\text{)}$$

**Step 6 — Gibbs** $G = H - TS$:

$$dG = dH - T\,dS - S\,dT = -S\,dT + V\,dP \quad \text{(Natural variables: } T, P\text{)}$$

> The exact differential form guarantees that all second partial derivatives are continuous and that **Maxwell's relations** (the cornerstone of all thermodynamic property derivations) hold.

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute pseudocritical temperature:**

$$T_{pc} = y_1 T_{c,1} + y_2 T_{c,2} = (0.70)(190.6) + (0.30)(305.4)$$

$$T_{pc} = 133.42 + 91.62 = 225.04 \; \text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Compute pseudocritical pressure:**

$$P_{pc} = y_1 P_{c,1} + y_2 P_{c,2} = (0.70)(46.0) + (0.30)(48.8)$$

$$P_{pc} = 32.20 + 14.64 = 46.84 \; \text{bar}$$

**Step 3 — Pseudoreduced properties:**

$$T_{pr} = \frac{T}{T_{pc}} = \frac{373.15}{225.04} = 1.658$$

$$P_{pr} = \frac{P}{P_{pc}} = \frac{50}{46.84} = 1.068$$

**Step 4 — Read $Z_m$ from generalized compressibility chart at $T_{pr} = 1.66, \; P_{pr} = 1.07$:**

$$Z_m \approx 0.93$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** Since $Z_m < 1$, attractive forces dominate at this condition, and the real gas occupies **7% less volume** than predicted by ideal gas law.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the **four Maxwell's relations** from the fundamental property relations using the **exactness criterion** (Euler's reciprocity theorem). State the **TdS equations** and show how **Maxwell's relations** are used in their derivation. **(8 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a substance obeying the **van der Waals equation of state** $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{V^2}\right)(V - b) = R_uT$ (per mole), derive an expression for the **entropy change** of an ideal gas between states 1 and 2 using the **TdS equation (form 1)**. Show that the result is path-independent. **(6 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 — Exactness criterion:** For an exact differential $dz = M\,dx + N\,dy$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$$

**Step 2 — Apply to $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$** (with $M = T, N = -P$):

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V \quad \text{(I)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply to $dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$** ($M = T, N = V$):

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P \quad \text{(II)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply to $dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV$** ($M = -S, N = -P$):

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V \quad \text{(III)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply to $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$** ($M = -S, N = V$):

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P \quad \text{(IV)}$$

**Step 6 — TdS Equations:**

From $S = S(T, V)$: $\;T\,dS = C_V\,dT + T(\partial P/\partial T)_V\,dV$

From $S = S(T, P)$: $\;T\,dS = C_P\,dT - T(\partial V/\partial T)_P\,dP$

**Step 7 — Use of Maxwell's relations:** In the first TdS equation, the term $(\partial P/\partial T)_V$ comes from the equation of state (e.g., van der Waals). For the second, $(\partial V/\partial T)_P$ is the **thermal expansion coefficient** $\alpha = (1/V)(\partial V/\partial T)_P$, which is **directly measurable** in a dilatometer. Maxwell's relations make the *otherwise unmeasurable* $(\partial S/\partial V)_T$ and $(\partial S/\partial P)_T$ accessible from EOS data alone.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(b):**

For an **ideal gas** obeying $PV = R_uT$ (i.e., $a = 0, b = 0$ limit of vdW), we have:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{R_u}{V}$$

**Substitute into TdS equation 1:**

$$T\,dS = C_V\,dT + T \cdot \frac{R_u}{V}\,dV = C_V\,dT + \frac{R_uT}{V}\,dV$$

**Integrate from state 1 to state 2:**

$$\int_1^2 T\,dS = \int_1^2 C_V\,dT + \int_1^2 \frac{R_uT}{V}\,dV$$

**Assuming $C_V$ = constant:**

$$T(s_2 - s_1) = C_V(T_2 - T_1) + R_uT \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

**Divide through by $T$** (entropy is state function, path-independent):

$$s_2 - s_1 = C_V \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + R_u \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

**Path independence:** Since $s$ is a **state function**, $\Delta s$ depends only on the end states $(T_1, V_1)$ and $(T_2, V_2)$ — it is the same whether we go via a constant-$V$ then constant-$T$ path, or constant-$T$ then constant-$V$, or any other reversible path. The TdS equation being an **exact differential** is the mathematical guarantee of this.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> **Common ways students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Sign errors in Maxwell's relations** — remember: a negative sign appears in I and IV because of the way the differential is written. Mnemonic: the $-P\,dV$ in $dU$ and $-S\,dT$ in $dA$ introduce one negative sign.
> 2. **Mixing up the natural variables** — examiners LOVE asking "what are the natural variables of G?" Answer: $(T, P)$. A wrong answer loses 1 mark immediately.
> 3. **Forgetting to cancel** $-TdS + TdS$ in deriving the four relations — partial cancellation mistakes will propagate and cost 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Kay's Rule units trap** — $T$ must be in **K** (not °C) and $P$ in consistent units (e.g., bar or kPa, not mixed).
> 5. **Forgetting to convert °C → K** when computing $T_{pr}$ — this is the #1 reason KTU students lose 2 marks in 7-mark problems.
> 6. **Writing Maxwell's relation without the subscript condition** — e.g., writing $(\partial T/\partial V) = -(\partial P/\partial S)$ without specifying "at constant $S$" and "at constant $V$" is **incomplete** and costs 1 mark.
> 7. **Using Kay's Rule for hydrogen/helium** — examiners sometimes set a "trick question" with H₂ mixture; Kay's Rule fails for quantum gases. Note the assumption in your answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD RAPID REVISION POINTS:**

- [ ] **Real gas mixtures** require pseudocritical properties; ideal mixing laws (Dalton/Amagat) fail at high pressure.
- [ ] **Kay's Rule:** $T_{pc} = \sum y_i T_{c,i}$, $P_{pc} = \sum y_i P_{c,i}$ — always use **mole fraction** (not mass fraction).
- [ ] **Pseudoreduced properties** are fed into the **Z-chart** to find $Z_m$.
- [ ] **Combined 1st + 2nd Law** gives $dU = TdS - PdV$ — the master exact differential.
- [ ] **Four potentials:** $U, H, A, G$ — each has its own natural variable pair.
- [ ] **Helmholtz** $A = U - TS$ is for constant $T, V$ (closed system work).
- [ ] **Gibbs** $G = H - TS$ is for constant $T, P$ (open system / phase equilibrium).
- [ ] **Always** convert $°C \to K$ for any $T$ in $T_{pr}$ or $\ln(T_2/T_1)$.
- [ ] **Maxwell's relations** are derived from the **equality of mixed second partials** (Euler's reciprocity).
- [ ] **TdS Equation 1:** $TdS = C_V dT + T(\partial P/\partial T)_V dV$ — use for $V$-varying processes.
- [ ] **TdS Equation 2:** $TdS = C_P dT - T(\partial V/\partial T)_P dP$ — use for $P$-varying processes.
- [ ] **Mayer's Relation:** $C_P - C_V = T \alpha^2 V / \beta_T$ where $\alpha$ = expansion coeff., $\beta_T$ = isothermal compressibility.
- [ ] **Maxwell IV** is the most-used in exam problems: $(\partial S/\partial P)_T = -(\partial V/\partial T)_P = -V\alpha$.
- [ ] **Sign convention:** negative signs appear in **Maxwell I** and **Maxwell IV** — memorize by **mnemonic square**.
- [ ] **Thermodynamic Square mnemonic:** The center of each **side** is a potential; coefficients of $dS$ and $dV$ etc. appear with signs based on rotation direction.
- [ ] **Engineering apps:** Maxwell III → entropy of gas expansion; Maxwell IV → Joule-Thomson coefficient; $dG = 0$ → phase equilibrium.
- [ ] For an **ideal gas** in TdS Eq. 1, $(\partial P/\partial T)_V = R_u/V$ → directly integrable.
- [ ] For an **ideal gas** in TdS Eq. 2, $(\partial V/\partial T)_P = R_u/P$ → directly integrable.
- [ ] **Path independence** of entropy change follows from the **exactness** of $dS$ — never assume $T dS = \delta Q$ is path-dependent; only the heat $\delta Q$ is.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tds Equations, The Clapeyron Equation, Throttling Process
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Tds Equations (Tds Relations)

### Formal Academic Definition
The **Tds Equations**, also known as the **Tds Relations** or **Gibbs Equations**, are a set of four fundamental thermodynamic identities that express the change in entropy ($ds$) in terms of measurable properties: temperature ($T$), pressure ($P$), specific volume ($v$), internal energy ($u$), enthalpy ($h$), and the specific heats ($C_p$, $C_v$). They are derived directly from the combined first and second laws of thermodynamics combined with Maxwell's relations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Tds equations form the **mathematical backbone** of classical thermodynamics. They are indispensable for expressing entropy change of real substances, deriving thermodynamic property tables, and analyzing phase change processes — all core to your ESE questions.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you are a **bank manager** trying to track the total wealth (entropy) flowing into and out of a vault (a thermodynamic system). The wealth can change due to:
- **Heat transfer (temperature effect)** — like deposits in the bank
- **Volume change (expansion work)** — like currency exchange fees
- **Pressure change (flow work)** — like transaction charges

The Tds equations are like a **financial ledger** that precisely accounts for *how* and *why* the wealth (entropy) changes for each type of transaction. Without these equations, you cannot balance the books of any thermodynamic process.

---

## 1.2 The Clapeyron Equation

### Formal Academic Definition
The **Clapeyron Equation** is a fundamental thermodynamic relationship that describes the slope of the phase boundary ($dP/dT$) between two phases of a pure substance at equilibrium. It quantitatively links the latent heat of phase change, the absolute temperature, and the specific volume change during the phase transition.

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{L}{T \cdot \Delta v} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}} = \frac{s_{fg}}{v_{fg}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Clapeyron Equation is **exact** for all pure substances undergoing phase change. The **Clausius-Clapeyron Equation** is its *specialized* form for ideal gas vapor (assuming $v_g \gg v_f$).

### Conceptual Analogy
Picture a **mountain range** on a pressure-temperature (P-T) diagram. Each ridge line represents the boundary where two phases coexist (e.g., solid-liquid, liquid-vapor). The Clapeyron Equation is the **"slope finder"** — it tells you exactly how steep each ridge is. Knowing this slope is critical for engineers designing boilers, condensers, and refrigeration systems that operate near saturation lines.

---

## 1.3 Throttling Process

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Throttling Process** (also called a **Joule-Thomson Expansion** or **Wire-Drawing Process**) is an irreversible, adiabatic, steady-flow process in which a fluid expands from a high-pressure region to a low-pressure region through a constriction (porous plug, throttle valve, or capillary tube), resulting in **no change in enthalpy** ($h_1 = h_2 = \text{constant}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The Joule-Thomson coefficient ($\mu_{JT}$) determines whether a gas **cools, heats, or remains constant** during throttling. This is the foundation of **gas liquefaction**, **refrigeration**, and **Linde-Hampson systems**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **crowded highway** suddenly encountering a narrow tunnel (the throttle). Cars must squeeze through, and after exiting:
- They experience a **drop in pressure** (from squeeze to open road)
- They exchange some **kinetic energy** with surroundings (temperature change)
- The **total energy** of each car remains the same — no engine work is done, no heat is added

Similarly, fluid molecules lose ordering (pressure drops) and may **cool down** as they expand through the constriction — this is the principle behind your household refrigerator's expansion valve.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values Used in This Module:**
> - Universal Gas Constant: **$R_u = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K**
> - For water at 100°C, 1 atm: $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg, $v_g = 1.673$ m³/kg, $v_f = 0.001043$ m³/kg
> - Standard atmospheric pressure: **$P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Tds Equations — Conceptual Foundation

Starting from the combined First and Second Law for a **closed system** (reversible):

$$T\,ds = du + P\,dv$$

And the definition of enthalpy: $h = u + Pv \implies du = dh - P\,dv - v\,dP$

Substituting and rearranging, we obtain the **two fundamental Tds equations**:

| # | Tds Equation | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T\,ds = du + P\,dv$ | Closed system analysis |
| 2 | $T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$ | Open system / flow processes |

Now, expressing $du$ and $dh$ in terms of $T, v$ and $T, P$ respectively:

$$du = C_v\,dT + \left[T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v - P\right]dv$$

$$dh = C_p\,dT + \left[v - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P\right]dP$$

Substituting back gives the **four standard Tds relations**:

| # | Tds Equation | Independent Variables |
|---|---|---|
| 3 | $T\,ds = C_v\,dT + T\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v dv$ | $T$ and $v$ |
| 4 | $T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$ | $T$ and $P$ |
| 5 | $T\,ds = C_v\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_v dP + C_p\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial v}\right)_P dv$ | $P$ and $v$ |
| 6 | $T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$ | $h$ and $P$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **Equation 3** → used for entropy change in isochoric (constant volume) processes
> - **Equation 4** → used for entropy change in isobaric (constant pressure) processes
> - **Equation 5** → used in deriving relations between $C_p$ and $C_v$
> - **Equation 6** → used in open system / flow process entropy analysis

---

## 2.2 Clapeyron Equation — Deep Theory

### Why This Equation Matters
At a **phase boundary** (saturation line), two phases coexist in thermodynamic equilibrium. The Gibbs function ($g$) of both phases must be equal:

$$g_f = g_g \quad \text{(along the saturation line)}$$

Taking the differential and using Maxwell's relations, we obtain the Clapeyron equation. This is a **thermodynamically exact** result.

### Clausius-Clapeyron Equation (Specialized Form)
For the **liquid-vapor** phase change, assuming:
1. Vapor behaves as an ideal gas: $v_g \approx RT/P$
2. $v_g \gg v_f$ (so $v_{fg} \approx v_g$)
3. $h_{fg}$ is approximately constant over a small temperature range

We integrate to get:

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Clausius-Clapeyron equation is used in **steam tables generation**, **atmospheric science** (boiling point vs. altitude), and **refrigeration cycle analysis** (saturation pressure lookup).

---

## 2.3 Throttling Process — Deep Theory

### Why Enthalpy Remains Constant
Applying the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** to a throttling device:

$$h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1 + q = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2 + w$$

For a throttling process:
- **No heat transfer:** $q = 0$ (adiabatic — process is fast)
- **No shaft work:** $w = 0$ (no turbine or compressor)
- **Negligible KE/PE changes:** $\Delta KE \approx 0$, $\Delta PE \approx 0$

This reduces to the **isenthalpic condition**:

$$h_1 = h_2$$

### Joule-Thomson Coefficient
The **Joule-Thomson coefficient** quantifies the temperature change with pressure drop at constant enthalpy:

$$\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h$$

- $\mu_{JT} > 0$ → gas **cools** upon expansion (most gases at room T)
- $\mu_{JT} < 0$ → gas **heats** upon expansion (e.g., H₂, He at room T)
- $\mu_{JT} = 0$ → **inversion temperature** (no temperature change)

The general thermodynamic expression:

$$\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_p}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P - v\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Applications:**
> - Liquefaction of gases (oxygen, nitrogen, helium)
> - Domestic refrigeration and air conditioning (expansion valve)
> - Steam throttling in Rankine cycle reheat processes
> - Natural gas pressure reduction stations

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T\,ds = du + P\,dv$ | Tds in terms of $u, v$ | Closed system, 1st + 2nd law |
| 2 | $T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$ | Tds in terms of $h, P$ | Open system / flow |
| 3 | $T\,ds = C_v\,dT + T\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v dv$ | Tds in terms of $T, v$ | Isochoric processes |
| 4 | $T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$ | Tds in terms of $T, P$ | Isobaric processes |
| 5 | $T\,ds = C_v\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_v dP + C_p\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial v}\right)_P dv$ | Tds in terms of $P, v$ | Deriving $C_p - C_v$ relation |
| 6 | $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}$ | Clapeyron Equation (exact) | Phase boundary slope |
| 7 | $\ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | Clausius-Clapeyron (ideal gas) | Saturation pressure calc |
| 8 | $h_1 = h_2$ | Throttling condition | Isenthalpic process |
| 9 | $\mu_{JT} = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h$ | Joule-Thomson coefficient | Cooling/heating effect |
| 10 | $\mu_{JT} = \dfrac{1}{C_p}\left[T\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P - v\right]$ | General $\mu_{JT}$ expression | Real gas analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **Clapeyron Equation** (exact) with the **Clausius-Clapeyron Equation** (approximate, ideal gas). The exam will explicitly mention which is required. Always check whether $v_g \gg v_f$ is stated.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Four Tds Equations

### Derivation of Equation 3: $T\,ds = C_v\,dT + T(\partial P/\partial T)_v\,dv$

**Starting Point:** The combined first and second law for a reversible closed system:
$$T\,ds = du + P\,dv \quad \text{...(i)}$$

We express $u$ as a function of $T$ and $v$: $u = u(T, v)$

Therefore, the total differential is:
$$du = \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial T}\right)_v dT + \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial v}\right)_T dv \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 1:** Recognize that by definition, the specific heat at constant volume is:
$$C_v = \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial T}\right)_v \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Step 2:** From the **Maxwell Relation** derived from $F = u - Ts$ (Helmholtz free energy):
$$\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial v}\right)_T = T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v - P \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute (iii) and (iv) into (ii):
$$du = C_v\,dT + \left[T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v - P\right]dv \quad \text{...(v)}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute (v) into (i):
$$T\,ds = C_v\,dT + \left[T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v - P\right]dv + P\,dv$$

**Step 5:** The $-P\,dv$ and $+P\,dv$ cancel:
$$T\,ds = C_v\,dT + T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v dv$$

$$\boxed{T\,ds = C_v\,dT + T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v dv}$$

### Derivation of Equation 4: $T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T(\partial v/\partial T)_P\,dP$

**Starting Point:** The alternative form of combined law:
$$T\,ds = dh - v\,dP \quad \text{...(i')}$$

We express $h$ as a function of $T$ and $P$: $h = h(T, P)$

$$dh = \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial P}\right)_T dP \quad \text{...(ii')}$$

**Step 1:** By definition, the specific heat at constant pressure is:
$$C_p = \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right)_P \quad \text{...(iii')}$$

**Step 2:** From the **Maxwell Relation** derived from $G = h - Ts$ (Gibbs free energy):
$$\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial P}\right)_T = v - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P \quad \text{...(iv')}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute (iii') and (iv') into (ii'):
$$dh = C_p\,dT + \left[v - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P\right]dP \quad \text{...(v')}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute (v') into (i'):
$$T\,ds = C_p\,dT + \left[v - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P\right]dP - v\,dP$$

**Step 5:** The $+v\,dP$ and $-v\,dP$ cancel:
$$T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$$

$$\boxed{T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P dP}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Clapeyron Equation

### Starting Point
At the **phase boundary**, the Gibbs function is continuous between two phases. For example, between liquid (f) and vapor (g):

$$g_f = g_g \quad \text{at the saturation line} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Take the differential along the saturation line:
$$dg_f = dg_g \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 2:** Recall the fundamental relation: $dg = v\,dP - s\,dT$

Therefore:
$$v_f\,dP - s_f\,dT = v_g\,dP - s_g\,dT \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to isolate $dP$ and $dT$:
$$(v_g - v_f)\,dP = (s_g - s_f)\,dT \quad \text{...(4)}$$

**Step 4:** Define $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f$ and $s_{fg} = s_g - s_f$:

$$v_{fg}\,dP = s_{fg}\,dT \quad \text{...(5)}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the slope $dP/dT$:
$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{s_{fg}}{v_{fg}} \quad \text{...(6)}$$

**Step 6:** During phase change at constant T, P, the entropy change is:
$$s_{fg} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T} \quad \text{...(7)}$$

**Step 7:** Substituting (7) into (6):

$$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}} = \frac{s_{fg}}{v_{fg}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is an **exact** thermodynamic result. No assumptions have been made about the nature of the substance or the phases involved.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Clausius-Clapeyron Equation

### Assumptions
1. **Vapor behaves as an ideal gas:** $Pv = RT$ (per unit mass: $Pv_g = RT/M$ or $Pv_g = \overline{R}T$ with specific gas constant)
2. **Liquid specific volume is negligible:** $v_f \ll v_g \implies v_{fg} \approx v_g = RT/P$
3. **Latent heat $h_{fg}$ is approximately constant** over the temperature range of interest.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Start with the Clapeyron equation:
$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}} \approx \frac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_g} \quad \text{(using $v_{fg} \approx v_g$)}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute ideal gas law $v_g = RT/P$:
$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg} \cdot P}{T \cdot RT} = \frac{h_{fg} \cdot P}{R T^2} \quad \text{...(A)}$$

**Step 3:** Separate variables:
$$\frac{dP}{P} = \frac{h_{fg}}{R T^2}\,dT \quad \text{...(B)}$$

**Step 4:** Integrate between states 1 and 2, assuming $h_{fg}$ is constant:
$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{h_{fg}}{R}\int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the integrals:
$$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{h_{fg}}{R}\left[-\frac{1}{T}\right]_{T_1}^{T_2}$$

**Step 6:** Simplify:
$$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(-\frac{1}{T_2} + \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Joule-Thomson Coefficient

### Starting Point
We want to find how temperature varies with pressure at **constant enthalpy**:
$$\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Enthalpy is a function of $T$ and $P$: $h = h(T, P)$
$$dh = \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

**Step 2:** At constant enthalpy, $dh = 0$:
$$0 = C_p\,dT + \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial P}\right)_T dP \quad \text{...(I)}$$

**Step 3:** From the Maxwell relation on Gibbs function:
$$\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial P}\right)_T = v - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P \quad \text{...(II)}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute (II) into (I):
$$0 = C_p\,dT + \left[v - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P\right]dP$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $dT/dP$ at constant $h$:
$$C_p\,dT = -\left[v - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P\right]dP$$

**Step 6:** Therefore:
$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h = \frac{1}{C_p}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P - v\right]$$

$$\boxed{\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_p}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P - v\right]}$$

---

## 3.5 Special Case: $\mu_{JT}$ for an Ideal Gas

### Derivation
**Step 1:** For an ideal gas, $v = RT/P$
$$\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{R}{P}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:
$$T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P - v = T \cdot \frac{R}{P} - \frac{RT}{P} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Therefore:
$$\mu_{JT}^{ideal} = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** An **ideal gas does not change temperature** during throttling. The Joule-Thomson effect is purely a **real gas** phenomenon arising from intermolecular forces and finite molecular volume.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

### Problem
Steam at 200°C and 1 MPa is throttled to 100 kPa in a steady flow process. Determine:
(a) The exit temperature (assuming ideal gas behavior with $C_p = 1.872$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.4615$ kJ/kg·K)
(b) The entropy change per kg

### Solution

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 200 + 273 = 473$ K
- $P_1 = 1$ MPa $= 1000$ kPa
- $P_2 = 100$ kPa
- $C_p = 1.872$ kJ/kg·K
- $R = 0.4615$ kJ/kg·K

**Part (a): Throttling → constant enthalpy**

For ideal gas, $h = C_p T$, so $h_1 = h_2$:
$$C_p T_1 = C_p T_2 \implies T_2 = T_1 = 473 \text{ K} = 200°C$$

> This confirms the ideal gas property: $\mu_{JT} = 0$ for throttling.

**Part (b): Entropy change per kg**

Using the Tds equation: $T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T(\partial v/\partial T)_P\,dP$

For ideal gas: $T(\partial v/\partial T)_P = T \cdot R/P = v$

$$ds = C_p \frac{dT}{T} - R\frac{dP}{P}$$

Integrating from state 1 to state 2:
$$s_2 - s_1 = C_p \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

**Substituting values** (with $T_1 = T_2$):
$$s_2 - s_1 = 0 - 0.4615 \times \ln\left(\frac{100}{1000}\right)$$

$$s_2 - s_1 = -0.4615 \times \ln(0.1) = -0.4615 \times (-2.3026)$$

$$s_2 - s_1 = +1.0627 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Even though enthalpy is constant, **entropy increases** because throttling is a highly **irreversible** process ($\Delta s_{gen} > 0$).

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State any two Tds equations and explain their significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The two fundamental Tds equations are:

**(i)** $T\,ds = du + P\,dv$ — relates entropy change to internal energy change and specific volume change. **Significance:** Used in closed system analysis for entropy change calculations.

**(ii)** $T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$ — relates entropy change to enthalpy change and pressure change. **Significance:** Used in open system / steady flow process analysis, e.g., turbines, compressors, throttling devices.

> These equations combine the **First and Second Laws** of thermodynamics and form the basis for deriving all other Tds relations using Maxwell's equations.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`
**Define the Joule-Thomson coefficient. What is its significance in gas liquefaction?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Joule-Thomson coefficient** is defined as the rate of change of temperature with respect to pressure at constant enthalpy:

$$\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h$$

**Significance in Gas Liquefaction:**
- A **positive $\mu_{JT}$** indicates that the gas **cools** upon expansion — this is the principle behind gas liquefaction plants (e.g., **Linde-Hampson process** for liquefying air, oxygen, nitrogen).
- A **negative $\mu_{JT}$** (e.g., H₂, He at room temperature) means the gas **heats up** upon expansion — these gases must be **precooled below their inversion temperature** before throttling can liquefy them.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format:** Answer **ONE** of the following.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the following Tds equation from the first principles:
$$T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P dP \quad (7 \text{ marks})$$

**(b)** A fluid undergoes a throttling process from 1.5 MPa, 300°C to 200 kPa. Assuming the fluid behaves as an ideal gas with $C_p = 1.05$ kJ/kg·K and $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K, determine:
   (i) The exit temperature
   (ii) The change in specific entropy
   (iii) The Joule-Thomson coefficient
   (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to B-A(a)

**Starting Point:** Combined first and second law:
$$T\,ds = dh - v\,dP \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 1:** Express enthalpy as a function of $T$ and $P$:
$$dh = \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial P}\right)_T dP \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 2:** Use the definition of specific heat at constant pressure:
$$C_p = \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right)_P \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Maxwell relation from $G = h - Ts$:
$$\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial P}\right)_T = v - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P \quad \text{...(4)}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute (3) and (4) into (2):
$$dh = C_p\,dT + \left[v - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P\right]dP \quad \text{...(5)}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute (5) into (1):
$$T\,ds = C_p\,dT + \left[v - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P\right]dP - v\,dP$$

**Step 6:** The $v\,dP$ terms cancel:
$$\boxed{T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P dP}$$

---

#### Solution to B-A(b)

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 1.5$ MPa $= 1500$ kPa, $T_1 = 300 + 273 = 573$ K
- $P_2 = 200$ kPa
- $C_p = 1.05$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K

**(i) Exit temperature (Throttling → $h_1 = h_2$):**

For an ideal gas, $h = C_p T$. Therefore $C_p T_1 = C_p T_2$:
$$T_2 = T_1 = 573 \text{ K} = 300°C$$

**(ii) Change in specific entropy:**

Using the Tds equation: $ds = C_p \frac{dT}{T} - R \frac{dP}{P}$

Integrating from 1 to 2:
$$\Delta s = C_p \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

$$\Delta s = 1.05 \times \ln(1) - 0.287 \times \ln\left(\frac{200}{1500}\right)$$

$$\Delta s = 0 - 0.287 \times \ln(0.1333) = -0.287 \times (-2.015)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta s = +0.578 \text{ kJ/kg·K}}$$

**Note:** Positive entropy change confirms the **irreversibility** of throttling.

**(iii) Joule-Thomson coefficient:**

For an ideal gas:
$$\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_p}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P - v\right] = \frac{1}{C_p}\left[\frac{RT}{P} - \frac{RT}{P}\right] = 0$$

$$\boxed{\mu_{JT} = 0}$$

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the **Clapeyron equation** for a phase change process. Discuss the **Clausius-Clapeyron equation** as a special case. (7 marks)

**(b)** The saturation pressure of water at 100°C is 101.325 kPa and at 110°C is 143.38 kPa. Calculate the latent heat of vaporization using:
   (i) The Clapeyron equation
   (ii) The Clausius-Clapeyron equation
   Take $v_f = 0.001$ m³/kg and $v_g = 1.673$ m³/kg at 100°C. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to B-B(a)

**Derivation of Clapeyron Equation:**

At the phase boundary between two phases (e.g., liquid and vapor), equilibrium requires:
$$g_f = g_g \quad \text{...(1)}$$

Differentiating along the saturation line:
$$dg_f = dg_g \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Using the fundamental relation $dg = v\,dP - s\,dT$:
$$v_f\,dP - s_f\,dT = v_g\,dP - s_g\,dT \quad \text{...(3)}$$

Rearranging:
$$(v_g - v_f)\,dP = (s_g - s_f)\,dT \quad \text{...(4)}$$

Defining $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f$ and $s_{fg} = s_g - s_f$:
$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{s_{fg}}{v_{fg}} \quad \text{...(5)}$$

Since $s_{fg} = h_{fg}/T$ during phase change at constant T:
$$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}}$$

**Clausius-Clapeyron as Special Case:**

When vapor behaves as an ideal gas and $v_f \ll v_g$:
- $v_{fg} \approx v_g = RT/P$
- $h_{fg} \approx$ constant

Substituting and integrating gives:
$$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

This is the **Clausius-Clapeyron equation**, used widely for estimating saturation pressures at temperatures not tabulated in steam tables.

---

#### Solution to B-B(b)

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 100 + 273 = 373$ K, $P_1 = 101.325$ kPa
- $T_2 = 110 + 273 = 383$ K, $P_2 = 143.38$ kPa
- $v_f = 0.001$ m³/kg, $v_g = 1.673$ m³/kg

**(i) Clapeyron Equation:**

The slope of saturation line:
$$\frac{dP}{dT} \approx \frac{P_2 - P_1}{T_2 - T_1} = \frac{143.38 - 101.325}{383 - 373} = \frac{42.055}{10} = 4.2055 \text{ kPa/K}$$

At $T \approx 378$ K (average), $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f = 1.673 - 0.001 = 1.672$ m³/kg

From Clapeyron equation:
$$h_{fg} = T \cdot v_{fg} \cdot \frac{dP}{dT}$$

$$h_{fg} = 378 \times 1.672 \times 4.2055 = 2658.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(ii) Clausius-Clapeyron Equation:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{143.38}{101.325}\right) = -\frac{h_{fg}}{0.4615}\left(\frac{1}{383} - \frac{1}{373}\right)$$

$$0.3471 = -\frac{h_{fg}}{0.4615}\left(0.002611 - 0.002681\right)$$

$$0.3471 = -\frac{h_{fg}}{0.4615} \times (-6.838 \times 10^{-5})$$

$$0.3471 = \frac{h_{fg} \times 6.838 \times 10^{-5}}{0.4615}$$

$$h_{fg} = \frac{0.3471 \times 0.4615}{6.838 \times 10^{-5}} = 2342.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison:** The actual $h_{fg}$ at 100°C is **2257 kJ/kg**. The Clapeyron value (2658) is higher because of linear approximation; the Clausius-Clapeyron (2342) is closer to the actual due to its exponential nature.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert temperature to Kelvin** in Clausius-Clapeyron calculations — this is the #1 reason students lose 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $C_p$ and $C_v$** in Tds equations — remember: $C_p$ goes with $T, P$ variables; $C_v$ goes with $T, v$ variables.
> 3. **Not stating the assumptions** in Clausius-Clapeyron derivation (ideal gas, $v_f$ negligible, $h_{fg}$ constant) — examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 4. **Missing the sign convention** in entropy change calculation — throttling always shows $\Delta s > 0$ (irreversible); never show $\Delta s < 0$.
> 5. **Conflating Clapeyron (exact) with Clausius-Clapeyron (approximate)** — the KTU paper is explicit about which is asked.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Tds Equations, Clapeyron & Throttling**

### 🔑 Key Equations to Memorize
- ✅ **Tds Eq 3:** $T\,ds = C_v\,dT + T(\partial P/\partial T)_v\,dv$
- ✅ **Tds Eq 4:** $T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T(\partial v/\partial T)_P\,dP$
- ✅ **Clapeyron:** $dP/dT = h_{fg}/(T \cdot v_{fg})$
- ✅ **Clausius-Clapeyron:** $\ln(P_2/P_1) = -(h_{fg}/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$
- ✅ **Throttling:** $h_1 = h_2$
- ✅ **Joule-Thomson:** $\mu_{JT} = (1/C_p)[T(\partial v/\partial T)_P - v]$

### 🎯 Critical Conceptual Points
- The **four Tds equations** are derived from the **combined 1st and 2nd laws** with **Maxwell's relations**
- Tds eq 3 is for **closed systems** ($T, v$); Tds eq 4 is for **flow systems** ($T, P$)
- The **Clapeyron equation is exact**; the **Clausius-Clapeyron is approximate** (ideal gas assumptions)
- Throttling is **isenthalpic** ($h = $ const) and **irreversible** ($\Delta s_{gen} > 0$)
- $\mu_{JT} = 0$ for **ideal gases**; cooling effect is a **real gas** phenomenon
- **Inversion temperature** separates cooling and heating regions on the T-P diagram

### ⚠️ Common Exam Traps
- ❌ Using $C_v$ instead of $C_p$ in flow process entropy calculations
- ❌ Forgetting Kelvin conversion in $1/T$ terms
- ❌ Treating Clapeyron and Clausius-Clapeyron as interchangeable
- ❌ Assuming temperature always drops in throttling (depends on inversion temperature)
- ❌ Missing the irreversibility argument (entropy must increase in adiabatic throttling)

### 📐 Units to Watch
- $h_{fg}$: **kJ/kg** (specific) or **kJ/kmol** (molar — for Clausius-Clapeyron, use $R = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K)
- $v_{fg}$: **m³/kg**
- $\mu_{JT}$: **K/kPa** or **K/bar** (sign matters!)
- $s_{fg}$: **kJ/kg·K**

### 🏭 Real-World Applications
- **Linde-Hampson process** → air liquefaction
- **Domestic refrigeration** → expansion valve cooling
- **Steam power plants** → reheat and regenerative throttling
- **Natural gas pipelines** → pressure reduction stations
- **Cryogenics** → helium, hydrogen liquefaction

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Joule Thomson Coefficient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Joule Thomson Coefficient

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Joule-Thomson Coefficient (μ_JT)** is a fundamental thermodynamic property of a real fluid that quantifies the rate of change of its temperature with respect to pressure during a **constant-enthalpy (isenthalpic) process**. Mathematically, it is rigorously defined as the partial derivative of temperature with respect to pressure while holding the enthalpy of the system constant:

$$\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H$$

The physical units of the Joule-Thomson coefficient are **Kelvin per Pascal (K/Pa)** in SI units, though it is commonly expressed in **K/bar** or **°C/atm** in engineering practice for readability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** The Joule-Thomson effect is strictly a property of **real (non-ideal) gases**. For a perfect (ideal) gas, the Joule-Thomson coefficient is **exactly zero**, meaning no temperature change occurs during an isenthalpic pressure drop.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

### The "Deodorant Can" Analogy
Hold a pressurized deodorant or compressed air spray can in your hand and release the valve for several seconds. Notice that the can becomes noticeably **cold** to the touch. This is the Joule-Thomson effect in action!

When the highly compressed gas inside the can (at very high pressure) is forced to escape through the valve into the lower-pressure atmosphere, the gas **expands suddenly and adiabatically**. The molecules, which were crowded together under high pressure, must now do work to push back the surrounding atmosphere as they spread out. This work is drawn from the **internal kinetic energy** of the gas molecules, which manifests macroscopically as a **drop in temperature**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why doesn't this happen with an ideal gas?** In an ideal gas, molecules experience **no intermolecular forces**. The internal energy depends only on temperature, not on volume. Therefore, when an ideal gas expands, it loses no internal energy to overcoming intermolecular attractions, and its temperature remains constant. Real gases, however, have attractive forces between molecules, and these must be "paid for" using kinetic energy during expansion, leading to cooling.

### Where You Encounter This in Engineering
- **Liquefaction of Gases** (Hydrogen, Helium, Nitrogen, Liquefied Natural Gas — LNG plants)
- **HVAC and Refrigeration Systems** (expansion valves)
- **Natural Gas Pipeline Operations** (pressure let-down stations)
- **Cryogenic Engineering** (production of low-temperature baths)
- **Petrochemical Throttling Processes** (Joule-Thomson valves in refining)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Joule-Thomson coefficient is the cornerstone of all **gas liquefaction cycles** (Linde-Hampson, Claude cycle) and is tested as a high-weightage topic in Module 3 of PCAUT403.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework

The Joule-Thomson experiment (originally conducted by Joule and William Thomson, later Lord Kelvin, in 1852) involves forcing a real gas through a **porous plug** or **throttling valve** under **adiabatic conditions**. The defining thermodynamic condition of this process is that the specific enthalpy remains **constant** (isenthalpic process):

$$H_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + g z_1 = H_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + g z_2$$

For a **well-insulated throttling device** with negligible kinetic energy and potential energy changes:

$$H_1 = H_2 \quad \text{(or } dh = 0 \text{)}$$

### The Three Regimes of the Joule-Thomson Effect

The sign of μ_JT determines what happens to the gas when it expands through a throttle:

| Sign of μ_JT | Temperature Change | Regime Name | Physical Cause |
|--------------|-------------------|-------------|----------------|
| **Positive (+)** | **Cooling** (T decreases as P decreases) | **Cooling Region** | Intermolecular **attractive** forces dominate; gas loses KE to overcome attractions during expansion |
