| **Zero (0)** | **No temperature change** | **Inversion State** | Attractive and repulsive forces exactly balance |
| **Negative (−)** | **Heating** (T increases as P decreases) | **Heating Region** | Intermolecular **repulsive** forces dominate; gas heats up because of molecular repulsion at high density |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Inversion Temperature (T_inv):** This is the temperature at which the Joule-Thomson coefficient changes sign (i.e., μ_JT = 0). For a van der Waals gas, T_inv = 2a/(Rb), which equals **twice the Boyle temperature**. Above T_inv, the gas will **heat up** on throttling; below T_inv, the gas will **cool down** on throttling. This is the fundamental principle exploited in gas liquefaction.

## 2.2 General Derivation Strategy

Starting from the fundamental relation for enthalpy:

$$dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$$

For the throttling process (dH = 0):

$$0 = T\,dS + V\,dP \implies dS = -\frac{V}{T}\,dP$$

Now, expressing dS in terms of dT and dP using the chain rule:

$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

Equating the two expressions and using the Maxwell relation $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P dT - \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP = -\frac{V}{T}\,dP$$

Since $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{C_P}{T}$, we obtain the **fundamental Joule-Thomson equation** after dividing both sides by dP at constant H:

$$\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H = \frac{1}{C_P}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right]$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning / Use |
|---|----------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | $\mu_{JT} = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H$ | **Definition** — rate of temperature change per unit pressure drop at constant enthalpy |
| 2 | $\mu_{JT} = \dfrac{1}{C_P}\left[T\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right]$ | **General expression** in terms of P, V, T state properties (valid for all fluids) |
| 3 | $\mu_{JT}^{ideal} = 0$ | Joule-Thomson coefficient of an **ideal gas** is identically zero |
| 4 | $\mu_{JT}^{vdW} \approx \dfrac{1}{C_P}\left(\dfrac{2a}{RT} - b\right)$ | **Approximate expression for van der Waals gas** (most important for KTU problems) |
| 5 | $T_{inv} = \dfrac{2a}{Rb}$ | **Inversion temperature** for van der Waals gas (set μ_JT = 0) |
| 6 | $T_{B} = \dfrac{a}{Rb}$ | **Boyle temperature** — T_inv = 2 × T_B |
| 7 | $C_P = T\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | Heat capacity at constant pressure (used in deriving μ_JT) |
| 8 | $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | **Maxwell relation** required in μ_JT derivation |
| 9 | $H_1 = H_2$ | **Energy balance** for adiabatic throttling (isenthalpic) |
| 10 | $\mu_{JT} = -\dfrac{1}{C_P}\left(\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T \cdot \dfrac{1}{(\partial H/\partial T)_P \cdot \text{ratio}}$ (cyclic rule variant) | Alternative form using cyclic rule for problem solving |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of μ_JT:** The sign and magnitude of the Joule-Thomson coefficient directly determine the **feasibility of gas liquefaction** by throttling. For industrial LNG, N₂, O₂, and H₂ production, the feed gas must always be **pre-cooled below its inversion temperature** before throttling can produce further cooling. The cascade of cooling stages in a Linde-Hampson liquefier is essentially a cascade of Joule-Thomson expansions operating in the **positive μ_JT regime**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: General Expression for μ_JT in Terms of State Variables

### Step 1 — Start from the Fundamental Thermodynamic Identity
The combined first and second laws of thermodynamics give the exact differential for enthalpy:

$$dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Isenthalpic Constraint
For a throttling process, the enthalpy is constant, so $dH = 0$:

$$0 = T\,dS + V\,dP$$

Solving for the entropy change during throttling:

$$dS = -\frac{V}{T}\,dP$$

### Step 3 — Express dS Using Two Independent Variables (T and P)
Since entropy is a state function, we can write dS in terms of dT and dP:

$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the Heat Capacity Relation
By definition, the heat capacity at constant pressure is:

$$C_P = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P \implies \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{C_P}{T}$$

### Step 5 — Apply a Maxwell Relation
The Maxwell relation derived from the fundamental relation $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$ gives:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

### Step 6 — Combine All the Pieces
Substituting Steps 4 and 5 into Step 3:

$$dS = \frac{C_P}{T}\,dT - \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$$

### Step 7 — Equate with the Isenthalpic Expression
Setting Step 6 equal to Step 2 (both expressions must hold for the same dS):

$$\frac{C_P}{T}\,dT - \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP = -\frac{V}{T}\,dP$$

### Step 8 — Isolate dT in Terms of dP
Rearranging the dT terms on one side:

$$\frac{C_P}{T}\,dT = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP - \frac{V}{T}\,dP$$

Multiplying both sides by T/C_P:

$$dT = \frac{T}{C_P}\left[\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - \frac{V}{T}\right] dP$$

### Step 9 — Formally Define μ_JT
At constant H, the ratio dT/dP is precisely the Joule-Thomson coefficient:

$$\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H = \frac{1}{C_P}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right]$$

> **Conclusion of Derivation I:** This is the **most general expression** for the Joule-Thomson coefficient and is valid for **any pure substance** (real gas, liquid, or even mixtures), provided C_P, V, and (∂V/∂T)_P are evaluated from an appropriate equation of state or experimental PVT data.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: μ_JT for an Ideal Gas (μ_JT = 0)

### Step 1 — Write the Ideal Gas Equation of State

$$PV = RT \quad \text{(per mole, basis: 1 kmol)}$$

### Step 2 — Compute (∂V/∂T)_P
Differentiating the ideal gas law with respect to T at constant P:

$$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{R}{P}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into the General μ_JT Formula

$$\mu_{JT}^{ideal} = \frac{1}{C_P}\left[T \cdot \frac{R}{P} - V\right] = \frac{1}{C_P}\left[\frac{RT}{P} - V\right]$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Ideal Gas Law Again
Since $RT/P = V$ for the ideal gas:

$$\mu_{JT}^{ideal} = \frac{1}{C_P}\left[V - V\right] = 0$$

> **Conclusion of Derivation II:** The Joule-Thomson coefficient of a perfect gas is **identically zero at all temperatures and pressures**. This confirms that the Joule-Thomson effect is purely a consequence of **non-ideal gas behavior** arising from intermolecular forces and finite molecular volume.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: μ_JT for a van der Waals Gas (Approximate Form)

### Step 1 — Write the van der Waals Equation of State

$$\left(P + \frac{a}{V^2}\right)(V - b) = RT$$

### Step 2 — Solve Explicitly for Pressure P

$$P = \frac{RT}{V - b} - \frac{a}{V^2}$$

### Step 3 — Assume Gases are Near-Ideal at Low Pressures
Under conditions of moderate pressure where V is large (V ≫ b), we can approximate $V - b \approx V$, so:

$$P \approx \frac{RT}{V} - \frac{a}{V^2} + \frac{RT b}{V^2} \cdot \frac{1}{...}$$

More rigorously, expanding to first order in (a, b) corrections:

$$V \approx \frac{RT}{P} + b - \frac{a}{RT}$$

### Step 4 — Differentiate V with Respect to T at Constant P

$$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P \approx \frac{R}{P} + \frac{a}{RT^2}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute into the General μ_JT Expression

$$\mu_{JT}^{vdW} \approx \frac{1}{C_P}\left[T\left(\frac{R}{P} + \frac{a}{RT^2}\right) - \left(\frac{RT}{P} + b - \frac{a}{RT}\right)\right]$$

### Step 6 — Simplify Term by Term

$$\mu_{JT}^{vdW} \approx \frac{1}{C_P}\left[\frac{RT}{P} + \frac{a}{RT} - \frac{RT}{P} - b + \frac{a}{RT}\right]$$

The RT/P terms cancel out:

$$\mu_{JT}^{vdW} \approx \frac{1}{C_P}\left[\frac{2a}{RT} - b\right]$$

### Step 7 — Final Result (vdW Approximate)

$$\boxed{\mu_{JT}^{vdW} \approx \frac{1}{C_P}\left(\frac{2a}{RT} - b\right)}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:**
> - The term **2a/(RT)** represents the **cooling effect** due to intermolecular attractive forces (dominant at low T).
> - The term **b** represents the **heating effect** due to finite molecular size and repulsive forces (dominant at high T and high density).
> - As T → ∞, μ_JT → −b/C_P < 0 (pure heating regime).
> - As T → 0, μ_JT → +∞ (extreme cooling regime).

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV: Inversion Temperature for a van der Waals Gas

### Step 1 — Set μ_JT = 0
The inversion temperature is defined as the temperature at which the Joule-Thomson coefficient is zero:

$$\frac{1}{C_P}\left(\frac{2a}{RT_{inv}} - b\right) = 0$$

### Step 2 — Solve for T_inv
Since C_P ≠ 0, the term in parentheses must vanish:

$$\frac{2a}{RT_{inv}} - b = 0 \implies \frac{2a}{RT_{inv}} = b$$

$$\boxed{T_{inv} = \frac{2a}{Rb}}$$

### Step 3 — Express in Terms of Critical Properties
Using the van der Waals critical constants:

$$T_c = \frac{8a}{27Rb}, \quad P_c = \frac{a}{27b^2}$$

We can also write the inversion temperature as:

$$T_{inv} = \frac{2a}{Rb} = \frac{27}{4} T_c \approx 6.75\,T_c$$

> **Conclusion of Derivation IV:** The inversion temperature of a van der Waals gas is **2.7 times its critical temperature** in this approximation. For engineering estimates using the Peng-Robinson or Redlich-Kwong equations of state, the inversion curve lies slightly lower, typically around **2 × T_c** for many real gases.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A real gas obeys the van der Waals equation with a = 0.138 Pa·m⁶/mol² and b = 3.18 × 10⁻⁵ m³/mol. The molar heat capacity at constant pressure is C_P = 29.1 J/(mol·K) and R = 8.314 J/(mol·K). Calculate the Joule-Thomson coefficient at T = 300 K. Is the gas in the cooling or heating regime?

**Solution:**

$$\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_P}\left(\frac{2a}{RT} - b\right)$$

Compute the first term:

$$\frac{2a}{RT} = \frac{2 \times 0.138}{8.314 \times 300} = \frac{0.276}{2494.2} = 1.107 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3/\text{mol}$$

Compute the bracketed term:

$$\frac{2a}{RT} - b = 1.107 \times 10^{-4} - 3.18 \times 10^{-5} = 7.89 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^3/\text{mol}$$

Therefore:

$$\mu_{JT} = \frac{7.89 \times 10^{-5}}{29.1} = 2.71 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{K/Pa}$$

> Since **μ_JT > 0**, the gas is in the **cooling regime** at 300 K. A throttling expansion will produce a temperature drop.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic of the Joule-Thomson Throttling Process

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the **physical architecture** of a Joule-Thomson expansion, including the upstream (high-pressure) and downstream (low-pressure) reservoirs separated by a porous plug or throttling valve.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Upstream Reservoir P1 High T1] --> B[Gas Forced Through Porous Plug Adiabatic Throttling Device]
    B --> C[Downstream Reservoir P2 Low T2]
    B --> D[Energy Balance: H1 equals H2]
    D --> E{Is mu_JT positive?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Cooling Regime: T2 less than T1]
    E -- No --> G[Heating Regime: T2 greater than T1]
    E -- Zero --> H[Inversion Point: T2 equals T1]
    F --> I[Application: Gas Liquefaction LNG LH2 LOX]
    G --> J[Application: High Pressure Gas Heating]
    H --> K[At T equals T_inv equals 2a over Rb]
    I --> L[Used in Linde Hampson and Claude Cycles]
```

## 4.2 The Three Regimes of μ_JT vs Temperature

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Temperature T]) --> Check{T compared with T_inv}
    Check -- T less than T_inv --> Cool[Positive mu_JT: Cooling on Expansion]
    Check -- T equals T_inv --> Zero[Zero mu_JT: No Temperature Change]
    Check -- T greater than T_inv --> Heat[Negative mu_JT: Heating on Expansion]
    Cool --> CoolApp[Gas Liquefaction Cryogenics Refrigeration]
    Zero --> ZeroApp[Inversion Curve Boundary]
    Heat --> HeatApp[Preheating Required Before Liquefaction]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Derivation Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Define mu_JT as partial T over partial P at constant H] --> B0[Apply Combined First and Second Law: dH equals T dS plus V dP]
    B0 --> C0[Apply Isenthalpic Constraint: dH equals 0]
    C0 --> D0[Express dS in Terms of dT and dP]
    D0 --> E0[Use Maxwell Relation and C_P Definition]
    E0 --> F0[Obtain General Formula]
    F0 --> G0[Substitute Ideal Gas Equation PV equals RT]
    G0 --> H0[Result: mu_JT equals 0 for Ideal Gas]
    F0 --> I0[Substitute van der Waals Equation of State]
    I0 --> J0[Apply Low Pressure Approximation]
    J0 --> K0[Result: mu_JT approx equals 2a over RT minus b over C_P]
    K0 --> L0[Set mu_JT equals 0 to Find T_inv]
    L0 --> M0[Result: T_inv equals 2a over Rb]
```

## 4.4 Sign Convention Summary Table (Rendered as Architecture Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RegA[Cooling Region: mu_JT positive]
        A1[T less than T_inv] --> A2[Attractive Forces Dominate]
        A2 --> A3[Gas Cools on Expansion]
    end
    subgraph RegB[Inversion: mu_JT zero]
        B1[T equals T_inv] --> B2[Attraction equals Repulsion]
        B2 --> B3[No Net Temperature Change]
    end
    subgraph RegC[Heating Region: mu_JT negative]
        C1[T greater than T_inv] --> C2[Repulsive Forces Dominate]
        C2 --> C3[Gas Heats on Expansion]
    end
    RegA --> RegB
    RegB --> RegC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The block diagrams map the **causal logic** of the Joule-Thomson phenomenon — from the initial high-pressure state, through the isenthalpic throttling, to the final temperature change. The three regimes (Cooling, Inversion, Heating) correspond to three distinct physical regimes of intermolecular interaction.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the Joule-Thomson coefficient. Under what conditions is it positive, negative, and zero?**

**Model Answer:**

The Joule-Thomson coefficient is defined as the rate of change of temperature with respect to pressure during a constant-enthalpy (throttling) process:

$$\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H$$

- **Positive (+):** When the gas temperature is **below the inversion temperature (T < T_inv)**, the gas **cools upon expansion**. This happens because intermolecular **attractive forces** dominate and work done against these attractions reduces the kinetic energy (temperature) of the gas.
- **Negative (−):** When the gas temperature is **above the inversion temperature (T > T_inv)**, the gas **heats up on expansion**. This occurs because intermolecular **repulsive forces** dominate at high density.
- **Zero (0):** At the **inversion temperature T_inv = 2a/(Rb)**, the attractive and repulsive contributions exactly cancel, and there is **no net temperature change** on throttling.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State the mathematical expression for the Joule-Thomson coefficient of a van der Waals gas and hence derive the expression for its inversion temperature.**

**Model Answer:**

The approximate Joule-Thomson coefficient for a van der Waals gas is:

$$\mu_{JT} \approx \frac{1}{C_P}\left(\frac{2a}{RT} - b\right)$$

where **a** is the attraction parameter and **b** is the co-volume parameter.

The inversion temperature is found by setting μ_JT = 0:

$$\frac{1}{C_P}\left(\frac{2a}{RT_{inv}} - b\right) = 0$$

Since C_P ≠ 0, we require:

$$\frac{2a}{RT_{inv}} = b \implies T_{inv} = \frac{2a}{Rb}$$

This is **twice the Boyle temperature** T_B = a/(Rb) and approximately **6.75 times the critical temperature** T_c for a van der Waals fluid.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(a)** Derive the general expression for the Joule-Thomson coefficient in terms of measurable state properties (P, V, T, C_P) starting from the first and second laws of thermodynamics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a van der Waals gas with a = 0.365 Pa·m⁶/mol², b = 4.28 × 10⁻⁵ m³/mol, and C_P = 35 J/(mol·K), calculate the inversion temperature and the Joule-Thomson coefficient at T = 400 K. Comment on whether the gas is in the cooling or heating regime. Use R = 8.314 J/(mol·K). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1 — Fundamental relation for enthalpy (Combined 1st and 2nd laws):**

$$dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$$

**Step 2 — Isenthalpic constraint for throttling:** dH = 0:

$$0 = T\,dS + V\,dP \implies dS = -\frac{V}{T}\,dP$$

**Step 3 — Express dS using T and P as independent variables:**

$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

**Step 4 — Apply C_P definition and Maxwell relation:**

$$C_P = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P \quad \text{and} \quad \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

So:

$$dS = \frac{C_P}{T}\,dT - \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$$

**Step 5 — Equate the two expressions for dS:**

$$\frac{C_P}{T}\,dT - \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP = -\frac{V}{T}\,dP$$

**Step 6 — Solve for dT/dP at constant H:**

$$\frac{C_P}{T}\,dT = \left[\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - \frac{V}{T}\right] dP$$

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H = \frac{T}{C_P}\left[\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - \frac{V}{T}\right]$$

**Step 7 — Final simplified expression:**

$$\boxed{\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H = \frac{1}{C_P}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right]}$$

This is the **general, equation-of-state-independent** expression for the Joule-Thomson coefficient.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1 — Calculate inversion temperature:**

$$T_{inv} = \frac{2a}{Rb} = \frac{2 \times 0.365}{8.314 \times 4.28 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$T_{inv} = \frac{0.730}{3.558 \times 10^{-4}} = 2051.7\,\text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate μ_JT at T = 400 K:**

First compute 2a/(RT):

$$\frac{2a}{RT} = \frac{2 \times 0.365}{8.314 \times 400} = \frac{0.730}{3325.6} = 2.195 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3/\text{mol}$$

Then compute the bracket:

$$\frac{2a}{RT} - b = 2.195 \times 10^{-4} - 4.28 \times 10^{-5} = 1.767 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3/\text{mol}$$

Now compute μ_JT:

$$\mu_{JT} = \frac{1.767 \times 10^{-4}}{35} = 5.05 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{K/Pa} = 5.05 \times 10^{-1}\,\text{K/bar}$$

**Step 3 — Comment on the regime:**

Since T = 400 K < T_inv = 2051.7 K and **μ_JT > 0**, the gas is in the **cooling regime**. Throttling at 400 K will produce a temperature drop. This is consistent with the physical picture: at temperatures well below the inversion temperature, the attractive intermolecular forces dominate the energy balance during expansion.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the physical significance of the Joule-Thomson coefficient. Discuss the three possible signs of μ_JT and their corresponding physical interpretations in terms of intermolecular forces. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A real gas at 10 bar and 300 K is throttled to 1 bar in an isenthalpic process. The Joule-Thomson coefficient at the average conditions is μ_JT = 0.25 K/bar. Estimate the temperature of the gas after throttling. Comment on the regime and suggest one engineering application. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Physical Significance of the Joule-Thomson Coefficient:**

The Joule-Thomson coefficient is a quantitative measure of how the temperature of a real fluid responds to a change in pressure under **adiabatic, isenthalpic conditions**. It captures the **deviation from ideal-gas behavior** and is the controlling parameter for all throttling-based cooling processes. In engineering thermodynamics, μ_JT is the property that enables gas liquefaction, refrigeration cycles, and cryogenic cooling.

**The Three Signs and Their Physical Meaning:**

- **μ_JT > 0 (Cooling Regime):** When the gas temperature is below the inversion temperature, the **intermolecular attractive forces** are the dominant effect. As the gas expands through the throttle, molecules move further apart against these attractions, doing work that comes at the expense of their **kinetic energy**. The macroscopic manifestation is a **drop in temperature**. This is the regime exploited in all gas liquefaction processes.

- **μ_JT = 0 (Inversion State):** At the inversion temperature, the cooling effect of attractive forces is **exactly balanced** by the heating effect of repulsive forces. The net temperature change is zero, regardless of the pressure drop. Mathematically, this defines the inversion curve on a P-T diagram.

- **μ_JT < 0 (Heating Regime):** When the gas temperature is above the inversion temperature, the **repulsive intermolecular forces** (caused by the finite volume of molecules, parameter b) dominate. As the gas expands, the molecules move out of the repulsive range and the **potential energy decreases**. Conservation of total energy forces the **kinetic energy to increase**, manifesting as a temperature rise. This is why a hot gas cylinder releases **hot air** rather than cool air.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1 — Identify the data:**

- P_1 = 10 bar, T_1 = 300 K
- P_2 = 1 bar
- μ_JT = 0.25 K/bar (assumed constant at average conditions)

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of μ_JT (constant value assumption):**

$$\Delta T \approx \mu_{JT} \cdot \Delta P$$

$$\Delta T = 0.25 \times (1 - 10) = 0.25 \times (-9) = -2.25\,\text{K}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate final temperature:**

$$T_2 = T_1 + \Delta T = 300 - 2.25 = 297.75\,\text{K}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on the regime:**

Since **μ_JT > 0**, the gas is in the **cooling regime**. The gas cools by 2.25 K during throttling.

**Step 5 — Engineering application:**

This Joule-Thomson cooling effect is the working principle of **Linde-Hampson liquefaction cycles** used to produce **liquid nitrogen, liquid oxygen, and liquefied natural gas (LNG)** in cryogenic plants. The throttling valve is the key expansion device in such systems, and the cooling produced per pass is precisely the Joule-Thomson effect calculated above.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Sign convention confusion:** Students frequently forget that dP in a throttling process is **negative** (pressure drops). The resulting temperature change ΔT = μ_JT × ΔP must carry the sign correctly. A positive μ_JT with a negative ΔP yields a **negative ΔT** (cooling). Writing "ΔT = +2.25 K" instead of "ΔT = −2.25 K" costs full marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to verify the isenthalpic assumption:** The entire derivation hinges on **dH = 0**. Students often write the Joule-Thomson coefficient as a general partial derivative without explicitly justifying the isenthalpic condition. Examiners specifically look for the **H subscript** and the energy balance H_1 = H_2.
>
> 3. **Mixing up Boyle temperature with inversion temperature:** T_B = a/(Rb) and T_inv = 2a/(Rb) are different. Examiners will deduct marks if T_B is given when T_inv is asked.
>
> 4. **Unit conversion errors in numerical problems:** μ_JT is often given in K/bar but the formula derivation gives K/Pa. Always convert: 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa. Failure to convert units is the most common numerical mistake in KTU answer sheets.
>
> 5. **Ideal gas trap:** Always check whether the problem specifies a real or ideal gas. For an ideal gas, μ_JT = 0 trivially, and any further calculation is unnecessary — a common trick question in KTU 2024 scheme exams.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme:**

- ✅ **Definition:** μ_JT = (∂T/∂P)_H — change in T per unit change in P at constant enthalpy.
- ✅ **Process:** Throttling is an **isenthalpic, adiabatic** steady-flow process with negligible KE/PE changes.
- ✅ **Ideal Gas Result:** μ_JT = 0 identically — Joule-Thomson effect does NOT occur in ideal gases.
- ✅ **General Formula:** μ_JT = (1/C_P) × [T(∂V/∂T)_P − V].
- ✅ **van der Waals Approximation:** μ_JT ≈ (1/C_P) × [2a/(RT) − b].
- ✅ **Inversion Temperature:** T_inv = 2a/(Rb) = 2 × T_Boyle ≈ 6.75 × T_c (vdW).
- ✅ **Three Regimes:** μ_JT > 0 → cooling (T < T_inv), μ_JT = 0 → inversion, μ_JT < 0 → heating (T > T_inv).
- ✅ **Physical Cause of Cooling:** Work done against intermolecular attractive forces reduces KE.
- ✅ **Physical Cause of Heating:** Decrease in repulsive PE on expansion increases KE.
- ✅ **Engineering Applications:** Gas liquefaction (Linde-Hampson, Claude), LNG production, cryogenics, refrigeration, throttling valves in pipelines.
- ✅ **Liquefaction Prerequisite:** Gas **must be cooled below T_inv** before throttling; otherwise pre-cooling is required (e.g., nitrogen pre-cooling stage for hydrogen liquefaction).
- ✅ **Maxwell Relation Used:** (∂S/∂P)_T = −(∂V/∂T)_P — must be cited explicitly in derivations.
- ✅ **Units:** μ_JT in K/Pa (SI) or K/bar (practical). Always state units in numerical answers.
- ✅ **Sign of ΔT:** ΔT = μ_JT × ΔP; for throttling ΔP < 0, so positive μ_JT gives ΔT < 0 (cooling).
- ✅ **Critical Distinction:** Throttling is **irreversible** but **isenthalpic**; it is NOT isentropic.
- ✅ **Exam Tip:** Always begin a derivation question by stating the assumption "dH = 0 for an adiabatic throttling process" — this is what examiners look for first.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inversion Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT TRANSFER > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Inversion Curve # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Inversion Curve (Definition as per KTU 2024 Syllabus)** > The **inversion curve** is the locus of all state points in the **T–P** (or **T–V**) plane of a real gas at which the **Joule–Thomson coefficient** becomes exactly zero: > $$\mu_{J.T.} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{H} = 0$$ > It physically demarcates the boundary between the **cooling region** (where expansion of a real gas lowers its temperature) and the **heating region** (where expansion raises its temperature) during an **isenthalpic (throttling) process**. Every real gas, when expanded through a throttle (porous plug, expansion valve, or capillary), experiences a temperature change. Whether the gas cools or heats depends on its **state** (T and P) **before** throttling. The inversion curve is the geographical boundary on the thermodynamic map that decides this outcome. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling vs Freezing" Line on a Map > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Intuition** > Imagine you are standing on a *hiking map* of a mountain. The mountain has two distinct terrains: a **glacier field** where the air is so cold that breathing makes frost, and a **desert slope** where the air is hot. Somewhere on the map, there is a **contour line** — neither freezing nor burning, but exactly neutral. That contour line is the *Inversion Curve*. > - If you are standing on the **cold side** (high T, low P for most gases) and you "step down" the mountain (expand the gas), you get **cooler** → gas cools on throttling. > - If you are on the **hot side** (low T, high P), stepping down makes you **warmer** → gas heats on throttling. > - If you happen to be standing **exactly on the contour line**, your temperature doesn't change at all when you expand → Joule–Thomson coefficient is zero. This boundary is what engineers exploit to liquefy gases (Linde process) and what they must avoid when designing nozzles and expanders in cryogenic plants. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!TIP] > **Key Engineering Numbers to Remember** > - **Joule–Thomson coefficient** symbol: $\mu_{J.T.}$ — units: **K / Pa** (or K/bar in practice) > - **Maximum inversion temperature** of **N₂**: $\approx 621\,\text{K}$ > - **Maximum inversion temperature** of **H₂**: $\approx 202\,\text{K}$ (notably low!) > - **Maximum inversion temperature** of **He**: $\approx 24\,\text{K}$ (this is why helium must be pre-cooled below 24 K before any throttling can cool it further — a famous cryogenics fact) > - Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$ ## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering The inversion curve is the **theoretical backbone** of all gas-liquefaction technology: - **Hydrogen and Helium liquefaction** would be impossible without first cooling the gas below its maximum inversion temperature using auxiliary refrigerants. - **Natural gas processing (LNG plants)** uses Joule–Thomson expansion at the correct operating point — chosen precisely with the help of the inversion curve. - **Refrigeration and air-conditioning** systems (which use expansion valves) implicitly operate either on the cooling or heating side depending on refrigerant choice. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Joule–Thomson Coefficient — Foundation The starting point is the **Joule–Thomson coefficient**, defined for an **isenthalpic** (constant-H) process: $$\mu_{J.T.} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{H}$$ Using the **cyclic relation** and the **Maxwell relation** derived from the fundamental equation $dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$, the coefficient can be rewritten as: $$\mu_{J.T.} = \frac{1}{C_{p}}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P} - V\right]$$ This single equation is the **gateway to understanding** the inversion curve. The sign of $\mu_{J.T.}$ — and therefore whether throttling cools or heats the gas — is governed entirely by the bracket term. ## 2.2 The Inversion Condition The inversion curve is defined by setting $\mu_{J.T.} = 0$: $$T_{i}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P} - V = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T_{i}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = V$$ This transcendental equation, when applied to any equation of state, defines the inversion curve for that gas. ## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of the Inversion Phenomenon - **Step 1 — Ideal Gas Check**: For an ideal gas, $PV = RT$, hence $(\partial V/\partial T)_P = R/P$. Substituting: $$T\cdot\frac{R}{P} - V = \frac{RT}{P} - V = 0$$ always holds. **The ideal gas therefore has no inversion curve** — it never changes temperature on throttling. This is consistent with the kinetic theory (no intermolecular forces). - **Step 2 — Real Gas Effect**: Real gases have intermolecular **attractive forces** (parameter **a**) and **finite molecular volume** (parameter **b**) in the van der Waals model. These two effects oppose each other in their influence on $\mu_{J.T.}$: - **Attractive forces dominate** → gas *cools* on expansion (decompression pulls molecules apart against cohesion → internal energy drops → T drops). - **Repulsive forces (finite size) dominate** → gas *heats* on expansion (work done against molecular volume → T rises). - **Step 3 — The Balance Point**: The inversion curve is precisely the locus where these two competing effects **exactly cancel** each other. Above the upper branch → attractive dominates (cooling region). Below the lower branch → repulsive dominates (heating region). - **Step 4 — Maximum Inversion Temperature**: As pressure → 0, the repulsive effect vanishes (molecules are too far apart to feel size), so the **inversion temperature asymptotically approaches a maximum value** $T_{i,\max} = 2a/(Rb)$ for van der Waals gases. - **Step 5 — Practical Takeaway**: A gas can only be cooled by throttling from a state whose T is **below** $T_{i,\max}$. For hydrogen and helium, this requires **pre-cooling**. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Equation | Units | Notes | |---|----------|----------|-------|-------| | 1 | Joule–Thomson coefficient | $\mu_{J.T.} = (\partial T/\partial P)_H$ | K/Pa | Definition | | 2 | $\mu_{J.T.}$ in terms of measurable properties | $\mu_{J.T.} = \dfrac{1}{C_p}\left[T\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P} - V\right]$ | K/Pa | Key derived form | | 3 | Inversion condition | $T_i(\partial V/\partial T)_P = V$ | — | Sets $\mu_{J.T.}=0$ | | 4 | Ideal gas: $\mu_{J.T.}$ | $0$ | K/Pa | No temperature change on throttling | | 5 | vdW: $(\partial v/\partial T)_P$ | $R/\left[P - \dfrac{a}{v^2} + \dfrac{2ab}{v^3}\right]$ | m³/(mol·K) | Differentiate vdW EOS at constant P | | 6 | vdW inversion curve (T–v form) | $T_i = \dfrac{2a}{Rb}\left(1 - \dfrac{b}{v}\right)^{2}$ | K | **Locus equation** | | 7 | vdW max inversion temperature | $T_{i,\max} = \dfrac{2a}{Rb}$ (as $v \to \infty$) | K | Upper asymptote | | 8 | vdW inversion curve (T–P form, implicit) | $T_i = \dfrac{2a}{Rb} + \dfrac{3aP}{2R^2}\cdot(\text{...})$ → see derivation | K | Implicit — solves for $T_i(P)$ | | 9 | Reduced inversion curve (Principle of Corresponding States) | $T_{i,r} = \dfrac{27}{4}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{3P_r v_r}\right)^{2}$ | — | Universal curve (same for **all** vdW-type gases!) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Reality Check — Corresponding States** > The **reduced inversion curve** (item 9) is *universal* for all gases that follow the van der Waals equation. This is a powerful result: the lab measurement on nitrogen can be used to predict hydrogen's inversion curve by simply rescaling with critical constants. This is one of the most exam-favorite corollaries in KTU Module 3. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility - **Cryogenics**: Selection of operating T and P in Linde / Claude / Hampson liquefiers. - **Petrochemical**: Joule–Thomson expansion at well-head for natural gas pressure letdown. - **Safety**: Avoiding undesirable heating during throttling of flammable gases (fire hazard). - **HVAC**: Choice of refrigerant — most refrigerants (R134a, R410A, NH₃) have inversion temperatures **far above room temperature**, ensuring reliable cooling across all operating conditions. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: General Form of the Joule–Thomson Coefficient ### Starting Identity We begin with enthalpy as a function of T and P: $H = H(T, P)$. Differentiating: $$dH = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_{P}dT + \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_{T}dP$$ ### Application of Constant-H (Isenthalpic) Constraint For a throttling process, $dH = 0$: $$0 = C_{p}\,dT + \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_{T}dP$$ Rearranging for the Joule–Thomson coefficient: $$\mu_{J.T.} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{H} = -\frac{1}{C_{p}}\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_{T}$$ ### Conversion to Measurable Variables The thermodynamic identity gives: $$\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = V - T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P}$$ ### Final Result $$\boxed{\;\mu_{J.T.} = \frac{1}{C_{p}}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P} - V\right]\;}$$ > **Conversion Logic**: This formula is the **master key** for all subsequent derivations. Every inversion curve derivation reduces to evaluating $T(\partial V/\partial T)_P$ for a given equation of state and equating it to $V$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Inversion Curve for a van der Waals Gas (T–v Form) ### Step 1: van der Waals Equation $$\left(P + \frac{a}{v^{2}}\right)(v - b) = RT$$ where $v$ is molar specific volume, $a$ is the attraction parameter, $b$ is the co-volume. ### Step 2: Differentiate Implicitly at Constant P Take $(\partial/\partial T)_P$ of both sides. Note that $P$ is held constant, so its derivative is zero: $$\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left[\left(P + \frac{a}{v^{2}}\right)(v - b)\right]_{P} = R$$ Expanding using the product rule: $$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{P}(v - b) + \left(P + \frac{a}{v^{2}}\right)\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_{P} + (v - b)\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{a}{v^{2}}\right)_{P} = R$$ ### Step 3: Simplify Each Term - Term 1: $(\partial P/\partial T)_P = 0$ (since $P$ is the constant) - Term 2: $(P + a/v^2)(\partial v/\partial T)_P$ - Term 3: $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial T}\!\left(\dfrac{a}{v^{2}}\right)_{P} = -\dfrac{2a}{v^{3}}\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_{P}$ So the equation becomes: $$\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_{P}\left[P + \frac{a}{v^{2}} - \frac{2a(v - b)}{v^{3}}\right] = R$$ ### Step 4: Combine the Bracket $$\frac{a}{v^{2}} - \frac{2a(v - b)}{v^{3}} = \frac{av - 2a(v - b)}{v^{3}} = \frac{av - 2av + 2ab}{v^{3}} = \frac{2ab - av}{v^{3}} = \frac{a(2b - v)}{v^{3}}$$ So the bracket is $P + \dfrac{a(2b - v)}{v^{3}} = P - \dfrac{a}{v^{2}} + \dfrac{2ab}{v^{3}}$. ### Step 5: Solve for $(\partial v/\partial T)_P$ $$\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = \frac{R}{P - \dfrac{a}{v^{2}} + \dfrac{2ab}{v^{3}}}$$ ### Step 6: Apply the Inversion Condition Set $T_i \cdot (\partial v/\partial T)_P = v$: $$T_{i}\cdot\frac{R}{P - \dfrac{a}{v^{2}} + \dfrac{2ab}{v^{3}}} = v$$ $$T_{i}\,R = v\!\left(P - \frac{a}{v^{2}} + \frac{2ab}{v^{3}}\right) = vP - \frac{a}{v} + \frac{2ab}{v^{2}}$$ ### Step 7: Substitute vdW Pressure From vdW EOS: $P = \dfrac{RT}{v - b} - \dfrac{a}{v^{2}}$ Plugging in: $$T_{i}R = v\!\left[\frac{RT_{i}}{v - b} - \frac{a}{v^{2}}\right] - \frac{a}{v} + \frac{2ab}{v^{2}}$$ $$T_{i}R = \frac{vRT_{i}}{v - b} - \frac{a}{v} - \frac{a}{v} + \frac{2ab}{v^{2}}$$ $$T_{i}R = \frac{vRT_{i}}{v - b} - \frac{2a}{v} + \frac{2ab}{v^{2}}$$ ### Step 8: Isolate $T_i$ Move the $vRT_i/(v-b)$ term to the left: $$T_{i}R - \frac{vRT_{i}}{v - b} = -\frac{2a}{v} + \frac{2ab}{v^{2}}$$ $$T_{i}R\!\left[1 - \frac{v}{v - b}\right] = -\frac{2a}{v} + \frac{2ab}{v^{2}}$$ $$T_{i}R\!\left[\frac{v - b - v}{v - b}\right] = -\frac{2a(v - b)}{v^{2}}$$ $$T_{i}R\!\left[\frac{-b}{v - b}\right] = -\frac{2a(v - b)}{v^{2}}$$ ### Step 9: Final Simplification Cancel the negative signs on both sides: $$\frac{T_{i} R b}{v - b} = \frac{2a(v - b)}{v^{2}}$$ $$T_{i} = \frac{2a(v - b)^{2}}{R b\,v^{2}}$$ $$\boxed{\;T_{i} = \frac{2a}{Rb}\left(1 - \frac{b}{v}\right)^{2}\;}$$ > **Conversion Logic**: This is the **inversion curve equation in the T–v plane** for a van der Waals gas. It is a parabolic-shaped curve in the v–T plane, opening towards higher $v$ values, with maximum at $T_{i,\max} = 2a/(Rb)$ as $v \to \infty$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Inversion Temperature Take the limit $v \to \infty$ (i.e., $P \to 0$) in the inversion curve equation: $$\lim_{v \to \infty} T_i = \lim_{v \to \infty}\frac{2a}{Rb}\left(1 - \frac{b}{v}\right)^{2} = \frac{2a}{Rb}\cdot 1^{2}$$ $$\boxed{\;T_{i,\max} = \frac{2a}{Rb}\;}$$ > **Conversion Logic**: This is the **highest temperature** at which the gas can still be cooled by throttling. Above this temperature, **no throttling operation can cool the gas** — expansion always heats it. For hydrogen, $T_{i,\max} \approx 202\,\text{K}$, requiring pre-cooling with liquid nitrogen before any JT expansion can liquefy it. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Reduced Inversion Curve (Principle of Corresponding States) ### Step 1: Introduce Reduced Variables Define $T_r = T/T_c$, $P_r = P/P_c$, $v_r = v/v_c$, where subscript $c$ denotes critical-point values. For a van der Waals gas, the critical constants are: $$T_c = \frac{8a}{27Rb}, \quad P_c = \frac{a}{27b^{2}}, \quad v_c = 3b$$ ### Step 2: Eliminate $a$, $b$, $R$ From critical-constant expressions: $$\frac{a}{R} = \frac{27\,T_c b}{8}, \quad b = \frac{v_c}{3}$$ Substitute into the inversion equation $T_i = (2a/Rb)(1 - b/v)^2$: $$T_i = \frac{2}{b}\cdot\frac{27T_c b}{8}\left(1 - \frac{b}{v}\right)^{2} = \frac{27 T_c}{4}\left(1 - \frac{b}{v}\right)^{2}$$ ### Step 3: Express $b/v$ in Reduced Form $$\frac{b}{v} = \frac{v_c/3}{v_c v_r} = \frac{1}{3 v_r}$$ ### Step 4: Final Reduced Form $$\frac{T_i}{T_c} = \frac{27}{4}\left(1 - \frac{1}{3 v_r}\right)^{2}$$ $$\boxed{\;T_{i,r} = \frac{27}{4}\left(1 - \frac{1}{3 v_r}\right)^{2}\;}$$ > **Conversion Logic**: This is the **Universal Reduced Inversion Curve** — it is *the same function* for all gases that obey the van der Waals EOS. This is the Principle of Corresponding States applied to the inversion phenomenon, and it explains why engineers use a single dimensionless chart for all real-gas throttling calculations. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Behaviour of Real Gases Across the Inversion Curve | Region of T–P Plane | Dominant Molecular Force | Sign of $\mu_{J.T.}$ | Throttling Effect | Practical Consequence | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Inside the curve (cooling region)** — high P, low-to-moderate T | Attractive (cohesion) | $\mu_{J.T.} > 0$ | Gas **cools** | Used in **gas liquefaction** | | **Outside the curve (heating region)** — low P, high T | Repulsive (size) | $\mu_{J.T.} < 0$ | Gas **heats** | Used in **throttling heaters** (rare); dangerous in cryogenics | | **On the inversion curve** | Attractive = Repulsive | $\mu_{J.T.} = 0$ | **No temperature change** | Design constraint for isenthalpic nozzles | | **Above $T_{i,\max}$ (any P)** | Repulsive dominates always | $\mu_{J.T.} < 0$ | Gas always **heats** | Cannot cool by throttling — must pre-cool | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Inversion Curve Topology (T–P Domain) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["T-P Diagram of a Real Gas"]:::plane B["Upper Inversion Branch
T_i = 2a/Rb x 1 - b/v squared"]:::branch C["Lower Inversion Branch
Asymptotic descent to critical point"]:::branch D["Cooling Region mu_JT greater than 0
Throttling produces cooling"]:::cool E["Heating Region mu_JT less than 0
Throttling produces heating"]:::heat F["Critical Point C: T_c, P_c, v_c"]:::critical G["Max Inversion Temperature
T_i_max equals 2a over Rb"]:::max A --> B A --> C B --> G C --> F B --> D C --> D B --> E C --> E D --> E classDef plane fill:#e8f0ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef branch fill:#fff4d6,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cool fill:#d6f0ff,stroke:#1e6fa3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef heat fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef critical fill:#e8ffe8,stroke:#2c7a2c,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef max fill:#fff0d6,stroke:#d2691e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > **Reading the diagram**: The inversion curve is a **closed dome** in the T–P plane (or an inverted parabola in the T–V plane). The **interior of the dome** is the cooling region; the **exterior** is the heating region. The **apex of the dome** is at $T_{i,\max}$. ## 4.2 Functional Architecture of Throttling Decision Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD S0["Real Gas Stream Enters Throttle
at State T_1, P_1"]:::input S1{"Is T_1 less than T_i_max?"}:::decision S2["Proceed to Throttling
Cooling or Heating Determined by P_1"]:::process S3["Pre-cooling Required
Use Auxiliary Refrigerant first"]:::process2 S4{"Inside Inversion Curve?
Check P_1 vs P_inv at T_1"}:::decision2 S5["Gas Cools After Throttle
T_2 less than T_1"]:::outputcool S6["Gas Heats After Throttle
T_2 greater than T_1"]:::outputheat S7["No Temperature Change
T_2 equals T_1"]:::outputneutral S0 --> S1 S1 -- YES --> S2 S1 -- NO --> S3 S2 --> S4 S4 -- YES, INTERIOR --> S5 S4 -- YES, EXTERIOR --> S6 S4 -- ON CURVE --> S7 S3 -.Pre-cooled gas.-> S2 classDef input fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef process fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#4d3a5e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef process2 fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c55a11,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outputcool fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2c7a2c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outputheat fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outputneutral fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#1e6fa3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Enthalpy–Temperature Diagram (Isenthalpic Curves) ```mermaid flowchart LR HAxis["H axis - Enthalpy"]:::axis TAxis["T axis - Temperature"]:::axis subgraph Isenthalps ["Isenthalpic Curves on T-H Plane"] direction TB I1["Isenthalp H1: dome-shaped, peak at inversion point T_i"]:::iso I2["Isenthalp H2: smaller dome, peak shifted to lower T_i"]:::iso I3["Isenthalp H3: very flat - nearly ideal gas behaviour"]:::iso end I1 --- TAxis I2 --- TAxis I3 --- TAxis P1["Inversion Locus: where all isenthalp peaks touch"]:::locus I1 --> P1 I2 --> P1 I3 --> P1 classDef axis fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#666,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef iso fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef locus fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` > **Interpretation**: Each curve is a constant-H (isenthalpic) trajectory. The **peak of each dome** lies on the inversion curve. To the left of the peak → cooling branch. To the right → heating branch. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** ### Q1. Define the inversion curve. What is its significance in gas liquefaction processes? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** The **inversion curve** is the locus of all thermodynamic states $(T, P)$ of a real gas at which the **Joule–Thomson coefficient** becomes zero: $\mu_{J.T.} = (\partial T/\partial P)_H = 0$. It separates the **cooling region** (interior of the inversion dome) from the **heating region** (exterior). - **(1 mark)** The curve has an upper bound — the **maximum inversion temperature** $T_{i,\max} = 2a/(Rb)$ for van der Waals gases. Above this temperature, the gas always heats on throttling. - **(1 mark)** **Significance**: Only when a gas is in a state **below** $T_{i,\max}$ **and** within the inversion curve can it be cooled by throttling. This is the operating principle of the **Linde process** for liquefying air, nitrogen, natural gas, etc. For gases like H₂ and He with low $T_{i,\max}$, **pre-cooling** with an auxiliary refrigerant is mandatory. --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** ### Q2. State the Joule–Thomson coefficient. For an ideal gas, show that $\mu_{J.T.} = 0$. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** The Joule–Thomson coefficient is defined as the rate of change of temperature with respect to pressure at **constant enthalpy** (isenthalpic process): $$\mu_{J.T.} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{H}$$ - **(1 mark)** The standard derived form is: $$\mu_{J.T.} = \frac{1}{C_p}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P} - V\right]$$ - **(1 mark)** For an ideal gas, $PV = RT$, so $(\partial V/\partial T)_P = R/P$. Substituting: $$\mu_{J.T.} = \frac{1}{C_p}\left[T\cdot\frac{R}{P} - V\right] = \frac{1}{C_p}\left[\frac{RT}{P} - \frac{RT}{P}\right] = 0$$ Hence an **ideal gas** experiences no temperature change on throttling, regardless of initial state. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Internal Choice Pattern]** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — July 2024]** > **(a)** Derive an expression for the **inversion curve** of a **van der Waals gas** in the **T–v** plane. From your result, obtain the **maximum inversion temperature**.    **[7 marks]** > **(b)** For a van der Waals gas with $a = 0.1387\,\text{Pa·m}^6/\text{mol}^2$ and $b = 3.91 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^3/\text{mol}$, compute (i) the maximum inversion temperature, and (ii) the inversion temperature at $v = 2 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3/\text{mol}$. Take $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$.    **[7 marks]** ### Model Solution — Question A **Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]:** **Step 1 [1 mark]**: van der Waals equation: $$\left(P + \frac{a}{v^2}\right)(v - b) = RT$$ **Step 2 [2 marks]**: Differentiate with respect to T at constant P. Since $P$ is held constant, $(\partial P/\partial T)_P = 0$: $$\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_{P}\left[P - \frac{a}{v^2} + \frac{2ab}{v^3}\right] = R$$ $$\Rightarrow \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = \frac{R}{P - \dfrac{a}{v^2} + \dfrac{2ab}{v^3}}$$ **Step 3 [1 mark]**: The general Joule–Thomson coefficient: $$\mu_{J.T.} = \frac{1}{C_p}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_{P} - v\right]$$ **Step 4 [1 mark]**: Set $\mu_{J.T.} = 0$: $$T_i\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = v \;\Rightarrow\; T_i = \frac{v}{(\partial v/\partial T)_P}$$ Substituting $(\partial v/\partial T)_P$ from Step 2 and the vdW pressure $P = RT/(v-b) - a/v^2$: $$T_iR = vP - \frac{a}{v} + \frac{2ab}{v^2} = \frac{vRT_i}{v-b} - \frac{2a}{v} + \frac{2ab}{v^2}$$ **Step 5 [1 mark]**: Algebraic simplification (as in Section 3.2, Steps 7–9): $$T_i = \frac{2a}{Rb}\left(1 - \frac{b}{v}\right)^{2}$$ **Step 6 [1 mark]**: Maximum inversion temperature — take $v \to \infty$: $$T_{i,\max} = \frac{2a}{Rb}$$ --- **Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 marks]:** **Step 1 [1 mark]**: Compute $T_{i,\max}$: $$T_{i,\max} = \frac{2 \times 0.1387}{8.314 \times 3.91 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.2774}{3.251 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 853.2\,\text{K}$$ **Step 2 [2 marks]**: Compute the dimensionless ratio $b/v$: $$\frac{b}{v} = \frac{3.91 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.1955$$ **Step 3 [2 marks]**: Compute $T_i$ at $v = 2 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3/\text{mol}$: $$T_i = T_{i,\max}\left(1 - \frac{b}{v}\right)^{2} = 853.2 \times (1 - 0.1955)^{2}$$ $$T_i = 853.2 \times (0.8045)^{2} = 853.2 \times 0.6472 \approx 552.2\,\text{K}$$ **Step 4 [1 mark]**: Physical interpretation: At $v = 2 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3/\text{mol}$, the inversion temperature has dropped to ~552 K from its maximum of ~853 K — confirming that **inversion temperature decreases as the gas becomes more dense** (smaller v). **Step 5 [1 mark]**: Final summary answer: - (i) $T_{i,\max} \approx 853.2\,\text{K}$ - (ii) $T_i(v = 2 \times 10^{-4}) \approx 552.2\,\text{K}$ --- ### **Question B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > **(a)** Define the **Joule–Thomson coefficient**. Using a fundamental relation, derive the expression $\mu_{J.T.} = (1/C_p)[T(\partial V/\partial T)_P - V]$.    **[7 marks]** > **(b)** A real gas obeys the van der Waals equation. Show that the gas undergoes (i) **cooling** during throttling if the initial state is inside the inversion curve, and (ii) **heating** if outside. Use a T–P sketch in your answer.    **[7 marks]** ### Model Solution — Question B **Part (a) [7 marks]:** **Step 1 [1 mark]**: **Definition**: $\mu_{J.T.} = (\partial T/\partial P)_H$ — temperature change with pressure during a throttling (isenthalpic) process. **Step 2 [2 marks]**: Express $H = H(T, P)$: $$dH = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_{P}dT + \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_{T}dP = C_p\,dT + \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_{T}dP$$ For an isenthalpic process, $dH = 0$: $$C_p\,dT = -\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_{T}dP \;\Rightarrow\; \mu_{J.T.} = -\frac{1}{C_p}\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_{T}$$ **Step 3 [2 marks]**: Use the identity $(\partial H/\partial P)_T = V - T(\partial V/\partial T)_P$ (derived from $dH = TdS + VdP$ and the Maxwell relation $(\partial S/\partial P)_T = -(\partial V/\partial T)_P$): $$\mu_{J.T.} = -\frac{1}{C_p}\left[V - T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P}\right] = \frac{1}{C_p}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P} - V\right]$$ **Step 4 [2 marks]**: Physical interpretation: - $T(\partial V/\partial T)_P > V$ → $\mu_{J.T.} > 0$ → gas cools - $T(\partial V/\partial T)_P < V$ → $\mu_{J.T.} < 0$ → gas heats - $T(\partial V/\partial T)_P = V$ → $\mu_{J.T.} = 0$ → on inversion curve --- **Part (b) [7 marks]:** **Step 1 [2 marks]**: For a vdW gas, the inversion curve equation is: $$T_i = \frac{2a}{Rb}\left(1 - \frac{b}{v}\right)^{2}$$ **Step 2 [2 marks]**: The vdW equation can be written as $P = RT/(v-b) - a/v^2$. For a given T, the inversion pressure is: $$P_i(T) = \frac{RT}{v_i - b} - \frac{a}{v_i^{2}}$$ where $v_i$ is the root of the inversion equation for that T. **Step 3 [2 marks]**: Sketch (described in words since ASCII is impractical): > **T–P Sketch Description:** > - Vertical axis: T (temperature). Horizontal axis: P (pressure). > - Draw a **closed dome-shaped curve** with its **apex at the top** at $(P = 0, T = T_{i,\max})$. > - The dome **opens downward** and meets the critical isotherm at the critical point. > - **Inside the dome** → label "COOLING REGION ($\mu_{J.T.} > 0$)". > - **Outside the dome** → label "HEATING REGION ($\mu_{J.T.} < 0$)". > - **On the curve** → label "INVERSION CURVE ($\mu_{J.T.} = 0$)". > - Show a **throttling arrow** from a high-P state to a low-P state: > - If the start point is inside the dome, T decreases (downward arrow) → cooling. > - If the start point is outside the dome, T increases (upward arrow) → heating. **Step 4 [1 mark]**: Conclude: For any throttling process, the temperature change is determined by which side of the inversion curve the initial state lies on. The inversion curve therefore acts as a **decision boundary** for throttling-induced cooling. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in KTU Valuation** > 1. **Forgetting $C_p$ in the formula** — A common 1-mark deduction. The full expression is $\mu_{J.T.} = (1/C_p)[\ldots]$; many students write only the bracket and lose a mark. > 2. **Sign confusion** — Always state explicitly whether the gas **cools** ($\mu > 0$) or **heats** ($\mu < 0$). A correct calculation with a wrong qualitative conclusion loses 1–2 marks. > 3. **Ideal-gas trap** — Students often claim "inversion curve exists for ideal gas" — **this is WRONG**; ideal gas has $\mu_{J.T.} \equiv 0$ everywhere and therefore **no inversion curve**. > 4. **Confusing inversion curve with saturation curve** — These are two entirely different things. The inversion curve is about *throttling behavior*; the saturation curve is about *phase equilibrium*. Examiners frequently set up questions that mix them. > 5. **Maximum inversion temperature units** — It is a **temperature**, in **K**, NOT a pressure. A shocking number of students write $P_{i,\max} = 2a/(Rb)$ which is dimensionally wrong. > 6. **Not sketching the dome** — KTU 2024 scheme Part B questions explicitly ask for a **T–P diagram** in many past papers; a textual answer without a sketch is penalized 1–2 marks. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Inversion Curve** > - ⭐ **Definition**: Locus of points where $\mu_{J.T.} = (\partial T/\partial P)_H = 0$. > - ⭐ **Master formula**: $\mu_{J.T.} = (1/C_p)[T(\partial V/\partial T)_P - V]$. > - ⭐ **Inversion condition**: $T_i(\partial V/\partial T)_P = V$. > - ⭐ **vdW inversion curve**: $T_i = (2a/Rb)(1 - b/v)^2$. > - ⭐ **Maximum inversion temperature**: $T_{i,\max} = 2a/(Rb)$, attained as $v \to \infty$ (i.e., $P \to 0$). > - ⭐ **Universal reduced curve**: $T_{i,r} = (27/4)(1 - 1/(3v_r))^2$ — same for all vdW gases (Principle of Corresponding States). > - ⭐ **Inside the dome** = cooling; **Outside** = heating; **On the curve** = no change. > - ⭐ **Ideal gas**: No inversion curve ($\mu_{J.T.} \equiv 0$). > - ⭐ **H₂ and He**: Have very low $T_{i,\max}$ (≈ 202 K and ≈ 24 K respectively) — must be pre-cooled. > - ⭐ **Engineering applications**: Linde process (gas liquefaction), LNG processing, refrigeration, cryogenics. > - ⭐ **Always remember**: The competition between **attractive** (parameter $a$, causes cooling) and **repulsive** (parameter $b$, causes heating) forces decides which side of the inversion curve the gas lies on.

topics.title: Overview of Conduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Overview of Conduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Conduction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Conduction** is the transfer of thermal energy (heat) through a solid or fluid medium **without any bulk macroscopic motion of the medium itself**, occurring due to the temperature gradient between two regions. Heat is transferred from the higher temperature region to the lower temperature region through molecular interactions, lattice vibrations, and free electron diffusion.

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, conduction is the **mode of heat transfer in which thermal energy is transported by direct molecular contact and energy carrier movement (electrons, phonons) within a substance, governed by Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction.**

The heat flux vector $\vec{q}$ is proportional to the negative temperature gradient:

$$\vec{q} = -k \nabla T$$

where the negative sign indicates heat flows from hot to cold (down the temperature gradient).

---

## 1.2 Physical Mechanism — Why Does Conduction Happen?

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Micro-Scopic Mechanisms Drive Conduction:**

1. **Molecular Collisions (in Fluids):** Molecules in hotter regions possess higher kinetic energy. Through continuous random collisions with adjacent molecules, they transfer kinetic energy to cooler regions.
2. **Lattice Vibrations — Phonons (in Non-Metallic Solids):** Atoms in a crystalline solid vibrate about their mean position. The vibrational energy (quantized as *phonons*) propagates through the lattice.
3. **Free Electron Diffusion (in Metals):** The dominant mechanism in pure metals. Free electrons act as the energy carrier, similar to how they conduct electricity. This explains the **Wiedemann-Franz Law** — good electrical conductors are also good thermal conductors.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: Heat as "Energy Water" Flowing Through a Pipe**

Imagine a copper rod held over a candle flame. The end touching the flame gets hot first, and gradually the other end becomes warm. This is **conduction** in action.

Think of heat energy as **invisible water**:
- The **temperature difference** ($\Delta T$) is like a **pressure difference** (driving force).
- The **thermal conductivity** ($k$) is like the **pipe diameter / ease of flow** — a copper pipe (high $k$) lets water flow easily, while a plastic pipe (low $k$) resists flow.
- The **cross-sectional area** ($A$) is like the **cross-section of the pipe**.
- The **length** ($L$) is like the **pipe length** (longer pipe = more resistance).

So Fourier's Law essentially says: **Heat Flow Rate = Driving Force ÷ Resistance**, just like Ohm's Law in electrical circuits!

$$\text{Heat Flow} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Electric Current}$$

$$\frac{Q}{t} = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{th}} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad I = \frac{V}{R_{elec}}$$

---

## 1.4 Classification of Heat Transfer Modes (Context)

> [!NOTE]
> Heat transfer in nature occurs through **three fundamental modes**:

| Mode | Carrier | Medium | Governing Law |
|------|---------|--------|----------------|
| **Conduction** | Molecules, electrons, phonons | Solids (dominant), stagnant fluids | **Fourier's Law** |
| **Convection** | Bulk fluid motion | Liquids & gases | **Newton's Law of Cooling** |
| **Radiation** | Electromagnetic waves | Vacuum or transparent media | **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** |

This module focuses exclusively on **Conduction** — the foundation of all thermal analysis.

---

## 1.5 Key Physical Quantities (Highlighted)

- **Thermal Conductivity ($k$)** — measured in **W/(m·K)**
- **Heat Flux ($q''$)** — measured in **W/m²**
- **Heat Transfer Rate ($Q$ or $\dot{Q}$)** — measured in **W (Watts)**
- **Thermal Diffusivity ($\alpha$)** — measured in **m²/s**
- **Temperature Gradient ($dT/dx$)** — measured in **K/m**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction (One-Dimensional Form)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fourier's Law (1807)** — the cornerstone of conduction analysis:
>
> *The instantaneous rate of heat flow through a homogeneous material is directly proportional to the negative temperature gradient and the cross-sectional area perpendicular to the direction of heat flow.*

### Mathematical Form (1D Steady State)

For a plane wall of thickness $L$ with surface temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$Q = -k A \frac{dT}{dx}$$

### Breakdown of Every Symbol

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard SI Unit |
|--------|---------|-------------------|
| $Q$ | Heat transfer rate (in time) | W (Watts) |
| $k$ | Thermal conductivity of the material | W/(m·K) |
| $A$ | Cross-sectional area normal to heat flow | m² |
| $dT/dx$ | Temperature gradient in the direction of flow | K/m |
| Negative sign | Indicates flow from high to low temperature | — |

### Generalised Vector Form (3D)

$$\vec{q} = -k \nabla T = -k \left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\hat{i} + \frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\hat{j} + \frac{\partial T}{\partial z}\hat{k} \right)$$

---

## 2.2 Thermal Conductivity ($k$) — The Defining Material Property

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Thermal conductivity is the measure of a material's ability to conduct heat. It is numerically equal to the **heat flow rate per unit area per unit temperature gradient**.

### Physical Interpretation
A material with $k = 400 \text{ W/(m·K)}$ (like copper) conducts heat **~20,000 times better** than a material with $k = 0.02 \text{ W/(m·K)}$ (like polystyrene foam).

### Typical Values at Room Temperature (300 K)

| Material | $k$ in W/(m·K) | Category |
|----------|----------------|----------|
| **Silver** | **429** | Excellent Conductor (Metal) |
| **Copper (pure)** | **401** | Excellent Conductor (Metal) |
| **Aluminium** | **237** | Good Conductor (Metal) |
| **Steel (Carbon)** | **54** | Moderate Conductor (Alloy) |
| **Concrete** | **0.8 – 1.4** | Poor Conductor (Building Mat.) |
| **Glass** | **0.78** | Poor Conductor |
| **Water (liquid)** | **0.613** | Poor Conductor (Fluid) |
| **Brick** | **0.72** | Poor Conductor |
| **Wood (Oak)** | **0.17** | Insulator |
| **Fibreglass** | **0.04** | Good Insulator |
| **Air (still)** | **0.026** | Excellent Insulator |
| **Polyurethane Foam** | **0.024** | Excellent Insulator |
| **Vacuum** | **0** (no medium) | Perfect Insulator |

---

## 2.3 Thermal Diffusivity ($\alpha$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Diffusivity** measures a material's **ability to undergo temperature change** when heat is applied. It combines thermal conductivity, density, and specific heat into one property.

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho \, c_p}$$

### Physical Meaning
- **High $\alpha$** → Material responds **quickly** to thermal changes (e.g., metals: copper $\alpha \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-4}$ m²/s)
- **Low $\alpha$** → Material **resists** temperature change and transmits slowly (e.g., plastic: $\alpha \approx 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s)

This is the governing property for **transient (unsteady) conduction**.

---

## 2.4 The Heat Transfer Coefficient ($h$)

For **convection** (a context-setting concept):

$$Q = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$$

where $h$ is the **convective heat transfer coefficient** in W/(m²·K). It bridges the gap between conduction analysis and convection by providing the boundary condition at solid-fluid interfaces.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — PIN THESE FOR EXAMS**

| # | Formula | Description | Governing Condition |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $Q = -kA\dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | Fourier's Law (1D) | Steady state, 1D |
| 2 | $Q = \dfrac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$ | Heat through plane wall | Steady state, constant $k$ |
| 3 | $q'' = \dfrac{Q}{A} = -k\dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | Heat flux | Per unit area |
| 4 | $R_{th} = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ | Thermal resistance (conduction) | Plane wall |
| 5 | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho c_p}$ | Thermal diffusivity | Material property |
| 6 | $\vec{q} = -k\nabla T$ | Fourier's Law (Vector) | 3D anisotropic |
| 7 | $Q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$ | Convection (Newton's Law) | Boundary condition |
| 8 | $\nabla^2 T + \dfrac{\dot{q}_{gen}}{k} = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t}$ | Heat Diffusion Equation | General 3D transient with source |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Engineers Study Conduction:**
> - **Building Design:** Insulation thickness calculation (energy conservation)
> - **Electronics Cooling:** Heat sink design, PCB thermal management
> - **Aerospace:** Re-entry vehicle heat shields (transient conduction)
> - **Nuclear Reactors:** Fuel rod temperature distribution
> - **Manufacturing:** Heat treatment of metals, casting solidification
> - **Refrigeration:** Wall thickness for insulating cold storage
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Flow Through a Plane Wall (Fourier's Law Integrated Form)

> [!EXCALIDRAW]
> **Problem Setup:** A plane wall of thickness $L$ has its two faces maintained at uniform temperatures $T_1$ (left, hot) and $T_2$ (right, cold), with $T_1 > T_2$. The material has constant thermal conductivity $k$ and cross-sectional area $A$. No internal heat generation. Find the steady-state heat transfer rate and temperature distribution.

---

### Step 1: State Fourier's Law (1D form)

Starting from the basic differential form of Fourier's Law for one-dimensional heat flow in the $x$-direction:

$$Q = -k A \frac{dT}{dx}$$

**Logic:** The negative sign ensures that heat flows in the direction of *decreasing* temperature, consistent with the Second Law of Thermodynamics.

---

### Step 2: Identify the Coordinate System & Boundary Conditions

Let the wall extend from $x = 0$ to $x = L$, with:
- Boundary Condition 1 (BC-1): At $x = 0$, $T = T_1$
- Boundary Condition 2 (BC-2): At $x = L$, $T = T_2$

---

### Step 3: Separate Variables

Rewrite Fourier's Law by separating the temperature and spatial variables:

$$Q \, dx = -k A \, dT$$

**Logic:** Under steady-state conditions with constant $k$ and $A$, the heat rate $Q$ is independent of $x$ (otherwise energy would accumulate). So $Q$ can be treated as a constant while integrating.

---

### Step 4: Integrate Both Sides

Integrate the left side from $x = 0$ to $x = L$ and the right side from $T = T_1$ to $T = T_2$:

$$\int_0^L Q \, dx = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} -k A \, dT$$

Since $Q$, $k$, and $A$ are constants:

$$Q \int_0^L dx = -k A \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

$$Q [x]_0^L = -kA [T]_{T_1}^{T_2}$$

---

### Step 5: Evaluate the Limits

$$Q \cdot L = -kA (T_2 - T_1)$$

$$Q \cdot L = kA (T_1 - T_2)$$

**Logic:** The negative sign disappears because $(T_1 - T_2) > 0$ (since $T_1 > T_2$), ensuring $Q$ is a positive quantity representing heat flowing in the positive $x$-direction.

---

### Step 6: Final Steady-State Heat Transfer Rate

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{kA (T_1 - T_2)}{L}}$$

This is the **famous integrated form of Fourier's Law for a plane wall**.

---

### Step 7: Derive the Temperature Distribution $T(x)$

Rearranging Step 5 and integrating with a general variable:

$$dT = -\frac{Q}{kA} dx$$

Integrating from $T_1$ at $x=0$ to a generic $T$ at position $x$:

$$\int_{T_1}^{T} dT = -\frac{Q}{kA} \int_0^x dx$$

$$T - T_1 = -\frac{Q}{kA} x$$

Substituting $Q = \dfrac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$:

$$T - T_1 = -\frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{kA \cdot L} x = -\frac{(T_1 - T_2)}{L} x$$

$$\boxed{T(x) = T_1 - \frac{(T_1 - T_2)}{L} x}$$

**Logic:** The temperature distribution is **linear** in $x$ for a plane wall with constant $k$ and no heat generation — a foundational result used everywhere in heat transfer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The General Heat Diffusion Equation (3D, Transient)

> [!EXCALIDRAW]
> **Problem Setup:** Derive the most general equation governing conduction in a homogeneous, isotropic medium with possible internal heat generation $\dot{q}_{gen}$ (W/m³).

---

### Step 1: Control Volume Approach

Consider a small cubical element of dimensions $dx \times dy \times dz$ within a solid. Apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics (Energy Conservation)**:

$$E_{in} - E_{out} + E_{generated} = E_{stored}$$

### Step 2: Net Heat Conduction In (in x-direction)

Heat entering the left face at $x$: $q_x \, dy \, dz$

Heat leaving the right face at $x+dx$: $q_{x+dx} \, dy \, dz$

By Taylor expansion:
$$q_{x+dx} = q_x + \frac{\partial q_x}{\partial x} dx$$

Net rate of heat addition in $x$-direction:

$$q_x \, dy\,dz - \left(q_x + \frac{\partial q_x}{\partial x} dx\right) dy\, dz = -\frac{\partial q_x}{\partial x} dx\, dy\, dz$$

### Step 3: Summing All Three Directions

The total net rate of heat entering the element by conduction is:

$$-\left(\frac{\partial q_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial q_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial q_z}{\partial z}\right) dx\, dy\, dz = -\nabla \cdot \vec{q} \, dV$$

### Step 4: Apply Fourier's Law Component-wise

From $\vec{q} = -k \nabla T$, the components are:

$$q_x = -k\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}, \quad q_y = -k\frac{\partial T}{\partial y}, \quad q_z = -k\frac{\partial T}{\partial z}$$

Substituting:

$$\text{Net In} = -\nabla \cdot (-k\nabla T) \, dV = k \nabla^2 T \, dV$$

(assuming constant $k$, i.e., homogeneous isotropic medium).

### Step 5: Rate of Internal Heat Generation

$$\dot{E}_{gen} = \dot{q}_{gen} \, dV$$

### Step 6: Rate of Energy Storage

The energy stored = (mass) × (specific heat) × (rate of temperature change):

$$\dot{E}_{stored} = \rho \, c_p \, \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} \, dV$$

### Step 7: Energy Balance

Combine all terms:

$$k \nabla^2 T \, dV + \dot{q}_{gen} \, dV = \rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} \, dV$$

Dividing by $dV$:

$$k \nabla^2 T + \dot{q}_{gen} = \rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}$$

### Step 8: Final Standard Form

Divide throughout by $k$ and use $\alpha = k / (\rho c_p)$:

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 T + \frac{\dot{q}_{gen}}{k} = \frac{1}{\alpha} \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}}$$

---

## 3.3 Special Cases of the Heat Diffusion Equation

| Case | Conditions | Simplified Form | Application |
|------|------------|------------------|-------------|
| **Steady-state, no source** | $\partial T/\partial t = 0$, $\dot{q}_{gen} = 0$ | $\nabla^2 T = 0$ (Laplace Eq.) | Plane wall, cylinder |
| **Steady-state, with source** | $\partial T/\partial t = 0$ | $\nabla^2 T + \dot{q}_{gen}/k = 0$ (Poisson Eq.) | Nuclear fuel rods |
| **Transient, no source** | $\dot{q}_{gen} = 0$ | $\nabla^2 T = (1/\alpha)\partial T/\partial t$ | Cooling of ingots |
| **Transient, with source** | General form | Full equation | Curling of nuclear cores |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!EXCALIDRAW]
> **Problem:** A glass window pane of thickness 5 mm and area 1 m × 1.5 m has inner surface temperature 20°C and outer surface temperature 5°C. The thermal conductivity of glass is 0.78 W/(m·K). Calculate:
> (a) The steady-state heat transfer rate through the window.
> (b) The temperature at the midpoint of the wall.

### Solution:

**Given:**
- $L = 5$ mm $= 0.005$ m
- $A = 1 \times 1.5 = 1.5$ m²
- $T_1 = 20°C$, $T_2 = 5°C$
- $k = 0.78$ W/(m·K)

**Part (a):** Heat transfer rate using derived formula:

$$Q = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L} = \frac{0.78 \times 1.5 \times (20 - 5)}{0.005}$$

$$Q = \frac{0.78 \times 1.5 \times 15}{0.005} = \frac{17.55}{0.005}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 3510 \text{ W} = 3.51 \text{ kW}}$$

**Part (b):** Temperature at midpoint ($x = L/2$):

$$T\left(\frac{L}{2}\right) = T_1 - \frac{(T_1 - T_2)}{L} \cdot \frac{L}{2} = T_1 - \frac{T_1 - T_2}{2}$$

$$T = 20 - \frac{15}{2} = 20 - 7.5$$

$$\boxed{T = 12.5°C}$$

This confirms the **linear temperature distribution** within the wall.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
