W per meter square"] style SOURCE fill:#d63031,color:#fff style EMIT fill:#e17055,color:#fff style ABSORB fill:#fdcb6e,color:#000 style KIRCH fill:#00b894,color:#fff style GOV fill:#0984e3,color:#fff style SINK fill:#74b9ff,color:#000 style NET fill:#6c5ce7,color:#fff ``` ## 4.4 Emissivity Spectrum Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph BB["Blackbody epsilon equals 1 flat line"] BB1["Absorbs all lambda"] BB2["Emits all lambda equally"] BB1 --> BB2 end subgraph GB["Gray Body epsilon equals constant less than 1"] GB1["Constant fraction absorbed"] GB2["E equals epsilon sigma T power 4"] GB1 --> GB2 end subgraph SE["Selective Surface lambda dependent"] SE1["High alpha in solar range"] SE2["Low epsilon in IR range"] SE1 --> SE2 end BB --> GB --> SE style BB1 fill:#2d3436,color:#fff style BB2 fill:#2d3436,color:#fff style GB1 fill:#636e72,color:#fff style GB2 fill:#636e72,color:#fff style SE1 fill:#00cec9,color:#000 style SE2 fill:#00cec9,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State and explain Stefan-Boltzmann law of blackbody radiation. Mention its significance.** **Model Answer:** The Stefan-Boltzmann law states that the **total emissive power** of a blackbody is directly proportional to the **fourth power** of its absolute temperature: $$E_b = \sigma T^{4}$$ where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant. **Significance:** - Forms the foundation of radiation heat transfer calculations between surfaces. - The $T^4$ dependence explains why even small temperature increases cause dramatic increases in radiated heat — a key reason furnace linings and spacecraft thermal shields must tolerate extreme radiant loads. - Validated experimentally by Josef Stefan (1879) and derived theoretically by Boltzmann (1884) using thermodynamic + electromagnetic arguments. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is Wien's displacement law? Give its mathematical form and physical significance.** **Model Answer:** Wien's displacement law states that the wavelength at which the spectral emissive power of a blackbody is maximum is inversely proportional to its absolute temperature: $$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2897.8 \; \mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$$ **Physical Significance:** - As temperature rises, the peak of the emission spectrum shifts toward shorter wavelengths (toward visible and UV). - Explains color change of heated iron: dull red → orange → white → blue-white as $T$ increases. - Used in optical pyrometry and astronomical temperature measurement (star color → surface temperature). --- ## 5.2 📝 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice — KTU ESE Pattern) --- ### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Derive the **Stefan-Boltzmann law** $E_b = \sigma T^{4}$ by integrating Planck's spectral distribution over all wavelengths. Clearly state all physical constants. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A blackbody surface at $2000\,\text{K}$ is replaced by a gray surface with emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.6$ at the same temperature. Calculate the percentage reduction in total emissive power. If this gray surface now exchanges radiation with a large surrounding enclosure at $300\,\text{K}$, find the net radiative heat flux. **(7 Marks)** #### 🟢 Model Solution — (a) **Step 1:** Write Planck's spectral distribution: $$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}\left[\exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1\right]}$$ **Step 2:** Integrate over all wavelengths: $$E_b = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}\left[\exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1\right]} \, d\lambda$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $x = C_2/(\lambda T)$, so $d\lambda = -C_2\,dx/(x^{2}T)$ and $\lambda = C_2/(xT)$: $$E_b = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{C_1 \, x^{5} T^{5}}{C_2^{5}(e^{x} - 1)} \cdot \frac{C_2}{x^{2} T}\, dx = \frac{C_1 T^{4}}{C_2^{4}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1}\,dx$$ **Step 4:** Evaluate standard integral: $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty}\frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1}dx = \frac{\pi^{4}}{15}$ $$E_b = \frac{C_1 \pi^{4}}{15 C_2^{4}}\, T^{4} = \sigma T^{4}$$ **Step 5:** Constants: $C_1 = 2\pi h c^{2} = 0.374 \times 10^{-15}\,\text{W}\cdot\text{m}^2$, $C_2 = hc/k = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$, and $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$. #### 🟢 Model Solution — (b) **Step 1:** Blackbody emissive power at $2000\,\text{K}$: $$E_b = \sigma T^{4} = (5.67 \times 10^{-8})(2000)^{4} = (5.67 \times 10^{-8})(1.6 \times 10^{13}) = 9.07 \times 10^{5}\,\text{W/m}^2$$ **Step 2:** Gray surface emissive power: $$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4} = 0.6 \times 9.07 \times 10^{5} = 5.44 \times 10^{5}\,\text{W/m}^2$$ **Step 3:** Percentage reduction: $$\%\text{ reduction} = \frac{9.07 \times 10^{5} - 5.44 \times 10^{5}}{9.07 \times 10^{5}} \times 100 = 40\%$$ **Step 4:** Net radiative heat flux with surroundings at $T_{\text{sur}} = 300\,\text{K}$: $$q = \varepsilon \sigma (T^{4} - T_{\text{sur}}^{4})$$ $$q = 0.6 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\left[(2000)^{4} - (300)^{4}\right]$$ $$q = 0.6 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1.6 \times 10^{13} - 8.1 \times 10^{9})$$ $$q \approx 0.6 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.6 \times 10^{13} \approx 5.44 \times 10^{5}\,\text{W/m}^2$$ --- ### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Starting from Planck's distribution, derive **Wien's displacement law** $\lambda_{\max} T = 2897.8\,\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** The sun has surface temperature $T_{\text{sun}} = 5800\,\text{K}$. Treating it as a blackbody, calculate (i) the wavelength of maximum emission, (ii) total emissive power, and (iii) compare with the Earth's blackbody temperature ($T_{\text{earth}} = 288\,\text{K}$) by computing the ratio of peak wavelengths and emissive powers. **(7 Marks)** #### 🟢 Model Solution — (a) **Step 1:** Differentiate $E_{b\lambda}$ w.r.t. $\lambda$ and set to zero. Using the substitution $u = C_2/(\lambda_{\max}T)$ and following logarithmic differentiation (as derived in Section 3.2), we obtain the transcendental equation: $$5(1 - e^{-u}) = u$$ **Step 2:** Numerical solution yields $u = 4.9651$. **Step 3:** Therefore: $$\lambda_{\max} T = \frac{C_2}{u} = \frac{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}}{4.9651} = 2.8978 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda_{\max} T = 2897.8\;\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}}$$ #### 🟢 Model Solution — (b) **(i)** Peak wavelength of Sun: $$\lambda_{\max,\text{sun}} = \frac{2897.8}{5800} = 0.4996\;\mu\text{m} \approx 0.5\;\mu\text{m}\;(\text{green light!})$$ **(ii)** Total emissive power of Sun: $$E_{b,\text{sun}} = (5.67 \times 10^{-8})(5800)^{4} = 6.42 \times 10^{7}\,\text{W/m}^2$$ **(iii)** Ratios: $$\frac{\lambda_{\max,\text{earth}}}{\lambda_{\max,\text{sun}}} = \frac{T_{\text{sun}}}{T_{\text{earth}}} = \frac{5800}{288} \approx 20.14$$ Earth's peak wavelength: $$\lambda_{\max,\text{earth}} = \frac{2897.8}{288} = 10.06\;\mu\text{m}\;(\text{thermal IR region})$$ $$\frac{E_{b,\text{sun}}}{E_{b,\text{earth}}} = \left(\frac{5800}{288}\right)^{4} = (20.14)^{4} \approx 1.646 \times 10^{5}$$ The Sun emits ~$1.65 \times 10^{5}$ times more energy per unit area than Earth — confirming the dominance of $T^4$ dependence. --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Trap:** Students often write $E_b$ in $\text{kW/m}^2$ without converting. Always keep SI units (W/m²) unless explicitly asked. > 2. **Temperature Trap:** Stefan-Boltzmann uses **absolute temperature (K)**, not °C. If the question gives 1500 °C, convert to 1773 K first. > 3. **Constant Confusion:** Mixing up $C_1$ and $C_2$ definitions — $C_1$ has $h$, $c$ only; $C_2$ has $h$, $c$, $k$. Board examiners check this. > 4. **Emissivity Oversight:** Forgetting to multiply by $\varepsilon$ in real-body problems. Marks deducted even if the rest is correct. > 5. **Derivative Sign Error:** In Wien's law derivation, sign slip leads to $(e^x - 1)^{-1}$ vs $(1 - e^{-x})^{-1}$ — practice the algebra carefully. --- ## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute KTU Revision):** > > ✅ **Blackbody = perfect absorber + perfect emitter** ($\alpha = \varepsilon = 1$). > > ✅ **Planck's Law:** $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}[e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1]}$ — the fundamental spectral equation. > > ✅ **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** $E_b = \sigma T^{4}$ — total energy over **all** wavelengths. > > ✅ **Wien's Law:** $\lambda_{\max} T = 2897.8\,\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$ — peak wavelength inversely proportional to $T$. > > ✅ **Kirchhoff's Law:** $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ — good emitter = good absorber at each wavelength. > > ✅ **Real surface:** $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ where $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$. > > ✅ **Constants to memorize:** > - $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ > - $C_1 = 0.374 \times 10^{-15}\,\text{W}\cdot\text{m}^2$ > - $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$ > > ✅ **Three laws interconnection:** Planck integrates → Stefan-Boltzmann; Planck differentiates → Wien's law. > > ✅ **Always use Kelvin** for $T$ in every formula. > > ✅ **Net radiation** between two blackbodies: $q = \sigma(T_1^{4} - T_2^{4})$. > > ✅ **Engineering links:** Furnaces ($\varepsilon \approx 0.9$), Solar collectors (selective surfaces), Spacecraft (low-$\varepsilon$ MLI), Pyrometers (Wien's law), Tungsten filament bulbs.

topics.title: Stefan-Boltzmann Law and Planck's Law, Heat Exchangers: Introduction and Types of Heat Exchangers, Parallel Flow and Counter-Flow Heat Exchangers, Concept of Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient Using LMTD and NTU Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stefan-Boltzmann Law and Planck's Law, Heat Exchangers: Introduction and Types of Heat Exchangers, Parallel Flow and Counter-Flow Heat Exchangers, Concept of Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient Using LMTD and NTU Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION AND INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Stefan-Boltzmann Law

### Formal Definition
The **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** states that the total energy radiated per unit surface area of a blackbody per unit time (the blackbody emissive power) is directly proportional to the **fourth power of the blackbody's absolute thermodynamic temperature**.

$$E_b = \sigma \, T^4$$

Where:
- $E_b$ = Blackbody emissive power (W/m²)
- $\sigma$ = **Stefan-Boltzmann constant = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature of the surface (K)

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "The Glow Factor":** Imagine a piece of iron being heated in a furnace. At 300 K you feel no heat from it; at 600 K you feel warmth; at 1000 K it glows dull red; at 1500 K it glows bright orange-white. The intensity of glow does **not** increase linearly with temperature — it explodes with the **fourth power**. Doubling the temperature increases the radiated heat by a factor of **2⁴ = 16 times**! This is the Stefan-Boltzmann law in action.

### Key Distinction – Real Surfaces
Real (non-black) bodies are characterized by **emissivity ($\varepsilon$)**, giving:

$$E = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^4$$

For a **gray body** (where $\varepsilon$ is constant over all wavelengths):
$$E = \varepsilon \, E_b = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^4$$

---

## 1.2 Planck's Law

### Formal Definition
**Planck's Law** describes the spectral distribution of electromagnetic radiation emitted by a blackbody in thermal equilibrium at a given temperature $T$. It gives the monochromatic emissive power as a function of wavelength $\lambda$:

$$E_{\lambda,b}(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1\right]}$$

Where:
- $C_1 = 2\pi h c^2 = 3.7418 \times 10^8 \text{ W}\cdot\mu\text{m}^4/\text{m}^2$
- $C_2 = \dfrac{hc}{k_B} = 1.4388 \times 10^4 \mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$
- $h$ = Planck's constant = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant = **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- $c$ = Speed of light = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "Rainbow of a Furnace":** As you heat a metal, it does not just emit "heat". It first glows red (long wavelength), then yellow, then white-blue (shorter wavelength). Planck's law precisely maps *how much energy is radiated at each wavelength* (color) for a given temperature. It is the "fingerprint" of thermal radiation. Integrating this fingerprint over all wavelengths gives back the Stefan-Boltzmann law.

### Wien's Displacement Law (Consequence of Planck's Law)
The wavelength at which the spectral emissive power is maximum:

$$\lambda_{max} \, T = 2898 \, \mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Planck's Law matters for KTU:** It is the foundation from which **both** the Stefan-Boltzmann law AND the Wien's displacement law can be mathematically derived. Planck's law is considered the most fundamental of the three radiation laws.

---

## 1.3 Heat Exchangers

### Formal Definition
A **Heat Exchanger** is a device that facilitates the transfer of heat between two or more fluids (or between a fluid and a solid surface) at different temperatures, while keeping them physically separated (no direct mixing).

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "Tea in a Cup":** When you pour hot tea into a steel cup, the cup wall transfers heat from the hot tea to the cold air outside — without the air ever touching the tea. The cup wall is essentially a *simple heat exchanger*. Industrial heat exchangers do the same job, but with much greater efficiency and on a much larger scale (e.g., in power plants, refrigerators, chemical plants, and air conditioners).

### Primary Classification of Heat Exchangers
| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Recuperator** | Both fluids flow simultaneously through the exchanger, separated by a solid wall (steady-state). |
| **Regenerator** | Hot and cold fluids alternately flow through the same matrix (unsteady periodic). |
| **Direct Contact** | Hot and cold fluids mix (open systems). |

### Flow Arrangement Classification
1. **Parallel-flow (Co-current)** — both fluids enter at the same end and flow in the *same direction*.
2. **Counter-flow (Counter-current)** — fluids enter at *opposite ends* and flow in *opposite directions*.
3. **Cross-flow** — fluid directions are perpendicular.

### Construction Type
- **Shell-and-tube** (most common industrial type)
- **Plate heat exchanger**
- **Finned tube**
- **Compact heat exchanger**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Stefan-Boltzmann Law — Theoretical Basis

### Derivation Logic (From Planck's Law)
1. Planck's law gives spectral emissive power $E_{\lambda,b}(\lambda, T)$.
2. The **total** emissive power is the integral of this over **all wavelengths** from 0 to ∞.
3. Performing the integration (using standard integrals involving exponential functions) yields:

$$E_b = \int_0^\infty E_{\lambda,b}\,d\lambda = \sigma T^4$$

### Physical Constants Used

| Symbol | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\sigma$ (Stefan-Boltzmann) | $5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | W/(m²·K⁴) |
| $h$ (Planck) | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | J·s |
| $k_B$ (Boltzmann) | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ | J/K |
| $c$ (Speed of light) | $2.998 \times 10^{8}$ | m/s |
| $C_1$ (First radiation) | $3.7418 \times 10^{8}$ | W·μm⁴/m² |
| $C_2$ (Second radiation) | $1.4388 \times 10^{4}$ | μm·K |
| Wien's constant | $2898$ | μm·K |

### Gray Body Radiation
For a **real surface** (gray body) at temperature $T_s$ surrounded by a much larger surface at $T_{sur}$:

$$q = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$$

### Radiation Shield
When $N$ identical radiation shields (large, parallel, gray, same emissivity $\varepsilon$) are placed between two large parallel plates:

$$q = \frac{1}{N+1} \cdot \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{2}{\varepsilon} - 1}$$

---

## 2.2 Heat Exchanger Theory — Operating Principle

### Energy Balance
For a heat exchanger with **no heat loss** to surroundings and **no phase change**:

$$Q = \dot{m}_h \, c_{p,h} \, (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c} \, (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$

### Capacity Rate
The capacity rate of a fluid stream:

$$C = \dot{m} \, c_p \quad \text{[W/K]}$$

For a heat exchanger, define:
- $C_{min} = \min(C_h, C_c)$
- $C_{max} = \max(C_h, C_c)$
- $C_r = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} \leq 1$ (**heat capacity ratio**)

---

## 2.3 LMTD Method (Log Mean Temperature Difference)

### Governing Equation
Heat transfer rate in a heat exchanger:

$$Q = U \, A \, \Delta T_{lm}$$

Where $\Delta T_{lm}$ is the **log-mean temperature difference**.

For **Parallel Flow**:

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

with $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$.

For **Counter Flow**:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}, \quad \Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$$

### Correction Factor $F$
For complex arrangements (cross-flow, multi-pass shell-and-tube), a correction factor $F$ is applied:

$$Q = U \, A \, F \, \Delta T_{lm,CF}$$

where $\Delta T_{lm,CF}$ is the log-mean temperature difference calculated as if the heat exchanger were **counter-flow**.

---

## 2.4 NTU Method (Number of Transfer Units)

### Definition
The **Number of Transfer Units (NTU)** is a dimensionless parameter:

$$NTU = \frac{U \, A}{C_{min}}$$

### Effectiveness $\varepsilon$
The ratio of actual heat transfer to the *maximum possible* heat transfer:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{C_h(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})}{C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})}$$

where $Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$.

### ε–NTU Relations

| Configuration | Effectiveness $\varepsilon$ |
|---|---|
| **Parallel Flow** | $\dfrac{1 - \exp\!\left[-NTU(1+C_r)\right]}{1 + C_r}$ |
| **Counter Flow** ($C_r < 1$) | $\dfrac{1 - \exp\!\left[-NTU(1-C_r)\right]}{1 - C_r \exp\!\left[-NTU(1-C_r)\right]}$ |
| **Counter Flow** ($C_r = 1$) | $\dfrac{NTU}{1 + NTU}$ |
| **Shell & Tube (1 shell pass, 2 tube passes)** | $2\left\{ (1+C_r) + \sqrt{1+C_r^2}\,\dfrac{1+\exp(-NTU\sqrt{1+C_r^2})}{1-\exp(-NTU\sqrt{1+C_r^2})} \right\}^{-1}$ |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | Stefan-Boltzmann (blackbody) |
| 2 | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Stefan-Boltzmann (gray body) |
| 3 | $E_{\lambda,b} = \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^5\left[\exp(C_2/\lambda T)-1\right]}$ | Planck's spectral law |
| 4 | $\lambda_{max} T = 2898\,\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$ | Wien's displacement |
| 5 | $Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}$ | LMTD heat transfer rate |
| 6 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | LMTD formula |
| 7 | $Q = U A F \Delta T_{lm,CF}$ | LMTD with correction |
| 8 | $NTU = \dfrac{U A}{C_{min}}$ | NTU definition |
| 9 | $C_r = C_{min}/C_{max}$ | Heat capacity ratio |
| 10 | $\varepsilon = Q_{actual}/Q_{max}$ | Effectiveness definition |
| 11 | $\dfrac{1}{UA} = \dfrac{1}{U_i A_i} = \dfrac{1}{h_i A_i} + \dfrac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2\pi k L} + \dfrac{1}{h_o A_o}$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient (cylindrical) |
| 12 | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient (plane wall) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table:** In all the equations above, $\ln$ denotes natural logarithm. The dimensions of $NTU$ are dimensionless. Emissivity $\varepsilon$ is unitless, $0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Stefan-Boltzmann / Planck**: Solar collectors, infrared thermography, fire safety, spacecraft thermal design, furnace design, cryogenic storage.
- **Heat Exchangers**: Power generation (condensers, boilers), HVAC (chillers, cooling coils), chemical reactors, petroleum refineries, refrigeration, food processing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS AND ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Stefan-Boltzmann Law from Planck's Law

### Starting Point
Planck's spectral emissive power:

$$E_{\lambda,b}(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[\exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1\right]}$$

The total hemispherical emissive power is the integral of the spectral emissive power over all wavelengths:

$$E_b(T) = \int_0^{\infty} E_{\lambda,b}(\lambda, T)\,d\lambda$$

### Step 1: Substitution
Let $x = \dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}$, then $dx = -\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda^2 T}\,d\lambda$, hence:

$$d\lambda = -\frac{\lambda^2 T}{C_2}\,dx$$

Also, $\lambda = \dfrac{C_2}{xT}$, so $\lambda^5 = \dfrac{C_2^5}{x^5 T^5}$.

### Step 2: Apply substitution

$$E_b(T) = \int_{\infty}^{0} \frac{C_1}{\dfrac{C_2^5}{x^5 T^5}\left[e^x - 1\right]} \left(-\frac{\lambda^2 T}{C_2}\right) dx$$

Simplifying the limits (swapping returns negative sign):

$$E_b(T) = \frac{C_1 T^4}{C_2^4} \int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1}\,dx$$

### Step 3: Use the Standard Integral
The definite integral:

$$\int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1}\,dx = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$

### Step 4: Final Result

$$E_b(T) = \frac{C_1 T^4}{C_2^4} \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15} = \frac{2\pi^5 k_B^4}{15 c^2 h^3}\,T^4 = \sigma T^4$$

Defining the **Stefan-Boltzmann constant**:

$$\sigma = \frac{2\pi^5 k_B^4}{15 c^2 h^3} \approx 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W/(m}^2\text{·K}^4)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reasoning Summary:** The integral of $\dfrac{x^3}{e^x-1}$ from 0 to ∞ gives the famous factor $\pi^4/15$. This factor arises because the same integral appears in the derivation of Planck's law using quantum statistics (Bose-Einstein). The law is thus deeply tied to quantum mechanics.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of LMTD for Parallel Flow Heat Exchanger

### Setup
For a differential area element $dA$ in a parallel-flow heat exchanger, the temperature difference between hot and cold fluids is $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$.

### Energy Balance on the Element

$$dQ = -C_h\,dT_h = C_c\,dT_c = U\,dA\,\Delta T$$

So:
$$dT_h = -\frac{U\,dA\,\Delta T}{C_h}, \quad dT_c = \frac{U\,dA\,\Delta T}{C_c}$$

### Step 1: Differential Change in $\Delta T$

$$d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c = -\frac{U\,dA\,\Delta T}{C_h} - \frac{U\,dA\,\Delta T}{C_c}$$

$$d(\Delta T) = -U\,dA\,\Delta T\left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right)$$

### Step 2: Define
Let $m = U\left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right)$, a constant. Then:

$$\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -m\,dA$$

### Step 3: Integrate from inlet ($A=0$, $\Delta T = \Delta T_1$) to outlet ($A=A$, $\Delta T = \Delta T_2$)

$$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -m\int_0^A dA$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -U A \left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right)$$

### Step 4: Express in terms of $Q$
The total heat transferred: $Q = C_h(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = C_c(T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$.

Using $Q = C_h \Delta T_1 - C_h \Delta T_2$ and $Q = C_c \Delta T_2 - C_c \Delta T_1$:

$$U A = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c}}$$

### Step 5: Substitute Back and Solve for $Q$

$$Q = U A \, \Delta T_{lm} = U A \, \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$$

This is the famous **LMTD equation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reasoning Logic:** The key insight is that the *temperature difference itself* decreases exponentially along the flow direction. The LMTD is the "effective" constant temperature difference that, if applied over the whole area, would give the same total heat transfer.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison: Parallel vs Counter Flow

### Key Differences

| Feature | Parallel Flow | Counter Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature variation of hot fluid | Decreases monotonically | Decreases monotonically |
| Temperature variation of cold fluid | Increases monotonically | Increases monotonically |
| $\Delta T_1$ (inlet) | $T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$ | $T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ |
| $\Delta T_2$ (outlet) | $T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$ | $T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ |
| Maximum possible heat transfer | $C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$ (only if $C_h = C_c$) | Always $C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$ |
| Cold fluid outlet temperature limit | Cannot exceed $T_{h,out}$ | Can exceed $T_{h,out}$ |
| Heat transfer effectiveness | Lower for same area | Higher for same area |
| Industrial preference | Rarely used (when uniform heating is required, e.g., viscous fluids) | Most preferred |

### Mathematical Statement
For the same $U$, $A$, $C_h$, $C_c$, and inlet temperatures:

$$\Delta T_{lm,\text{counter}} \geq \Delta T_{lm,\text{parallel}}$$

Equality only holds when $C_h = C_c$ (equal capacity rates).

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Effectiveness for Counter-Flow Exchanger

### Starting Setup
For a counter-flow exchanger, by similar differential analysis:

$$\frac{d(\Delta T)}{dA} = U \Delta T \left(\frac{1}{C_h} - \frac{1}{C_c}\right)$$

Let $C_h = C_{min}$ and $C_c = C_{max}$ (WLOG), so $C_r = C_{min}/C_{max}$.

### Integrating from $A=0$ ($\Delta T = \Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$) to $A=A$ ($\Delta T = \Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$):

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -U A \left(\frac{1}{C_{min}} - \frac{1}{C_{max}}\right)$$

### Substituting and using $NTU = UA/C_{min}$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -NTU(1 - C_r)$$

### Expressing in terms of effectiveness:
The maximum possible heat transfer: $Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$.

Energy balance on hot fluid: $Q = C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$, so $T_{h,out} = T_{h,in} - Q/C_{min}$.
Energy balance on cold fluid: $Q = C_{max}(T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$, so $T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + Q/C_{max}$.

### Computing $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) - \dfrac{Q}{C_{max}} = (T_{h,in}-T_{c,in})(1 - \varepsilon C_r)$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) - \dfrac{Q}{C_{min}} = (T_{h,in}-T_{c,in})(1 - \varepsilon)$$

### Substituting into the logarithmic equation:

$$\frac{1 - \varepsilon}{1 - \varepsilon C_r} = \exp\!\left[-NTU(1 - C_r)\right]$$

### Solving for $\varepsilon$:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp\!\left[-NTU(1 - C_r)\right]}{1 - C_r \exp\!\left[-NTU(1 - C_r)\right]}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reasoning Summary:** The effectiveness is bounded between 0 and 1. For a counter-flow exchanger, when $C_r \to 0$, the cold fluid can theoretically be heated to nearly the inlet temperature of the hot fluid — meaning the effectiveness can approach unity even for modest $NTU$ values. This is why counter-flow is industrially preferred.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**State and explain Stefan-Boltzmann law. Mention the significance of the Stefan-Boltzmann constant.**

**Model Answer:**
The Stefan-Boltzmann law states that the total hemispherical emissive power of a blackbody is directly proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature:

$$E_b = \sigma T^4$$

where $E_b$ is in W/m², $T$ is in Kelvin, and $\sigma$ is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant.

**Significance of $\sigma$:** The constant $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴) represents the emissive power of a blackbody at 1 K. It encapsulates fundamental constants: $\sigma = \dfrac{2\pi^5 k_B^4}{15 c^2 h^3}$, which links thermal radiation to quantum mechanics.

For a real (gray) surface, the equation is modified as $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$, where $\varepsilon$ is the emissivity (0 ≤ ε ≤ 1).

**Keyword tags:** Fourth power dependence, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴), blackbody, gray body, emissivity. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]
**What is a heat exchanger? Differentiate between parallel flow and counter flow heat exchangers.**

**Model Answer:**
A heat exchanger is a device that transfers heat between two or more fluids (or between a fluid and a solid) at different temperatures without mixing them.

**Differences:**

| Feature | Parallel Flow | Counter Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Flow direction | Same direction | Opposite direction |
| Outlet temperature of cold fluid | Cannot exceed $T_{h,out}$ | Can exceed $T_{h,out}$ |
| LMTD value | Lower | Higher |
| Heat transfer effectiveness | Lower | Higher |
| Industrial use | Limited | Widely preferred |

**Keyword tags:** Recuperator, co-current, counter-current, LMTD, effectiveness. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — With Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]

**a) Define Planck's law of blackbody radiation. Derive the Stefan-Boltzmann law from Planck's law.** (7 marks)

**b) A plate at 800 K radiates energy to a surrounding surface at 300 K. The plate has an emissivity of 0.7 and a surface area of 0.05 m². Determine the net rate of heat transfer by radiation. If three identical radiation shields (ε = 0.5 each) are placed between the plate and the surrounding surface, calculate the percentage reduction in heat transfer.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Planck's Law Definition:**
Planck's law gives the spectral emissive power of a blackbody at wavelength $\lambda$ and temperature $T$:

$$E_{\lambda,b}(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[\exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1\right]}$$

with $C_1 = 3.7418 \times 10^8$ W·μm⁴/m² and $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^4$ μm·K.

**Derivation of Stefan-Boltzmann Law:**

Step 1: Total emissive power is the integral of Planck's law over all wavelengths:

$$E_b = \int_0^\infty E_{\lambda,b}(\lambda, T)\,d\lambda = \int_0^\infty \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[\exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1\right]}\,d\lambda$$

Step 2: Apply substitution $x = C_2/(\lambda T)$:

$$E_b = \frac{C_1 T^4}{C_2^4} \int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1}\,dx$$

Step 3: Use the standard result $\int_0^\infty \dfrac{x^3}{e^x - 1}\,dx = \dfrac{\pi^4}{15}$:

$$E_b = \frac{C_1 T^4}{C_2^4} \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$

Step 4: Simplifying with $C_1 = 2\pi h c^2$ and $C_2 = hc/k_B$:

$$E_b = \frac{2\pi^5 k_B^4}{15 c^2 h^3} T^4 = \sigma T^4$$

This is the **Stefan-Boltzmann law**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Given:**
$T_1 = 800$ K, $T_2 = 300$ K, $\varepsilon_1 = 0.7$, $A = 0.05$ m²

**Step 1: Net heat transfer without shields (gray surface):**

$$Q_{no\,shield} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$$

$$T_1^4 = (800)^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{11} \text{ K}^4$$

$$T_2^4 = (300)^4 = 8.1 \times 10^{9} \text{ K}^4$$

$$T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 4.015 \times 10^{11} \text{ K}^4$$

$$Q_{no\,shield} = 0.7 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.05 \times 4.015 \times 10^{11}$$

$$Q_{no\,shield} = 0.7 \times 5.67 \times 0.05 \times 4.015 \times 10^{3}$$

$$Q_{no\,shield} = 0.7 \times 5.67 \times 0.05 \times 4015 = 796.78 \text{ W} \approx 797 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: With N = 3 shields, $\varepsilon_{shield} = 0.5$:**

$$Q_{with\,shields} = \frac{1}{N+1} \cdot \frac{\sigma A (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{2}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{2(N+1)}{\varepsilon_{shield}} - (N+2)}$$

For identical large parallel plates with shields between them:

$$Q_{with\,shields} = \frac{1}{N+1} \cdot \frac{\sigma A (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} + \dfrac{2N}{\varepsilon_{shield}} - (N+1) \cdot \text{[simplified]}}$$

Using the standard formula for parallel plate with N identical shields:

$$Q_{with\,shields} = \frac{1}{N+1} \cdot \frac{\sigma A (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{2}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{2(N-1)}{\varepsilon_{shield}} \cdot \text{(where applicable)}}$$

For simplicity with two large plates and $N$ identical shields of emissivity $\varepsilon_s$, the textbook result is:

$$Q_{with\,shields} = \frac{\sigma A (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{(N+1)\left[\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1\right] + N\left[\dfrac{2}{\varepsilon_s} - 1\right]}$$

For this problem, assuming both bounding surfaces are the same as the plate (so $\varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon_1 = 0.7$ is the plate and surrounding is also gray):

$$Q_{with\,shields} = \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.05 \times 4.015 \times 10^{11}}{4 \left[\dfrac{1}{0.7} + \dfrac{1}{0.7} - 1\right] + 3 \left[\dfrac{2}{0.5} - 1\right]}$$

$$= \frac{1138.4}{4[1.4286 + 1.4286 - 1] + 3[4 - 1]} = \frac{1138.4}{4[1.8572] + 3[3]} = \frac{1138.4}{7.4288 + 9} = \frac{1138.4}{16.4288}$$

$$Q_{with\,shields} \approx 69.30 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Percentage reduction:**

$$\%\text{ Reduction} = \frac{Q_{no\,shield} - Q_{with\,shields}}{Q_{no\,shield}} \times 100 = \frac{797 - 69.30}{797} \times 100 \approx 91.3\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often forget to use the *fourth power* of temperature and mistakenly use $T_1 - T_2$ instead. Always raise to the fourth power. Also, when computing $\sigma A (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$, do not pre-multiply $\sigma$ by $A$ in K⁴ units; convert to consistent SI (m², K). Marks are typically lost for unit mismatches.

**Answer: $Q_{no\,shield} \approx 797$ W; Reduction ≈ 91.3%** **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]

**a) What is LMTD? Derive the LMTD expression for a parallel flow heat exchanger.** (7 marks)

**b) In a counter-flow heat exchanger, oil ($c_p = 2100$ J/kg·K) enters at 450 K with a mass flow rate of 0.5 kg/s and is cooled to 350 K. Cooling water ($c_p = 4180$ J/kg·K) enters at 300 K with a mass flow rate of 0.3 kg/s. Calculate (i) the outlet temperature of water, (ii) the LMTD, and (iii) the heat transfer area if the overall heat transfer coefficient is 800 W/(m²·K).** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Definition:** The Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) is defined as the temperature difference that, if applied uniformly over the heat exchanger area, would give the same total heat transfer rate as actually occurs with the varying temperature differences along the exchanger.

**Derivation for Parallel Flow:**

For a differential area $dA$, the heat transfer:

$$dQ = U\,dA\,\Delta T = U\,dA\,(T_h - T_c)$$

From energy balance:
$$dQ = -C_h\,dT_h = C_c\,dT_c$$

Hence $dT_h = -\dfrac{U\,dA\,\Delta T}{C_h}$ and $dT_c = \dfrac{U\,dA\,\Delta T}{C_c}$.

Differentiating $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$:

$$d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c = -U\,dA\,\Delta T\left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right)$$

Separating variables and integrating from inlet ($\Delta T_1$ at $A=0$) to outlet ($\Delta T_2$ at $A=A$):

$$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U\left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right)\int_0^A dA$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -UA\left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right)$$

Using $Q = C_h(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = C_c(T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$:

$$UA = \frac{Q}{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}\ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{Q = UA \, \Delta T_{lm} = UA \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}}$$

where $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$ for parallel flow. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Given:** Hot fluid (oil): $T_{h,in} = 450$ K, $T_{h,out} = 350$ K, $c_{p,h} = 2100$ J/kg·K, $\dot{m}_h = 0.5$ kg/s.
Cold fluid (water): $T_{c,in} = 300$ K, $c_{p,c} = 4180$ J/kg·K, $\dot{m}_c = 0.3$ kg/s.
$U = 800$ W/(m²·K).

**(i) Outlet temperature of water:**

Capacity rates:
$$C_h = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h} = 0.5 \times 2100 = 1050 \text{ W/K}$$
$$C_c = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} = 0.3 \times 4180 = 1254 \text{ W/K}$$

Heat transfer rate:
$$Q = C_h (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = 1050 \times (450 - 350) = 105{,}000 \text{ W} = 105 \text{ kW}$$

Energy balance on cold fluid:
$$Q = C_c (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$
$$T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + \frac{Q}{C_c} = 300 + \frac{105{,}000}{1254} = 300 + 83.73 = 383.73 \text{ K}$$

**(ii) LMTD (Counter Flow):**

For counter flow:
$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 450 - 383.73 = 66.27 \text{ K}$$
$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 350 - 300 = 50 \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)} = \frac{66.27 - 50}{\ln(66.27/50)} = \frac{16.27}{\ln(1.3254)} = \frac{16.27}{0.2817}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm} \approx 57.75 \text{ K}$$

**(iii) Heat transfer area:**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{105{,}000}{800 \times 57.75} = \frac{105{,}000}{46{,}200} \approx 2.273 \text{ m}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students frequently use the **parallel flow** formula for $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ in a counter-flow problem. **For counter flow:** $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ — note the *opposite* assignment. Marks are deducted heavily for this reversal. Additionally, ensure that the *hot fluid outlet* is hotter than the *cold fluid outlet* in a counter-flow exchanger — if not, the configuration is physically infeasible.

**Answer: (i) $T_{c,out} \approx 383.73$ K, (ii) $\Delta T_{lm} \approx 57.75$ K, (iii) $A \approx 2.27$ m²** **(7 marks)**

---

## TOPIC RECAP AND IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### Quick Revision Checklist — Radiation Laws

- [ ] **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** $E_b = \sigma T^4$; $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴); raise $T$ to the **fourth power** — common source of error.
- [ ] **Planck's Law:** spectral distribution function; depends on $C_1$ and $C_2$; integral over all $\lambda$ gives Stefan-Boltzmann.
- [ ] **Wien's Displacement Law:** $\lambda_{max} T = 2898$ μm·K; useful for finding peak radiation wavelength.
- [ ] **Gray body:** $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$; $\varepsilon$ ranges 0 to 1.
- [ ] **Radiation between surfaces:** $q = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$; for two parallel plates: $q = \dfrac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{(1/\varepsilon_1)+(1/\varepsilon_2)-1}$.
- [ ] **Radiation shields:** $N$ shields reduce $Q$ to $\dfrac{1}{N+1}$ of original (for ideal shields).
- [ ] Stefan-Boltzmann constant is derived from quantum constants: $\sigma = \dfrac{2\pi^5 k_B^4}{15 c^2 h^3}$.

### Quick Revision Checklist — Heat Exchangers

- [ ] **Heat Exchanger:** device to transfer heat between fluids without mixing.
- [ ] **Types:** Recuperator (continuous), Regenerator (periodic), Direct contact (mixing).
- [ ] **LMTD Formula:** $Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}$ where $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$.
- [ ] **Parallel flow:** $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$.
- [ ] **Counter flow:** $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ (reverse order).
- [ ] **Counter flow > Parallel flow** in LMTD and effectiveness.
- [ ] **NTU:** $NTU = UA / C_{min}$; dimensionless.
- [ ] **Heat capacity ratio:** $C_r = C_{min}/C_{max} \leq 1$.
- [ ] **Effectiveness:** $\varepsilon = Q/Q_{max}$ where $Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$.
- [ ] **LMTD method** used when inlet/outlet temperatures are known.
- [ ] **NTU method** used when inlet temperatures and $UA$ are known.
- [ ] **Overall $U$ for tube wall:** $\dfrac{1}{UA} = \dfrac{1}{U_i A_i} = \dfrac{1}{h_i A_i} + \dfrac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2\pi k L} + \dfrac{1}{h_o A_o}$.
- [ ] **Fouling factors** add to total thermal resistance (industrial reality).
- [ ] Always check: Is $C_h \geq C_c$ or vice versa? Identify $C_{min}$ correctly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** For numerical problems, **always identify the hot and cold fluid first**, label all four temperatures ($T_{h,in}, T_{h,out}, T_{c,in}, T_{c,out}$), then compute $C_h$, $C_c$, $C_{min}$, $C_r$, and apply either LMTD or ε–NTU. In counter-flow problems, **double-check** the assignment of $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ — this is where 80% of students lose marks in KTU Board Exams.
